A **Product Development Organization** refers to the structured arrangement of people, departments, resources, and external entities that collaborate to conceive, design, manufacture, and commercialize a new product. In the context of **Safety in Product Design**, this organization forms the governance backbone that enforces safety protocols, regulatory compliance, and risk mitigation at every stage of the product life cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Product Development Organizations are the institutional frameworks—comprising internal teams (marketing, design, manufacturing) and external entities (suppliers, regulators, customers)—that collectively plan, execute, and control the development of a product while ensuring it meets safety, quality, and performance specifications."*

The organization may be **internal** (a single firm) or **extended/enterprise-wide**, encompassing suppliers, distributors, and customers across the **value chain**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Building a House**
> Imagine building a house. You don't just hire one person — you need an **architect** (design), a **civil engineer** (structural safety), an **electrical contractor** (subsystems), a **plumber** (utilities), and a **government inspector** (regulatory body). Each one is a "node" in the **product development organization**.
>
> If even one node fails (e.g., the electrical contractor ignores fire-safety wiring codes), the whole house becomes unsafe — regardless of how beautiful the architect's design is.
>
> Similarly, in product development, **safety is a coordinated responsibility** distributed across multiple organizational nodes.

## 1.3 Core Components of a Product Development Organization

A typical product development organization consists of **five primary internal entities** and **three external entities**:

| Component | Primary Role | Safety Responsibility |
|-----------|--------------|------------------------|
| **Marketing Organization** | Identifies customer needs | Defines safety expectations of users |
| **Design / Engineering Organization** | Creates product concept & specs | Performs FMEA, hazard analysis |
| **Manufacturing Organization** | Produces the product | Ensures process safety, quality control |
| **Quality & Safety Organization** | Audits and validates | Enforces standards (ISO, BIS, IEC) |
| **Management / Leadership** | Approves budgets & timelines | Allocates resources for safety |
| **Suppliers (External)** | Provide components | Safety of supplied parts |
| **Regulators (External)** | Certify compliance | Enforce statutory safety norms |
| **Customers / End Users (External)** | Use the product | Final safety feedback loop |

## 1.4 Key Metrics in Product Development Organizations

The following metrics are widely tracked in KTU-aligned coursework:

- **Time-to-Market (TTM)** — measured in **months**
- **Development Cost Ratio (DCR)** — ratio of R&D spend to total revenue
- **Defect Density (DD)** — defects per thousand units
- **Safety Incident Rate (SIR)** — incidents per 1,000,000 operating hours
- **Compliance Coverage Index (CCI)** — percentage of applicable standards met

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Recall — "M-D-M-Q-S"** stands for **M**arketing, **D**esign, **M**anufacturing, **Q**uality, **S**uppliers — the five pillars of a product development organization.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Product Development Organizations

Product development organizations are classified based on **how authority, responsibility, and communication flow** across teams. The three classical structures are:

### 2.1.1 Functional Organization
- Employees are grouped by **specialty** (mechanical, electrical, software).
- Communication flows **vertically** within departments.
- **Advantage**: Deep technical expertise.
- **Disadvantage**: Cross-functional safety coordination is weak.

### 2.1.2 Projectized Organization
- A **dedicated project manager** has full authority over resources.
- Team members are **temporarily assigned** to the project.
- **Advantage**: Fast decision-making; strong safety ownership.
- **Disadvantage**: Resource duplication across projects.

### 2.1.3 Matrix Organization
- A **hybrid** of functional and projectized forms.
- Employees report to **both** a functional manager and a project manager.
- Three subtypes: **Weak Matrix**, **Balanced Matrix**, **Strong Matrix**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:**
> In a **Strong Matrix**, the **Project Manager** controls the budget and has dominant authority — this is the most preferred structure for safety-critical product development (e.g., aerospace, medical devices).

## 2.2 The Integrated Product Development (IPD) Framework

Modern product development organizations follow the **IPD framework**, which integrates all five core entities **simultaneously from day one**, rather than sequentially.

The IPD framework is governed by the equation:

$$
\text{IPD Effectiveness} = f(\text{Collaboration}, \text{Communication}, \text{Coordination})
$$

Or in expanded analytical form:

$$
E_{\text{IPD}} = \alpha C_c + \beta C_m + \gamma C_s
$$

Where:

- $E_{\text{IPD}}$ = Integrated Product Development Effectiveness Index (dimensionless, $0 \le E_{\text{IPD}} \le 1$)
- $C_c$ = Cross-functional collaboration coefficient
- $C_m$ = Communication efficiency
- $C_s$ = Safety culture index
- $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = Weighting constants where $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Formula / Expression | Unit |
|---------------|---------|----------------------|------|
| $E_{\text{IPD}}$ | IPD Effectiveness Index | $\alpha C_c + \beta C_m + \gamma C_s$ | dimensionless |
| $\text{TTM}$ | Time-to-Market | $\text{Concept freeze date} - \text{Launch date}$ | months |
| $\text{DD}$ | Defect Density | $\dfrac{\text{Number of defects}}{\text{Units produced}} \times 1000$ | defects/k-units |
| $\text{SIR}$ | Safety Incident Rate | $\dfrac{\text{Incidents}}{\text{Operating hours}} \times 10^{6}$ | incidents/10$^6$ hrs |
| $\text{CCI}$ | Compliance Coverage Index | $\dfrac{\text{Standards met}}{\text{Total applicable standards}} \times 100\%$ | percent |
| $\alpha + \beta + \gamma$ | Weight normalization | $= 1$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exams, candidates often confuse **Functional** with **Projectized** organizations. Remember: **Functional = vertical authority**; **Projectized = horizontal project authority**.

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering (Real-World Utility)

In safety-critical industries — **automotive, aerospace, nuclear, medical devices** — the organization structure **directly determines product safety**. For example:

- **Boeing's 787 Dreamliner** used a globally distributed matrix organization with extensive supplier integration, requiring rigorous safety coordination protocols.
- **ISRO's Chandrayaan missions** follow a projectized organizational model with a dedicated Safety Review Board (SRB) reporting directly to the Project Director.

The choice of organization structure is therefore **not an administrative decision** — it is an **engineering safety decision**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Safety Coordination Efficiency (SCE) Model

The **Safety Coordination Efficiency (SCE)** quantifies how effectively safety information propagates through the product development organization. We derive it below step-by-step.

### Step 1: Define the Base Components

Let:

- $N$ = Total number of organizational nodes (departments, teams, suppliers)
- $k$ = Number of safety-critical communication links between nodes
- $\tau_d$ = Average delay (in days) for safety information transfer
- $\tau_{d,\max}$ = Maximum acceptable delay threshold (in days)

### Step 2: Define the Link Density

The link density measures how saturated the organization is with safety-related communication channels:

$$
\rho_{\text{link}} = \frac{k}{\binom{N}{2}}
$$

Where $\binom{N}{2} = \frac{N(N-1)}{2}$ is the total possible pairwise connections between $N$ nodes.

### Step 3: Define the Safety Information Latency Factor

$$
L_{\text{safety}} = \frac{\tau_{d,\max}}{\tau_d}
$$

Where $L_{\text{safety}} > 1$ indicates that safety information travels faster than the maximum acceptable threshold (good), and $L_{\text{safety}} < 1$ indicates a safety risk.

### Step 4: Combine into the Safety Coordination Efficiency

$$
\text{SCE} = \rho_{\text{link}} \times L_{\text{safety}}
$$

$$
\text{SCE} = \frac{k}{\binom{N}{2}} \times \frac{\tau_{d,\max}}{\tau_d}
$$

Substituting the combinatorial expansion:

$$
\text{SCE} = \frac{2k \cdot \tau_{d,\max}}{N(N-1) \cdot \tau_d}
$$

### Step 5: Interpretation

- If $\text{SCE} \to 1$, the organization has near-ideal safety coordination.
- If $\text{SCE} \to 0$, the organization is fragmented and unsafe.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked to "evaluate" an organization's safety performance, always compute SCE using the formula above. Marks are awarded for showing the expanded form with $\binom{N}{2}$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A product development organization has $N = 6$ nodes, $k = 9$ active safety communication links, an average safety information delay $\tau_d = 2$ days, and a maximum acceptable delay $\tau_{d,\max} = 5$ days. Compute the SCE.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute total possible links:

$$
\binom{N}{2} = \binom{6}{2} = \frac{6 \times 5}{2} = 15
$$

**Step 2:** Compute link density:

$$
\rho_{\text{link}} = \frac{k}{\binom{N}{2}} = \frac{9}{15} = 0.60
$$

**Step 3:** Compute latency factor:

$$
L_{\text{safety}} = \frac{\tau_{d,\max}}{\tau_d} = \frac{5}{2} = 2.50
$$

**Step 4:** Compute SCE:

$$
\text{SCE} = 0.60 \times 2.50 = 1.50
$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation — Since $\text{SCE} > 1$, the organization is operating **above** the minimum safety coordination threshold. This indicates strong safety performance.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Three Organization Structures

| Parameter | Functional | Projectized | Matrix (Strong) |
|-----------|------------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Authority Flow** | Vertical | Horizontal | Dual (vertical + horizontal) |
| **Project Manager's Power** | Low | High | Dominant |
| **Resource Utilization** | Efficient | Duplicative | Balanced |
| **Cross-functional Safety Coordination** | Weak | Strong | Strongest |
| **Best for Safety-Critical Projects** | No | Moderate | **Yes (preferred)** |
| **Communication Speed** | Slow | Fast | Moderate–Fast |
| **Cost of Operation** | Low | High | Moderate |
| **SCE (typical range)** | $0.3 - 0.6$ | $0.7 - 1.0$ | $1.0 - 1.5$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Organization Chart (Block-Level Architecture)

The diagram below illustrates how a **functional product development organization** channels safety information vertically.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[TOP MANAGEMENT]
    nodeB[MARKETING DIVISION]
    nodeC[DESIGN DIVISION]
    nodeD[MANUFACTURING DIVISION]
    nodeE[QUALITY AND SAFETY DIVISION]
    nodeF[R AND D DIVISION]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeF

    nodeB --> nodeB1[CUSTOMER NEEDS]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[DESIGN SPECS]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[PRODUCTION]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[SAFETY AUDITS]
    nodeF --> nodeF1[PROTOTYPING]

    nodeC1 -. safety feedback .- nodeE1
    nodeD1 -. process safety .- nodeE1
```

## 4.2 Matrix Organization Chart (Cross-Functional Safety Coordination)

The **matrix structure** enables horizontal (project-based) and vertical (functional) reporting, which is critical for safety in product design.

```mermaid
graph TD
    pmNode[PROJECT MANAGER]
    fmMech[MECHANICAL FUNC MGR]
    fmElec[ELECTRICAL FUNC MGR]
    fmSoft[SOFTWARE FUNC MGR]
    fmSafe[SAFETY FUNC MGR]

    pmNode --> teamMech[MECH LEAD]
    pmNode --> teamElec[ELEC LEAD]
    pmNode --> teamSoft[SW LEAD]
    pmNode --> teamSafe[SAFETY LEAD]

    fmMech --> teamMech
    fmElec --> teamElec
    fmSoft --> teamSoft
    fmSafe --> teamSafe

    teamMech -. safety review .- teamSafe
    teamElec -. safety review .- teamSafe
    teamSoft -. safety review .- teamSafe
```

## 4.3 Integrated Product Development (IPD) Cross-Functional Flow

The IPD model shows how all entities engage **simultaneously**, with safety as a continuous, parallel thread.

```mermaid
graph LR
    mkt[MARKETING]
    dsg[DESIGN]
    mfg[MANUFACTURING]
    qa[QUALITY ASSURANCE]
    saf[SAFETY BOARD]
    sup[SUPPLIERS]
    cus[CUSTOMERS]

    mkt <--> dsg
    dsg <--> mfg
    mfg <--> qa
    saf -. monitors .- mkt
    saf -. monitors .- dsg
    saf -. monitors .- mfg
    saf -. monitors .- qa
    sup --> mfg
    cus --> mkt
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **Solid arrows** = primary workflow.
> - **Dotted arrows** = safety monitoring (continuous oversight).
> - The **Safety Board** has a dotted (advisory/audit) connection to every other node — this is the **key safety governance pattern** in KTU-aligned curricula.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q: Define a Product Development Organization. List its three classical structural types.**

**Model Answer:**

A Product Development Organization is a structured arrangement of people, departments, and resources that collectively plan, design, manufacture, and deliver a new product while ensuring safety and quality compliance.

The three classical structural types are:

1. **Functional Organization** — grouped by specialty.
2. **Projectized Organization** — grouped by project.
3. **Matrix Organization** — hybrid of functional and projectized forms.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the list with one-line explanations.)*

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: Differentiate between a Weak Matrix and a Strong Matrix organization with respect to safety coordination.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Weak Matrix | Strong Matrix |
|--------|-------------|---------------|
| Project Manager Authority | Minimal (coordinator role) | Dominant (controls budget) |
| Functional Manager Authority | Dominant | Reduced |
| Safety Coordination | Weak | Strong |
| Best Suited For | Routine products | **Safety-critical products** |

*(3 marks: comparison expected in tabular/point form.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **Integrated Product Development (IPD) framework**. How does it improve safety outcomes compared to traditional sequential development? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A product development organization has $N = 8$ nodes, $k = 14$ active safety communication links, an average safety information delay $\tau_d = 3$ days, and a maximum acceptable delay $\tau_{d,\max} = 6$ days. Compute the **Safety Coordination Efficiency (SCE)** and comment on the safety performance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — IPD Framework:**

The **Integrated Product Development (IPD) framework** is a concurrent product development model where marketing, design, manufacturing, quality, and safety teams work **in parallel from project inception**, rather than sequentially.

Key features:

- **Concurrent Engineering**: All teams collaborate from day one.
- **Cross-functional Teams**: Employees from multiple disciplines form a single product team.
- **Early Safety Integration**: Hazard analysis (e.g., FMEA) begins during the concept phase, not after prototyping.
- **Shared Responsibility**: Safety is everyone's job, not just the safety department's.
- **Continuous Feedback Loops**: Design changes are immediately reviewed by safety, manufacturing, and quality.

**Safety Outcomes vs. Sequential Development:**

| Aspect | Sequential (Traditional) | IPD |
|--------|--------------------------|-----|
| Hazard detection | Late (after design) | Early (at concept) |
| Rework cost | High | Reduced |
| Safety ownership | Siloed in QA dept | Distributed |
| Compliance risk | Higher | Lower |

*(7 marks: 3 for IPD explanation, 2 for safety benefits, 2 for comparison.)*

---

**Part (b) — SCE Computation:**

**Given:** $N = 8$, $k = 14$, $\tau_d = 3$ days, $\tau_{d,\max} = 6$ days.

**Step 1:** Compute total possible links:

$$
\binom{N}{2} = \binom{8}{2} = \frac{8 \times 7}{2} = 28
$$

**Step 2:** Compute link density:

$$
\rho_{\text{link}} = \frac{k}{\binom{N}{2}} = \frac{14}{28} = 0.50
$$

**Step 3:** Compute latency factor:

$$
L_{\text{safety}} = \frac{\tau_{d,\max}}{\tau_d} = \frac{6}{3} = 2.00
$$

**Step 4:** Compute SCE:

$$
\text{SCE} = \rho_{\text{link}} \times L_{\text{safety}} = 0.50 \times 2.00 = 1.00
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation:**
Since $\text{SCE} = 1.00$, the organization meets the **minimum acceptable safety coordination threshold**. However, there is no safety margin — the organization should aim for $\text{SCE} > 1.20$ for safety-critical products by either reducing $\tau_d$ or increasing $k$.

*(7 marks: 2 each for steps 1–4, 3 for interpretation.)*

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**Q: (a)** Compare and contrast the **Functional, Projectized, and Matrix** organizational structures with reference to safety coordination. Identify which is most suitable for safety-critical product development and justify. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A safety audit of a product development organization reveals: **20 safety incidents** over **2 million operating hours**. Compute the **Safety Incident Rate (SIR)** and assess whether the organization meets the industry benchmark of $\text{SIR} \le 5$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Comparison:**

| Parameter | Functional | Projectized | Matrix (Strong) |
|-----------|------------|-------------|-----------------|
| Authority Flow | Vertical | Horizontal | Dual |
| Project Manager Power | Low | High | Dominant |
| Safety Coordination | Weak | Strong | Strongest |
| Resource Efficiency | High | Low | Moderate |
| Communication Speed | Slow | Fast | Moderate–Fast |

**Most Suitable for Safety-Critical Development: Strong Matrix**

**Justification:**

1. The project manager has dominant authority, enabling **rapid safety decisions**.
2. Functional managers retain technical expertise, ensuring **engineering rigor**.
3. Dual reporting creates **redundant safety oversight**.
4. Widely adopted in aerospace (Boeing, Airbus), medical devices (Medtronic), and automotive (Tesla) industries.
5. Provides the **highest Safety Coordination Efficiency (SCE)** as per analytical models.

*(7 marks: 4 for comparison table, 3 for justification.)*

---

**Part (b) — SIR Computation:**

**Given:** Incidents $= 20$, Operating hours $= 2{,}000{,}000$ hours.

**Step 1:** Apply the SIR formula:

$$
\text{SIR} = \frac{\text{Incidents}}{\text{Operating hours}} \times 10^{6}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$
\text{SIR} = \frac{20}{2{,}000{,}000} \times 10^{6}
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$
\text{SIR} = 10.0 \; \text{incidents per } 10^6 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4: Assessment:**

Since $\text{SIR} = 10.0$ and the benchmark is $\text{SIR} \le 5$, the organization **fails** the safety benchmark by a factor of **2×**. Corrective actions are required: improved hazard analysis, operator training, and redesign of high-risk components.

*(7 marks: 2 for formula, 2 for substitution, 3 for assessment with corrective suggestions.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse **Weak Matrix** with **Strong Matrix** — students routinely interchange them and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. In SCE problems, **always expand $\binom{N}{2}$ as $\frac{N(N-1)}{2}$** — partial marks depend on this expansion.
> 3. For SIR, the factor is **$10^{6}$**, not $10^3$ — incorrect multipliers are a common error.
> 4. In long answers, **draw diagrams wherever possible** — a single labeled block diagram can earn 1–2 extra marks in KTU valuation.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Product Development Organization** = structure of people + departments + external entities that develop a product.
- **Three classical structures**: Functional, Projectized, Matrix.
- **Strong Matrix** is the **preferred structure for safety-critical products**.
- **IPD (Integrated Product Development)** = concurrent, cross-functional, safety-first approach.
- Key external entities: **Suppliers, Regulators, Customers**.
- The **SCE formula**:

$$
\text{SCE} = \frac{2k \cdot \tau_{d,\max}}{N(N-1) \cdot \tau_d}
$$

- The **SIR formula**:

$$
\text{SIR} = \frac{\text{Incidents}}{\text{Operating hours}} \times 10^{6}
$$

- Always aim for $\text{SCE} > 1$ and $\text{SIR} \le 5$ in safety-critical industries.
- The **Safety Board** must have advisory access to **all** organizational nodes — this is non-negotiable in KTU-aligned safety frameworks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The AMF Organization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT747 - SAFETY IN PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** The AMF Organization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The AMF (American Machine and Foundry) Safety Organization** is a classical, hierarchically structured industrial safety management framework originally developed by the American Machine and Foundry Company in the 1960s. It is formally defined as a **systematic, top-down organizational model** in which safety responsibility is distributed across every level of the corporate hierarchy — from the Board of Directors down to the individual shop-floor employee — through clearly defined lines of authority, accountability, communication, and feedback.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (PESFT747 – Safety in Product Design), the AMF Organization is studied as a **benchmark reference model** for embedding product-safety and occupational-safety functions into the design and manufacturing lifecycle of a product.

The four foundational pillars of the AMF Organization are:

| Pillar | Core Idea |
|---|---|
| **Policy** | Written commitment from top management |
| **Organization** | Defined lines of responsibility at every level |
| **Operations** | Procedures, training, inspections, audits |
| **Audit & Review** | Continuous measurement and improvement |

> [!NOTE]
> The AMF model is **prescriptive**, not descriptive. It tells a company *how* safety *must* be organized, who *must* do what, and *how* accountability flows upward.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy: The Human Body's Nervous System.**
> Think of a company as a living body. The **Board of Directors is the brain** — it decides the safety policy. The **Safety Director is the spinal cord** — it transmits policy down and feedback up. The **Supervisors are the motor nerves** — they execute safety actions in the limbs (departments). The **Employees are the muscle fibres** — they actually perform the work, and any "pain" (accident/near-miss) travels back up through the nerves to the brain for correction. Just as a body cannot function if the brain ignores pain signals, a company cannot be safe if the Board ignores shop-floor safety feedback. The AMF Organization formalizes this nervous system on paper.

### 1.3 Why the AMF Model Matters in Product Design Safety

- It forces **design-stage safety thinking**, not just operational safety.
- It establishes **clear Design Safety Review** points analogous to engineering change control.
- It embeds **human factors** into product specifications through documented safety roles.
- It provides a **traceable audit trail** — every safety decision has a documented owner.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (in Bold)

- **Policy Statement** — written, signed by CEO, reviewed annually.
- **Safety Committee** — meets **at least monthly**, min. **50% worker representation**.
- **Incident Reporting Time** — within **24 hours** of occurrence.
- **Safety Training Hours** — minimum **16 hours/year per employee** in hazardous roles.
- **Audit Frequency** — **quarterly internal**, **annual external**.
- **First-Aid Response Time** — within **4 minutes** of incident.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Structure Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture of the AMF Organization

The AMF model operates on **six functional layers**. Each layer has a *distinct* responsibility, a *distinct* authority, and a *distinct* reporting channel.

#### Layer 1 — The Board of Directors (Apex)

- **Why:** Legal and fiduciary responsibility for safety rests here. In product-liability law, the Board is the *first* named defendant.
- **How:** Approves the corporate safety policy, allocates the safety budget, and reviews the Annual Safety Performance Report.
- **Key Output:** Signed, dated, version-controlled *Safety Policy Document*.

#### Layer 2 — The Managing Director / CEO

- **Why:** Translates Board intent into operational directives.
- **How:** Issues corporate safety directives, appoints the Safety Director, and chairs the *Corporate Safety Council*.
- **Key Output:** Quarterly Safety Directives to all Business Units.

#### Layer 3 — The Safety Director (Staff Function)

- **Why:** A dedicated, full-time, *independent* safety professional with direct access to the CEO — not buried under production.
- **How:** Develops safety procedures, conducts audits, investigates major incidents, and advises line management.
- **Key Output:** Annual Safety Plan, Audit Reports, Incident Investigation Reports.
- **Critical Rule:** The Safety Director has *staff authority* (advisory + veto) but *not line authority* (cannot stop production unilaterally). Stop-work authority must be granted by policy.

#### Layer 4 — The Safety Committee (Cross-Functional)

- **Why:** Safety cannot be a one-department silo; design, production, quality, and workers must all have a voice.
- **How:** Meets regularly, reviews incidents, reviews design safety reviews, suggests improvements, and tracks corrective actions.
- **Composition:** Typically **6–10 members**: Safety Director (Chair), Production Manager, Design Engineer, Quality Manager, HR Representative, **Worker Representatives (≥ 50%)**, Medical Officer.

#### Layer 5 — Line Supervisors / Foremen

- **Why:** The supervisor is the *only* person who sees the actual work being done.
- **How:** Conduct daily toolbox talks, perform pre-shift equipment checks, enforce PPE usage, and issue *Permit-to-Work* for hazardous tasks.
- **Key Output:** Daily Safety Checklist, Pre-Start Safety Review (PSSR) sign-off.

#### Layer 6 — The Employee / Designer / Operator

- **Why:** The person closest to the hazard is also the first line of defence.
- **How:** Follows SOPs, reports near-misses, participates in safety training, and has the *Right to Refuse Unsafe Work*.
- **Key Output:** Near-Miss Reports, Toolbox Talk Attendance, PPE Compliance.

### 2.2 The Three Lines of Defence Principle (Embedded in AMF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The AMF Organization maps cleanly onto the modern **Three Lines of Defence** model used in KTU-evaluated risk management:
>
> - **1st Line — Operational Management** (Supervisors + Employees): Owns and manages risks.
> - **2nd Line — Specialist Functions** (Safety Director + Safety Committee): Provides oversight, frameworks, and challenge.
> - **3rd Line — Internal Audit** (Reports to Board): Provides independent assurance.

### 2.3 The PDCA Loop Inside AMF

The AMF Organization operationalizes the **Deming PDCA Cycle** for safety:

| Step | AMF Activity |
|---|---|
| **Plan** | Annual Safety Plan by Safety Director, approved by CEO |
| **Do** | Training, inspections, design safety reviews, PPE provision |
| **Check** | Audits, incident investigations, safety KPIs |
| **Act** | Corrective actions, policy revision, design changes |

### 2.4 KTU Formula / Structure Cheat Sheet

| Element | Definition / Boundary Condition | Responsible Layer |
|---|---|---|
| Safety Policy | Single-page, signed, dated, annually reviewed | Board |
| Safety Budget | ≥ **2% of operating cost** in hazardous industries | CEO |
| Safety Committee Quorum | ≥ **50% worker reps** + Chair | Safety Director |
| Incident Report | Filed within **24 h** of event | Supervisor |
| Near-Miss Report | No penalty for filing (psychological safety) | Employee |
| Audit Cycle | Quarterly internal + Annual external | Safety Director |
| Training Hours | Min **16 h/year** per exposed employee | HR + Safety Director |
| Design Safety Review | At every **gate** of the stage-gate process | Design Engineer + Safety |
| Stop-Work Authority | Granted in writing to all employees | CEO Policy |
| Emergency Drill | **Once every 6 months minimum** | Safety Committee |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- Used as the **scaffolding** for ISO 45001 Occupational Health & Safety Management Systems.
- Forms the **organizational basis** for CE Marking technical files under the EU Machinery Directive.
- Provides the **defence-of-due-diligence** argument in product liability litigation.
- Maps directly to **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** responsibility assignments in product design.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Application Logic

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: How the AMF Model Processes a Safety Decision in Product Design

Because the AMF Organization is a *management framework* rather than a physical equation, the "derivation" below is the **logical derivation of the decision flow** when a new product safety issue is raised.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme frequently tests this topic by asking students to *trace* a safety decision through the AMF hierarchy. The step-by-step model below is the canonical trace.

**Scenario:** A Design Engineer identifies a sharp edge hazard in a new handheld product prototype.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Hazard Identification} &\rightarrow \text{Design Engineer (Layer 6) flags sharp edge during prototype review} \\
\text{Step 2: Initial Risk Assessment} &\rightarrow \text{Engineer rates Severity = 4, Probability = 3, Risk Score} \\
R_1 &= S \times P = 4 \times 3 = 12 \\
\text{Step 3: Escalation} &\rightarrow \text{Issue raised at next Safety Committee meeting (Layer 4)} \\
\text{Step 4: Design Safety Review (DSR)} &\rightarrow \text{Committee mandates DSR; Designer proposes 3 mitigations:} \\
&\quad (a)\ \text{Round edge with } r \geq 1.5 \text{ mm fillet} \\
&\quad (b)\ \text{Add protective rubber overmould} \\
&\quad (c)\ \text{Add warning label} \\
\text{Step 5: Mitigation Selection} &\rightarrow \text{Safety Director (Layer 3) applies Hierarchy of Controls:} \\
&\quad \text{Elimination} > \text{Substitution} > \text{Engineering Control} > \text{Admin} > \text{PPE} \\
&\quad \text{Selected: Option (a) — Engineering Control (highest reliability)} \\
\text{Step 6: Re-Calculate Residual Risk} &\rightarrow R_2 = 2 \times 1 = 2 \quad (\text{Achieved ALARP — As Low As Reasonably Practicable}) \\
\text{Step 7: Verification} &\rightarrow \text{Prototype re-tested by Quality + Safety team} \\
\text{Step 8: Documentation} &\rightarrow \text{Updated in Design History File (DHF) and Risk Management File (ISO 14971)} \\
\text{Step 9: Closure} &\rightarrow \text{Supervisor (Layer 5) confirms production-line compliance} \\
\text{Step 10: Audit} &\rightarrow \text{Safety Director audits DSR closure in next quarterly audit} \\
\text{Step 11: Board Reporting} &\rightarrow \text{Annual Safety Report to Board (Layer 1) with this case as a worked example}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 The Risk-Score Decision Rule Used in AMF-Driven Reviews

$$
R = S \times P
$$

Where:
- $R$ = Risk Score (dimensionless)
- $S$ = Severity Rating (typically $1$ to $5$)
- $P$ = Probability Rating (typically $1$ to $5$)

**Decision Boundaries (KTU-typical 5×5 matrix):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \leq R \leq 4 &\Rightarrow \text{Low — Acceptable, monitor} \\
5 \leq R \leq 9 &\Rightarrow \text{Medium — Action required within 30 days} \\
10 \leq R \leq 15 &\Rightarrow \text{High — Action required within 7 days} \\
16 \leq R \leq 25 &\Rightarrow \text{Critical — Stop production immediately}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A new power tool design has a documented failure mode: insulation breakdown, Severity $S = 5$, Probability $P = 3$.

**Step 1 — Calculate initial risk:**

$$
R_1 = S \times P = 5 \times 3 = 15
$$

This falls in the **High** band → Action within 7 days.

**Step 2 — After applying double insulation (Engineering Control):**

Probability reduces to $P = 1$.

$$
R_2 = 5 \times 1 = 5
$$

Now falls in the **Medium** band → Manage via procedure + training.

**Step 3 — Decision:** Design is approved for production with monitoring.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 AMF Safety Organization — Hierarchical Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[LAYER 1 BOARD OF DIRECTORS] --> A2[LAYER 2 CEO MANAGING DIRECTOR]
    A2 --> A3[LAYER 3 SAFETY DIRECTOR]
    A2 --> A4[LAYER 4 SAFETY COMMITTEE]
    A3 --> A5[LAYER 5 LINE SUPERVISORS]
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6[LAYER 6 EMPLOYEES DESIGNERS OPERATORS]
    A3 --> A7[AUDIT FUNCTION INDEPENDENT]
    A7 --> A1
    A3 --> A8[INCIDENT INVESTIGATION]
    A4 --> A9[DESIGN SAFETY REVIEW GATE]
    A9 --> A6
    A8 --> A1
```

**Interpretation of the Diagram:**

- The **Board → CEO → Safety Director → Supervisor → Employee** chain is the *downward command flow* (policy deployment).
- The *upward feedback loop* flows: **Employee → Supervisor → Safety Committee → Safety Director → CEO → Board**.
- The **Audit Function** is shown as a *lateral arrow* back to the Board, ensuring **independence** (3rd Line of Defence).
- **Design Safety Review** is shown as a *cross-functional node* — it pulls input from Designers (Layer 6) and routes through the Safety Committee (Layer 4).

### 4.2 Information-Flow Block Diagram for a Design Safety Decision

```mermaid
graph LR
    B1[HAZARD IDENTIFIED BY DESIGNER] --> B2[INITIAL RISK SCORE R1 S x P]
    B2 --> B3{IS R1 ABOVE ACCEPTABLE}
    B3 --> B4[RAISE AT SAFETY COMMITTEE]
    B3 --> B5[CLOSE WITH MONITORING]
    B4 --> B6[APPLY HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS]
    B6 --> B7[SELECTED MITIGATION DOCUMENTED]
    B7 --> B8[RESIDUAL RISK R2 S x P]
    B8 --> B9{IS R2 ALARP}
    B9 --> B10[APPROVE FOR PRODUCTION]
    B9 --> B11[RE-DESIGN CYCLE]
    B10 --> B12[UPDATE DESIGN HISTORY FILE]
    B12 --> B13[QUARTERLY AUDIT VERIFICATION]
    B13 --> B14[ANNUAL BOARD SAFETY REPORT]
```

### 4.3 The Six-Layer Functional Map

| Layer | Entity | Authority Type | Reporting To |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Board of Directors | Line + Policy | Shareholders |
| 2 | CEO / MD | Line | Board |
| 3 | Safety Director | Staff (advisory) | CEO |
| 4 | Safety Committee | Cross-functional staff | CEO (via Safety Director) |
| 5 | Line Supervisors | Line (operational) | Functional Manager |
| 6 | Employees / Designers | Line (executional) | Supervisor |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the AMF Organization. List its four foundational pillars.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **AMF (American Machine and Foundry) Safety Organization** is a classical, hierarchically structured industrial safety management framework in which safety responsibility is clearly distributed across every level of the corporate hierarchy, from the Board of Directors to the shop-floor employee, through defined lines of authority, accountability, communication, and feedback.

**Four foundational pillars (1 mark each, briefly):**

1. **Policy** — Top-management written commitment.
2. **Organization** — Defined lines of responsibility at each level.
3. **Operations** — Procedures, training, inspections, audits.
4. **Audit & Review** — Continuous measurement and improvement.

*(Award 1 mark for a clear one-line definition, 2 marks for correctly listing and briefly explaining the four pillars.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State any three responsibilities of the Safety Director as per the AMF model.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. Develop, implement, and periodically update the corporate safety procedures and the Annual Safety Plan.
2. Conduct scheduled and unannounced safety audits across all departments and report findings to the CEO and the Board.
3. Lead or supervise the investigation of all major incidents, near-misses, and design-safety review failures, and ensure that corrective and preventive actions (CAPA) are closed within stipulated timelines.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students commonly lose marks by **listing roles without explaining the *interaction* between layers**. The AMF question is a *systems* question, not a list-memorization question.
> - Do **not** confuse the *Safety Director's* staff authority with line authority. Markers specifically test this distinction.
> - Always mention **near-miss reporting** and the **Right to Refuse Unsafe Work** — these are high-frequency KTU keywords.
> - For numerical risk-score questions, **show the matrix boundaries**, not just the multiplication.
> - Always close the answer with a **feedback loop** mention (PDCA / upward reporting) — this is a 2-mark differentiator.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Model Question]**
*(a)* Explain the hierarchical structure of the AMF Safety Organization, describing the roles and responsibilities of each of its six functional layers. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A new chemical process plant is being commissioned. Using the AMF framework, design a step-by-step *Design Safety Review* (DSR) procedure that the Safety Committee should follow before authorizing production. Show how the PDCA cycle is embedded. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hierarchical Structure (7 marks):**

1. **Layer 1 — Board of Directors (1 mark):** Ultimate legal and fiduciary responsibility. Approves the written Safety Policy, allocates the safety budget (typically ≥ 2 % of operating cost in hazardous industries), and reviews the Annual Safety Performance Report.

2. **Layer 2 — CEO / Managing Director (1 mark):** Translates Board policy into operational directives, appoints the Safety Director, and chairs the Corporate Safety Council.

3. **Layer 3 — Safety Director (1 mark):** A full-time, independent safety professional with *staff authority* (advisory + veto on safety grounds) and direct access to the CEO. Develops procedures, conducts audits, leads incident investigations, and advises line management.

4. **Layer 4 — Safety Committee (1 mark):** A cross-functional body chaired by the Safety Director, comprising Production, Design, Quality, HR, Medical, and ≥ 50 % worker representatives. Meets at least monthly, reviews incidents and DSRs, and tracks CAPA closure.

5. **Layer 5 — Line Supervisors / Foremen (1 mark):** The *only* role that directly observes the work. Conducts daily toolbox talks, pre-shift equipment checks, PPE enforcement, and issues Permit-to-Work for hazardous tasks.

6. **Layer 6 — Employees / Designers / Operators (1 mark):** The closest-to-the-hazard personnel. Follow SOPs, file near-miss reports, attend training, and exercise the *Right to Refuse Unsafe Work*.

7. **Cross-cutting layer — Independent Audit (1 mark):** Reports directly to the Board, providing third-line assurance that Layers 1–6 are functioning as designed.

**(b) DSR Procedure with Embedded PDCA (7 marks):**

| Step | DSR Activity | PDCA Phase |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) by multi-disciplinary team | **Plan** |
| 2 | What-If / HAZOP study to identify deviations | **Plan** |
| 3 | Initial risk-scoring using $R = S \times P$ matrix | **Plan** |
| 4 | Apply Hierarchy of Controls (Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering → Admin → PPE) | **Do** |
| 5 | Re-score residual risk; confirm ALARP | **Check** |
| 6 | Document in Risk Management File (ISO 14971 style) | **Check** |
| 7 | Trial-run with mock operations; capture deviations | **Act** |
| 8 | CAPA closure; Safety Committee authorization for production | **Act** |
| 9 | Quarterly internal audit; Annual external audit; feedback to Board | **Plan (next cycle)** |

*(Examiner note: Award 4 marks for the correct DSR sequence, 2 marks for explicit PDCA mapping, 1 mark for ALARP verification.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Model Question]**
*(a)* With a neat block diagram, describe the upward and downward information flows in the AMF Safety Organization. Why is *staff authority* of the Safety Director considered a strength rather than a weakness? *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A manufacturing unit records 12 minor injuries, 2 lost-time injuries, and 1 major incident in a quarter. Compute the standard safety KPIs and interpret them. What AMF-mandated corrective actions would you recommend? *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Information Flows & Staff Authority (7 marks):**

**Downward flow (Policy deployment) — 3 marks:**

Board → CEO → Safety Director → Safety Committee + Supervisors → Employees.

**Upward flow (Feedback & Reporting) — 2 marks:**

Employees → Supervisors → Safety Committee + Safety Director → CEO → Board.
Plus the lateral *Independent Audit* arrow bypassing line management to the Board.

**Why staff authority is a strength — 2 marks:**

The Safety Director's *staff authority* (advisory + veto on safety) combined with *lack of line authority* (cannot stop production unilaterally) preserves the **separation of safety judgement from production pressure**. This prevents both (i) safety being compromised for output targets, and (ii) safety from becoming a production bottleneck owned by a non-specialist. The safety decision is *advocated* independently but *enforced* through the line.

**(b) KPI Computation & AMF Corrective Actions (7 marks):**

Assuming **1000 employees** and **2,00,000 man-hours worked** per quarter (typical KTU numerical).

**1. Total Recordable Injury Rate (TRIR) — 1 mark:**

$$
\text{TRIR} = \frac{\text{Recordable Injuries} \times 1{,}00{,}000}{\text{Man-hours}} = \frac{14 \times 1{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = 7.0
$$

**2. Lost-Time Injury Frequency Rate (LTIFR) — 1 mark:**

$$
\text{LTIFR} = \frac{\text{Lost-time Injuries} \times 1{,}00{,}000}{\text{Man-hours}} = \frac{2 \times 1{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = 1.0
$$

**3. Severity Rate (SR) — 1 mark:** Assuming 50 lost days for the major incident:

$$
\text{SR} = \frac{50 \times 1{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = 25
$$

**4. Interpretation — 2 marks:**

- TRIR of 7.0 is **above the manufacturing industry benchmark of ~3.0** — high.
- LTIFR of 1.0 is at the **threshold of concern** — moderate.
- Severity Rate of 25 indicates the major incident was **catastrophic** in lost-time impact.
- Overall trend: **Safety system is failing** — immediate AMF-mandated intervention is required.

**5. AMF-mandated Corrective Actions — 2 marks:**

- Convene an **emergency Safety Committee meeting** within 7 days.
- **Safety Director** to conduct a full root-cause analysis (5-Why / Fishbone) of the major incident.
- **Layer-5 Supervisors** to re-conduct toolbox talks and re-issue PPE for the affected areas.
- **Layer-6 Employees** to file near-miss reports without penalty (psychological safety).
- **CEO** to issue a written Safety Directive; **Board** to review the next quarter's KPIs.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — The AMF Organization**

- ✅ AMF = **American Machine and Foundry** Company safety model (1960s).
- ✅ **Four pillars:** Policy, Organization, Operations, Audit & Review.
- ✅ **Six layers:** Board → CEO → Safety Director → Safety Committee → Supervisor → Employee.
- ✅ Safety Director has **staff authority** (advisory + veto), **not line authority**.
- ✅ Safety Committee must have **≥ 50% worker representation**.
- ✅ Incident reported within **24 hours**; near-miss reporting is **penalty-free**.
- ✅ Training: minimum **16 h/year** per exposed employee.
- ✅ Audits: **quarterly internal + annual external**.
- ✅ **PDCA cycle** is embedded at every layer.
- ✅ **Three Lines of Defence** map directly onto AMF layers.
- ✅ Risk-score formula: $R = S \times P$ with **5×5 matrix**, boundaries at $R = 4,\ 9,\ 15,\ 25$.
- ✅ **Right to Refuse Unsafe Work** is a Layer-6 entitlement, not a privilege.
- ✅ **ALARP** = As Low As Reasonably Practicable — the acceptance threshold.
- ✅ DSR (Design Safety Review) is a **gate** in the stage-gate product development process.
- ✅ AMF is the **scaffolding** for ISO 45001, ISO 14971, and EU Machinery Directive compliance.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product Planning Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT747 - SAFETY IN PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Product Planning Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 2 — PRODUCT PLANNING PROCESS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Academic Definition

**Product Planning Process** is the structured, systematic, and continuous front-end engineering activity that translates customer needs, market opportunities, regulatory requirements, and organizational capabilities into a clearly defined product vision, specification envelope, and execution roadmap prior to the commencement of detailed design.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned, Outcome-Based Education framework), the Product Planning Process is treated as a **pre-design managerial–technical bridge** that ensures every subsequent activity in the Product Design & Development (PDD) life cycle is rooted in a validated safety, quality, and value proposition. It encompasses the formulation of a **product charter**, **conceptual design brief**, **target specifications (T-spec)**, **design for X (DFX) constraints**, and a **phased gate-based project plan**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PESFT747 / Module 2):**
> The Product Planning Process is the entry point of the generic product development process and is responsible for identifying *what* must be developed, *for whom*, *under what safety constraints*, and *with what business justification* — *before* any pen is put to the engineering drawing.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Building a House 🏠**
> Think of the Product Planning Process as the *architect's briefing stage* before construction begins.
> - The **client** is the *market / customer*.
> - The **architect** is the *product planning team*.
> - The **blueprint (bricks, rooms, plumbing, wiring layout)** is the *product specification*.
> - The **municipal building codes** are *safety standards and regulations*.
> - The **budget and timeline** are *cost & schedule targets*.
> - The **final house** is the *finished safe product*.
>
> Just as an architect must fix the number of floors, plot orientation, foundation type, fire-escape routes, and budget *before* a single brick is laid, the product planner must fix the product's purpose, safety envelope, cost band, and timeline *before* detailed engineering begins. Skipping this stage is equivalent to building a house without a plan — costly, unsafe, and almost always requires expensive rework.

### 1.3 Why Product Planning is Indispensable in Safety-Centric Design

In the PESFT747 course context, every product planning decision directly influences downstream **safety integrity**. The three guiding metrics are:

- **Safety** — Harm-free performance under foreseeable misuse.
- **Quality** — Fitness for the intended use.
- **Value** — Customer-perceived benefit relative to cost.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examiners:**
> Product planning is *not* a one-time pre-launch activity. It is an **iterative front-loading exercise** that updates as the design matures and as new information (test data, user feedback, regulatory changes) becomes available.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six-Stage Generic Product Planning Process

The KTU 2024 syllabus (PESFT747) structures product planning around six interlinked logical stages. Each stage has a defined **input**, **activity**, **output**, and **gate-review decision**.

#### Stage 1 — Opportunity Identification (Opportunity Selection)
- **Input:** Market trends, customer complaints, technology breakthroughs, regulatory changes.
- **Activity:** Scan the external environment (PESTLE), perform SWOT, and identify gaps where a new or improved product can deliver differentiated value.
- **Output:** A prioritized list of product opportunities.
- **Decision Gate:** *Go / No-Go* on each opportunity.

#### Stage 2 — Concept Generation & Screening
- **Input:** Approved opportunity statements.
- **Activity:** Generate multiple product concepts (brainstorming, TRIZ, SCAMPER, biomimicry) and screen them against safety, feasibility, and strategic fit.
- **Output:** A shortlist of 2–3 viable concepts.
- **Decision Gate:** Selection of the lead concept.

#### Stage 3 — Concept Development & System-Level Definition
- **Input:** Selected lead concept.
- **Activity:** Develop a system-level block diagram, define the **Target Specifications (T-spec)**, and conduct **Functional Decomposition** (Function Analysis System Technique — FAST diagram).
- **Output:** System architecture, functional block diagram, and a draft T-spec.
- **Decision Gate:** Approval of the system architecture for detailed planning.

#### Stage 4 — Feasibility Analysis (Technical, Economic, Legal, Operational, Schedule — TELOS)
- **Input:** Draft T-spec, system architecture, regulatory landscape.
- **Activity:** Validate that the product can be designed, manufactured, certified, distributed, and supported within the planned cost, time, and risk envelope.
- **Output:** Feasibility report with **Risk Register** and **FMEA seed list**.
- **Decision Gate:** *Proceed / Re-plan / Kill*.

#### Stage 5 — Product Charter (Project Definition)
- **Input:** Approved feasibility report and T-spec.
- **Activity:** Author the **Product Charter / Project Definition Document** that consolidates vision, mission, success metrics, deliverables, milestones, resource plan, and **safety & compliance roadmap**.
- **Output:** Signed-off Product Charter and a **Stage-Gate (Phase-Gate) Plan**.
- **Decision Gate:** Formal authorization to enter Detailed Design.

#### Stage 6 — Economic Justification & Business Case
- **Input:** Cost estimates, market-sizing data, revenue forecast.
- **Activity:** Build the financial model — NPV, IRR, Payback Period, Break-Even Volume.
- **Output:** Approved business case.
- **Decision Gate:** Release of development budget.

### 2.2 The Target Specification (T-spec) — The Heart of Product Planning

A T-spec is a **numerical target** for each measurable design metric. It is derived from customer needs, competitive benchmarking, and safety / regulatory minima. The classical formulation is:

$$
M_{i,\text{target}} \;=\; f(\text{Customer Need}_i,\;\text{Competitor Benchmark}_i,\;\text{Regulatory Minimum}_i)
$$

For a *safety-critical* product, regulatory minima act as a **hard floor**, not a soft target:

$$
M_{i,\text{spec,final}} \;=\; \max\bigl(M_{i,\text{regulatory floor}},\;M_{i,\text{customer target}},\;M_{i,\text{technical feasible}}\bigr)
$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept / Framework | Symbol / Equation | Units / Boundary Condition | Purpose in Planning |
|---|--------------------|-------------------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Net Present Value | $\mathrm{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{N} \dfrac{\mathrm{CF}_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}$ | $\mathrm{CF}_t$ = cash flow in year $t$; $r$ = discount rate | Economic justification of the product |
| 2 | Internal Rate of Return | $\mathrm{NPV}(r=\mathrm{IRR}) = 0$ | Dimensionless rate, $\mathrm{IRR} \geq$ hurdle rate | Investment viability test |
| 3 | Payback Period | $P_{pb} = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$ | Years; shorter is better | Risk-adjusted recovery horizon |
| 4 | Break-Even Volume | $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{P - V_c}$ | Units; $P$ = price, $V_c$ = variable cost / unit | Minimum sales for non-loss |
| 5 | Target Specification Floor | $M_{spec} = \max(M_{reg}, M_{cust}, M_{tech})$ | Safety minima cannot be negotiated | T-spec authoring |
| 6 | Safety Integrity Level (SIL proxy) | $\mathrm{PFD}_{avg} \leq 10^{-n}$ | $n = 1..4$ for IEC 61508 | Safety planning threshold |
| 7 | Risk Priority Number | $\mathrm{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Dimensionless index | Risk-based planning input |
| 8 | Time-to-Market | $T_{TM} = T_{plan} + T_{design} + T_{proto} + T_{launch}$ | Months / Days | Schedule risk in charter |
| 9 | Stage-Gate Review | Binary (Go / Kill / Recycle) | Decision event | Planning-to-execution handoff |
| 10 | Concept Screening Score | $S_{j} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} w_k \cdot r_{jk}$ | Weighted sum; $w_k$ = criterion weight | Concept selection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real industry, the product plan is the single document that the **CEO, CTO, CFO, Quality Manager, and Safety Officer** all sign jointly. It is the contractual and engineering handshake between *what the market needs* and *what the engineers will build*.

### 2.4 The Stage-Gate® Logic (Cooper's Model)

The stage-gate framework is the dominant KTU-referenced planning architecture:

$$
\text{Plan} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Activity}}\; \text{Gate Decision} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Approved}}\; \text{Next Stage}
$$

A project does not advance until deliverables at each gate are objectively reviewed against the **gate criteria** — a set of *must-meet* and *should-meet* conditions written *during* the planning stage itself.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Building the Target Specification (T-spec)

The following exhaustive derivation shows how a raw customer voice is converted into a binding engineering specification.

**Step 1 — Voice of the Customer (VoC) Capture**
Customer needs are recorded as raw statements:

$$
\text{VoC}_i \;=\; \text{``} \text{``The kettle should boil water quickly and not burn my hand on the body.''}
$$

**Step 2 — Affinity Diagramming & Needs Clustering**
Each VoC is clustered under a measurable **engineering attribute**:

$$
\text{Boil quickly} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Time-to-Boil},\quad \text{Not burn hand} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Surface Temperature}
$$

**Step 3 — Importance Rating (1–5 scale)**
Customers rate the importance of each attribute:

$$
I_{i} \in \{1,2,3,4,5\}
$$

**Step 4 — Competitive Benchmarking**
Each competitor product is scored on the same attribute:

$$
B_{ij} \quad \text{(product } j \text{'s score on attribute } i\text{)}
$$

**Step 5 — Customer Satisfaction Benchmark**
The relationship between an attribute's value and customer satisfaction is plotted. The **Kano-type satisfaction curve** distinguishes:

- **Must-be** (basic) — satisfaction flatlines above threshold.
- **One-dimensional** — linear with performance.
- **Attractive (delighter)** — exponential above threshold.

**Step 6 — Setting the Target**
For a one-dimensional attribute, the target is set above the best competitor:

$$
M_{i,\text{target}} \;=\; \max_{j} B_{ij} \;+\; \delta_{i}
$$

where $\delta_i$ is the planned **differential advantage** margin.

**Step 7 — Applying the Safety Floor**
For safety attributes (e.g., surface temperature during normal use), the regulatory standard (e.g., **IEC 60335-2-15** for kettles) is the binding floor:

$$
M_{i,\text{spec,final}} \;=\; \max\!\bigl(M_{i,\text{regulatory}},\; M_{i,\text{target}}\bigr)
$$

**Step 8 — Documenting Limits**
Each spec is published with a **Direction-of-Improvement** ($\uparrow$ for higher-is-better, $\downarrow$ for lower-is-better), **Units**, and a **Verification Method** (Test / Analysis / Inspection / Demonstration):

$$
M_{i,\text{spec}} \;=\; \bigl\{ \text{value},\; \text{unit},\; \text{direction},\; \text{verification method} \bigr\}
$$

> This eight-step procedure is the **gold-standard** expected in KTU 14-mark answers on Product Planning.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Planning Effort vs Planning Risk

| Product Type | Planning Effort | Planning Risk | Typical Time-on-Plan |
|--------------|-----------------|---------------|----------------------|
| Breakthrough / Radical | **High (25–30 %)** | High | 6–12 months |
| Platform / Derivative | Medium (10–15 %) | Medium | 2–4 months |
| Incremental / Refresh | Low (3–5 %) | Low | 2–6 weeks |
| Custom / Contract | High (Variable) | High | Project-dependent |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** Map the *Planning Effort* in **bold** as a percentage of total project effort. This is a high-frequency KTU theory question.

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation — Break-Even Volume

Starting from the profit equation:

$$
\Pi(Q) \;=\; R(Q) \;-\; C(Q)
$$

Substituting revenue $R(Q) = P \cdot Q$ and total cost $C(Q) = F_c + V_c \cdot Q$:

$$
\Pi(Q) \;=\; P \cdot Q \;-\; F_c \;-\; V_c \cdot Q
$$

At break-even, $\Pi(Q_{BE}) = 0$:

$$
0 \;=\; P \cdot Q_{BE} \;-\; F_c \;-\; V_c \cdot Q_{BE}
$$

Collecting $Q_{BE}$ terms:

$$
P \cdot Q_{BE} \;-\; V_c \cdot Q_{BE} \;=\; F_c
$$

$$
(P \;-\; V_c) \cdot Q_{BE} \;=\; F_c
$$

Solving for $Q_{BE}$:

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{BE} \;=\; \dfrac{F_c}{P \;-\; V_c}\,}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** $Q_{BE}$ is the **minimum sales volume** that the product plan must defend in the business case. If market-sizing predicts sales below $Q_{BE}$, the plan must be re-priced, re-scoped, or killed.

### 3.4 Analytical Derivation — Risk Priority Number (RPN)

$$
\mathrm{RPN} \;=\; S \times O \times D
$$

- $S$ = Severity (1–10)
- $O$ = Occurrence (1–10)
- $D$ = Detection (1–10)

Action threshold (industry-typical):

$$
\mathrm{RPN} \geq 100 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{Mitigation action required in plan}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

### 4.1 The Six-Stage Product Planning Process — Functional Flow

flowchart TD
