## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "Product Planning Process" and list any four of its key activities.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Product Planning Process is the structured front-end phase of product development in which customer needs, market opportunities, regulatory constraints, and organizational capabilities are translated into a defined product vision, target specifications, project charter, and a stage-gate execution roadmap before detailed design begins.

**Four Key Activities (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. Opportunity identification and selection.
2. Concept generation and screening.
3. Target Specification (T-spec) development.
4. Feasibility analysis (TELOS).
5. Product charter authoring.
6. Economic justification / business case.

*(Any four, fully stated, carry full marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is a "Product Charter" and why is it considered the most critical deliverable of the planning phase?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Product Charter** (also called a *Project Definition Document*) is a consolidated, signed-off document that captures the **vision, mission, success metrics, deliverables, milestones, resource plan, safety & compliance roadmap, and risk register** of a product development effort.

It is the most critical deliverable because:
- It is the **single source of truth** for the entire cross-functional team (2 marks).
- It is the **formal authorization** to release development budget and resources.
- It is the **contractual alignment** between marketing, engineering, quality, safety, and finance.
- It is the **baseline** against which scope-creep, change-orders, and gate-review decisions are measured (1 mark).

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**
> *(a)* Explain in detail the **six stages of the Product Planning Process** with their inputs, activities, outputs, and gate-review decisions. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* With a suitable example of a **domestic electric kettle**, demonstrate how a **Target Specification (T-spec)** is derived from the Voice of the Customer, including the application of the **safety floor** mandated by IEC 60335-2-15. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

**Stage 1 — Opportunity Identification (1 mark):**
Inputs: market trends, customer complaints, regulatory changes. Activity: PESTLE, SWOT, gap analysis. Output: prioritized opportunity list. Gate: Go / No-Go.

**Stage 2 — Concept Generation & Screening (1 mark):**
Inputs: approved opportunities. Activity: brainstorming, TRIZ, SCAMPER; screening against safety & feasibility. Output: 2–3 shortlisted concepts. Gate: lead-concept selection.

**Stage 3 — Concept Development & System Definition (1 mark):**
Inputs: lead concept. Activity: system block diagram, FAST diagram, draft T-spec. Output: system architecture, T-spec draft. Gate: architecture approval.

**Stage 4 — Feasibility Analysis — TELOS (1 mark):**
Inputs: draft T-spec, regulatory map. Activity: Technical, Economic, Legal, Operational, Schedule validation. Output: feasibility report + seed FMEA + risk register. Gate: Proceed / Re-plan / Kill.

**Stage 5 — Product Charter Authoring (1 mark):**
Inputs: approved feasibility report. Activity: draft vision, mission, deliverables, milestones, resource & safety roadmap. Output: signed-off Product Charter + Stage-Gate Plan. Gate: charter sign-off.

**Stage 6 — Economic Justification & Business Case (1 mark):**
Inputs: cost estimates, market size, revenue forecast. Activity: build NPV, IRR, Payback, Break-Even models. Output: approved business case. Gate: budget release.

**Synthesis statement (1 mark):**
The six stages form a **closed-loop front-loading mechanism** in which the output of each stage becomes the input of the next, and any gate may recycle the project back for rework or kill it altogether.

---

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

**VoC Example (1 mark):**
> *"I want my water to boil in under 3 minutes, and the kettle body should stay cool enough that my child cannot be scalded."*

**Clustering (1 mark):**
- "Boil in 3 min" → Engineering attribute: **Time-to-Boil** (Direction ↑).
- "Body stays cool" → Engineering attribute: **Surface Temperature during normal use** (Direction ↓).

**Importance & Benchmark (1 mark):**
- Time-to-Boil: Customer importance = 5/5; best competitor = 4.2 min.
- Surface Temperature: Customer importance = 5/5; best competitor = 62 °C.

**Target Setting (1 mark):**
$$
M_{\text{Time-to-Boil,target}} = 4.2 - 0.7 = 3.5\ \text{min}
$$

$$
M_{\text{Surface Temp,target}} = 62 - 8 = 54\ \text{°C}
$$

**Safety Floor Application — IEC 60335-2-15 (1 mark):**
The standard mandates that the **external accessible surface temperature must not exceed 65 °C** during normal operation (a regulatory *ceiling* for the *floor* of the spec).

$$
M_{\text{Surface Temp,final}} = \max(54, 65) = 65\ \text{°C}
$$

Wait — this is the *upper limit* the product must satisfy, not the *target*. The actual T-spec is:

$$
\text{Surface Temperature} \leq 65\ \text{°C}\quad \text{(Direction} \downarrow\text{)}
$$

**Final T-spec Documentation (1 mark):**

| Attribute | Target | Unit | Direction | Verification |
|-----------|--------|------|-----------|--------------|
| Time-to-Boil (1 L) | $\leq 3.5$ | min | $\downarrow$ | Test (IEC 60530) |
| Surface Temperature | $\leq 65$ | °C | $\downarrow$ | Test (IEC 60335-2-15) |
| Power Consumption | 1500 – 2200 | W | $=$ | Inspection |
| Capacity | 1.0 – 1.7 | L | $=$ | Inspection |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The T-spec is now a binding engineering document that simultaneously addresses *customer delight* and *regulatory safety*, satisfying the PESFT747 objective of safety-integrated product planning.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**
> *(a)* Discuss the **Stage-Gate® model** as a planning-to-execution bridge. Identify and explain the **six gates** typically used in a product development lifecycle. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A startup is planning a new **cordless, child-safe hair dryer**. The fixed development cost is ₹80 lakh, the expected selling price is ₹1,800, and the variable cost per unit is ₹1,200. Compute the **break-even volume**. If market research forecasts 10,000 units in year 1, what is the **first-year profit (or loss)**? Recommend whether the plan should be **proceeded, re-planned, or killed** based on a payback expectation of 2 years. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

**Stage-Gate Concept (1.5 marks):**
Stage-Gate is a **linear–iterative planning model** in which the project advances through a series of *stages* (cross-functional activities) and *gates* (decision points) at which deliverables are reviewed against pre-defined *must-meet* and *should-meet* criteria before authorization to proceed.

**The Six Gates (1 mark each = 6 marks; remainder = 0.5 synthesis):**

1. **Gate 1 — Idea / Opportunity Screen:** Quick judgment on whether the opportunity is worth scoping.
2. **Gate 2 — Second-Screen / Concept Approval:** Approval of the lead concept after screening.
3. **Gate 3 — Development Plan Approval:** Sign-off on the detailed plan, T-spec, and architecture.
4. **Gate 4 — Development Gate:** Validation of prototypes against T-spec and safety requirements.
5. **Gate 5 — Testing & Validation Gate:** Confirmation that the product meets all specs, including safety & regulatory.
6. **Gate 6 — Launch Gate:** Final go/no-go decision for market release based on business case and operational readiness.

**Synthesis (0.5 mark):**
Gates transform an *idea* into a *controlled, evidence-based, and financially-justified* product launch.

---

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify the given values (1 mark):**
- Fixed cost $F_c$ = ₹80,00,000
- Selling price $P$ = ₹1,800 per unit
- Variable cost $V_c$ = ₹1,200 per unit
- Forecast volume $Q$ = 10,000 units

**Step 2 — Compute contribution per unit (1 mark):**
$$
C_{\text{contrib}} = P - V_c = 1800 - 1200 = 600\ \text{₹/unit}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute break-even volume (2 marks):**
$$
Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F_c}{P - V_c} = \dfrac{80{,}00{,}000}{600} = 13{,}333.33\ \text{units}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute first-year profit/loss (1.5 marks):**
$$
\Pi = (P - V_c) \cdot Q - F_c = 600 \times 10{,}000 - 80{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\Pi = 6{,}00{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}00{,}000 = -20{,}00{,}000\ \text{₹}
$$

The project incurs a **loss of ₹20 lakh** in year 1.

**Step 5 — Recommendation (1.5 marks):**
- Forecast volume (10,000) < $Q_{BE}$ (13,334) → Below break-even.
- Year 1 loss = ₹20 lakh.
- Payback required: 2 years → Recovery target = ₹80 lakh in 2 years.
- Required contribution = ₹40 lakh/year → Required volume = 40,00,000 / 600 = **6,667 units/year**.
- Forecast (10,000) exceeds this 2-year recovery rate.

**Final Verdict:** Although year 1 is loss-making, the year-2 trajectory likely recovers the investment, *provided* safety certification, market uptake, and price assumptions hold. The recommendation is to **RE-PLAN** — revisit variable-cost reduction, target a selling price of ₹1,950, or negotiate a higher year-2 volume forecast — rather than outright kill.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Product Planning with Detailed Design.** Planning is *what & why*; design is *how*. Students frequently mix the two and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the Safety Floor.** In every T-spec question, the **regulatory minimum must be applied as a floor** before finalizing the spec. Forgetting this loses the safety-integrity mark.
> 3. **Skipping Gate Decisions.** Each stage must end with an *explicit* gate decision. Omitting it loses 0.5–1 mark per stage.
> 4. **Units in Numerical Answers.** Always write **₹, units, min, °C, mm** with the value. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 5. **One-Dimensional Final Answer in Choice Questions.** In Part B, the examiner expects both (a) AND (b) answered fully; a half-answer on (b) caps the score at ~9/14.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Product Planning Process** is the **front-end**, **pre-design** phase that scopes *what* will be built, *for whom*, and *under what safety envelope*.
- The process is structured as **six stages** with **six gate decisions** — Opportunity → Concept → Architecture → Feasibility → Charter → Business Case.
- The **Product Charter** is the single most important deliverable; it is the cross-functional **contract**.
- **Target Specifications (T-spec)** are derived from **VoC → Clustering → Importance → Benchmarking → Kano → Target → Safety Floor → Documentation**.
- The **safety floor is non-negotiable** — every spec must satisfy **$M_{\text{spec,final}} = \max(M_{\text{regulatory}}, M_{\text{customer}}, M_{\text{technical}})$**.
- **Stage-Gate®** is the de-facto KTU-referenced planning model; gates ensure **evidence-based, risk-controlled** advancement.
- Key planning equations: **NPV, IRR, Payback, Break-Even, RPN**.
- Planning effort scales with **product novelty** — radical products require **25–30 %** of total effort on planning.
- The output of planning is a **T-spec, Charter, Stage-Gate Plan, Risk Register, and Business Case** — collectively the *handoff package* to detailed design.
- **Iterative front-loading** is the KTU-recommended practice — the plan is a *living document* updated as the design matures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Quick-Revision Sentence:**
> *"The Product Planning Process converts a fuzzy customer need into a sharp, safety-bounded, economically justified engineering brief — and gets cross-functional sign-off — before a single line of detailed design begins."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Identify Opportunities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT747 - SAFETY IN PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Identify Opportunities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Identify Opportunities** is the second stage of the Design Thinking process (within the context of Product Safety Engineering), where designers, safety engineers, and multidisciplinary teams systematically discover, articulate, and prioritize latent and explicit areas where safety improvements, hazard mitigations, or protective innovations can be introduced into a product's lifecycle — spanning conception, design, manufacture, use, maintenance, and end-of-life disposal.

In the strict vocabulary of **Safety in Product Design**, opportunity identification is the structured translation of *observed human, technical, regulatory, and environmental signals* into **actionable safety intervention points**. It is a **non-negotiable precursor** to ideation, prototyping, and implementation, ensuring that engineering creativity is anchored to real-world harm reduction rather than speculative novelty.

The KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework expects students to demonstrate:
- **CO2 (Module 2 mapping):** *Identify and analyse opportunities for integrating safety principles into product design using structured frameworks and human-centred research.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a hospital emergency room. Doctors don't randomly invent treatments — they first **listen to patients**, study **past case files**, examine **lab reports**, and scan for **new medical research**. Only after gathering these "clues" do they spot the *opportunities* where a new therapy could save lives.

> **Analogy:** *Identify Opportunities is the "diagnostic scan" of product safety.* Just as a doctor gathers symptoms before prescribing, a safety designer gathers user pain points, accident reports, and technology trends before inventing a safer product.

In simple words:

> [!NOTE]
> **Identify Opportunities = "Finding the Gaps Where Safety Can Be Improved."**
> It is the step where you stop, look around, and ask:
> *"Where is the product hurting people, frustrating users, or breaking rules? Where can we intervene to make it safer?"*

---

## 1.3 Core Components of Opportunity Identification

The process rests on **four foundational pillars**, each requiring a distinct mindset and toolset:

| Pillar | Core Question Answered | Typical Tools |
|---|---|---|
| **User Empathy Research** | "How does the user actually behave, fail, or get hurt?" | Observation, Interviews, Empathy Maps, Journey Maps |
| **Data & Failure Analysis** | "What went wrong, how often, and why?" | FMEA, Accident Statistics, Root Cause Analysis |
| **Technology & Trend Scanning** | "What new tools, materials, or regulations exist?" | Horizon Scanning, Patent Analysis, PESTLE |
| **Stakeholder & Ecosystem Mapping** | "Who else is affected, and what do they need?" | Stakeholder Matrices, Value Chain Analysis |

---

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following performance metrics are **industry-standard benchmarks** used to score, filter, and prioritize opportunities once identified. Every KTU answer referring to opportunity identification should reference at least **two** of these metrics explicitly.

- **Risk Priority Number (RPN):** *Threshold for acceptance* = **RPN ≤ 100** (industry convention from FMEA methodology).
- **Severity Score (S):** Scale of **1 to 10**, where **10 = catastrophic fatality** and **1 = negligible**.
- **Occurrence Score (O):** Scale of **1 to 10**, where **10 = very frequent (≥ 1 in 2)** and **1 = remote (≤ 1 in 1,000,000)**.
- **Detection Score (D):** Scale of **1 to 10**, where **10 = cannot detect** and **1 = highly detectable**.
- **Hazard Rate (λ):** Failures per million hours, typically **λ ≤ 10⁻⁶ / hr** for safety-critical components.
- **Safety Integrity Level (SIL):** Per IEC 61508, ranging from **SIL 1 (low)** to **SIL 4 (highest)**.

---

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

> [!NOTE]
> Module 2 of PESFT747 builds directly on **Module 1 (Empathize / Understand the User)**. Without disciplined opportunity identification, safety designs risk becoming *engineer-led assumptions* rather than *user-led solutions*. The KTU examiner explicitly tests whether students can **map raw signals → structured opportunities**, hence the heavy weight on frameworks like SWOT, PESTLE, and VOC (Voice of Customer) analysis in this module.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation — How Opportunities Are Discovered

Opportunity identification in safety design is **not a single activity**. It is a **layered investigative process** that moves from broad exploration to sharply defined safety intervention points. The KTU syllabus expects students to articulate the **stages** and the **logic of transition** between them.

### 2.1.1 Stage-Wise Logic of Identification

**Stage 1 — Signal Gathering (Broad & Qualitative)**
- Collect *unfiltered* observations: user behaviour, complaints, accident reports, social-media chatter, return-data, warranty claims.
- Mindset: **curiosity, no judgement**. The goal is volume, not quality.

**Stage 2 — Signal Filtering (Pattern Recognition)**
- Cluster observations into themes: *"users drop the kettle," "children open the lid," "electric shock during cleaning."*
- Use affinity diagrams, KJ analysis, or thematic coding.

**Stage 3 — Hazard Translation (Risk Conversion)**
- Each theme is converted into a **formal hazard statement** using the syntax:
  > *"Hazard Source → Mechanism → Harm → Affected Person"*
- Example: *"Boiling water spillage (mechanism) → Scald burns (harm) → Elderly user with reduced grip strength (affected person)."*

**Stage 4 — Opportunity Articulation (Solution-Space Framing)**
- Convert the hazard statement into a *positive opportunity question*:
  > *"How might we prevent scalding from boiling water spillage during pouring?"*
- This frames the problem **without prescribing a solution**, keeping ideation space open.

**Stage 5 — Prioritisation (Resource Allocation)**
- Score each opportunity using **RPN, Severity × Occurrence × Detection**, or **Impact-Effort matrices**.
- Top-scoring opportunities advance to ideation in Module 3.

---

### 2.1.2 The Five Provenance Streams of Opportunity

| # | Stream | Origin | Typical Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **User-Led** | Field studies, VOC, ethnography | Latent safety needs, unstated workarounds |
| 2 | **Failure-Led** | FMEA, FTA, accident databases | Known hazards requiring mitigation |
| 3 | **Regulatory-Led** | Standards (ISO 12100, IEC 61508), litigation | Compliance gaps, emerging legal risks |
| 4 | **Technology-Led** | Patents, research papers, supplier roadmaps | Enablement of previously impossible safeguards |
| 5 | **Competitive-Led** | Benchmarking, recalls, safety awards | Differentiation and parity opportunities |

---

## 2.2 The Core Frameworks (Theory + Application)

### Framework A — SWOT Analysis (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats)

A 2×2 strategic matrix used to convert internal product/team capabilities and external market/regulatory realities into **safety-relevant opportunities**.

| | **Helpful (Internal)** | **Harmful (Internal)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | ↓ | ↓ |
| **External (Positive)** | **S-O Strategy:** *Use strength to capture opportunity* | **W-O Strategy:** *Overcome weakness via opportunity* |
| **External (Negative)** | **S-T Strategy:** *Use strength to defuse threat* | **W-T Strategy:** *Minimise weakness, avoid threat* |

> **Safety-engineering reading of SWOT:** The *Opportunities* quadrant (external-positive) is the primary harvest zone, but the **W-O cell** is often the richest source of safety interventions because weaknesses (e.g., poor grip, unclear labelling) frequently hide hazards.

### Framework B — PESTLE Analysis (Macro-Environment Scan)

| Letter | Factor | Safety Relevance Example |
|---|---|---|
| **P** | Political | New government safety mandates, import bans |
| **E** | Economic | Insurance liability shifts, recall cost trends |
| **S** | Social | Aging population, child-safety awareness |
| **T** | Technological | IoT sensors, AI-based hazard detection |
| **L** | Legal | Product Liability Directive updates |
| **E** | Environmental | Recyclability, RoHS, REACH compliance |

### Framework C — Voice of Customer (VOC) Triangulation

A safety opportunity is considered **validated** only when at least **two of three** VOC sources confirm it:
1. **Direct user statements** (interviews, surveys)
2. **Observed behaviour** (field studies, ride-alongs)
3. **Indirect artefacts** (complaint logs, return data, social media)

### Framework D — Jobs-to-be-Done (JTBD) for Safety

Customers "hire" products to do a *job*. Safety opportunities arise when the product's current safety performance **fails the emotional or functional job**, e.g.:
- Functional job: *"Boil water quickly."*
- Emotional job: *"Feel in control and safe while cooking."*
- Safety opportunity: *Auto-shutoff + child-lock addresses both jobs simultaneously.*

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every quantitative formula** examiners may expect on this topic. Memorise the formulae and the **units** — losing a mark on units is the single most common KTU pitfall.

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Form | Variables & Units | Application in Opportunity Identification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Risk Priority Number** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | $S$: Severity (1–10) · $O$: Occurrence (1–10) · $D$: Detection (1–10) · RPN: dimensionless | Quantitatively ranks identified opportunities; threshold for action is typically **RPN > 100** |
| 2 | **Hazard Rate** | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | $f(t)$: failures · $R(t)$: survivors · $\lambda$: failures/hour | Identifies opportunities in components with elevated hazard rates |
| 3 | **Mean Time Between Failures** | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda$: failures/hour · MTBF: hours | Sets reliability benchmark for prioritising opportunities |
| 4 | **Probability of Failure on Demand** | $PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$ | $\lambda_{DU}$: dangerous undetected failure rate (hr⁻¹) · $T_{proof}$: proof-test interval (hr) | Used per IEC 61511 for safety-instrumented opportunities |
| 5 | **SIL Availability** | $Availability = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | MTTR: Mean Time To Repair (hr) | Helps choose whether redundancy (high availability) is an opportunity |
| 6 | **Opportunity Impact Score (custom)** | $I = (S + O) \times V$ | $V$: number of affected users (millions) | Used to weight opportunities by population reach |
| 7 | **Cost of Inaction** | $COI = N \times P \times L$ | $N$: population exposed · $P$: probability · $L$: loss per incident (₹/$) | Justifies investment in safety opportunity |
| 8 | **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** | $BCR = \dfrac{PV_{benefits}}{PV_{costs}}$ | PV: present value (₹/$) | Filters opportunities with $BCR > 1$ |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> Identifying opportunities is not an academic exercise — it is the **commercial engine** of safety innovation. In industry:
>
> - **Automotive:** Tesla's over-the-air recall opportunities originate from fleet-wide telemetry (technology-led stream).
> - **Medical Devices:** Insulin-pump safety upgrades are driven by FDA MAUDE database mining (failure-led stream).
> - **Consumer Electronics:** Apple's privacy labels emerged from regulatory pressure (GDPR + user-led) — a PESTLE-Led opportunity.
> - **Industrial Machinery:** Collaborative robots (cobots) arose from observed worker injuries in traditional robotic cells (user-led + failure-led).
>
> In every case, the **structured opportunity-identification framework preceded the engineering solution**. Skipping it leads to feature-bloat, regulatory non-compliance, and unsafe products.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — From Raw Complaint to Prioritised Opportunity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following worked example illustrates the **complete transformation pipeline** from raw user complaint to a quantified, prioritised safety opportunity. This is the exact workflow KTU examiners reward in Part B answers.

---

### Case Study: Electric Kettle — "Water Splashes When Poured"

A manufacturer receives 247 customer complaints in 6 months stating: *"Water splashes when I pour the kettle, sometimes scalding my hand."*

#### **Step 1 — Signal Classification**

The complaint is recorded under **Stage 1 (Signal Gathering)** and tagged as a *User-Led* stream.

*Logic:* Raw qualitative data enters the system. No interpretation yet.

#### **Step 2 — Pattern Filtering**

Affected users are clustered:
- 142 reports from users aged **55+** (elderly)
- 88 reports from users with **arthritis or weak grip**
- 17 reports from caregivers assisting **disabled individuals**

*Logic:* Demographics and physical capability emerge as moderators. The complaint is *not* universal — it is mediated by **grip strength and dexterity**.

#### **Step 3 — Hazard Translation (Formal Syntax)**

Apply the hazard-statement grammar:

$$\text{Hazard Statement} = \text{[Agent]} + \text{[Mechanism]} + \text{[Harm]} + \text{[Affected Person]}$$

Substituting:

$$\text{HS} = \text{Boiling water (100°C)} \;+\; \text{Splash during pour} \;+\; \text{2nd-degree scald burn} \;+\; \text{Elderly user with reduced grip}$$

*Logic:* The vague complaint is now a **formal engineering hazard statement** amenable to risk scoring.

#### **Step 4 — RPN Computation**

The cross-functional team assigns scores based on industry FMEA tables:

| Factor | Score | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Severity (S)** | $S = 7$ | 2nd-degree scald requiring medical attention (serious, not catastrophic) |
| **Occurrence (O)** | $O = 6$ | Occurs in ~30% of pours by affected demographic (moderately frequent) |
| **Detection (D)** | $D = 8$ | User cannot predict splash before it happens; no visual cue |

$$\begin{aligned}
RPN &= S \times O \times D \\
    &= 7 \times 6 \times 8 \\
    &= 336
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* The calculated RPN of **336** far exceeds the industry threshold of **100**, confirming this is a **high-priority safety opportunity**.

#### **Step 5 — Opportunity Articulation ("How Might We…?")**

Convert the hazard into a positively framed opportunity:

> *"How might we prevent boiling-water splashing during pour for users with reduced grip strength?"*

*Logic:* The framing is **solution-neutral** — it invites multiple design responses (lid geometry, drip-free spout, auto-cutoff tilt-sensor, two-handle design, etc.) without prematurely committing to one.

#### **Step 6 — Cost of Inaction Estimation**

Assume an exposed population $N$:

- Annual sold units: $1{,}000{,}000$
- Affected fraction: $12\%$
- Probability per user-year of scald: $0.05$
- Average liability per scald (medical + litigation): ₹$50{,}000$

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= 1{,}000{,}000 \times 0.12 = 120{,}000 \text{ exposed users} \\
P &= 0.05 \\
L &= 50{,}000 \; \text{₹} \\
COI &= N \times P \times L \\
    &= 120{,}000 \times 0.05 \times 50{,}000 \\
    &= 300{,}000{,}000 \; \text{₹} \; (= ₹30 \text{ million / year})
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* The **expected annual liability** quantifies the *value* of the opportunity, allowing ROI comparison against design investment.

#### **Step 7 — Prioritisation Decision**

Using the opportunity scorecard:

| Opportunity | RPN | COI (₹) | Affected Users | Strategic Fit | Rank |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Splash-during-pour mitigation | **336** | **30 M** | **120 K** | High | **1** |
| Cord-tangle during storage | 84 | 2 M | 50 K | Medium | 2 |
| Lid-opening force (children) | 245 | 12 M | 80 K | High | 2 |

> **Decision:** The splash-pour opportunity advances immediately to Module 3 (Ideation).

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Identification Method

| If the opportunity is… | Use this method | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Unknown / latent** | Ethnography, observation, empathy maps | User cannot articulate what they don't know |
| **Complaint-driven** | VOC analysis, complaint clustering | Patterns already exist in textual data |
| **Failure-driven** | FMEA, FTA, accident forensics | Quantitative rigor is mandatory |
| **Regulatory-driven** | Standards gap-analysis, horizon scanning | Ensures legal compliance and future-proofing |
| **Technology-driven** | Patent landscaping, technology roadmaps | Reveals enablers that unlock new safeguards |
| **Stakeholder-conflict-driven** | Stakeholder matrix, value-network mapping | Surfaces misalignments between parties |

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem — Benefit-Cost Ratio

> A safety opportunity requires ₹**8 million** investment (PV). Over 5 years, it is expected to prevent incidents valued at ₹**2.5 million / year** (declining 10% annually due to adoption saturation). Should the firm invest?

**Yearly benefit stream:**
- $B_1 = 2.5$ M
- $B_2 = 2.5 \times 0.9 = 2.25$ M
- $B_3 = 2.25 \times 0.9 = 2.025$ M
- $B_4 = 2.025 \times 0.9 = 1.8225$ M
- $B_5 = 1.8225 \times 0.9 = 1.64025$ M

**Sum (undiscounted):**

$$\begin{aligned}
PV_{benefits} &= 2.5 + 2.25 + 2.025 + 1.8225 + 1.64025 \\
              &= 10.23775 \; \text{M} \\
              &\approx 10.24 \; \text{M}
\end{aligned}$$

**Benefit-Cost Ratio:**

$$\begin{aligned}
BCR &= \frac{PV_{benefits}}{PV_{costs}} \\
    &= \frac{10.24}{8} \\
    &= 1.28
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* Since **BCR > 1**, the opportunity is **economically justified**. Even without discounting (which would make it less attractive), the investment passes.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow — Opportunity Identification Pipeline

The following Mermaid block renders the **complete identification workflow** from raw signal to prioritised opportunity. Use it as the visual anchor for any 14-mark answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Signals] --> B[Stage 1: Signal Gathering]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Pattern Filtering via Affinity Diagrams]
    C --> D[Stage 3: Hazard Translation to Formal Statement]
    D --> E[Stage 4: RPN Scoring and Threshold Check]
    E --> F{RPN greater than 100?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Stage 5a: Opportunity Articulation]
    F -- No --> H[Archive as Low Priority]
    G --> I[Stage 5b: Cost of Inaction and BCR]
    I --> J{BCR greater than 1?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Qualifies as Prioritised Safety Opportunity]
    J -- No --> L[Re-evaluate or Defer]
    K --> M[Module 3: Ideation Entry Point]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Five Provenance Streams

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Streams[Five Provenance Streams of Opportunity]
        direction TB
        S1[User Led: Field Studies, VOC]
        S2[Failure Led: FMEA, Accident Data]
        S3[Regulatory Led: ISO, IEC, BIS]
        S4[Technology Led: Patents, IoT]
        S5[Competitive Led: Benchmarking, Recalls]
    end
    Streams --> HUB[Central Opportunity Hub: Cluster and Theme]
    HUB --> ART[Opportunity Statement: How Might We]
    ART --> PRI[Prioritisation Engine: RPN plus COI plus BCR]
    PRI --> DEL[Deliverable: Ranked Opportunity Backlog]
```

---

## 4.3 SWOT-to-Opportunity Conversion Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Product and Market Inputs] --> SW[Build SWOT Matrix]
    SW --> SO[S plus O Cell: Amplify]
    SW --> WO[W plus O Cell: OVERCOME WEAKNESS via OPPORTUNITY]
    SW --> ST[S plus T Cell: Defend]
    SW --> WT[W plus T Cell: Minimise]
    SO --> OUT[Identified Opportunity Set]
    WO --> OUT
    ST --> OUT
    WT --> OUT
    OUT --> RANK[Rank via RPN and BCR]
```

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU answers, the **W-O cell** deserves special emphasis: it is the classic "leverage point" where a *known internal weakness* meets an *external opportunity* — exactly the configuration that produces breakthrough safety innovations (e.g., a weak grip-design weakness + an opportunity in soft-touch polymer materials = safer-handle redesign).

---

## 4.4 PESTLE Mapping to Safety Opportunities (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Political and Legal] --> REG[Regulatory Compliance Opportunities]
    E[Economic] --> ECO[Cost of Risk and Insurance Opportunities]
    S[Social] --> SOC[Demographic and Behavioural Opportunities]
    T[Technological] --> TECH[Sensor and Connectivity Opportunities]
    L[Environmental] --> ENV[Sustainability and End of Life Opportunities]
    REG --> OUT[Integrated Opportunity Portfolio]
    ECO --> OUT
    SOC --> OUT
    TECH --> OUT
    ENV --> OUT
```

---

## 4.5 Voice-of-Customer Triangulation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V1[Direct Statements: Interviews, Surveys] --> T{Two of Three Agree?}
    V2[Observed Behaviour: Field Studies] --> T
    V3[Indirect Artefacts: Logs, Returns] --> T
    T -- Yes --> VAL[Validated Safety Opportunity]
    T -- No --> INV[Return to Field for More Data]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "Identify Opportunities" in the context of safety in product design. List any three structured methods used for opportunity identification.*

**Model Answer (Concise, KTU Pattern):**

> **Definition:** Identify Opportunities is the structured process of discovering, framing, and prioritising latent and explicit areas in a product's lifecycle where safety improvements or hazard mitigations can be introduced, based on user research, failure data, regulatory landscape, technology trends, and competitive benchmarks.
>
> **Three structured methods:**
> 1. **SWOT Analysis** — converts internal strengths/weaknesses and external opportunities/threats into actionable interventions.
> 2. **PESTLE Analysis** — scans the macro-environment (Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, Environmental) for emerging safety drivers.
> 3. **Voice of Customer (VOC) Triangulation** — validates opportunities through two-of-three agreement across user statements, observed behaviour, and indirect artefacts.

*(Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for methods with brief justification)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*State the Risk Priority Number (RPN) formula. What is the generally accepted action threshold? Why is RPN alone insufficient for prioritising modern safety opportunities?*

**Model Answer:**

> **Formula:**
> $$RPN = S \times O \times D$$
> where $S$ = Severity, $O$ = Occurrence, $D$ = Detection; each rated 1–10.
>
> **Action threshold:** Industry convention accepts **RPN ≤ 100** as tolerable; values above require mitigation.
>
> **Why insufficient alone:**
> 1. RPN treats all dimensions as equally weighted, ignoring **economic scale** (one hazard affecting millions outweighs another affecting hundreds).
> 2. It ignores **detectability evolution** as technology improves.
> 3. Modern practice (per IEC 60812:2015 updates) recommends augmenting RPN with **Cost of Inaction (COI)** and **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** for resource-aware prioritisation.

*(Marks: 1 formula + 1 threshold + 1 critique)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice (ESE Pattern)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students must answer **one full question** of 14 marks from the Module-2 internal choice. The two choices below are **fully independent** — answer either **Question A** or **Question B**.

---

### ⭐ **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the five provenance streams of opportunity identification in safety product design. Illustrate each stream with one real-world engineering example.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A domestic gas stove receives 89 user complaints over 4 months regarding flame instability and unexpected blow-out during low-flow operation. Using the Stage 1-to-Stage 5 framework, walk through the complete identification process, calculate the RPN assuming S = 8, O = 5, D = 7, and articulate the opportunity statement.*

---

#### Model Solution to Q(a)

The **five provenance streams** are:

1. **User-Led Stream:** Arises from direct user research — interviews, ethnography, surveys.
   *Example:* Dyson identified the opportunity for a bladeless, finger-safe fan after observing children insert objects into traditional fan grilles.

2. **Failure-Led Stream:** Emerges from accident databases, warranty claims, FMEA outputs.
   *Example:* Samsung Galaxy Note 7 battery-fire opportunities arose from CPSC failure data clustering.

3. **Regulatory-Led Stream:** Driven by standards updates, court rulings, government mandates.
   *Example:* The European Union's updated Machinery Regulation (2023/1230) created safety-opportunity mandates for collaborative robot force-limiting sensors.

4. **Technology-Led Stream:** Enabled by emerging materials, sensors, software.
   *Example:* Low-cost MEMS accelerometers enabled tilt-detection safety cutoffs in modern kettles and irons — an opportunity that did not exist a decade earlier.

5. **Competitive-Led Stream:** Surfaced through benchmarking and recall monitoring.
   *Example:* Volvo's pedestrian airbag (introduced in 2012) emerged from observing competitor fatality statistics and pedestrian-airbag patent landscaping.

> **Synthesis:** Combining all five streams produces a **multi-perspective opportunity map**, reducing the risk of blind spots inherent in any single-stream approach.

---

#### Model Solution to Q(b)

**Stage 1 — Signal Gathering:**
- 89 complaints in 4 months ≈ 22/month, a statistically significant signal.
- Source: VOC complaint logs.

**Stage 2 — Pattern Filtering:**
- 62% of complaints relate to **low-flow blow-out**; 38% relate to **ignition delay**.
- Affected demographic: 71% are users in **high-altitude regions** (lower atmospheric pressure).

**Stage 3 — Hazard Translation:**

$$\text{HS} = \text{Unburned LPG leakage during flame blow-out} + \text{Delayed ignition by spark} + \text{Flash fire or explosion} + \text{Adult home cook}$$

**Stage 4 — RPN Computation:**

Given $S = 8$, $O = 5$, $D = 7$:

$$\begin{aligned}
RPN &= 8 \times 5 \times 7 \\
    &= 280
\end{aligned}$$

Since $RPN = 280 > 100$, the opportunity qualifies as **high-priority**.

**Stage 5 — Opportunity Articulation:**

> *"How might we maintain stable flame ignition across low-flow and high-altitude operating conditions to prevent LPG accumulation and delayed ignition?"*

The opportunity is now ready for **ideation** in Module 3.

*(Marks: 1 per stage + 1 for calculation setup + 1 for final RPN + 1 for opportunity statement = 7 marks)*

---

### ⭐ **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the PESTLE framework as applied to safety opportunity identification. Identify at least two PESTLE-derived safety opportunities relevant to the Indian two-wheeler market.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A children's toy manufacturer is considering a redesign opportunity for a battery compartment. The current design allows easy access by children under 5 (causing chemical-burn incidents). The redesign will cost ₹15 million and is expected to prevent 800 incidents per year at an average liability of ₹25,000 per incident. Calculate the BCR and recommend whether the firm should proceed.*

---

#### Model Solution to Q(a)

**PESTLE Framework (with diagram reference to Section 4.4):**

| Factor | Description | Indian Two-Wheeler Opportunity |
|---|---|---|
| **P – Political** | Government policy, subsidies, bans | FAME-II subsidies for EV two-wheelers with regenerative safety braking |
| **E – Economic** | Insurance premiums, fuel pricing | Opportunity to integrate telematics-based usage-based insurance to incentivise safer riding |
| **S – Social** | Aging riders, youth adoption, gender usage | Opportunity for step-through low-seat-height designs for women and elderly users |
| **T – Technological** | IoT, ABS, ADAS miniaturisation | Opportunity to embed low-cost ABS modules in entry-level bikes (currently absent in sub-₹1 lakh segment) |
| **L – Legal** | Motor Vehicle Act amendments, recall rules | Opportunity to design tamper-proof speed governors compliant with Section 194D penalties |
| **E – Environmental** | Emission norms (BS-VII), EV push | Opportunity for battery thermal-runaway mitigation systems in electric two-wheelers |

**Synthesis:** PESTLE generates opportunities spanning **policy compliance, market positioning, demographic inclusivity, technology adoption, legal risk reduction, and environmental alignment** — making it a comprehensive macro-screening tool.

---

#### Model Solution to Q(b)

**Given Data:**
- Investment $PV_{costs} = ₹15$ million
- Incidents prevented per year $N = 800$
- Liability per incident $L = ₹25{,}000$

**Annual benefit:**

$$\begin{aligned}
B_{annual} &= N \times L \\
           &= 800 \times 25{,}000 \\
           &= 20{,}000{,}000 \; \text{₹} = 20 \; \text{million / year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Assume project lifetime = 1 year (simplification, no discounting) for safety redesign:**

$$\begin{aligned}
PV_{benefits} &= 20 \; \text{million} \\
PV_{costs} &= 15 \; \text{million}
\end{aligned}$$

**Benefit-Cost Ratio:**

$$\begin{aligned}
BCR &= \frac{PV_{benefits}}{PV_{costs}} \\
    &= \frac{20}{15} \\
    &= 1.33
\end{aligned}$$

**Decision Rule:** Since $BCR = 1.33 > 1$, the firm **should proceed** with the redesign.

**Caveat:** Even at a multi-year horizon with discounting, if the same ₹20 M annual benefit persists for ≥ 1 year, the investment is justified. Additionally, intangible benefits (brand trust, regulatory goodwill, child-safety award eligibility) further strengthen the case beyond pure BCR math.

*(Marks: 1 data extraction + 1 benefit formula + 1 BCR formula + 1 substitution + 1 final ratio + 1 decision + 1 caveat = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Identify-Opportunities Answers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "opportunity" with "solution."** Students often jump directly to a design fix (e.g., "add a child-lock"). The opportunity must be a *problem framing* (How-Might-We question), not a *solution*. Marks are reserved for **framing discipline**.
>
> 2. **Omitting units.** Forgetting ₹, hr, °C, /yr in numerical answers costs 0.5–1 mark per instance. Always write the unit explicitly.
>
> 3. **Skipping the threshold check.** Computing RPN without stating that RPN > 100 is the **action trigger** loses the "interpretation" mark.
>
> 4. **Single-stream answers.** Citing only VOC or only FMEA in a 7-mark question signals shallow analysis. Examiners expect **multi-stream triangulation**.
>
> 5. **No reference to standard frameworks.** Naming a framework (SWOT, PESTLE, JTBD) without explaining *why it was chosen* loses the application mark.
>
> 6. **Confusing Identify-Opportunities with Ideate.** Identify = **what to solve**; Ideate = **how to solve**. Many answers blur this boundary.
>
> 7. **Ignoring human factors.** Even a numerically perfect RPN answer is incomplete if the *affected user demographic* (age, ability, context) is not mentioned.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 20 high-yield points every KTU 2024 student must internalise before entering the exam hall:**

- ☐ **Identify Opportunities = structured translation of signals into safety intervention points.**
- ☐ It is the **second stage of design thinking**, bridging empathy and ideation.
- ☐ Five provenance streams: **User, Failure, Regulatory, Technology, Competitive.**
- ☐ Five stages: **Signal Gathering → Pattern Filtering → Hazard Translation → RPN Scoring → Opportunity Articulation.**
- ☐ Hazard-statement syntax: **Agent + Mechanism + Harm + Affected Person.**
- ☐ Opportunity-statement syntax: **"How might we … for [user] in [context]?"**
- ☐ **RPN formula:** $RPN = S \times O \times D$; threshold **> 100** triggers action.
- ☐ Severity scale: **1 (negligible) → 10 (catastrophic fatality)**.
- ☐ Detection scale: **1 (highly detectable) → 10 (undetectable)** — note the *inverse* convention.
- ☐ **Cost of Inaction:** $COI = N \times P \times L$ quantifies latent liability.
- ☐ **BCR > 1** justifies investment in a safety opportunity.
- ☐ **SWOT** is best for internal-external leverage analysis; the **W-O cell** is the richest safety-opportunity source.
- ☐ **PESTLE** is best for macro-environment scanning and regulatory foresight.
- ☐ **VOC Triangulation** requires **2-of-3 agreement** to validate an opportunity.
- ☐ **JTBD** reframes safety as functional + emotional job completion.
- ☐ **Hazard rate λ** and **MTBF** are foundational reliability metrics for opportunity prioritisation.
- ☐ **SIL 1–4** (per IEC 61508) classifies safety-integrity opportunities in industrial systems.
- ☐ Industry examples to remember: **Tesla OTA recall, Dyson bladeless fan, Samsung Note 7, Volvo pedestrian airbag.**
- ☐ Always reference **affected demographic** (age, ability, context) in opportunity statements.
- ☐ Distinguish **Identify (what to solve)** from **Ideate (how to solve)** — KTU examiners test this boundary explicitly.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluate and Prioritize Projects
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT747 - SAFETY IN PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Evaluate and Prioritize Projects

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Project Evaluation** is the systematic, analytical process of assessing the technical, economic, safety, and strategic viability of proposed engineering projects using quantitative metrics and qualitative judgment frameworks. **Project Prioritization** is the subsequent ranking of these evaluated projects into an ordered sequence for resource allocation, execution scheduling, and capital deployment, based on criteria such as Net Present Value (NPV), Internal Rate of Return (IRR), Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR), Risk Priority Number (RPN), and Safety Integrity Level (SIL) compliance.

In the context of **Safety in Product Design**, evaluation and prioritization extend beyond mere profitability to incorporate the **ALARP principle (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)**, hazard mitigation effectiveness, and regulatory compliance under frameworks such as **IEC 61508** and **ISO 12100**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** The Board Examiner expects students to articulate not only the *financial mathematics* of evaluation but also the *safety-weighted decision matrix* approach. Memorizing only NPV/IRR formulas is insufficient — you must be able to discuss **multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA)** with safety weighting factors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a parent with a limited monthly budget and five children asking for different things: school fees, a new bicycle, a music class, a medical checkup, and a family vacation. You cannot afford all five. Before you say "yes" to anyone, you **evaluate** each request (cost vs. benefit, urgency, long-term impact) and then **prioritize** them. The medical checkup might score highest on safety, the school fees on long-term return, and the vacation on emotional value. You rank them and allocate funds accordingly.

Similarly, in product design safety engineering, a company has limited capital, engineering hours, and testing facilities. Multiple safety improvement projects compete for these scarce resources. **Evaluation** determines *which projects are worth doing*; **prioritization** determines *which to do first*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - *Evaluation* = Determining the merit/viability of a single project (Is it good?).
> - *Prioritization* = Ranking multiple viable projects against each other (Which is best relative to others?).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

The following metrics are universally applied in KTU-aligned project evaluation:

- **NPV (Net Present Value)** — measured in monetary units (₹, $, €)
- **IRR (Internal Rate of Return)** — dimensionless percentage (%)
- **BCR (Benefit-Cost Ratio)** — dimensionless ratio
- **PP (Payback Period)** — measured in years or months
- **RPN (Risk Priority Number)** — dimensionless score from FMEA
- **DCF (Discounted Cash Flow)** — monetary units
- **MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return)** — discount rate benchmark, typically **8%–15%** in industrial projects
- **WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital)** — used as discount rate in corporate settings

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Project Evaluation

Project evaluation operates on three classical pillars:

1. **Technical Feasibility** — Can the project be engineered and built with available technology?
2. **Economic Feasibility** — Will the project generate returns exceeding the cost of capital?
3. **Safety & Regulatory Feasibility** — Does the project meet mandatory safety standards and reduce risk to tolerable levels?

### 2.1.1 Step 1 — Cash Flow Identification
Every project generates a stream of **cash inflows** (revenue, salvage value, risk reduction savings) and **cash outflows** (capex, opex, compliance cost, training, insurance) over its life cycle $n$ (in years).

### 2.1.2 Step 2 — Time Value Adjustment
A rupee today is worth more than a rupee next year due to **inflation, opportunity cost, and risk**. All future cash flows are discounted to **Present Value (PV)** using the discount rate $i$:

$$PV = \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

### 2.1.3 Step 3 — Aggregate and Compare
The discounted cash flows are summed (NPV) or compared as ratios (BCR, IRR) against acceptance thresholds.

### 2.1.4 Step 4 — Safety Weighting
For safety-driven product design projects, an **adjustment multiplier** $w_s$ is applied to the NPV to reflect the avoided cost of accidents, recalls, litigation, and reputational damage. This is often called the **Risk-Adjusted NPV (RA-NPV)**.

### 2.1.5 Step 5 — Multi-Criteria Scoring
Projects are scored across multiple dimensions (cost, safety benefit, strategic alignment, technical risk) and aggregated via weighted summation to produce a final **Priority Index (PI)**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value bars in formulas below use `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity. Do **not** use the vertical pipe character `|` inside table cells.

| # | Metric | Formula | Decision Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|--------|---------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | Accept if $NPV \geq 0$ | Monetary units (₹, $) |
| 2 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$ | Accept if $IRR \geq MARR$ | Percent (%) — solved iteratively |
| 3 | **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** | $BCR = \dfrac{\sum \dfrac{B_t}{(1+i)^t}}{\sum \dfrac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}}$ | Accept if $BCR \geq 1$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | **Payback Period (PP)** | $PP = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Flow}}$ | Accept if $PP \leq \text{Target Years}$ | Years (for uniform CF) |
| 5 | **Discounted Payback Period** | Solve: $\sum_{t=0}^{PP^*} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t} = 0$ | Accept if $PP^* \leq \text{Target}$ | Years |
| 6 | **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Mitigate if $RPN \geq \text{Threshold (typically 100)}$ | $S$ = Severity, $O$ = Occurrence, $D$ = Detection |
| 7 | **Risk-Adjusted NPV** | $RA\text{-}NPV = NPV - \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{EAC_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | Accept if $RA\text{-}NPV \geq 0$ | $EAC$ = Expected Accident Cost |
| 8 | **Priority Index (PI)** | $PI = \sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j \cdot s_{ij}$ | Rank descending | $w_j$ = weight, $s_{ij}$ = score, $\sum w_j = 1$ |
| 9 | **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** | $EMV = \sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k \cdot X_k$ | Choose action with max EMV | $p_k$ = probability, $X_k$ = outcome |
| 10 | **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)** | $EAC = \dfrac{NPV \cdot i}{1-(1+i)^{-n}}$ | Used for comparing unequal-life projects | Monetary units / year |
| 11 | **Safety Weighting Factor** | $w_s = 1 + \dfrac{\Delta R}{R_{acceptable}}$ | Higher $w_s$ for high-$\Delta R$ projects | Dimensionless multiplier |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **automotive safety design**, when a manufacturer considers projects like (a) adding side-curtain airbags, (b) upgrading brake-by-wire, (c) introducing a new ADAS calibration cell, and (d) retooling for ISO 26262 ASIL-D compliance, each project is evaluated on cost, NHTSA safety rating impact, and recall-avoidance savings. The **highest RPN-reduction-per-rupee** project is prioritized.

In **process industries**, project evaluation underpins **HAZOP-driven capital expenditure** — for instance, deciding whether to install a flame detector, a relief valve, or a secondary containment. The BCR for each is computed using avoided-loss-of-containment figures, and the project with the highest safety-weighted BCR is executed first.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Net Present Value (NPV) from First Principles

**Starting Premise:** The time value of money dictates that a cash flow $CF_t$ received at time $t$ years in the future is worth less than the same cash flow received today, by a factor that depends on the discount rate $i$.

**Step 1:** Define the present value of a single future cash flow $CF_t$ at time $t$:

$$PV_t = \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

**Step 2 (Rationale):** If you invest $PV_t$ today at compound interest rate $i$, after $t$ years it grows to $PV_t \cdot (1+i)^t = CF_t$. Therefore, the present equivalent is $CF_t$ divided by the growth factor $(1+i)^t$.

**Step 3:** Sum all discounted cash flows from $t = 0$ (initial investment, usually negative) to $t = n$ (project end):

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

**Step 4 (Decision Logic):** If $NPV > 0$, the project generates value above the discount rate (MARR) and is acceptable. If $NPV < 0$, the project destroys value. If $NPV = 0$, the project breaks even on a discounted basis.

**Step 5 (Numerical Illustration):** Consider a safety project with:
- Initial investment $CF_0 = -\text{₹}10,00,000$ (negative outflow)
- Annual savings $CF_1 = CF_2 = CF_3 = \text{₹}4,00,000$ (avoided accident costs)
- Project life $n = 3$ years
- Discount rate $i = 10\%$

**Step 5a — Year 1 PV calculation:**

$$PV_1 = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{(1.10)^1} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{1.10} = \text{₹}3{,}63{,}636.36$$

**Step 5b — Year 2 PV calculation:**

$$PV_2 = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{(1.10)^2} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{1.21} = \text{₹}3{,}30{,}578.51$$

**Step 5c — Year 3 PV calculation:**

$$PV_3 = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{(1.10)^3} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{1.331} = \text{₹}3{,}00{,}525.92$$

**Step 5d — Aggregate NPV:**

$$\begin{aligned}
NPV &= -10{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}63{,}636.36 + 3{,}30{,}578.51 + 3{,}00{,}525.92 \\
&= -10{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}94{,}740.79 \\
&= -\text{₹}5{,}259.21
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5e — Decision:** Since $NPV < 0$, the project **does not meet** the 10% MARR threshold. It should be rejected *on purely financial grounds*. However, the evaluator may invoke the **Safety Premium** — the project reduces RPN from 200 to 60, saving potential liability of ₹50,00,000 in worst-case accident scenarios. The risk-adjusted NPV then becomes positive, justifying the project.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

**Step 1:** By definition, IRR is the discount rate $i^*$ that makes NPV equal to zero:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i^*)^t} = 0$$

**Step 2 (Rationale):** IRR represents the *break-even* rate of return. If the project's actual return exceeds the cost of capital (MARR), it is acceptable.

**Step 3 (Analytical Limit):** For the simple case of $CF_0 = -I$ (initial outlay) and uniform annual cash flow $A$ for $n$ years, the equation reduces to:

$$-I + A \cdot \frac{1-(1+i^*)^{-n}}{i^*} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging:

$$\frac{I}{A} = \frac{1-(1+i^*)^{-n}}{i^*}$$

**Step 5 (Numerical Example):** For $I = \text{₹}10,00,000$, $A = \text{₹}4,00,000$, $n = 3$ years:

$$\frac{10{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} = 2.5 = \frac{1-(1+i^*)^{-3}}{i^*}$$

**Step 5a — Trial $i^* = 9\%$:**

$$\frac{1-(1.09)^{-3}}{0.09} = \frac{1 - 0.7722}{0.09} = \frac{0.2278}{0.09} = 2.531$$

**Step 5b — Trial $i^* = 10\%$:**

$$\frac{1-(1.10)^{-3}}{0.10} = \frac{1 - 0.7513}{0.10} = \frac{0.2487}{0.10} = 2.487$$

**Step 5c — Interpolation:** Target value is 2.5, lies between 2.531 (at 9%) and 2.487 (at 10%):

$$i^* \approx 9\% + \frac{2.531 - 2.5}{2.531 - 2.487} \times 1\% = 9\% + \frac{0.031}{0.044} \times 1\% \approx 9.70\%$$

**Step 5d — Decision:** If $MARR = 10\%$, then $IRR = 9.70\% < 10\%$, project is **rejected** on IRR basis. If $MARR = 8\%$, project is **accepted**.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Priority Index (PI) in Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis

**Step 1:** Define $m$ evaluation criteria, each with weight $w_j$ such that:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j = 1$$

**Step 2:** For project $i$, assign a normalized score $s_{ij} \in [0, 10]$ against each criterion $j$.

**Step 3:** Aggregate the weighted scores:

$$PI_i = \sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j \cdot s_{ij}$$

**Step 4 (Safety-Inclusive Example):** Consider three safety projects $P_1, P_2, P_3$ evaluated on three criteria: Cost-Effectiveness, RPN-Reduction, and Regulatory Compliance, with weights $w_1 = 0.4$, $w_2 = 0.4$, $w_3 = 0.2$:

| Project | Cost-Effectiveness | RPN-Reduction | Regulatory Compliance | $PI_i$ |
|---------|--------------------|---------------|-----------------------|--------|
| $P_1$ | 8 | 9 | 7 | $0.4(8)+0.4(9)+0.2(7) = 8.2$ |
| $P_2$ | 7 | 8 | 9 | $0.4(7)+0.4(8)+0.2(9) = 7.8$ |
| $P_3$ | 6 | 9 | 8 | $0.4(6)+0.4(9)+0.2(8) = 7.6$ |

**Step 5 (Prioritization):** Rank order is $P_1 \succ P_2 \succ P_3$. Project $P_1$ receives the highest priority for execution.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Strengths of Methods

| Method | Strength | Weakness | Best Used When |
|--------|----------|----------|----------------|
| NPV | Captures full cash flow; theoretically superior | Requires accurate discount rate | Long-life, large-capital projects |
| IRR | Communicates intuitive % return | Multiple IRRs possible with non-conventional flows | Comparing single projects to MARR |
| BCR | Useful for public/safety sector projects | Sensitive to classification of disbenefits | Government or regulatory safety projects |
| Payback | Simple, focuses on liquidity | Ignores post-payback cash flows | Screening of high-uncertainty projects |
| RPN | Integrates FMEA into prioritization | Subjective scoring ($S, O, D$) | Reliability and safety-driven projects |
| PI | Handles multiple criteria systematically | Requires careful weight elicitation | Portfolio prioritization with strategic + safety factors |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Net Present Value (NPV) and state the decision rule for accepting or rejecting a project based on NPV.**

**Model Answer:**
Net Present Value is the algebraic sum of all discounted cash flows (inflows and outflows) associated with a project over its entire economic life, discounted back to time zero using a specified discount rate (MARR). Mathematically,

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

**Decision Rule:** A project is accepted if $NPV \geq 0$ (it generates at least the required rate of return) and rejected if $NPV < 0$ (it fails to recover the cost of capital in present-value terms). When multiple mutually exclusive projects are compared, the one with the **highest positive NPV** is selected.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is Risk Priority Number (RPN) in FMEA, and how is it used in project prioritization for safety in product design?**

**Model Answer:**
The Risk Priority Number is a quantitative risk-scoring metric used in Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) to rank the relative risk of identified failure modes. It is the product of three ordinal ratings:

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

where $S$ is Severity (1–10), $O$ is Occurrence (1–10), and $D$ is Detection difficulty (1–10). A higher RPN indicates a more critical failure mode demanding urgent mitigation.

In project prioritization, the **reduction in RPN** (i.e., $RPN_{before} - RPN_{after}$) achieved by each proposed safety project is computed. Projects yielding the largest RPN reduction per unit cost are prioritized, ensuring that engineering effort is directed toward the most hazardous failure modes in the product.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**A manufacturing company is evaluating three safety improvement projects for a CNC machining cell. The data are:**

| Parameter | Project A: Light Curtain | Project B: Interlock + Guard | Project C: Auto-Stop Sensor |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Initial Cost (₹) | 5,00,000 | 8,00,000 | 12,00,000 |
| Annual Savings (₹) | 1,80,000 | 3,00,000 | 4,50,000 |
| Project Life (years) | 5 | 5 | 5 |
| RPN Reduction | 80 | 150 | 220 |

**MARR = 12%. Determine:**
**(a)** The NPV of each project and identify which are financially viable. (7 marks)
**(b)** Compute the BCR for each viable project and rank them. Apply a safety weighting of 0.5 to the BCR of each project based on RPN reduction, and recommend the final priority order. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — NPV Calculation:**

The discount factor formula applied: $PV = \dfrac{CF_t}{(1.12)^t}$

**Project A (Light Curtain):**
- $CF_0 = -5,00,000$
- Annual cash flow $A = 1,80,000$ for $t = 1$ to $5$

The present value annuity factor (PVAF) for $n = 5$, $i = 12\%$:

$$PVAF = \frac{1-(1.12)^{-5}}{0.12} = \frac{1 - 0.5674}{0.12} = \frac{0.4326}{0.12} = 3.6048$$

$$\begin{aligned}
NPV_A &= -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 \times 3.6048 \\
&= -5{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}48{,}864 \\
&= \text{₹}1{,}48{,}864
\end{aligned}$$

**Project B (Interlock + Guard):**
- $CF_0 = -8,00,000$
- $A = 3,00,000$

$$\begin{aligned}
NPV_B &= -8{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 \times 3.6048 \\
&= -8{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}81{,}440 \\
&= \text{₹}2{,}81{,}440
\end{aligned}$$

**Project C (Auto-Stop Sensor):**
- $CF_0 = -12,00,000$
- $A = 4,50,000$

$$\begin{aligned}
NPV_C &= -12{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 \times 3.6048 \\
&= -12{,}00{,}000 + 16{,}22{,}160 \\
&= \text{₹}4{,}22{,}160
\end{aligned}$$

**Decision on Financial Viability:** All three projects have $NPV > 0$, so **all are financially viable** at MARR = 12%.

**Part (b) — BCR and Safety-Weighted Ranking:**

The BCR formula is:

$$BCR = \frac{PV \text{ of Benefits}}{PV \text{ of Costs}} = \frac{A \times PVAF}{I_0}$$

**Project A:**

$$BCR_A = \frac{1{,}80{,}000 \times 3.6048}{5{,}00{,}000} = \frac{6{,}48{,}864}{5{,}00{,}000} = 1.298$$

**Project B:**

$$BCR_B = \frac{3{,}00{,}000 \times 3.6048}{8{,}00{,}000} = \frac{10{,}81{,}440}{8{,}00{,}000} = 1.352$$

**Project C:**

$$BCR_C = \frac{4{,}50{,}000 \times 3.6048}{12{,}00{,}000} = \frac{16{,}22{,}160}{12{,}00{,}000} = 1.352$$

**Step 2 — Apply Safety Weighting (0.5 weight on RPN reduction, normalized):**

First, normalize the RPN reductions: $RPN_A = 80$, $RPN_B = 150$, $RPN_C = 220$, total $= 450$.

- Normalized RPN weight for A: $80/450 = 0.178$
- Normalized RPN weight for B: $150/450 = 0.333$
- Normalized RPN weight for C: $220/450 = 0.489$

Safety-weighted BCR (with 0.5 weight on RPN score, 0.5 on financial BCR):

$$BCR_A^{sw} = 0.5(1.298) + 0.5(0.178) = 0.649 + 0.089 = 0.738$$

$$BCR_B^{sw} = 0.5(1.352) + 0.5(0.333) = 0.676 + 0.167 = 0.843$$

$$BCR_C^{sw} = 0.5(1.352) + 0.5(0.489) = 0.676 + 0.245 = 0.921$$

**Step 3 — Final Priority Order (descending safety-weighted BCR):**

$$\boxed{P_C \;(\text{Auto-Stop Sensor}) \succ P_B \;(\text{Interlock + Guard}) \succ P_A \;(\text{Light Curtain})}$$

**Recommendation:** The company should execute Project C first, followed by Project B, then Project A.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**A consumer electronics company is reviewing four safety projects related to lithium-ion battery packs. The initial investments and uniform annual cash flows (avoided warranty/recall costs) over a 4-year life are:**

| Project | Initial Investment (₹) | Annual Cash Flow (₹) | Severity (S) | Occurrence (O) | Detection (D) |
|---------|------------------------|----------------------|---------------|-----------------|----------------|
| P1: Thermal cut-off | 6,00,000 | 2,50,000 | 8 | 4 | 5 |
| P2: Cell balancing BMS | 9,00,000 | 3,50,000 | 9 | 3 | 4 |
| P3: Venting membrane | 4,00,000 | 1,50,000 | 7 | 5 | 6 |
| P4: Fire-retardant casing | 11,00,000 | 4,20,000 | 8 | 2 | 3 |

**MARR = 15%. Compute:**
**(a)** The IRR for each project and identify the financially acceptable ones. (7 marks)
**(b)** The RPN for each project, then construct a Priority Index (PI) using weights 0.4 for IRR score, 0.3 for RPN score, and 0.3 for S/10 safety impact. Recommend the final priority order. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — IRR Calculation:**

For uniform cash flow over $n = 4$ years, IRR solves:

$$\frac{I}{A} = \frac{1-(1+i^*)^{-4}}{i^*}$$

**Project P1 (Thermal cut-off):** $I/A = 6{,}00{,}000 / 2{,}50{,}000 = 2.4$

Trial $i^* = 25\%$: $\frac{1-(1.25)^{-4}}{0.25} = \frac{1 - 0.4096}{0.25} = \frac{0.5904}{0.25} = 2.362$

Trial $i^* = 24\%$: $\frac{1-(1.24)^{-4}}{0.24} = \frac{1 - 0.4225}{0.24} = \frac{0.5775}{0.24} = 2.406$

Interpolation: $i^* \approx 24\% + \frac{2.406 - 2.4}{2.406 - 2.362} \times 1\% = 24\% + \frac{0.006}{0.044} \approx 24.14\%$

$$\boxed{IRR_{P1} \approx 24.14\%}$$

**Project P2 (Cell balancing BMS):** $I/A = 9{,}00{,}000 / 3{,}50{,}000 = 2.571$

Trial $i^* = 14\%$: $\frac{1-(1.14)^{-4}}{0.14} = \frac{1 - 0.5921}{0.14} = \frac{0.4079}{0.14} = 2.914$

Trial $i^* = 17\%$: $\frac{1-(1.17)^{-4}}{0.17} = \frac{1 - 0.5337}{0.17} = \frac{0.4663}{0.17} = 2.743$

Trial $i^* = 18\%$: $\frac{1-(1.18)^{-4}}{0.18} = \frac{1 - 0.5158}{0.18} = \frac{0.4842}{0.18} = 2.690$

Trial $i^* = 19\%$: $\frac{1-(1.19)^{-4}}{0.19} = \frac{1 - 0.4986}{0.19} = \frac{0.5014}{0.19} = 2.639$

Trial $i^* = 20\%$: $\frac{1-(1.20)^{-4}}{0.20} = \frac{1 - 0.4823}{0.20} = \frac{0.5177}{0.20} = 2.589$

Interpolating between 19% (2.639) and 20% (2.589) for target 2.571:

$$i^* \approx 19\% + \frac{2.639 - 2.571}{2.639 - 2.589} \times 1\% = 19\% + \frac{0.068}{0.050} \times 1\% \approx 20.36\%$$

$$\boxed{IRR_{P2} \approx 20.36\%}$$

**Project P3 (Venting membrane):** $I/A = 4{,}00{,}000 / 1{,}50{,}000 = 2.667$

Trial $i^* = 17\%$: factor = 2.743 (from above)
Trial $i^* = 20\%$: factor = 2.589

Interpolating for 2.667:

$$i^* \approx 17\% + \frac{2.743 - 2.667}{2.743 - 2.589} \times 3\% = 17\% + \frac{0.076}{0.154} \times 3\% \approx 18.48\%$$

$$\boxed{IRR_{P3} \approx 18.48\%}$$

**Project P4 (Fire-retardant casing):** $I/A = 11{,}00{,}000 / 4{,}20{,}000 = 2.619$

Trial $i^* = 20\%$: factor = 2.589
Trial $i^* = 19\%$: factor = 2.639

Interpolating for 2.619:

$$i^* \approx 19\% + \frac{2.639 - 2.619}{2.639 - 2.589} \times 1\% = 19\% + \frac{0.020}{0.050} \times 1\% \approx 19.40\%$$

$$\boxed{IRR_{P4} \approx 19.40\%}$$

**Decision on Financial Acceptance (MARR = 15%):** All four projects have $IRR > 15\%$, hence **all are financially acceptable**.

**Part (b) — RPN and Priority Index:**

**RPN Computation:**

| Project | S | O | D | RPN |
|---------|---|---|---|-----|
| P1 | 8 | 4 | 5 | **160** |
| P2 | 9 | 3 | 4 | **108** |
| P3 | 7 | 5 | 6 | **210** |
| P4 | 8 | 2 | 3 | **48** |

**Normalized scores (out of 10):**

- IRR score: $IRR \times 0.1$ capped at 10 → $P1: 2.41, P2: 2.04, P3: 1.85, P4: 1.94$. Hmm, these are all too low. Better to normalize relative to max.

Let me normalize each metric on a 0–10 scale using max-value normalization:

- **IRR (max = 24.14%):** Normalized: $P1: 10.00, P2: 8.43, P3: 7.65, P4: 8.04$
- **RPN (max = 210, but high RPN = bad, so use $(210 - RPN)/210 \times 10$, i.e., safety improvement):** 
  - $P1: (210-160)/210 \times 10 = 2.38$
  - $P2: (210-108)/210 \times 10 = 4.86$
  - $P3: (210-210)/210 \times 10 = 0.00$ (no safety improvement, since the project's purpose is to handle the high-RPN risk that P3's RPN itself represents; better to score RPN as hazard severity where high = high priority)
  - $P4: (210-48)/210 \times 10 = 7.71$

Actually, the cleanest interpretation: **higher RPN = more critical need = higher priority score**. Use raw RPN normalized:

- RPN score: $P1: 160/210 \times 10 = 7.62$, $P2: 108/210 \times 10 = 5.14$, $P3: 210/210 \times 10 = 10.00$, $P4: 48/210 \times 10 = 2.29$
- **S/10 safety impact:** $P1: 0.8, P2: 0.9, P3: 0.7, P4: 0.8$ → multiply by 10: $P1: 8, P2: 9, P3: 7, P4: 8$

**Priority Index Computation (weights: 0.4 IRR, 0.3 RPN, 0.3 S/10):**

$$\begin{aligned}
PI_{P1} &= 0.4(10.00) + 0.3(7.62) + 0.3(8.00) = 4.00 + 2.29 + 2.40 = 8.69 \\
PI_{P2} &= 0.4(8.43) + 0.3(5.14) + 0.3(9.00) = 3.37 + 1.54 + 2.70 = 7.61 \\
PI_{P3} &= 0.4(7.65) + 0.3(10.00) + 0.3(7.00) = 3.06 + 3.00 + 2.10 = 8.16 \\
PI_{P4} &= 0.4(8.04) + 0.3(2.29) + 0.3(8.00) = 3.22 + 0.69 + 2.40 = 6.31
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Priority Order (descending PI):**

$$\boxed{P1 \;(8.69) \succ P3 \;(8.16) \succ P2 \;(7.61) \succ P4 \;(6.31)}$$

**Recommendation:** The company should prioritize **Thermal cut-off (P1)** first, followed by **Venting membrane (P3)**, then **Cell balancing BMS (P2)**, and finally **Fire-retardant casing (P4)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to discount cash flows in Payback Period:** Simple (undiscounted) payback is acceptable only when the question explicitly says "simple payback." Otherwise, always use the **Discounted Payback Period** method. Loss: 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up IRR decision rule:** Many students write "Accept if IRR > 0" — the correct rule is **"Accept if IRR ≥ MARR."** Loss: 1 mark.
> 3. **Ignoring Safety Weighting:** A purely financial answer without acknowledging safety/RPN criteria will lose **3–4 marks** in a 14-mark question on this topic.
> 4. **Not converting $CF_0$ to negative:** Always explicitly state that the initial investment is a **cash outflow** (negative sign) in NPV. Forgetting this leads to NPV sign errors. Loss: 1 mark.
> 5. **Using single NPV comparison for unequal-life projects:** Always convert unequal-life alternatives to **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)** before ranking. Loss: 2 marks.
> 6. **Weight summation error in MCDA:** Weights must satisfy $\sum w_j = 1$. Validators deduct marks if normalization is wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Evaluate and Prioritize Projects**

- **Project Evaluation** assesses *individual project merit*; **Project Prioritization** ranks *multiple projects* relative to one another.
- The three pillars of evaluation are **Technical, Economic, and Safety/Regulatory** feasibility.
- **NPV** uses the formula $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t}$; accept if $NPV \geq 0$.
- **IRR** is the discount rate that makes NPV = 0; accept if $IRR \geq MARR$.
- **BCR** = (PV of benefits) / (PV of costs); accept if $BCR \geq 1$.
- **Payback Period** = Initial Investment / Annual Cash Flow (for uniform flows); accept if $PP \leq$ target years.
- **Discounted Payback Period** uses discounted cash flows; the more rigorous version.
- **RPN** in FMEA = $S \times O \times D$, with each rating on a 1–10 scale.
- **Risk-Adjusted NPV (RA-NPV)** = NPV − Expected Accident Cost, used when safety premiums must be quantified.
- **Priority Index (PI)** = $\sum w_j \cdot s_{ij}$ with $\sum w_j = 1$, used in MCDA.
- **EMV (Expected Monetary Value)** = $\sum p_k \cdot X_k$ — used for probabilistic outcomes.
- **EAC (Equivalent Annual Cost)** is mandatory when comparing projects of unequal lives.
- **Safety weighting factor** $w_s = 1 + \Delta R / R_{acceptable}$ amplifies NPV for high-risk-reduction projects.
- **ALARP principle** (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) is the philosophical anchor — a safety project with negative financial NPV may still be justified if the residual risk would otherwise exceed ALARP.
- In KTU answers, **always present a 3-step structure**: (1) Compute financial metric, (2) Compute safety/risk metric, (3) Combine via weighted scoring to produce a final rank.
- The **PVAF (Present Value Annuity Factor)** for $n = 5, i = 12\%$ is **3.6048** — this is a frequently tested value.
- The **standard MARR range** assumed by KTU problems is **10%–15%** unless stated otherwise.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Allocate Resources and Plan Timing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT747 - SAFETY IN PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Allocate Resources and Plan Timing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resource Allocation & Timing Planning (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> *Resource Allocation and Timing Planning* in safety-oriented product design is the systematic process of identifying, estimating, scheduling, and assigning the necessary **human, financial, material, equipment, and informational resources** across the entire product life-cycle — from concept, design, prototyping, verification, validation, production, and decommissioning — while simultaneously constructing a realistic, risk-weighted, time-bound execution schedule that satisfies regulatory safety obligations (ISO 12100, IEC 61508, OSHA 29 CFR 1910) and the organization's strategic milestones.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this topic directly maps to **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Plan and allocate resources for safety integration across the product design life-cycle, and develop time-bound schedules consistent with risk control hierarchies."*

The two principal pillars are:

| Pillar | Engineering Purpose | Safety Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Resource Allocation** | Distribute scarce assets optimally | Ensure every safety task has a competent owner and budget |
| **Timing Plan** | Schedule tasks chronologically | Guarantee that risk-reduction measures are validated **before** user exposure |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Building a House with Fire Safety:**
> Imagine you are building a three-storey house. Before a single brick is laid, you must (a) decide **how many electricians, fire-exit engineers, alarm installers, and fire extinguishers** you need (resource allocation) and (b) decide **when** the smoke-detector test must happen (it must be *before* the family moves in, not after). If you run out of money in Month 6, the fire-escape staircase will never be finished — and the family will live in a dangerous home. **Allocate Resources and Plan Timing** is exactly that pre-move-in checklist, scaled up to an industrial product.

A simpler analogy: think of it as **packing for a mountain expedition**. You must decide (1) *what* food, ropes, oxygen, and Sherpas to take (resources) and (2) *when* to leave base camp so you reach the summit before the storm (timing). Mistime the oxygen refill, and the climber dies at altitude.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

The following **standard safety-project planning metrics** are universally applied across KTU-evaluated problems:

- **$T_{ES}$** = Earliest Start Time
- **$T_{EF}$** = Earliest Finish Time
- **$T_{LS}$** = Latest Start Time
- **$T_{LF}$** = Latest Finish Time
- **$\text{Slack} = T_{LS} - T_{ES}$**
- **$\text{CPM}_{\text{critical\_path}} = \sum D_{\text{critical}}$**
- **$\sigma_{\text{PERT}} = \sqrt{\sum \sigma_i^2}$**
- **$E[T] = \dfrac{O + 4M + P}{6}$** (PERT expected duration)
- **Standard safety budget reserve** = **10 % to 15 %** of total project cost (industry norm for ISO 12100-compliant projects)

---

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Syllabus-Mapped Sub-Topics

The KTU 2024 Scheme module 2 specifically lists the following sub-bullets under *Allocate Resources and Plan Timing*:

1. Identification of safety-project resources (people, money, tools, information)
2. Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) for safety tasks
3. Time estimation techniques (deterministic vs probabilistic)
4. Gantt charts, Milestone charts, Network diagrams (AOA / AON)
5. Critical Path Method (CPM) and Program Evaluation Review Technique (PERT)
6. Resource levelling and resource smoothing
7. Safety milestone gating (concept → design → verification → validation)
8. Contingency planning and schedule buffers
9. Earned Value Management (EVM) for safety tasks

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Resource-Allocation Logic Chain (Operational Breakdown)

The process of allocating resources in a safety-engineered design project follows a deterministic six-step logical chain:

1. **Decompose** the safety scope into a *Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)*. Each leaf-node must be a *verifiable safety deliverable* (e.g., "Validate Emergency-Stop response time ≤ 50 ms").
2. **Estimate** the labour-hours, machine-hours, and material cost of every leaf-node using historical data, vendor quotes, or expert judgement.
3. **Assign** each leaf-node to a *competent resource pool* (e.g., functional safety engineer, certified test lab, FMEA facilitator).
4. **Aggregate** the resource demands across all parallel work packages to detect **over-allocation** (a person assigned 14 hours/day) or **under-allocation** (idle safety engineer).
5. **Level / Smooth** the demand profile using resource-levelling algorithms (integer programming, heuristics, or simply manual shifting).
6. **Lock** the allocation against a *baseline budget and baseline schedule*; any variance triggers a Change Control Board (CCB) review under ISO 9001 / ISO 14001.

### 2.2 The Timing-Planning Logic Chain

1. **Sequence** the WBS tasks using *precedence relationships* (finish-to-start, start-to-start, finish-to-finish, start-to-finish).
2. **Estimate** duration $D_i$ for every activity using deterministic ($D_i$) or three-point PERT ($O, M, P$) inputs.
3. **Build** an Activity-on-Arrow (AOA) or Activity-on-Node (AON) network.
4. **Forward pass** to compute $T_{ES}$ and $T_{EF}$.
5. **Backward pass** to compute $T_{LS}$ and $T_{LF}$.
6. **Compute slack** $\text{Slack}_i = T_{LS} - T_{ES}$.
7. **Identify** activities with $\text{Slack}_i = 0$ — these form the **Critical Path**.
8. **Gate the safety milestones** at phase boundaries (Concept Review, Preliminary Design Review, Critical Design Review, Production Readiness Review).

### 2.3 Why and How — Engineering Reasoning

| Step | *Why* it is necessary in safety design | *How* it is implemented in practice |
|---|---|---|
| WBS | Breaks an intangible "safety" goal into auditable tasks | Hierarchical tree, leaf = verifiable artefact |
| Resource estimate | Prevents cost over-run and missing safety skill | Bottom-up estimating, parametric models |
