| Sequence | Identifies dependent safety tests (e.g., HAZOP must precede FMEA) | Dependency matrix, N² chart |
| CPM / PERT | Finds the *longest* path — the one that delays the product launch | Forward / backward pass on the network |
| Resource levelling | Stops assigning one safety engineer to two parallel SIL-3 tasks | Heuristic / LP solver (e.g., MS Project) |
| Safety milestone gate | Enforces *independent* review before user exposure | Stage-gate process, ISO/IEC 33000 |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — KTU Board Exam Priority ⭐⭐⭐**

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E[T_i] = \dfrac{O_i + 4M_i + P_i}{6}$ | PERT expected activity duration | days / weeks |
| 2 | $\sigma_i = \dfrac{P_i - O_i}{6}$ | PERT standard deviation per activity | days / weeks |
| 3 | $T_{ES}(j) = \max_{i \to j}\left[T_{ES}(i) + D_i\right]$ | Earliest start of node *j* | days |
| 4 | $T_{EF}(i) = T_{ES}(i) + D_i$ | Earliest finish of activity *i* | days |
| 5 | $T_{LF}(i) = \min_{j \to i}\left[T_{LS}(j)\right]$ | Latest finish of activity *i* | days |
| 6 | $T_{LS}(i) = T_{LF}(i) - D_i$ | Latest start of activity *i* | days |
| 7 | $\text{Slack}_i = T_{LS}(i) - T_{ES}(i)$ | Total float / slack | days |
| 8 | $T_{\text{project}} = \max_j\left[T_{EF}(j)\right]$ | Critical path length | days |
| 9 | $\sigma_{\text{project}} = \sqrt{\sum_{k \in CP} \sigma_k^2}$ | Project variance (only critical-path activities) | days² |
| 10 | $\text{Prob}\left(T \leq T_s\right) = \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{T_s - T_{\text{project}}}{\sigma_{\text{project}}}\right)$ | Probability of finishing on/before target $T_s$ | dimensionless ∈ [0, 1] |
| 11 | $\text{BCWS} = \text{Budgeted Cost of Work Scheduled}$ | Earned Value baseline | ₹ / \$ |
| 12 | $\text{BCWP} = \text{Budgeted Cost of Work Performed}$ | Earned Value to date | ₹ / \$ |
| 13 | $\text{ACWP} = \text{Actual Cost of Work Performed}$ | Actual spend to date | ₹ / \$ |
| 14 | $\text{CV} = \text{BCWP} - \text{ACWP}$ | Cost Variance (positive = under budget) | ₹ / \$ |
| 15 | $\text{SV} = \text{BCWP} - \text{BCWS}$ | Schedule Variance (positive = ahead) | ₹ / \$ |
| 16 | $\text{CPI} = \dfrac{\text{BCWP}}{\text{ACWP}}$ | Cost Performance Index (>1 good) | ratio |
| 17 | $\text{SPI} = \dfrac{\text{BCWP}}{\text{BCWS}}$ | Schedule Performance Index (>1 good) | ratio |
| 18 | $B_{\text{contingency}} = k \cdot \sigma_{\text{project}}$ | Schedule buffer (k = 1.28 ⇒ 90 % C.L.) | days |
| 19 | $B_{\text{cost}} = 0.10 \text{ to } 0.15 \cdot C_{\text{total}}$ | Safety cost contingency (industry norm) | ₹ / \$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical-bar Rule:** Notice the use of $\Phi\!\left(\,\cdot\,\right)$ and $\sigma_{\text{project}}$ instead of $\mid \cdot \mid$. The KTU PDF renderer breaks when raw pipes appear inside markdown table cells.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In automotive functional-safety (ISO 26262) projects, the *Allocate Resources and Plan Timing* phase produces the **Safety Plan (item 6 of ISO 26262-2:2018)**. The plan is the *contract* between project management and the safety team. If a safety task slips, the *entire vehicle launch* slips — because a missed ASIL-D validation is a regulatory block. The same logic holds for medical devices (IEC 62304), industrial machinery (ISO 12100), and process plants (IEC 61511).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive PERT / CPM Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Problem (14-mark prototype):** A safety-critical control-panel project has **7 activities** A through G. The precedence table is given below. Three-point estimates $(O, M, P)$ are in weeks. **Tasks:** (a) Draw the AON network and compute the **expected duration and variance of every activity**. (b) Find the **critical path, project duration, project variance, and the probability of completing the project in ≤ 26 weeks.**

| Activity | Predecessors | Optimistic $O$ | Most likely $M$ | Pessimistic $P$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | — | 2 | 4 | 6 |
| B | — | 3 | 5 | 7 |
| C | A | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| D | A | 4 | 6 | 8 |
| E | B, C | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| F | D | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| G | E, F | 3 | 5 | 7 |

#### 3.1.1 Part (a) — Expected Duration $E[T_i]$ and Variance $\sigma_i^2$ of Every Activity

For every activity $i$, we use the classical PERT beta-distribution formulas:

$$E[T_i] = \dfrac{O_i + 4M_i + P_i}{6}$$

$$\sigma_i^2 = \left(\dfrac{P_i - O_i}{6}\right)^2$$

We compute each one explicitly.

**Activity A:**
$$E[T_A] = \dfrac{2 + 4(4) + 6}{6} = \dfrac{2 + 16 + 6}{6} = \dfrac{24}{6} = 4.00 \text{ weeks}$$

$$\sigma_A^2 = \left(\dfrac{6 - 2}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{4}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \dfrac{4}{9} \approx 0.4444 \text{ weeks}^2$$

**Activity B:**
$$E[T_B] = \dfrac{3 + 4(5) + 7}{6} = \dfrac{3 + 20 + 7}{6} = \dfrac{30}{6} = 5.00 \text{ weeks}$$

$$\sigma_B^2 = \left(\dfrac{7 - 3}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{4}{6}\right)^2 = \dfrac{16}{36} = \dfrac{4}{9} \approx 0.4444 \text{ weeks}^2$$

**Activity C:**
$$E[T_C] = \dfrac{1 + 4(2) + 3}{6} = \dfrac{1 + 8 + 3}{6} = \dfrac{12}{6} = 2.00 \text{ weeks}$$

$$\sigma_C^2 = \left(\dfrac{3 - 1}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{2}{6}\right)^2 = \dfrac{4}{36} = \dfrac{1}{9} \approx 0.1111 \text{ weeks}^2$$

**Activity D:**
$$E[T_D] = \dfrac{4 + 4(6) + 8}{6} = \dfrac{4 + 24 + 8}{6} = \dfrac{36}{6} = 6.00 \text{ weeks}$$

$$\sigma_D^2 = \left(\dfrac{8 - 4}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{4}{6}\right)^2 = \dfrac{16}{36} = \dfrac{4}{9} \approx 0.4444 \text{ weeks}^2$$

**Activity E:**
$$E[T_E] = \dfrac{2 + 4(3) + 4}{6} = \dfrac{2 + 12 + 4}{6} = \dfrac{18}{6} = 3.00 \text{ weeks}$$

$$\sigma_E^2 = \left(\dfrac{4 - 2}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{2}{6}\right)^2 = \dfrac{4}{36} = \dfrac{1}{9} \approx 0.1111 \text{ weeks}^2$$

**Activity F:**
$$E[T_F] = \dfrac{1 + 4(2) + 3}{6} = \dfrac{1 + 8 + 3}{6} = \dfrac{12}{6} = 2.00 \text{ weeks}$$

$$\sigma_F^2 = \left(\dfrac{3 - 1}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{2}{6}\right)^2 = \dfrac{4}{36} = \dfrac{1}{9} \approx 0.1111 \text{ weeks}^2$$

**Activity G:**
$$E[T_G] = \dfrac{3 + 4(5) + 7}{6} = \dfrac{3 + 20 + 7}{6} = \dfrac{30}{6} = 5.00 \text{ weeks}$$

$$\sigma_G^2 = \left(\dfrac{7 - 3}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{4}{6}\right)^2 = \dfrac{16}{36} = \dfrac{4}{9} \approx 0.4444 \text{ weeks}^2$$

**Consolidated Analytical Matrix (Part-a result table):**

| Activity | $E[T_i]$ (weeks) | $\sigma_i^2$ (weeks²) |
|---|---|---|
| A | 4.00 | 0.4444 |
| B | 5.00 | 0.4444 |
| C | 2.00 | 0.1111 |
| D | 6.00 | 0.4444 |
| E | 3.00 | 0.1111 |
| F | 2.00 | 0.1111 |
| G | 5.00 | 0.4444 |

#### 3.1.2 Part (b) — Forward / Backward Pass, Critical Path, Project Variance, Probability

**Step 1 — Enumerate all paths from Start to End:**

The AON network yields the following paths:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Path 1:} \quad & A \to D \to F \to G \\
\text{Path 2:} \quad & A \to C \to E \to G \\
\text{Path 3:} \quad & B \to E \to G
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the expected length of every path:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_1 &= E[T_A] + E[T_D] + E[T_F] + E[T_G] = 4 + 6 + 2 + 5 = 17 \text{ weeks} \\
L_2 &= E[T_A] + E[T_C] + E[T_E] + E[T_G] = 4 + 2 + 3 + 5 = 14 \text{ weeks} \\
L_3 &= E[T_B] + E[T_E] + E[T_G] = 5 + 3 + 5 = 13 \text{ weeks}
\end{aligned}$$

The longest path is **Path 1: A → D → F → G** with $T_{\text{project}} = 17$ weeks.

Therefore, the **Critical Path** is:

$$\boxed{\text{CP} = \{A, D, F, G\}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the project variance** (sum of variances *only* of critical-path activities):

$$\sigma_{\text{project}}^2 = \sigma_A^2 + \sigma_D^2 + \sigma_F^2 + \sigma_G^2$$

Substituting the values:

$$\sigma_{\text{project}}^2 = \dfrac{4}{9} + \dfrac{4}{9} + \dfrac{1}{9} + \dfrac{4}{9}$$

$$\sigma_{\text{project}}^2 = \dfrac{4 + 4 + 1 + 4}{9} = \dfrac{13}{9} \approx 1.4444 \text{ weeks}^2$$

Taking the square root:

$$\sigma_{\text{project}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{13}{9}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{13}}{3} \approx \dfrac{3.6056}{3} \approx 1.2018 \text{ weeks}$$

**Step 4 — Probability of completing in ≤ 26 weeks:**

We use the standardized normal variable:

$$Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_{\text{project}}}{\sigma_{\text{project}}} = \dfrac{26 - 17}{1.2018} = \dfrac{9}{1.2018} \approx 7.49$$

Because $Z \approx 7.49 \gg 3$, the standard normal CDF is essentially **1.0 (≈ 100 %)**. In words: *there is virtually no risk of missing a 26-week target* for this project, so the project manager should consider *tightening* the target — a classic KTU-evaluator trap question.

$$\boxed{\text{Prob}\left(T \leq 26 \text{ weeks}\right) = \Phi(7.49) \approx 1.0000 \;\;\text{(essentially certain)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **What-if?** If management asked for $T_s = 18$ weeks instead, then
> $$Z = \dfrac{18 - 17}{1.2018} = 0.832 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \Phi(0.832) \approx 0.797$$
> i.e. only an **≈ 79.7 %** chance of finishing in 18 weeks.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Earned Value Management (EVM) for Safety Tasks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EVM Worked Problem:** A safety project has *Budget-at-Completion (BAC) = ₹ 50,00,000*. At the end of Month 6, status is:
> $\text{BCWS} = \text{₹ } 25,00,000$, $\text{BCWP} = \text{₹ } 22,00,000$, $\text{ACWP} = \text{₹ } 26,00,000$.
> **Tasks:** Compute *CV, SV, CPI, SPI* and *EAC (Estimate at Completion)*.

**Cost Variance:**

$$CV = BCWP - ACWP = 22,00,000 - 26,00,000 = -4,00,000 \text{ ₹}$$

*Negative ⇒ over budget.*

**Schedule Variance:**

$$SV = BCWP - BCWS = 22,00,000 - 25,00,000 = -3,00,000 \text{ ₹}$$

*Negative ⇒ behind schedule.*

**Cost Performance Index:**

$$CPI = \dfrac{BCWP}{ACWP} = \dfrac{22,00,000}{26,00,000} \approx 0.846$$

*CPI < 1 ⇒ every rupee spent produces only ₹ 0.846 of safety work.*

**Schedule Performance Index:**

$$SPI = \dfrac{BCWP}{BCWS} = \dfrac{22,00,000}{25,00,000} = 0.880$$

*SPI < 1 ⇒ only 88 % of planned safety work has been completed in 50 % of the elapsed time.*

**Estimate at Completion (assuming current performance continues):**

$$EAC = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI} = \dfrac{50,00,000}{0.846} \approx 59,10,165 \text{ ₹}$$

**Variance at Completion:**

$$VAC = BAC - EAC = 50,00,000 - 59,10,165 = -9,10,165 \text{ ₹}$$

The project will overshoot its safety budget by **≈ ₹ 9.10 lakh** if corrective action is not taken.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Activity-on-Node (AON) Network — Safety Project

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["START"]:::startNode --> nodeA["A: HAZOP Study\nE=4 wk, var=0.44"]
    A1 --> nodeB["B: Hazard ID\nE=5 wk, var=0.44"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["C: SIL Allocation\nE=2 wk, var=0.11"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["D: FMEA Build\nE=6 wk, var=0.44"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["E: Risk Matrix\nE=3 wk, var=0.11"]
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF["F: Validation Plan\nE=2 wk, var=0.11"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["G: Safety Case\nE=5 wk, var=0.44"]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeEnd["END: Product Launch"]:::endNode

    classDef startNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef endNode fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **thick red path** conceptually traverses **A → D → F → G** — the **critical path** of length **17 weeks**.
- All other branches (B, C, E) carry positive slack and can be delayed without delaying the launch.

### 4.2 Resource-Allocation Block Diagram (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT STAGE"]
        inp1["Safety Scope\n(ISO 12100 / IEC 61508)"]:::inpBox
        inp2["WBS Leaf Tasks"]:::inpBox
    end

    subgraph PROC["ALLOCATION AND TIMING ENGINE"]
        proc1["Resource Estimator\n(Labour, Cost, Tool)"]:::procBox
        proc2["Dependency Sequencer\n(AOA / AON Builder)"]:::procBox
        proc3["CPM / PERT Solver\nForward + Backward Pass"]:::procBox
        proc4["Resource Leveller\n(Heuristic / LP)"]:::procBox
        proc5["EVM Tracker\nCV, SV, CPI, SPI"]:::procBox
    end

    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT STAGE"]
        out1["Gantt Chart + Network"]:::outBox
        out2["Baseline Budget"]:::outBox
        out3["Schedule Buffer"]:::outBox
        out4["Stage-Gate Approval"]:::outBox
    end

    inp1 --> proc1
    inp2 --> proc2
    proc1 --> proc4
    proc2 --> proc3
    proc3 --> proc4
    proc4 --> proc5
    proc5 --> out1
    proc5 --> out2
    proc4 --> out3
    proc5 --> out4

    classDef inpBox fill:#E0F2FF,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef procBox fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#7A5C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef outBox fill:#D5F5D5,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Safety-Stage Gate Model (V-Model Cross-Section)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    g1["G1: Concept\nSafety Plan Issued"]:::gate
    g2["G2: Design\nPHA + SIL Alloc"]:::gate
    g3["G3: Detailed Design\nFMEA + FTA"]:::gate
    g4["G4: Verification\nHardware + Software Tests"]:::gate
    g5["G5: Validation\nSite Acceptance Test"]:::gate
    g6["G6: Production\nSafety Case Closure"]:::gate

    g1 --> g2 --> g3 --> g4 --> g5 --> g6

    classDef gate fill:#FFE082,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each gate is a *frozen baseline* under change-control. Crossing a gate without the corresponding safety deliverable is a **KTU-board-exam red-flag** violation (and a real-world ISO audit non-conformance).

### 4.4 Earned Value Management Flow (EVM Pulse Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a["BCWS\nPlanned Value"]:::pv --> c["Compute Variances"]
    b["BCWP\nEarned Value"]:::ev --> c
    d["ACWP\nActual Cost"]:::ac --> c
    c --> e["CV = BCWP - ACWP"]:::res
    c --> f["SV = BCWP - BCWS"]:::res
    c --> g["CPI = BCWP / ACWP"]:::res
    c --> h["SPI = BCWP / BCWS"]:::res
    c --> i["EAC = BAC / CPI"]:::res
    c --> j["VAC = BAC - EAC"]:::res

    classDef pv fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef ev fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef ac fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef res fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** Define *Work Breakdown Structure* in the context of safety-project planning. Why is it the foundation for both resource allocation and timing?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** is a *deliverable-oriented hierarchical decomposition* of the total safety scope into smaller, manageable work packages. Each *leaf node* is a verifiable safety artefact (e.g., "Validate emergency-stop response ≤ 50 ms", "Compile IEC 61508 SIL-3 evidence dossier").
> **Why it is foundational:** (i) It converts the abstract goal of *"designing a safe product"* into cost- and time-estimable tasks. (ii) It enables **bottom-up resource aggregation** — summing leaf demands gives the total labour and material load. (iii) It defines the *activity set* whose precedence relationships feed into the CPM/PERT network for timing analysis. Without a WBS, neither resource nor schedule estimation can be defended at an ISO audit.

---

**Q2. (3 Marks)** Differentiate between *resource levelling* and *resource smoothing*. State one safety-engineering scenario where levelling is mandatory.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> | Aspect | Resource Levelling | Resource Smoothing |
> |---|---|---|
> | Objective | Eliminate *over-allocation* (no resource exceeds capacity) | *Smooth* demand profile to minimise peaks, keeping project finish date fixed |
> | Finish date | May slip | Held constant |
> | Driver | Hard resource capacity constraint | Soft preference for stable workforce |
> | **Safety scenario (mandatory levelling)** | A single *certified Functional-Safety Engineer* cannot be assigned to two parallel ASIL-D tasks simultaneously; over-allocation would compromise *independence*, violating **ISO 26262-2:2018 § 6.4.3** competence-and-independence clauses. Levelling is therefore **mandatory**, not optional. |

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Module Internal Choice (2 × 14 = 28 Marks)

> **[KTU 2024 ESE Pattern — Module 2 Internal Choice]**

---

#### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q A (a). [7 Marks]** A safety-critical industrial washing-machine design project has the following six activities:

| Activity | Predecessors | Duration (weeks) |
|---|---|---|
| A | — | 4 |
| B | — | 6 |
| C | A | 3 |
| D | A, B | 5 |
| E | C | 2 |
| F | D, E | 4 |

*(i)* Draw the AON network. *(ii)* Perform forward and backward passes. *(iii)* Identify the critical path and project duration. *(iv)* Compute the float of every non-critical activity.

**Model Solution (step-by-step, 7 marks):**

**Step 1 — AON Diagram (textual, since ASCII is allowed):**
```
START → A → C → E → F → END
START → B ↘
START → A → D ↗
```

**Step 2 — Forward pass (Earliest times):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{ES}(A) &= 0, & T_{EF}(A) &= 0 + 4 = 4 \\
T_{ES}(B) &= 0, & T_{EF}(B) &= 0 + 6 = 6 \\
T_{ES}(C) &= T_{EF}(A) = 4, & T_{EF}(C) &= 4 + 3 = 7 \\
T_{ES}(D) &= \max\{T_{EF}(A),\, T_{EF}(B)\} = \max\{4, 6\} = 6, & T_{EF}(D) &= 6 + 5 = 11 \\
T_{ES}(E) &= T_{EF}(C) = 7, & T_{EF}(E) &= 7 + 2 = 9 \\
T_{ES}(F) &= \max\{T_{EF}(D),\, T_{EF}(E)\} = \max\{11, 9\} = 11, & T_{EF}(F) &= 11 + 4 = 15
\end{aligned}$$

Project duration:

$$T_{\text{project}} = T_{EF}(F) = 15 \text{ weeks}$$

**Step 3 — Backward pass (Latest times):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{LF}(F) &= 15, & T_{LS}(F) &= 15 - 4 = 11 \\
T_{LF}(E) &= T_{LS}(F) = 11, & T_{LS}(E) &= 11 - 2 = 9 \\
T_{LF}(D) &= T_{LS}(F) = 11, & T_{LS}(D) &= 11 - 5 = 6 \\
T_{LF}(C) &= T_{LS}(E) = 9, & T_{LS}(C) &= 9 - 3 = 6 \\
T_{LF}(B) &= T_{LS}(D) = 6, & T_{LS}(B) &= 6 - 6 = 0 \\
T_{LF}(A) &= \min\{T_{LS}(C),\, T_{LS}(D)\} = \min\{6, 6\} = 6, & T_{LS}(A) &= 6 - 4 = 2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Float (Slack) of every activity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Slack}(A) &= T_{LS}(A) - T_{ES}(A) = 2 - 0 = 2 \text{ weeks} \\
\text{Slack}(B) &= T_{LS}(B) - T_{ES}(B) = 0 - 0 = 0 \text{ weeks} \\
\text{Slack}(C) &= 6 - 4 = 2 \text{ weeks} \\
\text{Slack}(D) &= 6 - 6 = 0 \text{ weeks} \\
\text{Slack}(E) &= 9 - 7 = 2 \text{ weeks} \\
\text{Slack}(F) &= 11 - 11 = 0 \text{ weeks}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Critical path** (zero-slack activities):

$$\boxed{\text{Critical Path} = B \to D \to F, \quad T_{\text{CP}} = 6 + 5 + 4 = 15 \text{ weeks (matches project duration ✓)}}$$

**Critical-path activities have zero float. Activities A, C, E each have 2 weeks of float and can be delayed without affecting launch.**

---

**Q A (b). [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the *Earned Value Management (EVM)* system with reference to its four key metrics — CV, SV, CPI, SPI. For a safety project with BAC = ₹ 40 lakh, BCWS = ₹ 20 lakh, BCWP = ₹ 18 lakh, ACWP = ₹ 22 lakh at the end of Month 8, compute the four metrics, the EAC, and the VAC. Comment on the project's health.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Theory (≈ 3 marks):** EVM integrates *scope, schedule, and cost*. The three baseline values are:
- **BCWS** (Planned Value) — what you *planned* to spend by now.
- **BCWP** (Earned Value) — what you *actually earned* (work completed × budgeted rate).
- **ACWP** (Actual Cost) — what you *actually spent*.

**Variance metrics (≈ 1 mark):**
- **CV = BCWP − ACWP** ⇒ *cost variance* (₹; >0 under budget)
- **SV = BCWP − BCWS** ⇒ *schedule variance* (₹; >0 ahead)

**Index metrics (≈ 1 mark):**
- **CPI = BCWP / ACWP** ⇒ efficiency of spend (>1 healthy)
- **SPI = BCWP / BCWS** ⇒ efficiency of progress (>1 healthy)

**Numerical Computation (≈ 2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
CV &= 18 - 22 = -4 \text{ lakh ₹} \;\;(\text{over budget}) \\
SV &= 18 - 20 = -2 \text{ lakh ₹} \;\;(\text{behind schedule}) \\
CPI &= \dfrac{18}{22} = 0.818 \\
SPI &= \dfrac{18}{20} = 0.900
\end{aligned}$$

$$\text{EAC} = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI} = \dfrac{40}{0.818} \approx 48.90 \text{ lakh ₹}$$

$$\text{VAC} = BAC - EAC = 40 - 48.90 = -8.90 \text{ lakh ₹}$$

**Project Health Comment:** Both CPI (0.818) and SPI (0.900) are **below 1**, so the project is *over budget* and *behind schedule*. The VAC of **−₹ 8.90 lakh** signals an expected overrun of ≈ 22 % at completion. Corrective action: re-baseline, request additional budget, or descope non-critical safety tasks (after formal CCB approval).

---

#### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative choice*

**Q B (a). [7 Marks]** Explain the *six-step resource-allocation logic chain* for a safety-engineering project. For each step, state one artefact that is produced and one ISO/IEC standard reference that governs it.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| # | Step | Artefact Produced | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Decompose** the safety scope | Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) | **ISO 12100:2010 § 5.3** (risk reduction by inherently safe design measures) |
| 2 | **Estimate** resource demand | Bottom-up cost / labour estimate | **ISO/IEC 33000** (process assessment) |
| 3 | **Assign** competent resources | RACI matrix, resource calendar | **ISO 26262-2:2018 § 6.4.3** (competence & independence) |
| 4 | **Aggregate** demand vs capacity | Resource histogram | **PMBOK 7th Ed.** (Resource Management) |
| 5 | **Level / Smooth** | Leveled schedule | **ISO 9001:2015 § 8.5.1** (control of resources) |
| 6 | **Lock baseline + control** | Change-controlled baseline | **ISO 9001:2015 § 8.5.6** (control of changes); **IEC 61508** functional-safety lifecycle |

**Each step's contribution (≈ 5 lines):**
- Step 1 turns the abstract *"design safe product"* goal into auditable leaf nodes.
- Step 2 anchors every leaf in measurable hours and money.
- Step 3 ensures every task has a competent owner; ISO 26262 explicitly mandates *independence* between assessor and implementer for ASIL items.
- Step 4 detects *over-allocation* (e.g., one engineer booked 14 h/day) which would silently corrupt safety quality.
- Step 5 redistributes load using heuristics or LP solvers.
- Step 6 freezes the plan into a *baseline*; any future deviation is a formal change request.

---

**Q B (b). [7 Marks]** A safety project has the following PERT estimates. Compute the expected duration, project variance, and probability of completing within 30 weeks.

| Activity | Pred. | $O$ | $M$ | $P$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | — | 3 | 5 | 7 |
| B | A | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| C | A | 4 | 6 | 8 |
| D | B | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| E | C, D | 3 | 4 | 5 |

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Expected durations and variances:**

$$E[T_A] = \dfrac{3 + 20 + 7}{6} = 5, \quad \sigma_A^2 = \left(\dfrac{7-3}{6}\right)^2 = \dfrac{4}{9}$$

$$E[T_B] = \dfrac{2 + 12 + 4}{6} = 3, \quad \sigma_B^2 = \left(\dfrac{4-2}{6}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{9}$$

$$E[T_C] = \dfrac{4 + 24 + 8}{6} = 6, \quad \sigma_C^2 = \left(\dfrac{8-4}{6}\right)^2 = \dfrac{4}{9}$$

$$E[T_D] = \dfrac{1 + 8 + 3}{6} = 2, \quad \sigma_D^2 = \left(\dfrac{3-1}{6}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{9}$$

$$E[T_E] = \dfrac{3 + 16 + 5}{6} = 4, \quad \sigma_E^2 = \left(\dfrac{5-3}{6}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{9}$$

**Step 2 — Enumerate paths:**

$$\text{Path 1: } A \to B \to D \to E = 5 + 3 + 2 + 4 = 14 \text{ weeks}$$

$$\text{Path 2: } A \to C \to E = 5 + 6 + 4 = 15 \text{ weeks}$$

**Step 3 — Critical path = Path 2 (A → C → E), project duration = 15 weeks.**

**Step 4 — Project variance:**

$$\sigma_{\text{proj}}^2 = \sigma_A^2 + \sigma_C^2 + \sigma_E^2 = \dfrac{4}{9} + \dfrac{4}{9} + \dfrac{1}{9} = \dfrac{9}{9} = 1.000 \text{ wk}^2$$

$$\sigma_{\text{proj}} = 1.000 \text{ week}$$

**Step 5 — Probability of finishing within 30 weeks:**

$$Z = \dfrac{30 - 15}{1.000} = 15$$

$$\text{Prob}(T \le 30) = \Phi(15) \approx 1.000$$

The project has *essentially zero schedule risk* against a 30-week target — a tell-tale sign that the target is loose and the project manager should tighten the commitment.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (read carefully):**
> 1. **Mark loss trap #1 — Variance over-scope:** students compute $\sigma^2$ by summing variances of *all* activities, not just the critical path. Examiner deducts **2 marks**. Only activities on the **CP** contribute.
> 2. **Mark loss trap #2 — Three-point substitution:** students plug $(O + M + P)/3$ instead of $(O + 4M + P)/6$. Examiner deducts **1–2 marks** as the *expected value* is wrong.
> 3. **Mark loss trap #3 — Float sign confusion:** students report *negative* floats as "critical". Float cannot be negative in a valid forward-passed network; negative float means the **finish date is already breached**. Examiner deducts **1 mark**.
> 4. **Mark loss trap #4 — EVM denominator:** students compute CPI = ACWP/BCWP (inverted). Examiner deducts **1 mark** because CPI must be *earned over actual*.
> 5. **Mark loss trap #5 — Missing ISO clause reference:** for any safety-engineering question, examiners award **½ to 1 bonus mark** when the answer cites the relevant ISO/IEC clause (e.g., ISO 12100 § 5.3, ISO 26262-2 § 6.4.3).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⭐ EXAM QUICK-FIRE REVISION CARD ⭐**

- ✅ **WBS is the foundation** of both resource allocation and timing — always start here.
- ✅ **PERT expected duration** = $(O + 4M + P) / 6$; **variance** = $((P - O)/6)^2$.
- ✅ **CPM critical path** = longest path through the network = path with **zero total float**.
- ✅ **Slack / Float** = $T_{LS} - T_{ES}$. Zero slack ⇒ critical.
- ✅ **Project variance** = **sum of variances of critical-path activities only**.
- ✅ **Z-value** = $(T_s - T_{\text{project}}) / \sigma_{\text{proj}}$; consult standard normal table for $\Phi(Z)$.
- ✅ **EVM rule of thumb:** CPI < 1 → over budget; SPI < 1 → behind schedule.
- ✅ **EAC** (assuming current performance) = BAC / CPI.
- ✅ **Resource levelling** may slip the finish date; **smoothing** never does.
- ✅ **Safety contingency reserve** = **10–15 %** of total safety-project cost.
- ✅ Always reference **ISO 12100, ISO 26262-2, IEC 61508, or ISO 9001** for safety context — bonus marks guaranteed.
- ✅ Always show **units** (weeks, ₹, days²) — examiners deduct marks for "dimensionless" answers in CPM/EVM.
- ✅ **Stage gates**: Concept → Design → Detailed Design → Verification → Validation → Production. Each gate freezes a baseline.
- ✅ **Independent assessor** principle — never let the implementer self-certify the same safety task (ISO 26262-2 § 6.4.3).
- ✅ **Gantt chart** for *what* and *when*; **Network diagram** for *logical dependency*; **Resource histogram** for *who*; **EVM chart** for *how-much-done*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reflect All the Results and the Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT747 - SAFETY IN PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reflect All the Results and the Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Safety in Product Design**, *reflection* refers to the **systematic, post-design/ post-deployment analytical process** of examining both the **outcome (result)** of a product's safety performance and the **operational workflow (process)** that led to that outcome. It is a structured critical review aimed at converting experiential data — incidents, near-misses, compliance gaps, test failures, and user feedback — into **actionable design intelligence** for continuous improvement.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes (COs)** for PESFT747, reflection is the terminal phase of the safety design lifecycle, where the designer validates whether the **as-designed safety intent** matches the **as-used safety reality**.

$$ \text{Reflection} = f(\text{Result}, \text{Process}, \text{Context}) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examinable):** Reflection in safety product design is the iterative learning loop wherein design teams audit completed projects, compare predicted safety outcomes against realized outcomes, and feed corrective insights back into the requirements specification, risk register, and design controls of the next iteration (or retrofit of the current product).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The "Black Box" of a Flight**

Imagine a pilot landing an aircraft. The flight itself is the **process**, and the landing outcome (smooth, hard, or crashed) is the **result**. After the flight, the pilot does two things:

1. **Reflects on the result** — Did the landing match the pre-flight plan? Were passengers safe? Did the brakes work?
2. **Reflects on the process** — Was the descent rate correct? Were checklists followed? Did communication fail at any point?

This combined reflection is then documented in the **flight logbook** so that the *next* flight is safer. **That logbook is the "Reflection Deliverable"** in product safety.

Now, replace the pilot with a **product design engineer**, the aircraft with a **product** (say, an electric scooter), and the flight logbook with a **Post-Design Safety Review (PDSR)** document. The same logic applies — except with engineering rigour: hazard logs, FMEA updates, residual-risk re-classification, and standards compliance audit.

---

## 1.3 Why Reflection Is a Mandatory KTU Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three reasons this topic carries high weightage in KTU ESE:
> 1. **Legal defensibility** — A documented reflection trail is the only evidence an OEM has in a product liability lawsuit.
> 2. **Regulatory compliance** — ISO 14971 (Risk Management), IEC 61508 (Functional Safety), and ISO 12100 (Safety of Machinery) all **mandate** post-market surveillance and reflective review.
> 3. **Learning organization principle** — Without reflection, every new project starts at zero, and the industry repeats tragedies (e.g., Takata airbags, Samsung Galaxy Note 7).

---

## 1.4 Key Physical / Process Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric / Constant | Symbol | Standard Reference | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceptable Risk Threshold | **ALARP** | ISO 12100 / IEC 61508 | As Low As Reasonably Practicable |
| Severity Scale | **S** (1–10) | ISO 14971 Annex C | Catastrophic to Negligible |
| Probability Scale | **P** (1–10) | ISO 14971 Annex D | Frequent to Improbable |
| Risk Priority Number | **RPN** | AIAG-VDA FMEA | Severity × Occurrence × Detection |
| Continuous Improvement Cycle | **PDCA** | Deming Cycle | Plan–Do–Check–Act |
| Post-Market Surveillance Period | **PMS** | EU MDR Article 83 | Ongoing, proportional to risk class |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Reflection in Safety Design

Reflection is not a single activity; it is a **tri-pillar analytical construct**:

### Pillar A — Reflection on the **Result** (Outcome Audit)
- Were the predicted hazards actually realized?
- Did residual risks fall within ALARP bounds?
- What is the **field incident rate** vs. the **predicted incident rate**?
- Were safety goals (e.g., SIL, PL, Cat.) achieved in operation?

### Pillar B — Reflection on the **Process** (Procedural Audit)
- Was the **V-Model / Stage-Gate process** followed?
- Were verification & validation (V&V) activities completed as planned?
- Were traceability matrices maintained?
- Did documentation comply with ISO 14971 / IEC 61508?

### Pillar C — Reflection on the **Context** (Stakeholder & Environmental Audit)
- Did the user behave as assumed in the use-misuse analysis?
- Did the operating environment diverge from design assumptions?
- Were supply-chain and manufacturing constraints properly handled?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** A common 14-mark question asks students to distinguish between *Result Reflection* and *Process Reflection*. Always structure your answer along these three pillars — it fetches full marks.

---

## 2.2 Structured Logic Steps — The Reflection Workflow

1. **Step 1 — Data Collection:** Gather incident reports, warranty claims, user complaints, test failures, audit non-conformances.
2. **Step 2 — Comparison Baseline:** Compare against the original **Safety Requirements Specification (SRS)** and the **Risk Register**.
3. **Step 3 — Gap Analysis:** Identify deltas — what was missed, what was over-engineered, what was correctly predicted.
4. **Step 4 — Root Cause Analysis:** Apply 5-Whys, Ishikawa (Fishbone), or Fault Tree Analysis to each gap.
5. **Step 5 — Lessons Learned Documentation:** Formalize into a **Lessons Learned Register (LLR)**.
6. **Step 6 — Knowledge Re-use:** Feed insights into the next design cycle's **Requirements Elicitation** phase.
7. **Step 7 — Stakeholder Communication:** Present findings to management, regulators (if required), and the design community.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every equation, framework, and metric a KTU student must know for this topic. **Memorize this verbatim.**

| # | Equation / Framework | LaTeX Form | Application Domain | Units / Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Risk Magnitude | $R = S \times P$ | Product Hazard Analysis | Dimensionless score |
| 2 | Risk Priority Number | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | FMEA | 1 – 1000 |
| 3 | ALARP Test | $\text{Cost of further reduction} \;\vert\; \gg \;\vert\; \text{Risk reduction achieved}$ | Regulatory Defence | Cost ratio |
| 4 | PDCA Iteration | $\text{Quality}_{n+1} = \text{Quality}_n + \Delta_{\text{act}}$ | Process Improvement | Iterative |
| 5 | Reflection Effectiveness Index | $\text{REI} = \dfrac{N_{\text{issues closed}}}{N_{\text{issues identified}}} \times 100\%$ | Audit Closure | Percentage (%) |
| 6 | Field-to-Predicted Ratio | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{field}}}{\lambda_{\text{predicted}}}$ | Reliability Validation | Ratio (ideally $\approx 1$) |
| 7 | Learning Curve Equation | $Y = aX^{-b}$ | Cost/Defect Reduction | Per unit |
| 8 | Borda Count for Hazard Ranking | $B_i = \sum_j N - r_{ij}$ | Multi-criteria Hazard Ranking | Score |
| 9 | Kaizen Improvement | $\Delta_{\text{kaizen}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \delta_i$ | Cumulative small gains | Sum of micro-changes |
| 10 | Hazard Closure Rate | $\text{HCR} = \dfrac{H_{\text{closed}}(t)}{H_{\text{open}}(t_0)}$ | Safety KPI | Fraction |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Reflection frameworks are used by:
- **Automotive OEMs** — post-recall root cause analysis (Toyota AAR after unintended acceleration).
- **Medical device manufacturers** — Post-Market Surveillance under EU MDR / US FDA 21 CFR 820.
- **Aerospace** — AS9100 mandatory Continual Improvement clauses.
- **Consumer electronics** — Failure Analysis Reports after thermal runaway events (e.g., lithium-ion battery fires).
- **Industrial machinery** — ISO 12100 safety-of-machinery periodic reviews.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU exam answers**, when asked "give an example of reflection in safety design," pick a *named, verifiable* real-world case (e.g., Boeing 787 battery grounding 2013, Hyundai Kona EV recall 2021). Examiners award bonus marks for concrete case-study linkage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Frameworks

## 3.1 Derivation of the Reflection Effectiveness Index (REI)

Since "Reflect All the Results and the Process" is fundamentally a **process-engineering topic** rather than a physics derivation, the analytical derivations here are **structured frameworks** expressed as mathematical relations. We treat reflection as a measurable, optimizable process.

### 3.1.1 Defining the Variables

Let:
- $N_{\text{identified}}$ = Total number of safety issues, non-conformances, or lessons-learned items captured during the reflection phase.
- $N_{\text{closed}}$ = Number of those items that were formally closed (i.e., corrective action implemented, verified, and documented) within the reflection cycle.
- $N_{\text{regressed}}$ = Number of closed items that *reopened* during a subsequent review (indicating incomplete reflection).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

The base Reflection Effectiveness Index is the ratio of closed issues to identified issues:

$$
\text{REI}_{\text{base}} = \frac{N_{\text{closed}}}{N_{\text{identified}}}
$$

To penalize *reopened* issues (a sign of superficial reflection), we introduce a regression penalty term $N_{\text{regressed}}$ with a weighting factor $\alpha \in [0, 1]$:

$$
\text{REI}_{\text{net}} = \frac{N_{\text{closed}} - \alpha \cdot N_{\text{regressed}}}{N_{\text{identified}}}
$$

Normalizing to a percentage scale:

$$
\text{REI}_{\%} = \left( \frac{N_{\text{closed}} - \alpha \cdot N_{\text{regressed}}}{N_{\text{identified}}} \right) \times 100\%
$$

For typical KTU-level analysis, we set $\alpha = 0.5$ (half-weight), so:

$$
\text{REI}_{\%} = \left( \frac{N_{\text{closed}} - 0.5 \cdot N_{\text{regressed}}}{N_{\text{identified}}} \right) \times 100\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic behind each step:**
> - Numerator captures *forward progress* (closed issues).
> - Penalty term captures *backward drift* (issues that came back).
> - Denominator normalizes the metric so that different projects can be compared.
> - Percentage scaling makes the metric board-room friendly.

---

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

A product safety team conducted a post-launch reflection on a home treadmill:

$$
N_{\text{identified}} = 24, \quad N_{\text{closed}} = 18, \quad N_{\text{regressed}} = 4
$$

Plugging in:

$$
\text{REI}_{\%} = \left( \frac{18 - 0.5 \times 4}{24} \right) \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{REI}_{\%} = \left( \frac{18 - 2}{24} \right) \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{REI}_{\%} = \left( \frac{16}{24} \right) \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{REI}_{\%} = 66.67\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Only two-thirds of the identified safety issues were *genuinely* closed. The team needs to revisit the four regressed issues.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Field-to-Predicted Reliability Ratio (FPR)

The reflection on *results* is incomplete without comparing predicted safety metrics against field data.

### 3.2.1 Setup

Let $\lambda_{\text{predicted}}$ be the failure rate predicted during design (e.g., via reliability prediction handbooks like Telcordia, MIL-HDBK-217F, or IEC 61709). Let $\lambda_{\text{field}}$ be the failure rate observed in the field after deployment.

$$
\text{FPR} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{field}}}{\lambda_{\text{predicted}}}
$$

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow

- If $\text{FPR} \approx 1.0$: prediction was accurate — reflection confirms design assumptions.
- If $\text{FPR} > 1.0$: field is worse than predicted — *result reflection* reveals a design gap.
- If $\text{FPR} < 1.0$: field is better than predicted — *result reflection* reveals over-engineering (cost waste).

$$
\text{Reflection Action} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Initiate root-cause analysis if} & \text{FPR} > 1.2 \\
\text{Document as "validated" if} & 0.8 \leq \text{FPR} \leq 1.2 \\
\text{Review design margin if} & \text{FPR} < 0.8
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.2.3 Numerical Example

A pressure relief valve was predicted to fail at $\lambda_{\text{predicted}} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour. After 18 months in the field, 3 failures were observed across 50,000 units operating for 5,000 hours each:

$$
\text{Total operating hours} = 50{,}000 \times 5{,}000 = 2.5 \times 10^{8} \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\lambda_{\text{field}} = \frac{3}{2.5 \times 10^{8}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-8} \text{ failures/hour}
$$

$$
\text{FPR} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-8}}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.012
$$

**Interpretation:** FPR $\ll 1$ — the design was severely over-engineered. The reflection phase should recommend reducing safety margins to optimize cost.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Reflection Coverage Across Design Phases

The following matrix (to be replicated in the answer script) demonstrates *where* in the design lifecycle reflection feeds back:

| Design Phase | Forward Output | Reflection Feedback Into | KTU Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Concept Design | Safety Concept | Next concept re-scoped | PESFT747 M1 |
| Detailed Design | SRS / SDD | Next detailed design | PESFT747 M2 |
| Prototype Testing | V&V Reports | Test plan improvement | PESFT747 M3 |
| Production | Field Data | Manufacturing tolerances | PESFT747 M4 |
| End-of-Life | Disposal audit | Future eco-design rules | PESFT747 M5 |

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Cumulative Learning Benefit

Let each reflection cycle produce an improvement $\delta_i$. Then cumulative learning:

$$
L_{n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \delta_i
$$

The asymptotic limit of $L_n$ is bounded by organizational absorptive capacity $C_{\max}$:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} L_n \leq C_{\max}
$$

This means **infinite reflection does not yield infinite improvement** — the design organization eventually saturates. This is a key insight for the KTU exam when asked about the *limits* of reflection.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Reflection Loop — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Phase - Plan] --> B[Safety Design Activities - Do]
    B --> C[Verification and Validation - Check]
    C --> D{Reflect Results}
    D --> E{Reflect Process}
    E --> F[Lessons Learned Register]
    F --> G[Knowledge Base Update]
    G --> H{Action Decision}
    H -->|Improve| A
    H -->|Accept and Archive| I[Document Repository]
    D -->|Gap Detected| J[Root Cause Analysis]
    E -->|Gap Detected| J
    J --> K[Corrective Action Plan]
    K --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The outer loop (A → B → C → D → E → H) represents the **Deming PDCA cycle with reflection**.
- D and E are the **two reflection decision gates** — one for result, one for process.
- J is the **escape hatch for non-conformances** — gaps bypass the normal closure path.
- G is the **organizational memory** — the Lessons Learned Register feeds future projects.

---

## 4.2 Reflection Trigger Matrix

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1[Field Incident Report] --> R1{Result Reflection}
    T2[Customer Complaint] --> R1
    T3[Audit Non-Conformance] --> R2{Process Reflection}
    T4[Test Failure] --> R1
    T5[Supplier Defect] --> R2
    T6[Regulatory Update] --> R2
    R1 --> O1[Update Risk Register]
    R1 --> O2[Revise Safety Case]
    R2 --> O3[Update Procedure SOP]
    R2 --> O4[Retrain Team]
```

---

## 4.3 Stakeholder Map for Reflection Outputs

```mermaid
graph TD
    RL[Reflection Output] --> S1[Design Engineers]
    RL --> S2[Quality Team]
    RL --> S3[Production Team]
    RL --> S4[Regulatory Affairs]
    RL --> S5[Top Management]
    RL --> S6[Customers - via Safety Bulletins]
    S1 --> N1[Next Design Cycle]
    S2 --> N2[Audit Closure]
    S3 --> N3[Process CAPA]
    S4 --> N4[Regulatory Filing]
    S5 --> N5[Strategic Decision]
    S6 --> N6[Field Trust]
```

---

## 4.4 Reflection Depth vs. Project Maturity

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Concept - Reflection Depth Low] --> P2[Prototype - Medium]
    P2 --> P3[Production - High]
    P3 --> P4[Field - Maximum Depth]
    P4 --> P5[Retirement - Comprehensive]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Always annotate your reflection diagrams with the **dual arrows** — one for "result feedback" and one for "process feedback." Examiners explicitly check for this duality.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Reflection" in the context of safety in product design. List any four benefits of conducting a structured post-design reflection.
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`** **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** *similar pattern*

**Model Answer:**

> Reflection in safety product design is the **systematic, post-design audit** of both the *safety outcomes (results)* realized during use and the *engineering workflow (process)* that produced the product. It converts field incidents, test failures, and audit non-conformances into **actionable design intelligence** for continuous improvement.

**Four benefits:**
1. **Closure of residual safety gaps** identified post-launch.
2. **Regulatory compliance evidence** for ISO 14971, IEC 61508, EU MDR.
3. **Knowledge capitalization** — prevents repeating the same design errors.
4. **Cost optimization** — identifies over-engineered safety margins.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between *Reflection on Results* and *Reflection on Process*. Give one example of each.
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Reflection on Results | Reflection on Process |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | *What* safety outcomes occurred | *How* the design activities were executed |
| **Data Source** | Field incidents, warranty data, customer feedback | Audit reports, V&V logs, design reviews |
| **Question Asked** | "Did our safety predictions match reality?" | "Did we follow the safety process correctly?" |
| **Example** | Analysing why a child car seat buckle failed in field use | Auditing why the DFMEA was skipped at Stage 3 |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B Module-Internal Choice pattern: **Answer ANY ONE full question (14 marks)**, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. We provide two independent choices below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Describe the structured methodology for conducting a post-design safety reflection. Explain each step with reference to ISO 14971. (7 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer:**

The post-design safety reflection follows a **7-step methodology** aligned with ISO 14971:2019 clauses 9–10 (Production and Post-Production information):

1. **Step 1 — Information Gathering (ISO 14971 §9):** Collect data from production, installation, operation, maintenance, and customer feedback channels.

2. **Step 2 — Incident & Near-Miss Triaging:** Classify each event by severity (S), probability (P), and detectability to derive a current Risk Priority Number.

3. **Step 3 — Comparison with Risk Register:** Compare observed events against the originally approved **Risk Management Report (RMR)**. Any unaccounted risk triggers a non-conformance.

4. **Step 4 — Root Cause Analysis:** Apply structured tools — 5-Whys, Fishbone (Ishikawa), or Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) — to identify causal chains.

5. **Step 5 — Impact Assessment:** Evaluate whether the new information affects the **residual risk evaluation** or **benefit-risk analysis**.

6. **Step 6 — Corrective & Preventive Action (CAPA):** Per ISO 14971 §10, formulate CAPA, implement, verify, and document.

7. **Step 7 — Update of Risk Management File:** All findings are recorded into the **Risk Management File (RMF)**, which becomes part of the post-market surveillance report.

---

#### (b) A manufacturer of industrial lifting equipment observes 4 hoist cable failures in 12 months across 2,000 deployed units. The predicted failure rate was $0.5 \times 10^{-5}$ failures/hour. Total operating hours = $1.6 \times 10^8$. Compute the Field-to-Predicted Ratio, classify the reflection outcome, and recommend corrective action. (7 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** (composite numerical)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute observed field failure rate:**

$$
\lambda_{\text{field}} = \frac{4}{1.6 \times 10^{8}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-8} \text{ failures/hour}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute FPR:**

$$
\text{FPR} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-8}}{0.5 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-8}}{5.0 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\text{FPR} = 5.0 \times 10^{-3} = 0.005
$$

**Step 3 — Classification:**

Since FPR $= 0.005 \ll 0.8$, this is a **"Field Better Than Predicted"** outcome. The product is *over-engineered* from a cable-strength perspective.

**Step 4 — Recommended Actions:**
- **Reflection on Result:** Reassess cable diameter; consider reducing one standard size for cost saving (without violating ALARP).
- **Reflection on Process:** Investigate why the original prediction was so conservative — likely the reliability handbook (e.g., MIL-HDBK-217F) was misapplied.
- **Corrective Action:** Update the reliability prediction model, revalidate safety margin, document in RMF.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle as applied to reflection in safety design. How does it differ from a one-time post-project review? (7 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Model Answer:**

**The PDCA Cycle in Safety Reflection:**

- **PLAN:** Before the design cycle, identify safety goals, hazard list, and risk acceptance criteria.
- **DO:** Execute the design activities — implement controls, conduct reviews, deploy to the field.
- **CHECK (Reflection):** This is the **reflective step**. Compare actual outcomes against planned goals. Reflection occurs on both *result* (did the safety goal hold?) and *process* (was the procedure followed?).
- **ACT:** Based on the reflection, either **standardize** (if successful) or **iterate** (if gaps found) — feed lessons back to the next PLAN.

**Key Distinction from One-Time Post-Project Review:**

| Feature | PDCA + Reflection | One-Time Review |
|---|---|---|
| Timing | Continuous / cyclical | End-of-project only |
| Knowledge Capture | Real-time | Delayed |
| Correction Cost | Low (caught early) | High (caught late) |
| Regulatory Acceptance | ISO 9001, AS9100 compliant | Insufficient alone |
| Organizational Learning | Compounding | Isolated |

PDCA is the **dynamic, iterative** model; one-time reviews are **static, terminal**. KTU examiners expect the student to clearly articulate the *iterative compounding* of learning.

---

#### (b) Discuss the Reflection Effectiveness Index (REI). A safety team identifies 30 issues post-deployment, closes 22, but 5 closed issues reopen in the next audit. Calculate REI% and interpret the result. Recommend two improvements. (7 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify the variables:**

$$
N_{\text{identified}} = 30, \quad N_{\text{closed}} = 22, \quad N_{\text{regressed}} = 5, \quad \alpha = 0.5
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**

$$
\text{REI}_{\%} = \left( \frac{N_{\text{closed}} - \alpha \cdot N_{\text{regressed}}}{N_{\text{identified}}} \right) \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{REI}_{\%} = \left( \frac{22 - 0.5 \times 5}{30} \right) \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{REI}_{\%} = \left( \frac{22 - 2.5}{30} \right) \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{REI}_{\%} = \left( \frac{19.5}{30} \right) \times 100\% = 65\%
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

REI = 65% is **moderate**. Only about two-thirds of the safety issues were *genuinely* and *durably* closed. The 5 regressions indicate **shallow root-cause analysis** — corrective actions addressed symptoms, not causes.

**Step 4 — Two Recommended Improvements:**
1. **Mandatory 8D / 5-Why depth:** Require all closed safety issues to undergo a structured 8D report with verified root cause before closure sign-off.
2. **30-day regression audit:** Implement a mandatory re-audit window (30 days post-closure) to catch regressions early and reinforce the closed status.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse reflection with testing.** Reflection happens *after* validation, *after* deployment. Testing is a forward-looking activity; reflection is retrospective.
> 2. **Do NOT skip the numerical working** — KTU examiners award 2–3 marks purely for correctly applying formulas (FPR, REI, RPN).
> 3. **Do NOT write vague answers** like "reflection is important." Always tie to **standards (ISO 14971 / IEC 61508)**, **tools (FMEA, FTA, 5-Why)**, and **deliverables (RMF, LLR, AAR)**.
> 4. **Always include both reflection dimensions** — result AND process. A 14-mark answer that ignores one dimension loses ~5 marks.
> 5. **Cite a real-world case** in part (b) wherever possible (e.g., Takata, Boeing 787, Samsung Note 7) — it demonstrates industrial maturity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision — read these bullets 5 times before entering the exam hall.**

- **Definition:** Reflection = systematic post-design audit of **results + process + context**.
- **Three Pillars:** Outcome Audit + Procedural Audit + Stakeholder/Context Audit.
- **7-Step Methodology:** Gather → Triage → Compare → Root Cause → Impact → CAPA → Update RMF.
- **PDCA Link:** Reflection is the **CHECK** phase of the Deming cycle.
- **Key Equations to Memorize:**
  - $R = S \times P$ (Risk magnitude)
  - $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ (FMEA)
  - $\text{FPR} = \lambda_{\text{field}} / \lambda_{\text{predicted}}$ (Reflection on results)
  - $\text{REI}_{\%} = \dfrac{N_{\text{closed}} - 0.5 \cdot N_{\text{regressed}}}{N_{\text{identified}}} \times 100\%$ (Reflection effectiveness)
- **Standards to Quote:** ISO 14971 (Risk Management), IEC 61508 (Functional Safety), ISO 12100 (Machinery Safety), EU MDR (Medical Devices).
- **Tools to Name:** FMEA, FTA, 5-Whys, Ishikawa, 8D Report, AAR (After-Action Review).
- **Deliverables to Mention:** Risk Management File (RMF), Lessons Learned Register (LLR), Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) Report, Corrective and Preventive Action (CAPA).
- **Limits of Reflection:** Asymptotic saturation — bounded by organizational absorptive capacity $C_{\max}$.
- **Real-World Cases (memorize 2):**
  1. **Takata Airbags (2014–2017)** — failure of process reflection; ignored test data for over a decade.
  2. **Samsung Galaxy Note 7 (2016)** — successful result reflection; rapid global recall after battery fires.
- **Common 14-Mark Traps:** (i) Skipping the process dimension, (ii) Not referencing standards, (iii) Omitting numerical analysis, (iv) Confusing verification with reflection.
- **Mnemonic for the 7 Steps:** **"G-T-C-R-I-C-U"** — Gather, Triage, Compare, Root-Cause, Impact, CAPA, Update.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Identifying Customer Needs: Gather Raw Data from Customers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT747 - SAFETY IN PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Identifying Customer Needs: Gather Raw Data from Customers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Identifying Customer Needs** is the foundational phase of the product development process (per Ulrich, Eppinger, and the KTU PESFT747 Product Design framework) in which the design team systematically discovers, captures, and articulates the *latent* and *expressed* requirements of the target market. The sub-activity **"Gather Raw Data from Customers"** specifically refers to the *primary data collection* stage — directly interacting with end users, lead users, and other stakeholders to record their experiences, problems, frustrations, desires, and work-arounds in their own words, *before* any filtering, interpretation, or translation into engineering specifications.

**Key syllabus terminology (must memorize for KTU viva/ESE):**
- **Voice of Customer (VoC):** The actual statements, expressions, and observable behaviors of customers regarding a product, process, or service.
- **Raw Data:** Unprocessed, unedited customer statements, observations, and recorded artifacts collected in the field. It has *not yet* been converted into design specifications.
- **Lead User:** A user who experiences needs months or years before the majority of the market and who benefits significantly from an innovation (per **Eric von Hippel**, MIT).
- **Primary Data:** Data collected first-hand by the design team (interviews, observations). Contrast with **Secondary Data** (market reports, patents, literature).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Doctor's First Visit:** Imagine you walk into a doctor's clinic with a chronic headache. Before the doctor prescribes a medicine (the *design specification*), the doctor must first **listen to your story** — when it started, what makes it worse, how it affects your daily life, what you have already tried. The doctor writes down your exact words and observations. Only then does she convert those notes into a diagnosis. Similarly, in product design, the team must first **gather raw data from customers** in their *own* words and contexts *before* translating them into engineering specifications. Skipping this step is like a doctor writing a prescription without ever asking the patient a question — guaranteed failure.

## 1.3 Why This Step is Critical for Safety in Product Design

In the context of **PESFT747 — Safety in Product Design**, gathering raw customer data is not just a marketing formality. Safety hazards are often:
- **Unrecognized by customers themselves** (latent needs)
- **Discovered only in extreme use cases** (lead-user territory)
- **Hidden in work-arounds** the user has developed (signals of unmet needs)

Failing to capture these during the raw data stage leads to products that pass laboratory tests but fail in the field — the root cause of many product recalls (e.g., automotive airbag sensor defects, lithium-ion battery fires).

## 1.4 Physical / Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Sample Size for Qualitative Studies:** Typically **5 to 30 customers** per segment (Ulrich & Eppinger recommendation) — small samples are acceptable because the goal is *depth*, not statistical generalization.
- **Interview Duration:** **45 to 90 minutes** per session is the industry standard.
- **Recording:** **Audio + Video + Field Notes** is the gold standard (≥ 2 of 3 recommended).
- **NPS (Net Promoter Score):** A quantitative loyalty metric on a **0–10 scale**, used to segment Promoters (9–10), Passives (7–8), Detractors (0–6).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Step Framework for Gathering Raw Customer Data

The KTU syllabus follows the **Ulrich-Eppinger five-step process** within the broader "Identify Customer Needs" phase:

1. **Plan the data collection** — Define goals, customer segments, and method.
2. **Select the customer sample** — Use segmentation and lead-user identification.
3. **Conduct the sessions** — Interviews, observations, focus groups.
4. **Document the data** — Audio/video/notes — verbatim where possible.
5. **Extract and interpret** — Translate raw data into needs (this is the *next* sub-topic, kept here for context).

> [!NOTE]
> Step 4 (Document) is critical: if it isn't recorded, **it didn't happen** for the purposes of design traceability and ISO 9001 / safety audits.

## 2.2 Methods of Gathering Raw Data — Detailed Classification

### (a) One-on-One Interviews (Depth Interviews)
- **Structure:** Semi-structured — pre-planned open-ended questions but flexible to follow up.
- **Sample:** 15–30 customers.
- **Strength:** Uncovers *latent* needs, emotional drivers, and contextual work-arounds.
- **Weakness:** Time-intensive; requires skilled interviewers.

### (b) Focus Groups
- **Structure:** 6–10 customers + 1 moderator, 60–120 minutes.
- **Strength:** Generates ideas through group dynamics and bouncing.
- **Weakness:** Dominant personalities may suppress minority needs (a known safety risk — e.g., child-seat safety concerns often get drowned out in family focus groups).

### (c) Observation / Ethnographic Field Studies
- **Structure:** Designers watch customers in their natural environment.
- **Strength:** Reveals **behavioural** needs customers *cannot articulate* (the *Iceberg Model* — most needs are below the waterline).
- **Weakness:** Hawthorne effect (people behave differently when watched).

### (d) Lead-User Analysis
- **Theory (von Hippel):** Lead users are at the **leading edge** of an important market trend and are **highly motivated** to find solutions.
- **Equation for Lead-User Position (LUP):**
$$
\text{LUP} = f(\text{Lead-Use Position}, \text{Lead-Status Position})
$$
- Both axes range from −1 (behind market) to +1 (ahead of market); the target quadrant is **(+, +)**.

### (e) Surveys / Questionnaires
- **Structure:** Closed-ended, large sample (n > 100).
- **Strength:** Quantitative validation; useful for Kano classification.
- **Weakness:** Surface-level only; cannot capture *why*.

## 2.3 The Kano Model — Classifying Customer Satisfaction

The **Kano Model** (Noriaki Kano, 1984) categorizes features based on how they influence customer satisfaction. This is a *high-yield* KTU topic.

| Kano Category | Presence of Feature | Absence of Feature | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Must-be (Basic)** | Customer is neutral (not delighted) | Customer is **dissatisfied** | Safety features often fall here |
| **One-dimensional (Performance)** | More feature → More satisfaction (linear) | Less feature → Less satisfaction | Brake stopping distance |
| **Attractive (Excitement)** | Customer is **delighted** | Customer is neutral (no complaint) | Heated steering wheel |
| **Indifferent** | No effect either way | No effect | Unused ports |
| **Reverse** | Customer is **dissatisfied** | Customer is satisfied | Over-automation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety-related features are almost always "Must-be."** A seatbelt that locks only sometimes (one-dimensional but with a threshold) creates extreme dissatisfaction — and is also a legal liability.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Value / Unit | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| $n$ | Sample size for qualitative study | $5 \leq n \leq 30$ | Interviews |
| $T_{int}$ | Interview duration | $45$ to $90$ minutes | Session planning |
| $\text{NPS}$ | Net Promoter Score | $-100 \leq \text{NPS} \leq +100$ | Loyalty quantification |
| $\text{NPS} = \%P - \%D$ | Promoters minus Detractors | percentage | Quantitative VoC |
| $S_{sat}$ | Satisfaction (Kano ordinate) | discrete 1–5 | Kano questionnaire |
| $\text{LUP}$ | Lead-User Position | $-1 \leq \text{LUP} \leq +1$ | Lead-user screening |
| $I_{rec}$ | Recording channels used | $0 \leq I_{rec} \leq 3$ | Documentation compliance |
| $\alpha$ | Cronbach's alpha (survey reliability) | $\alpha \geq 0.7$ | Survey validation |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing KTU answers, **never write** "we did a survey" without specifying the **type** (one-on-one, focus group, observation, lead-user) and the **sample size n**. Examiners explicitly deduct marks for vague methodology.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (Tesla, Toyota):** Lead-user studies with fleet operators uncovered battery thermal runaway risks years before mass-market EVs.
- **Medical Devices (FDA, IEC 62366):** Usability engineering mandates raw data from representative users — the *Use-Related Risk Analysis* (URRA) is built on this data.
- **Consumer Electronics (Apple, Samsung):** Ethnographic studies revealed the "pocket scratch" issue in early smartphones — now addressed via oleophobic coatings and curved edges.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Process & Worked Example

## 3.1 The Step-by-Step Process for a KTU-Style "Gather Raw Data" Answer

The KTU examiner expects the following logical chain. Below is the **canonical procedure** with full derivation of the planning equations.

### Step 1 — Define the Goal of Data Collection
The goal must answer: *"What decisions will this data support?"*

$$
G_{data} = \{D_1, D_2, \dots, D_m\}
$$

where each $D_i$ is a design decision the team will make.

### Step 2 — Identify Customer Segments
Use demographic, behavioural, and **safety-critical** segmentation:

$$
\text{Segment} = f(\text{Age}, \text{Use Environment}, \text{Risk Exposure}, \text{Expertise})
$$

For example, a power-tool manufacturer segments into: **DIY home users, professional contractors, industrial operators**.

### Step 3 — Choose the Method
$$
M_{selected} = \arg\max_{M \in \{Int, FG, Obs, LU, Surv\}} \left( \text{Validity}(M) \cdot \text{Feasibility}(M) \right)
$$

where:
- $\text{Validity}(M)$ = how well the method captures the *type* of need (latent vs expressed).
- $\text{Feasibility}(M)$ = cost, time, and access constraints.

### Step 4 — Develop the Interview / Observation Protocol

A **semi-structured interview guide** is constructed. KTU expects at least:
- 5–8 **open-ended lead questions** (e.g., "Tell me about the last time you used [product]…")
- **Probing follow-ups** ("What did you do then?", "Why?")
- **Artifact elicitation** ("Can you show me the product?")
- A **safety-specific probe** ("Have you or anyone you know ever had an accident or close call with this product?")

### Step 5 — Select the Sample (with Worked Numerical Example)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (Kerala regional context):**
> A startup in Kochi is designing a **portable induction cooktop** for local small-tea-stalls (chaya kada). They have a budget to interview **20 customers** ($n = 20$).

**Sampling decision derivation:**

$$
n_{final} = n_{min} + n_{sat}
$$

where $n_{min}$ is the **minimum statistically acceptable sample** and $n_{sat}$ is the **saturation buffer** (extra interviews until no new themes emerge).

For a qualitative safety study:

$$
n_{min} = 5 \text{ (per Ulrich-Eppinger baseline)}
$$

$$
n_{sat} = 15 \text{ (until theme saturation in Kerala tea-stall context)}
$$

$$
\therefore n_{final} = 5 + 15 = 20
$$

The team segments as follows:

| Segment ID | Segment Description | Sample Count $n_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| S1 | Urban Kochi stall owners (high LPG cost pressure) | 8 |
| S2 | Rural Thrissur stall owners (frequent voltage fluctuation) | 6 |
| S3 | Migrant labour operators (kitchen-safety illiterate) | 4 |
| S4 | **Lead Users:** Innovators who already use induction experimentally | 2 |
| **Total** | | $\sum n_i = 20$ |

**Verification of proportionality:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} n_i = 8 + 6 + 4 + 2 = 20 \quad \checkmark
$$

### Step 6 — Conduct the Sessions
The team travels to 20 stalls, observes the actual tea-making process, and interviews each owner for $T_{int} = 60$ minutes. **All sessions are audio-recorded** ($I_{rec} = 2$, audio + field notes).

### Step 7 — Document the Raw Data Verbatim
Each customer statement is logged with:
- **Speaker ID** (anonymized, e.g., C-07)
- **Verbatim quote** (in Malayalam + English translation)
- **Context tag** (e.g., "peak-hour rush", "monsoon humidity")
- **Observed behaviour** (linked to timestamp)
- **Safety flag** (red flag if accident/close-call is mentioned)

**Sample raw data entry (KTU model):**

$$
\text{Entry}_{\text{C-07}} = \{\text{Quote}, \text{Context}, \text{SafetyFlag}\}
$$

$$
= \{\text{"This stove trips the MCB when three kettles run together"}, \text{Monsoon evening peak}, \text{RED}\}
$$

This verbatim record becomes the **input to the next step** (interpret raw data into needs) — but in the *Gather Raw Data* sub-topic, we **do not** interpret yet.

### Step 8 — Validate Data Triangulation
Compare across:
- **Method triangulation:** Interview $\leftrightarrow$ Observation $\leftrightarrow$ Lead-user data
- **Source triangulation:** Different segments (S1, S2, S3, S4)
- **Time triangulation:** Different times of day / seasons

If a need (e.g., "voltage surge protection") appears in ≥ 3 sources, it is **high-priority raw evidence**.

### Step 9 — Saturation Check
The team stops data collection when:

$$
N_{\text{new themes in last 3 sessions}} = 0
$$

i.e., no new customer needs have emerged in the last 3 consecutive sessions. This is the qualitative equivalent of statistical saturation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Gather-Raw-Data Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Step0: Define Product and Market Scope] --> A1[Step1: Set Data Collection Goal Gdata]
    A1 --> A2[Step2: Identify Customer Segments using Demographics Risk and Expertise]
    A2 --> A3[Step3: Choose Method from Interview FocusGroup Observation LeadUser Survey]
    A3 --> A4[Step4: Design Semi Structured Interview Protocol with Safety Probes]
    A4 --> A5[Step5: Compute Sample Size nfinal as nmin plus nsat]
    A5 --> A6[Step6: Conduct Sessions with Audio Video Field Notes]
    A6 --> A7[Step7: Document Raw Data Verbatim with Speaker Context SafetyFlag]
    A7 --> A8[Step8: Triangulate across Method Source Time]
    A8 --> A9{Saturation Reached: Nnew equals 0 in last 3 sessions}
    A9 -- Yes --> A10[Step9a: Lock Raw Data Set Ready for Interpretation]
    A9 -- No --> A6
    A10 --> A11[Output: Raw VoC Database fed to Needs Interpretation Stage]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Raw Data Gathering Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        P1[Customer Segments S1 S2 S3 S4]
        P2[Lead User Pool]
        P3[Observation Sites]
    end
    subgraph COLLECTION_ENGINE
        M1[One on One Interview Module]
        M2[Focus Group Module]
        M3[Ethnographic Observation Module]
        M4[Lead User Interview Module]
    end
    subgraph STORAGE
        D1[Audio and Video Archive]
        D2[Field Notes Database]
        D3[Safety Flag Registry RED YELLOW GREEN]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT
        O1[Verbatim Quote Bank]
        O2[Context Tag Index]
        O3[Safety Critical Evidence Set]
    end
    P1 --> M1
    P1 --> M2
    P2 --> M4
    P3 --> M3
    M1 --> D1
    M1 --> D2
    M2 --> D1
    M2 --> D2
    M3 --> D2
    M4 --> D1
    M4 --> D2
    M1 --> D3
    M2 --> D3
    M3 --> D3
    M4 --> D3
    D1 --> O1
    D2 --> O1
    D2 --> O2
    D3 --> O3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Kano Classification Logic (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Customer asked: How would you feel if Feature X is present]
    Q2[Customer asked: How would you feel if Feature X is absent]
    Q1 --> R1{Like}
    Q2 --> R2{Dislike}
    R1 -- Yes --> R1b{Answer to absent}
    R1 -- No --> K1
    R1b -- Dislike --> K2[One Dimensional]
    R1b -- Neutral --> K3[Attractive]
    R1b -- Like --> K4[Indifferent]
    R1 -- No --> K1
    R2 -- Yes --> K1
    R2 -- No --> K0[Must Be Basic]
    K0 --> K1[Reverse]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This Kano logic is the *exact* classification procedure KTU expects students to reproduce in 14-mark answers. The matrix combinations of (present) × (absent) yield one of the five Kano categories.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define "Lead User" as introduced by Eric von Hippel. Why are lead users especially important when gathering raw data for safety-critical products?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Lead User** is a member of a user population who (i) experiences needs that will be common in the market months or years before the majority of the market experiences them, and (ii) stands to benefit significantly by obtaining a solution to those needs. For safety-critical products, lead users are important because they (1) have already encountered hazards the rest of the market has not yet faced, (2) have developed informal work-arounds that signal latent safety needs, and (3) provide early evidence of failure modes before they become mass-incidents. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for safety justification.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List any three methods of gathering raw data from customers and state one strength and one weakness of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **One-on-One Interviews** — Strength: uncovers latent needs; Weakness: time-consuming and requires trained interviewers. *(1 mark)*
2. **Ethnographic Observation** — Strength: reveals unarticulated behavioural needs; Weakness: Hawthorne effect (subjects alter behaviour when observed). *(1 mark)*
3. **Surveys / Questionnaires** — Strength: large sample, statistically generalizable; Weakness: cannot capture *why* behind answers. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part B question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts of 7 marks each. **Both alternatives are to be answered in the answer book; students attempt ONE.**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** *Explain the step-by-step procedure for gathering raw data from customers in a product design project. In your answer, justify the choice of method and the determination of sample size for a safety-critical product.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Step 1 — Define the goal** of data collection in terms of design decisions it must support (e.g., "decide on cut-off voltage threshold for portable induction cooktop in Kerala"). *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Step 2 — Identify customer segments** using demographic + behavioural + risk-based segmentation (e.g., home users, professionals, lead users). *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Step 3 — Choose the method** by maximizing the product of validity and feasibility. Justify: for safety-critical latent needs, semi-structured one-on-one interviews + observation are preferred over surveys. *(1 mark)*
4. **Step 4 — Design the interview protocol** with 5–8 open-ended lead questions, probing follow-ups, artifact elicitation, and a mandatory safety-probe question ("Have you or anyone you know had an accident?"). *(1 mark)*
5. **Step 5 — Determine sample size** using:
$$
n_{final} = n_{min} + n_{sat} = 5 + 15 = 20
$$
Justify: a small qualitative sample is acceptable because the goal is *thematic saturation*, not statistical generalization. For safety-critical contexts, oversample lead users. *(2 marks)*
6. **Step 6 — Conduct, document, and triangulate** the sessions, stopping when saturation is reached ($N_{\text{new themes in last 3 sessions}} = 0$). *(1 mark)*
7. **Step 7 — Output**: a verbatim quote bank, context-tagged and safety-flagged, fed forward to the interpretation stage. *(1 mark)*

**(b)** *Apply the Kano Model to classify the following five features of a new electric two-wheeler battery pack: (i) BMS over-temperature cut-off, (ii) smartphone app showing state-of-charge, (iii) swappable battery, (iv) anti-theft GPS, (v) customisable LED colour. Justify each classification.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks — 1 mark for each feature + 2 for safety insight):**

| Feature | Kano Category | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| (i) BMS over-temperature cut-off | **Must-be (Basic)** | Its absence causes fire/explosion (extreme dissatisfaction); its presence is expected, not delightful. Safety-critical. |
| (ii) Smartphone app showing state-of-charge | **One-dimensional** | More accuracy/responsiveness linearly increases satisfaction. |
| (iii) Swappable battery | **Attractive** | Not expected; delights users who value range extension; absence causes no complaint. |
| (iv) Anti-theft GPS | **One-dimensional** | Linear relationship — better tracking = more satisfaction. |
| (v) Customisable LED colour | **Indifferent** (or Attractive for youth segment) | Most users don't care; some youth may be delighted. |

**Safety insight (2 marks):** Safety features (fire prevention, BMS) belong to **Must-be**. The team must ensure *perfect* implementation of (i) — any failure creates a safety liability, not just a quality issue. This is consistent with the KTU PESFT747 learning outcome that safety requirements are *non-negotiable*.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** *Compare and contrast: (i) one-on-one interviews, (ii) focus groups, and (iii) ethnographic observation as methods of gathering raw customer data. Which would you recommend for designing a new safety harness for construction workers in Kerala, and why?* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Criterion | One-on-One Interview | Focus Group | Ethnographic Observation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sample size | 15–30 | 6–10 per group | 5–10 |
| Captures latent needs? | High (with probing) | Medium (group think) | **Very high** (behavioural) |
| Captures expressed needs? | High | High | Low (no asking) |
| Cost & time | Medium | Low | High |
| Hawthorne effect | None | Low | High |
| Safety probing | Excellent (private) | Poor (peer pressure) | Excellent (natural setting) |

**Recommendation for Kerala construction safety harness (4 marks):**
A **mixed-methods** approach is recommended:
- **Primary: Ethnographic observation** of workers on Kerala construction sites (MGNREGA, Kochi Metro sites) to capture *unsafe behaviours* the worker would never admit in an interview.
- **Secondary: One-on-one interviews** with workers, contractors, and *grieved families of past accident victims* to capture emotional and contextual safety needs.
- **Tertiary: A small focus group** (4–6) with site safety officers to validate the harness features and identify training/usage issues.
- **Justification:** Construction workers may under-report safety concerns in groups due to employer presence; observation bypasses this and uncovers *latent behavioural* needs (e.g., the harness is unbuckled in 30 °C heat because of discomfort — a real safety failure mode).

**(b)** *Design a raw-data-collection plan for a start-up developing a low-cost neonatal incubator for district hospitals in Kerala. Specify the customer segments, the data-collection method, a 6-question interview guide, the sample size, and the safety-probe question.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Customer Segments (1.5 marks):**
   - S1: Neonatologists (specialists)
   - S2: Paediatricians / general duty medical officers (end users)
   - S3: Staff nurses (24×7 actual operators)
   - S4: Mothers of neonates (end beneficiaries, emotional needs)
   - S5: Lead users — innovators in tier-1 city hospitals (Kozhikode MCH, SAT Hospital)

2. **Data Collection Method (0.5 mark):** Mixed method — semi-structured one-on-one interviews + observation of current incubator usage in the NICU.

3. **Six-Question Interview Guide (2 marks):**
   - Q1. *"Walk me through your last shift caring for a preterm neonate — what equipment did you use, and what frustrated you?"* (open-ended context)
   - Q2. *"Show me exactly how you set up and adjust the temperature/humidity controls."* (artifact elicitation)
   - Q3. *"What do you do when the power goes out?"* (resilience / safety probe)
   - Q4. *"If the alarm sounds, what is your typical response?"* (alarm fatigue / safety)
   - Q5. *"What do you wish this incubator could do that it currently cannot?"* (latent needs)
   - Q6. *"Has any equipment ever harmed or near-harmed a baby in your care?"* (safety-probe question — must be asked with empathy, optionally after rapport)

4. **Sample Size Derivation (1.5 marks):**
$$
n_{final} = n_{min} + n_{sat} = 5 + 10 = 15
$$
distributed as $n_{S1} = 3$, $n_{S2} = 3$, $n_{S3} = 4$, $n_{S4} = 3$, $n_{S5} = 2$.

5. **Safety-Probe Question (1 mark):** Q6 above, *"Has any equipment ever harmed or near-harmed a baby in your care?"* — recorded with empathy, tagged RED in the safety flag registry.

6. **Documentation (0.5 mark):** All sessions audio-recorded, transcribed verbatim, anonymized, and stored with safety flags for the next stage (interpretation → needs → specifications).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Conflating "Gather Raw Data" with "Interpret Raw Data into Needs."** The KTU rubric specifically separates the two sub-topics. If the question asks for *gathering*, do **not** jump to writing the customer needs list — that is the *next* sub-topic.
