Neither method is ideal for $n > 20$ because:
- Pairwise requires $n(n-1)/2 = 190$ comparisons for $n = 20$ — fatiguing and error-prone.
- AHP suffers from severe consistency degradation (Saaty himself recommended $n \leq 15$).

**Preferred Approach for $n > 20$:** Use **clustering** (group needs into categories using Affinity Diagram / KJ Method) to reduce effective $n$ to $\leq 10$, then apply AHP at the *cluster* level, and re-distribute weights within clusters.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Six Customer Needs for a High-Rise Fire Alarm:** **(1 Mark)**

1. Smoke detection latency
2. Alarm loudness (dB)
3. Battery backup duration
4. False-alarm immunity
5. Aesthetic design
6. Network connectivity (to central panel)

**Hypothetical Data and Computation:** **(4 Marks)**

| Need | $CI_i$ | $IF_i$ | $SP_i$ | $WI_i$ | $\hat{w}_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Smoke detection latency | 5.0 | 2.0 | 1.0 | 10.00 | **0.318** |
| Alarm loudness | 4.8 | 1.5 | 1.0 | 7.20 | **0.229** |
| Battery backup duration | 4.5 | 1.5 | 1.0 | 6.75 | **0.215** |
| False-alarm immunity | 4.7 | 1.8 | 1.2 | 10.15 | **0.323** |
| Aesthetic design | 3.0 | 1.0 | 1.2 | 3.60 | **0.115** |
| Network connectivity | 4.0 | 1.0 | 1.0 | 4.00 | **0.127** |
| **Total** | | | | **41.70** | **1.000** |

**Computation Check (for false-alarm immunity):**

$$
WI_4 = 4.7 \times 1.8 \times 1.2 = 10.152 \quad ; \quad \hat{w}_4 = \frac{10.152}{41.70} = 0.2435
$$

(Revised totals after re-check: $\sum WI = 41.702$, so weights are: latency 0.240, loudness 0.173, battery 0.162, false-alarm 0.243, aesthetic 0.086, connectivity 0.096 — sum = 1.000.)

**Why Smoke Detection Latency Must Carry a Higher $IF$ than Aesthetic Design:** **(2 Marks)**

1. **Legal Mandate:** Standards like **NFPA 72** and **IS 2189** specify maximum detection latency; failure to comply is a *criminal* offense in many jurisdictions.
2. **Severity of Consequence:** A 30-second delay in smoke detection can mean the difference between *evacuation* and *fatality* — a consequence asymmetry that dwarfs any aesthetic concern.
3. **Reversibility:** Aesthetic flaws are *recoverable* through redesign; lives lost to slow detection are *not* recoverable.
4. **Defense in Depth:** Setting $IF_{latency} = 2.0$ ensures the *redundant* sensor architecture (optical + ionization + CO) is funded even if the initial business case is marginal.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Relative Importance** is established by combining **Customer Importance (CI)**, **Improvement Factor (IF)**, and **Sales Point (SP)** multiplicatively.
- ✅ The final output is a **normalized weight vector** $\hat{w}_i$ that sums to **1**.
- ✅ The formula is $WI_i = CI_i \cdot IF_i \cdot SP_i$ and $\hat{w}_i = WI_i / \sum WI_j$.
- ✅ **Reflection** is the *meta-cognitive* audit step that validates both the **results** (is the ranking correct?) and the **process** (was the survey fair?).
- ✅ **Safety-critical needs must never be deprioritized** — apply a Safety-Weighted Multiplier ($\alpha \geq 1.5$) to force-precedence.
- ✅ **AHP Consistency Ratio $CR$** must be $\leq 0.10$ for pairwise judgments to be valid.
- ✅ **Pairwise comparison** is best for $n \leq 15$; for larger sets, use **clustering + AHP**.
- ✅ **Kano Model** differentiates *Must-be* (weight 1.0), *One-Dimensional* (0.8), and *Attractive* (0.5) needs.
- ✅ **Iteration is mandatory** — if reflection reveals a flaw, loop back to the survey step (PDCA cycle).
- ✅ **Documentation is the legal shield** — a written reflection log is the firm's best defense in liability cases.
- ✅ The ordering **Safety > Performance > Comfort > Aesthetics** is the *canonical priority order* in safety-related product design.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept Generation: Activities of Concept Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT747 - SAFETY IN PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept Generation: Activities of Concept Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept Generation** is the *systematic, iterative phase of the product design process* in which multiple potential design solutions (concepts) are created, developed, and refined to satisfy an approved set of customer needs, engineering specifications, and—critically for this course—**safety constraints and regulatory requirements**.

In the **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme** framework (Outcome-Based Education under NEP 2020), Concept Generation is positioned immediately after *Problem Definition* and *Concept Selection*. It is the **divergent** phase of the design funnel: the design team explores *many* ideas before *converging* on the most promising ones.

The **Activities of Concept Generation** refer to the structured, sequential tasks that designers perform to move from a *design brief* to a *set of feasible, well-documented concept variants*. These activities form the "creative engine" of the design process.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Idea Bakery"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Analogy:** Imagine you are a baker tasked with making a cake for a customer who says, *"I want something sweet, for a birthday, but I'm diabetic."*
> - You would not just bake one cake. You would: (1) **clarify** the problem (lactose-free? sugar-free? eggless?), (2) **search** for existing recipes (external search), (3) **combine ingredients** in new ways (internal search + ideation), (4) **bake several test cakes** (concept sketching/prototyping), and finally (5) **present** the customer with 3–4 best options to taste (concept screening).
> - Each of these is an **activity of concept generation**.

The same logic applies to engineering products. Safety adds an extra ingredient: every "cake" must be **non-toxic, hygienic, and not cause allergic reactions**—these are constraints woven into every activity.

### 1.3 Why Safety is Embedded in Every Activity

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** In *Safety in Product Design*, concept generation is not a "free creativity" exercise. The KTU syllabus mandates that every concept be evaluated against *foreseeable misuse, hazards, and risk-reduction principles* (Hazard Identification, FMEA, Design for Safety Hierarchy). Creativity without safety screening is **incomplete design**.

### 1.4 Physical Standards & Metrics (Highlighted)

Key standardized metrics used during concept generation in safety-critical products:

- **Severity Categories:** Catastrophic (Level 4), Critical (3), Marginal (2), Negligible (1) — per **ISO 12100:2010 / IEC 61508**.
- **Risk Priority Number (RPN):** **RPN = Severity × Occurrence × Detection** (with values 1–10 each).
- **ALARP Principle:** As Low As Reasonably Practicable — risk must be reduced unless the cost of further reduction is *grossly disproportionate* to the safety benefit.
- **Fail-Safe State:** The condition to which a system automatically reverts upon failure to prevent injury.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Core Activities of Concept Generation

Concept Generation in the KTU 2024 product design curriculum is divided into **six sequential and partially overlapping activities**. Each activity is treated below with its "Why" and "How".

### Activity 1 — Clarify the Design Problem (Problem Restatement)

**Why:** Eliminates ambiguity before brainstorming begins. Designers must separate *needs* (what the user wants) from *specifications* (measurable targets).

**How:**
1. Re-read the **Mission Statement / Design Brief**.
2. Convert customer *needs* into measurable *engineering specifications* (e.g., "should be light" → mass ≤ 2 kg).
3. List **Safety Constraints explicitly** — regulatory standards (BIS, ISO, IEC), foreseeable misuse scenarios, and applicable hazard logs from Activity 1 of Module 1.
4. Identify **Constraints** (budget, materials, manufacturing process, ergonomics, environment).

### Activity 2 — External Search (State of the Art Review)

**Why:** Avoids "reinventing the wheel" and surfaces existing solutions—including their **failure modes**—for benchmarking.

**How:**
- Search **patents** (Indian Patent Office database, WIPO, Google Patents).
- Search **technical literature** (IEEE Xplore, ASME Digital Collection, ScienceDirect).
- Search **competitive products**, supplier catalogues, and reverse-engineering reports.
- Search **incident databases** for similar products (e.g., CPSC recalls in the US, CDSCO alerts in India).
- Document **safety lessons learned** from competitor failures.

### Activity 3 — Internal Search (Personal/Team Knowledge)

**Why:** Taps the *tacit knowledge* of the design team, stakeholders, and end-users.

**How:**
- Conduct **interviews** with operators, maintenance staff, and end-users.
- Apply **personal experience** of senior engineers.
- Cross-pollinate from **adjacent industries** (e.g., medical safety principles applied to consumer products).
- Use **Analogous Problem Solving** — look at how *other domains* solved a similar problem.

### Activity 4 — Ideation (Concept Generation Methods)

**Why:** The actual creative explosion phase. Multiple structured and unstructured techniques are used to *diverge* toward many concepts.

**How (Key Methods):**

| # | Method | Description | Safety Applicability |
|---|--------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Brainstorming** | Group free-flow of ideas without criticism | Generate "what-if" hazard scenarios |
| 2 | **SCAMPER** | Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put-to-other-use, Eliminate, Reverse | Each prompt forces a new safety perspective |
| 3 | **Morphological Analysis** | Decompose into functions, combine sub-solutions | Quickly survey safety features per sub-function |
| 4 | **TRIZ (Theory of Inventive Problem Solving)** | Resolve technical contradictions using 40 inventive principles | Resolve e.g., "strong vs. lightweight" contradictions |
| 5 | **Sketching** | Hand or CAD sketches (multiple views) | Visualize guard placement, pinch points |
| 6 | **Concept Combination & Variation** | Hybridize two or more concepts | Combine fail-safe feature with ergonomic feature |

### Activity 5 — Concept Screening and Selection (Filtering)

**Why:** Reduces a large concept set (often 20–50) to a manageable shortlist (3–5) using **structured decision matrices**.

**How:**
1. **Concept Screening Matrix:** Score each concept against criteria (cost, manufacturability, **safety**, reliability, ergonomics, aesthetics, environment).
2. **Concept Scoring Matrix:** Apply weighted scoring (weights sum to 1.0).
3. Use **Pugh Matrix** (baseline concept vs. alternatives; +, 0, – scoring).
4. **Mandatory safety filter:** Reject any concept that *inherently* cannot be made safe or that violates a regulatory constraint.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Practice:** Safety is treated as a **non-negotiable pass/fail gate** *before* weighted scoring. A concept with a beautiful weighted score is eliminated if it has a Catastrophic-severity hazard without mitigation.

### Activity 6 — Concept Documentation and Communication

**Why:** Without documentation, the design intent is lost. Documentation is also **legal evidence** of due diligence in safety design.

**How:**
- **Concept Description Document** (1–2 pages per shortlisted concept).
- **Sketches/CAD renders** with safety features highlighted in red.
- **Bill-of-Materials (BOM) estimate**.
- **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA) sheet** for each concept.
- **Design for X (DFX) checklist:** DFA (assembly), DFM (manufacturing), DFS (safety), DFE (environment), DFD (disposal).
- **Decision Log** — why each rejected concept was rejected (especially safety reasons).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S$ | Severity (1–10) | dimensionless | Failure-mode rating |
| $O$ | Occurrence (1–10) | dimensionless | Likelihood of failure |
| $D$ | Detection (1–10) | dimensionless | Difficulty of detection |
| $RPN$ | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | dimensionless | Concept screening (FMEA) |
| $w_i$ | Weight of criterion $i$, $\sum w_i = 1$ | dimensionless | Concept scoring matrix |
| $S_{total}$ | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i$ where $s_i$ is the score on criterion $i$ | dimensionless | Weighted concept score |
| $R_{residual}$ | $R = S \times L$ (ISO 12100 simplified) | dimensionless | Residual risk after mitigation |
| $ALARP$ | Cost of further reduction ≫ safety benefit | INR / USD | Justification for residual risk |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** For safety-critical design, $S = 10$ (Catastrophic) and $O \geq 6$ triggers an automatic **concept rejection** regardless of weighted score. Memorize the RPN formula — it is the most-frequently asked formula in Part A questions.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Concept generation produces variants before a chosen platform enters safety testing (NCAP, AIS-052, FMVSS).
- **Medical Devices:** FDA's *Design Controls* (21 CFR 820.30) and EU MDR require documented concept generation with hazard analysis.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Battery safety concepts (thermal runaway containment) originate in the concept generation phase.
- **Industrial Machinery:** ISO 12100 mandates that hazards be eliminated "by design" first — this happens at concept generation, not retrofit stage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Methodology & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Canonical Workflow (Step-by-Step)

Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step procedure** for executing the six activities of concept generation. Each step is written for an engineering student attempting a design project.

### Step 1 — Problem Clarification (Duration: ~10% of project time)

1. Gather the **Design Brief** from the customer/stakeholder.
2. List **customer needs** (CN1, CN2, …, CN*n*) as plain-language statements.
3. Translate each need into a **measurable engineering specification** (ES1, ES2, …, ES*m*). Use metrics such as *mass, dimensions, force, voltage, response time*.
4. Construct a **House of Quality (HoQ)** if QFD is being used.
5. List all **applicable safety standards and regulations**.
6. Construct a **preliminary hazard log** with at least 5 foreseeable hazards (severity-tagged).
7. Sign-off the problem statement with the supervisor/customer.

### Step 2 — External Search (Duration: ~15%)

1. Open patent databases (WIPO, USPTO, IPO India) with **3–5 key search terms** derived from the engineering specifications.
2. Read **at least 5 prior art patents** — note their claims, embodiments, and any safety mechanisms.
3. Read **at least 5 academic papers** on the technical problem.
4. Benchmark **3–5 competitor products** — photograph/document safety features and **documented failures** (recalls, lawsuits, incident reports).
5. Maintain a **search log table** with columns: Source | Concept Idea | Safety Lesson Learned.

### Step 3 — Internal Search (Duration: ~10%)

1. Schedule **structured interviews** with target users (≥ 5 participants).
2. Conduct **expert elicitation sessions** with senior engineers and domain specialists.
3. Map the **product life cycle** (use, maintenance, transport, disposal) and ask: *"What could go wrong at each stage?"*
4. Document findings in a **knowledge inventory table**.

### Step 4 — Ideation (Duration: ~25% — the largest block)

1. Convene a **brainstorming session** (5–8 people, 60–90 min, no-criticism rule).
2. Generate **a target of 30+ raw ideas** (do not stop at 10).
3. Cluster similar ideas into **concept families** (typically 5–8 families).
4. For each family, **develop 1–2 representative concepts** with hand sketches or CAD models.
5. Apply **SCAMPER** to each representative concept to grow the concept count to ≥ 20.
6. Apply **TRIZ contradiction matrix** for the dominant technical contradiction.
7. Run a **preliminary hazard analysis (PHA)** on each concept; flag those with unmitigable high-severity hazards.
8. Shortlist **5–8 concepts** for the next activity.

### Step 5 — Concept Screening & Selection (Duration: ~20%)

1. Build the **Concept Screening Matrix**:
   - Rows: shortlisted concepts (C1…C5).
   - Columns: selection criteria (cost, manufacturability, safety, reliability, ergonomics, sustainability, aesthetics).
   - Use a simple **+ / 0 / –** rating vs. a baseline concept (reference).
2. Compute the **net score** for each concept: $N = \sum (\text{plusses}) + \sum (\text{minuses})$.
3. Eliminate concepts with $N \leq -2$ or with **any "–" in the safety column**.
4. Survivors enter the **Concept Scoring Matrix**:
   - Assign weights $w_i$ such that $\sum w_i = 1$ (e.g., 0.30 safety, 0.20 cost, 0.15 manufacturability, …).
   - Score each survivor on 1–5 scale per criterion.
   - Compute weighted total $S_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i$.
5. Build a **Pugh Matrix** for visual confirmation.
6. Document the **decision rationale** in a short paragraph per surviving concept.

### Step 6 — Concept Documentation (Duration: ~20%)

1. Write a **Concept Description Document** for each surviving concept (typically 1–2 pages):
   - Title, sketch/CAD render, principle of operation, key dimensions.
   - Bill-of-Materials estimate.
   - Manufacturability notes.
   - **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA) sheet.**
   - Cost estimate (target vs. estimated).
   - Strengths, weaknesses, open questions.
2. Compile a **Final Concept Selection Report** with the recommended concept and full audit trail.
3. Hand-off to **Concept Detailed Design** (next phase).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — RPN Calculation

> **Problem:** A designer has shortlisted 3 concepts for a lithium-ion powered cordless drill. Apply FMEA-RPN ranking to assist concept selection.

**Step 1 — Severity table (extract):**

| Failure Mode | Severity $S$ | Occurrence $O$ | Detection $D$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Battery thermal runaway | 9 | 5 | 6 |
| Trigger switch stuck ON | 7 | 4 | 3 |
| Chuck bit loosening | 6 | 6 | 5 |

**Step 2 — Compute RPN for each:**

$$
RPN_1 = 9 \times 5 \times 6 = 270
$$

$$
RPN_2 = 7 \times 4 \times 3 = 84
$$

$$
RPN_3 = 6 \times 6 \times 5 = 180
$$

**Step 3 — Rank and decide:**

$$
RPN_1 > RPN_3 > RPN_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Battery thermal runaway is the highest priority hazard}
$$

**Step 4 — Concept Selection Guidance:**

Concepts that *inherently* reduce $O$ or $D$ of Failure Mode 1 (e.g., solid-state battery, BMS with cell-level monitoring) rank higher than concepts that rely on user awareness (warning label). The RPN ranking must feed into the weighted concept score — typically with safety criteria weighted at **0.25–0.35**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Guide

| When the design problem is… | Best Concept Generation Method | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Well-defined with clear functions | **Morphological Analysis** | Systematic combinatorial coverage |
| Has a strong technical contradiction | **TRIZ** | Provides proven inventive principles |
| Highly innovative / unknown territory | **Brainstorming + Analogies** | Encourages lateral thinking |
| Safety-critical | **Brainstorming + FMEA-driven ideation** | Forces hazard consideration |
| Cost-driven, simple product | **SCAMPER on existing product** | Cheap, fast, low-risk |
| Cross-domain (mech + elec + software) | **Concept Combination + TRIZ** | Manages multi-physics trade-offs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Concept Generation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Approved Design Brief] --> B[Activity 1: Clarify the Problem]
    B --> B1[Define Customer Needs]
    B1 --> B2[Translate to Engineering Specs]
    B2 --> B3[Identify Safety Constraints and Standards]
    B3 --> C[Activity 2: External Search]
    C --> C1[Patent Search]
    C1 --> C2[Literature and Standards Review]
    C2 --> C3[Competitor and Incident Analysis]
    C3 --> D[Activity 3: Internal Search]
    D --> D1[User Interviews]
    D1 --> D2[Expert Elicitation]
    D2 --> D3[Life-Cycle Hazard Walkthrough]
    D3 --> E[Activity 4: Ideation]
    E --> E1[Brainstorming]
    E1 --> E2[SCAMPER and TRIZ]
    E2 --> E3[Sketching and CAD Variants]
    E3 --> E4[Preliminary Hazard Analysis on Each Concept]
    E4 --> F[Activity 5: Screening and Selection]
    F --> F1[Concept Screening Matrix plus minus zero]
    F1 --> F2[Eliminate Safety Failures]
    F2 --> F3[Concept Scoring Matrix with Weights]
    F3 --> F4[Pugh Matrix Confirmation]
    F4 --> G[Activity 6: Documentation]
    G --> G1[Concept Description Document per Survivor]
    G1 --> G2[PHA Sheets and DFX Checklist]
    G2 --> G3[Final Concept Selection Report]
    G3 --> H[End: Handoff to Detailed Design Phase]
```

```mermaid

    subgraph INPUTS
        IN1[Design Brief]

    subgraph OUTPUTS

    IN1 --> A1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree — Concept Safety Gate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Does concept have any Catastrophic severity hazard unmitigable by design?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> X1[REJECT concept immediately]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{RPN of any failure mode greater than 150?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Send back to Ideation for redesign]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Are all applicable safety standards met inherently?}
    Q4 -- No --> Q5[Send back to Ideation]
    Q4 -- Yes --> A[ACCEPT for weighted scoring in Concept Scoring Matrix]
```
