<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define TRIZ. List any two of its 40 inventive principles with one engineering example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

TRIZ (Theory of Inventive Problem Solving) is a systematic methodology developed by **Genrich Altshuller** in 1946 for solving inventive problems, based on the analysis of over 200,000 patents. It asserts that inventive solutions follow repeatable patterns that can be codified.

**Principle 1 — Segmentation (3 marks coverage):**
- *Definition:* Divide a system into independent parts that can be flexibly arranged.
- *Example:* Modular car dashboards where the infotainment module can be replaced without dismantling the air-conditioning module.

**Principle 2 — Preliminary Action (3 marks coverage):**
- *Definition:* Perform a required action or change in advance so that it is ready when needed.
- *Example:* Pre-tensioned seatbelts that activate a fraction of a second before the crash impact.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for one principle with example, 1 mark for second principle with example.)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Compare Brainstorming and Lateral Thinking in terms of (i) origin, (ii) methodology, and (iii) typical output.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Brainstorming | Lateral Thinking |
|--------|---------------|------------------|
| (i) Origin | Alex Osborn, 1939 | Edward de Bono, 1967 |
| (ii) Methodology | Group-based free association, 4 rules (no criticism, freewheeling, quantity, combine) | Individual-based PO (provocation) operations and 6 Thinking Hats |
| (iii) Typical Output | Large volume of incremental ideas | Few but highly novel reframings of the problem |

*(1 mark per row.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) regulations, every Module 2 question carries an **internal choice**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**A safety engineer is tasked with reducing the heat dissipation from a high-power lithium-ion battery pack in an electric two-wheeler. The existing aluminum housing weighs 4 kg, and increasing its thickness (for better cooling) pushes the total vehicle weight beyond the legal limit.**

#### (a) Apply TRIZ to identify the engineering contradiction and recommend at least three applicable inventive principles. Justify each principle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the Contradiction (1 mark):**

- Parameter to Improve: Heat dissipation capability (TRIZ Parameter #17 — *Temperature*).
- Parameter that Worsens: Weight of moving object (TRIZ Parameter #1 — *Weight of moving object*).

$$\text{Contradiction: Improving Temperature} \Rightarrow \text{Worsens Weight}$$

**Step 2 — Matrix Lookup (1 mark):**

Using the 39×39 Contradiction Matrix at row 17, column 1, the recommended principles are:

$$\{2,\ 14,\ 17,\ 25,\ 35,\ 36,\ 40\}$$

(Taking Out, Spheroidality, Another Dimension, Self-Service, Parameter Change, Phase Change, Composite Materials)

**Step 3 — Apply Three Principles (5 marks, ~1.7 each):**

1. **Principle 40 — Composite Materials:**
   Replace solid aluminum with an **aluminum-matrix composite reinforced with graphene**. Same heat capacity, 30% lower weight.

2. **Principle 36 — Phase Change:**
   Embed **phase-change material (PCM)** micro-capsules in the housing. Heat absorbed during endothermic phase transition, flattening temperature spikes.

3. **Principle 17 — Another Dimension (2D → 3D):**
   Move from a solid flat housing wall to a **3D-printed lattice structure** with internal cooling channels. Surface area for convective heat transfer increases 4×.

---

#### (b) Now solve the same problem using the Brainstorming method. Generate at least 6 distinct ideas, then apply clustering and selection criteria. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Problem Frame (0.5 mark):**
*"How might we cool a 4 kg battery housing without adding weight?"*

**Step 2 — Apply 4 Cardinal Rules (0.5 mark):**
No criticism, freewheeling welcome, quantity desired, combine ideas.

**Step 3 — Idea Generation (3 marks, 0.5 per strong idea):**

| # | Idea |
|---|------|
| 1 | Replace aluminum with carbon-fiber reinforced polymer (CFRP) |
| 2 | Use forced-air cooling using vehicle's motion |
| 3 | Embed Peltier coolers powered by solar cells on the body |
| 4 | Phase-change material (PCM) lining inside the housing |
| 5 | Heat pipes transferring heat to the rear mudguard (high surface area) |
| 6 | Spray-on liquid cooling film that evaporates and recondenses |
| 7 | Use the motor's waste heat to pre-warm the housing in winter (combined with PCM) |
| 8 | 3D-printed gyroid lattice structure for maximum stiffness-to-weight |

**Step 4 — Clustering (1 mark):**

- *Material substitution:* 1, 4
- *Active cooling:* 2, 3
- *Geometry optimization:* 5, 6, 8
- *Smart integration:* 7

**Step 5 — Selection Criteria (1 mark):**

Use weighted score: **Safety (40%) + Cost (30%) + Manufacturability (30%).**

Top 2 selected: **Idea 4 (PCM lining)** and **Idea 8 (3D gyroid lattice)** — both passive, high-safety, no extra electrical failure modes.

**Step 6 — Convergence (1 mark):**
Combine ideas 4 + 8 → Hybrid PCM-infiltrated 3D lattice housing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often **skip Step 1 (problem framing)** and jump straight to ideas. This loses 0.5–1 mark. Always write the problem as a *How-Might-We* question before generating.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A consumer product company finds that users are injuring themselves while using a hand-held electric mixer. Initial vertical-thinking solutions (longer handle, thicker grip, warning labels) have failed to reduce injury rates over three years.**

#### (a) Apply Edward de Bono's **Lateral Thinking** methodology to reframe this safety problem. Generate at least 4 PO (Provocation) statements and derive one novel product redefinition. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Diagnose Why Vertical Thinking Failed (1 mark):**
The problem is being framed at the *user-product interaction* level. Injury occurs because of the *physical coupling* between the rotating blade and the human hand. Labels and grips do not eliminate the blade — they only attempt to slow contact.

**Step 2 — Generate PO Statements (3 marks, ~0.75 each):**

- **PO 3 — Wishful Thinking:** "PO: What if the mixer had **no moving blade** at all?"
- **PO 6 — Exaggeration:** "PO: What if the mixer were so **small** that it could only blend a single drop?"
- **PO 7 — Opposite:** "PO: What if the **user's hand moved** instead of the blade?"
- **PO 1 — Escape:** "PO: What if **the food moved** while the mixer stayed still?"

**Step 3 — Derive a Reframed Product (3 marks):**

A **counter-rotating bowl + stationary whisk** concept:
- The food spins inside a sealed container.
- A fixed, **fully enclosed whisk** (no exposed blades) processes the food from outside via magnetic coupling.
- The user's hand **never enters the mixing zone**, eliminating blade-contact injuries entirely.

This is a *lateral leap* — the problem is redefined from "How to warn the user" to **"How to remove the dangerous surface altogether."**

---

#### (b) Compare TRIZ, Brainstorming, and Lateral Thinking across **at least 8** dimensions. Recommend the most appropriate technique for solving the original mixer safety problem and justify your choice. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Comparative Table (5 marks, ~0.6 per row):**

| Dimension | TRIZ | Brainstorming | Lateral Thinking |
|-----------|------|---------------|------------------|
| Origin | Altshuller (1946) | Osborn (1939) | de Bono (1967) |
| Approach | Algorithmic | Associative | Provocative |
| Output Volume | Few, high-quality | Many, mixed | Few, highly novel |
| Skill Required | High | Low | Medium |
| Time to Apply | Hours–Days | 30–60 min | Minutes–Hours |
| Group vs. Individual | Either | Group-based | Individual-based |
| Conflict Handling | Resolves contradictions | Generates alternatives | Redefines the problem |
| Best for Safety | Root-cause elimination | Hazard brainstorming | Stuck problems |

**Step 2 — Recommendation (2 marks):**

For the mixer problem where *vertical thinking has already failed*, the recommended approach is **Lateral Thinking (primary) followed by TRIZ (secondary).**

**Justification:**
- The problem is "stuck" because the question itself is wrong.
- **Lateral thinking** redefines the problem, generating breakthrough reframings (enclosed whisk, no exposed blade).
- **TRIZ** then resolves the *new* technical contradictions introduced (e.g., magnetic coupling efficiency vs. torque transmission).
- **Brainstorming alone** is insufficient here because the team has likely already brain-stormed in the past three years and is anchored.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often **recommend brainstorming** as a one-size-fits-all. Always **justify the choice** based on whether the problem is *well-defined (use TRIZ)*, *open-ended (use Brainstorming)*, or *stuck (use Lateral)*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall #2:** In Part (b), students frequently list fewer than 8 dimensions or skip the *justification* — both lose marks. KTU examiners reward **explicit reasoning**, not just bullet points.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Bullet Point Summary — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- TRIZ = **Theory of Inventive Problem Solving**, founded by **Genrich Altshuller (1946)**, based on analysis of **200,000+ patents**.
- TRIZ's 5 core tools: **39 Parameters, 40 Inventive Principles, 39×39 Contradiction Matrix, Su-Field Analysis, 76 Standard Solutions, ARIZ algorithm**.
- **Ideality** = Useful / (Harmful + Cost). Goal = approach 1.
- TRIZ's **4 phases of technical evolution**: Infant → Growth → Maturity → Decline (S-curve).
- Brainstorming founded by **Alex Osborn, 1939**. Core rules: **No Criticism, Freewheeling, Quantity, Combine**.
- **NGT (Nominal Group Technique)** = silent written round-robin variant; **EBS** = electronic anonymous.
- Lateral Thinking founded by **Edward de Bono, 1967**. Key tools: **6 Thinking Hats** (White, Red, Black, Yellow, Green, Blue) and **7 PO operations**.
- **PO = Provocation** statements deliberately absurd to break mental patterns.
- **Lateral thinking** *redefines* problems; **vertical thinking** *solves within* problem definitions.
- TRIZ is **data-driven**; Brainstorming is **psychology-driven**; Lateral Thinking is **cognitive-driven**.
- For **product safety**, use **TRIZ for root contradictions, Brainstorming for hazard enumeration, Lateral Thinking for re-stuck problems**.
- The **Contradiction Matrix** recommends principles, it does not guarantee them — engineering judgment still applies.
- **Su-Field model**: $S_1 \rightarrow \text{Field} \rightarrow S_2$ — every system has a tool, an object, and an energy interaction.
- **Most-tested KTU dimensions** for comparison: origin, methodology, output type, time, group vs. individual, conflict handling, best-use case.
- **Avoid these buzzword traps:** "TRIZ is just brainstorming with rules" (NO — it is algorithmic and patent-derived).
- **Avoid these buzzword traps:** "Lateral thinking replaces logical thinking" (NO — it *complements* it).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TRIZ Tools Ideality and IFR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT747 - SAFETY IN PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** TRIZ Tools Ideality and IFR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is TRIZ?

**TRIZ** (Russian acronym: *Теория решения изобретательских задач*) is the **Theory of Inventive Problem Solving**, developed by Soviet engineer **Genrich Altshuller** and his colleagues between 1946 and 1985. It is a systematic methodology for inventive problem solving that uses a knowledge base of cross-domain patterns of invention extracted from a worldwide study of patents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** TRIZ is taught as a *non-conventional, contradiction-based* design methodology that complements classical safety design tools like FMEA, FTA, and HAZOP covered in earlier modules.

### 1.2 The Concept of Ideality

**Ideality** is the central, philosophical engine of TRIZ. It expresses a single, deceptively simple idea: *an ideal system delivers its useful function while possessing zero harmful side-effects and consuming zero resources.*

Formally, the **Ideality Index ($I_d$)** is defined as:

$$
I_d \;=\; \frac{\sum \text{Benefits}}{\sum \text{Harms} \;+\; \sum \text{Costs}}
$$

The aim of every inventive effort, according to TRIZ, is to drive $I_d$ **towards unity (1.0)** without violating the physical, technical, or safety constraints of the system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ideality matters in Safety-in-Product-Design:** A *safe* product is one whose harms (injury, pollution, failure) are minimized. Ideality forces the designer to **explicitly value harm reduction**, making it a natural fit for safety-driven engineering design.

### 1.3 The Ideal Final Result (IFR)

The **Ideal Final Result (IFR)** is a thought-experiment that operationalizes ideality. When faced with a problem, the designer formulates the IFR as a short, paradoxical sentence of the form:

> *"The system itself performs the required function, **does not exist**, and is **harmless**."*

The IFR is not a real solution to be built — it is a **directional target** that guides inventive search. Every real solution should be compared against the IFR to assess how close it is to the ideal.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **car airbag system**. The functional requirement is: *"Protect the occupant's head during a crash without injuring them."*

- A **conventional (zero-ideality)** approach: add a heavy metal crash bar that transfers force to the chest (helps head but harms chest).
- A **TRIZ-driven approach** moves toward IFR: *"The occupant's head is perfectly protected, the airbag does not exist, and no one is harmed."*
- Real systems approach this — modern dual-stage airbags inflate, deflate, and disappear, leaving **no permanent mass** in the cabin.

This is exactly the trajectory that increasing **Ideality** describes: maximum benefit, minimum (ideally zero) harm and cost.

### 1.5 Physical / Mathematical Constants & Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Target |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| $I_d$ | Ideality Index | $0 \le I_d \le 1$ (drive $\rightarrow 1$) |
| $B$ | Sum of beneficial functions | Maximize |
| $H$ | Sum of harms | Minimize ($\rightarrow 0$) |
| $C$ | Sum of costs (mass, energy, $) | Minimize ($\rightarrow 0$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** When asked "Define IFR," students must include **all three** clauses — *function performed, system does not exist, system is harmless*. Missing any clause costs marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Ideality Equation — Stepwise Logic

The Ideality equation is not a measurement tool; it is a **qualitative compass**. Its logic unfolds as follows:

1. **Numerator ($\sum \text{Benefits}$):** Lists *all useful effects* the system delivers to the operator, environment, and process.
2. **Denominator ($\sum \text{Harms}$):** Lists *all harmful side-effects* — including safety hazards, pollution, ergonomic strain, and noise.
3. **Denominator ($\sum \text{Costs}$):** Lists *all resource expenditures* — material, energy, time, money, mass, and complexity.
4. **Driving $I_d \rightarrow 1$:** Means increasing the numerator while *simultaneously* shrinking both denominator terms. The two-fold objective is the **Genrich Altshuller Principle of Ideality**.

### 2.2 The Five Levels of Inventive Solutions (Altshuller's Taxonomy)

Altshuller classified all patented solutions into five increasing levels of inventiveness:

| Level | Type of Solution | Triangles Solved | Ideality Achieved |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | Routine design (use known methods) | 0 | No change |
| 2 | Minor improvement (correct one parameter) | 1 | Marginal |
| 3 | Substantial improvement (resolve technical contradiction) | ~15 | Significant |
| 4 | Innovation across disciplines (resolve physical contradiction) | ~40 | High |
| 5 | Scientific discovery / pioneering invention | ~40+ | Approaching IFR |

> [!NOTE]
> **Safety connection:** Most product-safety improvements occur at **Levels 1–3**. True accident-prevention breakthroughs (e.g., crash-absorbing crumple zones, fail-safe braking) belong to **Levels 4–5**, requiring IFR thinking.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Expression / Form | Boundary / Note |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ideality Index | $I_d = \dfrac{\sum B}{\sum H + \sum C}$ | $I_d \in [0,\; 1]$ in the limit |
| IFR Statement | "X performs F, X is absent, X is harmless" | Three-clause structure mandatory |
| Altshuller's Law | A technical system evolves toward $\uparrow I_d$ | Valid for evolving systems |
| Su-Field Minimum | One S (subject) + one F (field) + no V (vessel) | Marks a complete, useful function |
| Trimmed Element Rule | Element that does **not** carry load can be removed | Targets $\downarrow$ cost in ideality |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Advisory (KTU Board):** All absolute values or magnitudes in your written answers must be expressed using $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ — **never** the bare `|` character inside tables, as it breaks LaTeX rendering during digital valuation.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Safety Engineering

The ideality framework maps directly onto modern **safety-by-design** principles:

- **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** by Kletz: "Eliminate the hazard rather than control it." This is *exactly* moving $I_d \rightarrow 1$ by shrinking the **harm** term.
- **Subsystem Independence (STPA context):** IFR forces the designer to ask *"What if this protective subsystem didn't exist but the function was still delivered?"* — driving towards passive safety (e.g., pressure-relief via membrane rather than active valve).
- **Eco-design & Circular Safety:** Ideality aligns with Cradle-to-Cradle — *zero waste, zero toxicity, zero energy cost* is the IFR of sustainable product design.

### 2.5 The IFR Solution Checklist

A valid IFR statement **must** satisfy four logical conditions:

1. **Beneficiary clearly identified** — *who* is protected/served?
2. **Active verb describing the function** — *what* must happen?
3. **The system itself performs the action** — no human intervention.
4. **The system does not exist** — paradoxical clause, preserved to force inventive thinking.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation of the Ideality Index

Although the Ideality Index is empirical, its **boundary behavior** can be derived rigorously. Let us assume a system with continuous benefit and harm functions $B(t)$, $H(t)$, and resource cost $C(t)$ over its operating time $T$.

The instantaneous ideality rate is:

$$
I_d(t) \;=\; \frac{B(t)}{H(t) + C(t)}
$$

Taking the time average over the product's operational life $T$:

$$
\bar{I_d} \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \frac{B(t)}{H(t) + C(t)}\, dt
$$

To obtain the **steady-state design ideality** (used for comparing design alternatives during the KTU design phase), we assume $B$, $H$, and $C$ are approximately constant over the active operating window. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{I_d} &\;\approx\; \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \frac{B}{H+C}\, dt \\[6pt]
&\;=\; \frac{1}{T} \cdot \frac{B}{H+C} \cdot \int_{0}^{T} dt \\[6pt]
&\;=\; \frac{1}{T} \cdot \frac{B}{H+C} \cdot T \\[6pt]
&\;=\; \frac{B}{H + C}
\end{aligned}
$$

This recovers the classical Altshuller expression $\displaystyle I_d = \frac{B}{H + C}$.

### 3.2 Limiting Behaviour (KTU Favourite Conceptual Question)

**Case 1 — Harmless utopia:** As $H \rightarrow 0$ and $C \rightarrow 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{H,C \rightarrow 0} I_d \;&=\; \lim_{H,C \rightarrow 0} \frac{B}{H + C} \\[6pt]
&\;=\; \frac{B}{0} \;\;\longrightarrow\;\; \infty \;\;\text{(unbounded ideal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

In practice we **cap** $I_d$ at $1$ to keep the index interpretable as a *fractional ideality score*.

**Case 2 — Useless system:** As $B \rightarrow 0$ with finite $H, C$:

$$
\lim_{B \rightarrow 0} I_d \;\longrightarrow\; 0
$$

A zero-benefit system is **anti-ideal** — it consumes resources while delivering nothing.

**Case 3 — Net-harmful system:** If $H$ includes a dominating safety hazard (e.g., radiation leak, sharp edge, toxic emission), the system is rejected on safety grounds regardless of $B$. This is the **safety veto clause** of TRIZ.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Safety Helmet Design

A student team is comparing three safety-helmet designs for construction workers. The benefit, harm, and cost vectors (normalized on a 10-point scale) are:

| Design | $B$ (Head protection, comfort) | $H$ (Neck strain, heat) | $C$ (Mass $\vert$ in kg, price) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| D1 — Conventional hard hat | 6 | 4 | 5 |
| D2 — Carbon-fiber helmet | 8 | 2 | 7 |
| D3 — IFR Concept (no helmet, active air-cushion) | 9 | 1 | 9 |

**Solution:**

For D1:
$$
I_{d1} \;=\; \frac{6}{4 + 5} \;=\; \frac{6}{9} \;\approx\; 0.667
$$

For D2:
$$
I_{d2} \;=\; \frac{8}{2 + 7} \;=\; \frac{8}{9} \;\approx\; 0.889
$$

For D3:
$$
I_{d3} \;=\; \frac{9}{1 + 9} \;=\; \frac{9}{10} \;=\; 0.900
$$

**KTU Interpretation:**
- D3 has the **highest ideality** (closest to 1.0), but only if the *active cushion* technology is realizable.
- D2 is the **engineering-best balance** today.
- D1 is rejected by the safety veto — although $B$ exists, $H$ is excessive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Valuation Tip:** Always state *units are dimensionless* and *all terms are normalized* before quoting an $I_d$ value. Examiners specifically look for this disclaimer.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How IFR Resolves Technical Contradictions

| Classical Design Problem | IFR Reformulation | Inventive Direction |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Need strong bolt $\Rightarrow$ adds mass | Bolt holds load, **bolt does not exist** | Use shape-memory alloy that grips under load |
| Need bright headlamp $\Rightarrow$ drains battery | Light is bright, lamp is **not present** | Use luminescent/photoluminescent paint |
| Need safety guard $\Rightarrow$ blocks access | Guard protects hand, **guard vanishes** | Use light-curtain that stops machine on intrusion |
| Need airbag $\Rightarrow$ uses chemical propellant | Head is protected, **airbag is absent** | Use pre-compressed gas + smart trigger |

Each row is an explicit *ideality-trail* — moving from real (heavy) to virtual (absent) while preserving function.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs below are alphanumeric-only (prefixed with letters). No reserved TRIZ/markdown keywords appear as node IDs. Labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text — no bold, italic, or HTML tags.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — TRIZ Ideality Engine Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["PROBLEM STATEMENT"] --> nodeB["FORMULATE IFR"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["FUNCTION ANALYSIS"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["CONTRADICTION IDENTIFICATION"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["APPLY TRIZ PRINCIPLES"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["CANDIDATE SOLUTIONS"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["EVALUATE IDEALITY INDEX"]
    nodeG --> nodeH{"ID CLOSE TO UNITY"}
    nodeH -->|"YES"| nodeI["ACCEPT SOLUTION"]
    nodeH -->|"NO"| nodeJ["ITERATE OR TRIM"]
    nodeJ --> nodeC
    nodeI --> nodeK["SAFETY VERIFIED DESIGN"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Components of the Ideality Formula

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeN["NUMERATOR BENEFITS B"] --> nodeR["IDEALITY INDEX ID"]
    nodeD1["DENOMINATOR HARMS H"] --> nodeR
    nodeD2["DENOMINATOR COSTS C"] --> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeT["TARGET ID APPROACHES 1"]
    nodeR --> nodeU["SAFETY VETO IF H DOMINATES"]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — IFR Construction Checklist

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA["IDENTIFY STAKEHOLDER"] --> startB["STATE REQUIRED FUNCTION"]
    startB --> startC["ASSIGN FUNCTION TO SYSTEM ITSELF"]
    startC --> startD["ADD ABSENT CLAUSE"]
    startD --> startE["ADD HARMLESS CLAUSE"]
    startE --> outF["VALID IFR STATEMENT READY"]
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Altshuller Five Inventive Levels vs Ideality

```mermaid
graph LR
    lev1["LEVEL 1 ROUTINE ID 0"] --> lev2["LEVEL 2 IMPROVEMENT ID 0.2"]
    lev2 --> lev3["LEVEL 3 TECHNICAL CONTRADICTION ID 0.6"]
    lev3 --> lev4["LEVEL 4 PHYSICAL CONTRADICTION ID 0.85"]
    lev4 --> lev5["LEVEL 5 DISCOVERY ID APPROACHES 1"]
```

### 4.6 Functional Architecture — IFR-Driven Safety Design Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    faA["HAZARD IDENTIFICATION"] --> faB["DEFINE PROTECTION FUNCTION F"]
    faB --> faC["DRAFT IDEAL FINAL RESULT"]
    faC --> faD["SEARCH SUBSYSTEMS WITH ZERO MASS OR COST"]
    faD --> faE["APPLY TRIMMING RULES"]
    faE --> faF["PASSIVE SAFETY BY DESIGN"]
    faF --> faG["QUANTIFY IDEALITY INDEX"]
    faG --> faH["FINAL SAFETY COMPLIANT DESIGN"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the Ideal Final Result (IFR) as used in TRIZ. State the four mandatory clauses of a valid IFR statement.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Ideal Final Result (IFR)** is a TRIZ thought-experiment in which the designer formulates the *ideal* solution to a problem as if the system performed its required function while **not existing** and **causing no harm**.
A valid IFR contains four clauses:
1. **Beneficiary** clearly identified — *who* is served.
2. **Active verb** describing the required function.
3. **The system itself** performs the action (no external agent).
4. **The system does not exist** — paradoxical clause to force inventive thinking, plus a *harmless* qualifier.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four clauses.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Write the expression for the Ideality Index ($I_d$) used in TRIZ and explain each term.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$$
I_d \;=\; \frac{\sum \text{Benefits}}{\sum \text{Harms} \;+\; \sum \text{Costs}}
$$
- $\sum \text{Benefits}$ — total useful functions delivered by the system.
- $\sum \text{Harms}$ — all harmful side-effects, including safety hazards.
- $\sum \text{Costs}$ — mass, energy, money, time, and complexity.
An ideal system drives $I_d \rightarrow 1$ by maximizing the numerator and minimizing the denominator. *(1 mark per term, 0 marks for unsupported formula.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU Internal Choice: **Answer ANY ONE** from the two alternatives.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** *With the help of a suitable safety-engineering example, derive the Ideality Index equation and explain how it guides the designer toward the Ideal Final Result.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — State the empirical form (1 mark):**
$$
I_d \;=\; \frac{\sum B}{\sum H + \sum C}
$$

**Step 2 — Define each term with units (2 marks):**
- $B$ = benefits (normalized benefit units).
- $H$ = harms (includes safety, environmental, ergonomic).
- $C$ = costs (mass $\lvert$kg$\rvert$, energy $\lvert$J$\rvert$, money $\lvert$₹$\rvert$).

**Step 3 — Worked safety example (3 marks):**
Consider a power-tool with a mechanical guard. Original design: $B=5$, $H=4$, $C=5$.

$$
I_d \;=\; \frac{5}{4 + 5} \;=\; \frac{5}{9} \;\approx\; 0.556
$$

After redesign using a light-curtain: $B=8$, $H=1$, $C=7$.

$$
I_d \;=\; \frac{8}{1 + 7} \;=\; \frac{8}{8} \;=\; 1.000
$$

**Step 4 — Link to IFR (1 mark):**
The light-curtain approaches IFR: *The operator's hand is protected, the guard does not exist, and the system is harmless.* This demonstrates that IFR-driven redesign raised $I_d$ from $0.556$ to $1.000$, validating the methodology.

---

**(b)** *Discuss the relationship between the Ideality concept and Inherently Safer Design (ISD) as advocated by Trevor Kletz. Provide two industrial case illustrations.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):**
Both Ideality and ISD share the philosophy of *eliminating* hazards rather than *controlling* them. Ideality reduces $H$ to zero; ISD removes the hazard at source.

**Step 2 — Mapping of Kletz's principles to $I_d$ equation (2 marks):**
- *Minimize* → reduce $C$.
- *Substitute* → re-express $B$ with safer inputs.
- *Moderate* → reduce $H$.
- *Simplify* → reduce $C$ and complexity.

**Step 3 — Case illustrations (3 marks):**
- **Bhopal (1984):** Methyl isocyanate storage. IFR would have been *"The toxic gas is contained, the storage tank does not exist."* Solution: replace MIC with less hazardous intermediate. Reduces $H$ to near zero.
- **Texas City Refinery (2005):** Vapor-cloud explosion. IFR: *"Flammable vapor is handled, no tank exists."* Solution: venturi scrubber and inventory reduction — reduces $C$ and $H$ simultaneously.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** *Explain Altshuller's Five Levels of Inventive Solutions. How does each level relate to Ideality achievement?* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
Altshuller classified all patented inventions into five levels based on the number of *triangles* (universal sub-system patterns) used in the solution.

**Step 2 — Description of each level with Ideality link (5 marks):**

| Level | Description | $I_d$ Trajectory |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | Routine design within profession | $\Delta I_d \approx 0$ |
| 2 | Minor improvement, corrects one parameter | $\Delta I_d \approx +0.1$ |
| 3 | Substantial improvement, resolves technical contradiction | $\Delta I_d \approx +0.3$ |
| 4 | Cross-disciplinary innovation, resolves physical contradiction | $\Delta I_d \approx +0.2$ but $H$ drops sharply |
| 5 | Pioneering scientific discovery | $I_d \rightarrow 1$ |

**Step 3 — Safety interpretation (1 mark):**
Most safety regulations today sit at **Level 1–2**; breakthrough safety products (crumple zones, ABS, G-sensor airbags) belong to **Level 4**, while paradigm shifts (autonomous fail-operational systems) move toward **Level 5**.

---

**(b)** *For a domestic LPG cylinder regulator, formulate the Ideal Final Result (IFR) and apply the Ideality equation to compare three design alternatives. Show all calculations.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Problem (1 mark):**
A domestic LPG regulator must deliver constant low-pressure gas while preventing leak hazards.

**Step 2 — IFR formulation (1 mark):**
*"The gas pressure is regulated, the regulator does not exist, and the system is harmless."*

**Step 3 — Design alternatives (1 mark):**

| Design | $B$ (Flow stability, safety) | $H$ (Leak, fire) | $C$ (Mass $\lvert$g$\rvert$, cost) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Alt-A: Conventional metal regulator | 6 | 4 | 6 |
| Alt-B: Composite diaphragm regulator | 8 | 2 | 5 |
| Alt-C: IFR Concept (passive venturi nozzle, no moving parts) | 9 | 1 | 8 |

**Step 4 — Calculations (3 marks):**

For Alt-A:
$$
I_{dA} \;=\; \frac{6}{4 + 6} \;=\; \frac{6}{10} \;=\; 0.600
$$

For Alt-B:
$$
I_{dB} \;=\; \frac{8}{2 + 5} \;=\; \frac{8}{7} \;\approx\; 1.143 \;\;\text{(capped to }1.0\text{)}
$$

For Alt-C:
$$
I_{dC} \;=\; \frac{9}{1 + 8} \;=\; \frac{9}{9} \;=\; 1.000
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
Alt-C is the IFR-perfect design if technology allows. Alt-B is the pragmatic, immediately deployable choice because it offers the best safety-to-cost balance today.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marks-losing mistakes observed by KTU board examiners:**
> 1. **Omitting the "does not exist" clause** of an IFR statement. *(Lose 1 mark instantly.)*
> 2. **Quoting the Ideality Index without normalizing** the $B$, $H$, $C$ terms or stating units. *(Lose 0.5–1 mark.)*
> 3. **Confusing IFR with "ideal machine"** — IFR is a thought-experiment, *not* a literal blueprint.
> 4. **Writing $I_d = B/(H-C)$** — the minus sign is **wrong**. Both denominator terms are added because both are *resources consumed*.
> 5. **Forgetting the Safety Veto** — if $H$ dominates the denominator, the design is rejected regardless of $B$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TRIZ** is the *Theory of Inventive Problem Solving* by **Genrich Altshuller**, derived from worldwide patent analysis.
- **Ideality Index** $\displaystyle I_d = \frac{B}{H + C}$ — the master compass of TRIZ; target $I_d \rightarrow 1$.
- **IFR** = *System performs function + System does not exist + System is harmless*. Always write all three clauses.
- **Five Inventive Levels** — Routine (1) → Improvement (2) → Technical Contradiction (3) → Physical Contradiction (4) → Discovery (5).
- **Safety-Veto Clause** — A design with a dominating harm term is rejected *regardless* of its benefits.
- **ISD alignment** — Kletz's *Minimize / Substitute / Moderate / Simplify* principles map directly to reducing $H$ and $C$ in the Ideality equation.
- **Numerical tip** — Always cap $I_d$ at $1.0$ when interpreting results; quote normalized values and dimensionless units.
- **LaTeX safety** — Use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ for absolute values inside tables; never bare `|` characters.
- **Mermaid safety** — All node IDs must be alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`), and labels must be plain uppercase alphanumeric text.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Problem Formulation and Functional Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT747 - SAFETY IN PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Problem Formulation and Functional Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Problem Formulation — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Formulation** is the disciplined, systematic process of translating an identified market need, customer requirement, or societal challenge into a clear, bounded, and quantified design brief. It defines the **scope**, **objectives**, **constraints (including safety)**, **success criteria**, and **boundary conditions** of an engineering design task *before* any concept generation begins.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Problem Formulation is the **first phase of the Engineering Design Process** and forms the foundation for all subsequent safety analyses such as **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)**, **FTA (Fault Tree Analysis)**, and **Hazard and Operability Studies (HAZOP)**.

The formal statement produced is called the **Design Brief** or **Problem Statement**, which must contain:

- **Purpose / Need** — *Why* the product must exist
- **Customer / User Profile** — *Who* will use it
- **Functions** — *What* the product must do
- **Specifications & Performance Metrics** — *How well* it must do it
- **Constraints** — Legal, economic, environmental, manufacturing, **safety**
- **Boundaries** — What is in scope and what is out of scope

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Problem Formulation is the most safety-critical step:** *Over 60 % of product safety failures trace back to poorly formulated problem statements where hazards were not identified at the very beginning.* KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly trains students to embed **safety constraints** into the design brief itself, not as an afterthought.

---

### 1.2 Functional Analysis — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Functional Analysis** is the structured decomposition of a product, system, or process into its elementary **functions**, represented using the standard **verb–noun** syntax (e.g., *transform energy*, *support load*, *transmit signal*). It clarifies the **flow of energy, material, and signal** between sub-functions and the external environment.

The primary output is a **Function Structure** (also called a **Function Tree** or **Function Model**), on which safety techniques like **FMEA** and **Hazard Analysis** are directly applied.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — Building a House 🏠**
>
> Imagine you are asked to build a house. The client says: *"I need a home."*
>
> - **Problem Formulation** is the architect sitting down with the client and asking the right questions:
>   *How many rooms? How many floors? What is the budget? Are there earthquake codes? Is it in a flood zone?* The architect then writes a **Design Brief** with a list of **requirements and safety constraints** (e.g., *"must withstand Zone-IV seismic loads"*).
>
> - **Functional Analysis** is the architect then asking, *"What must this house actually DO?"* and writing answers like:
>   - *Support load* (walls, beams)
>   - *Provide shelter* (roof, walls)
>   - *Regulate temperature* (insulation, HVAC)
>   - *Allow access* (doors, windows)
>   - *Illuminate spaces* (windows, lights)
>
> Each function can then be checked for **safety**: *"What if the load-support function fails?"* → collapse hazard → addressed in FMEA.

In the same way, a car must *transport passengers*, *convert fuel*, *brake safely*, *illuminate the road*, *protect occupants in a crash* — each function is then examined for **potential failure modes**.

---

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics in Product Safety

| Term | Definition | Typical Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Hazard** | Source of potential harm | Qualitative + Risk Score |
| **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** | Severity × Occurrence × Detection | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ |
| **Severity (S)** | Consequence of failure | Scale 1 to 10 |
| **Occurrence (O)** | Likelihood of failure | Scale 1 to 10 |
| **Detectability (D)** | Ability to detect before harm | Scale 1 to 10 |
| **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** | Risk reduction target | SIL 1 to SIL 4 |
| **MTBF** | Mean Time Between Failures | hours |
| **MTTR** | Mean Time To Repair | hours |
| **Failure Rate (λ)** | Failures per unit time | $1/\text{hours}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All **RPN**, **SIL**, and **FMEA-related metrics** are **scaled numerical quantities** — never fabricated absolute values. They depend on the specific industry standard being used (e.g., **AIAG-VDA FMEA 2019** for automotive, **IEC 61508** for functional safety, **ISO 12100** for machinery safety).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Building Blocks of Problem Formulation

A **complete, safety-aware problem formulation** always contains the following six components, evaluated in this logical order:

1. **Need Statement**
   - The *unmet* market, societal, or user need.
   - Example: *"Users in rural areas lack a low-cost, durable water purifier that works without electricity."*

2. **Customer (Voice of Customer — VoC)**
   - Captured via interviews, surveys, focus groups, complaint logs.
   - Translated into **Engineering Characteristics (ECs)**.

3. **Specifications & Performance Metrics**
   - Quantified, measurable, and **verifiable**.
   - Bad: *"Filter should be good."* (unverifiable)
   - Good: *"Filter must reduce turbidity from 100 NTU to < 1 NTU at 10 L/h."*

4. **Constraints (Hard + Soft)**
   - **Hard constraints** — cannot be violated (safety regulations, legal standards).
   - **Soft constraints** — desired but negotiable (cost, aesthetics).

5. **Boundaries of the System**
   - What is **inside** the system vs **outside** (environment).
   - Defines the **black-box** interface.

6. **Success Criteria / Acceptance Criteria**
   - Quantitative thresholds that decide if the design is *accepted*.
   - Critical for safety: *"Brake must stop the vehicle from 100 km/h in ≤ 50 m on dry asphalt."*

---

### 2.2 The Three Phases of Functional Analysis

Phase 1 — **Identify Overall Function (Black-Box Model)**
- Represent the product as a single block.
- Inputs: Energy, Material, Signal
- Outputs: Modified Energy, Material, Signal

Phase 2 — **Decompose into Sub-Functions (Transparent-Box Model)**
- Break the overall function into 5–10 elementary sub-functions.
- Each sub-function is described in **verb–noun** form.

Phase 3 — **Establish Function Flow (FAST Diagram)**
- **FAST = Function Analysis System Technique** (developed by Charles Bytheway, 1965).
- Shows **why–how** and **when** relationships.
- Critical path flows left → right; supporting/branched paths flow top → bottom.

---

### 2.3 The Function Verb–Noun Syntax Rule

> [!NOTE]
> Every function **MUST** be written in the form: **[Active Verb] + [Measurable Noun]**
>
> Examples of **correct** syntax:
> - *Transform electrical energy into mechanical energy*
> - *Transmit torque from motor to wheel*
> - *Absorb kinetic energy during impact*
>
> Examples of **incorrect** syntax (common student mistakes):
> - ❌ *"Brake"* (noun only, no function)
> - ❌ *"To brake the car"* (passive, unmeasurable noun)
> - ❌ *"Safety device"* (feature, not function)

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below are all the high-yield formulas and metrics associated with Problem Formulation, Functional Analysis, and downstream safety analyses (FMEA, FTA, risk assessment). KTU examiners frequently test the **RPN** and **Risk Matrix** concepts.

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Meaning | Unit / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| $RPN$ | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Risk Priority Number in FMEA | integer, typically $1 \le RPN \le 1000$ |
| $S$ | Severity of failure consequence | How bad is the harm? | $1 \le S \le 10$ |
| $O$ | Occurrence probability | How often does it happen? | $1 \le O \le 10$ |
| $D$ | Detection difficulty | Can we catch it early? | $1 \le D \le 10$ |
| $\lambda$ | Failure rate | Failures per unit time | $1/\text{hr}$ |
| $MTBF$ | $MTBF = 1 / \lambda$ | Mean Time Between Failures | $\text{hr}$ |
| $MTTR$ | Repair time average | Mean Time To Repair | $\text{hr}$ |
| $Availability$ $A$ | $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$ | Operational availability | dimensionless, $0 \le A \le 1$ |
| $Risk$ $R$ | $R = P \times C$ | Risk = Probability × Consequence | scaled units |
| $SIL$ | Discrete safety integrity level | Target risk reduction | $SIL \in \{1,2,3,4\}$ |
| $PFD_{avg}$ | Average Probability of Failure on Demand | Safety function reliability | $0 < PFD < 1$ |
| $PFH$ | Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour | Continuous-mode safety | $1/\text{hr}$ |
| $RRF$ | $RRF = 1 / PFD$ | Risk Reduction Factor | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 valuation tip:** When asked *"What is the maximum possible RPN?"*, the answer is **1000** (when $S=10, O=10, D=10$). When asked *"Is RPN 800 always worse than RPN 700?"*, the answer is **No**, because RPN is a relative prioritization tool, not a continuous physical quantity — modern standards (AIAG-VDA 2019) actually **discourage** using RPN as the sole metric in favor of the **Action Priority (AP)** approach.

---

### 2.5 Connection to Functional Safety Standards

Functional Analysis feeds directly into:

- **ISO 12100:2010** — General principles for design — Risk assessment and risk reduction
- **IEC 61508** — Functional safety of E/E/PE safety-related systems
- **ISO 26262** — Functional safety in road vehicles (Automotive)
- **IEC 62061** — Functional safety of safety-related control systems (Machinery)
- **ANSI/AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook (2019)** — Failure Mode and Effects Analysis methodology

> [!NOTE]
> All five standards require a **function-level decomposition** of the product before any hazard or failure-mode analysis can be performed. **Functional Analysis is the prerequisite for safety analysis.**

---

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive:** Function analysis of an ABS brake system identifies *sensors*, *ECU*, *hydraulic modulator* — each sub-function is FMEA-analyzed.
- **Medical Devices:** Functional decomposition of an insulin pump is mandated by **IEC 62304** and **ISO 14971** for risk management.
- **Aerospace:** Functional analysis feeds directly into **FHA (Functional Hazard Assessment)** and **SSA (System Safety Assessment)** per **SAE ARP 4761**.
- **Industrial Machinery:** Functional safety per **ISO 13849-1** uses **Performance Level (PL)** derived from function reliability analysis.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This topic is **methodological and analytical** rather than purely mathematical. The "derivations" below are the **structured, exhaustive procedural derivations** that KTU examiners expect a student to reproduce step-by-step in the answer book. **No step-skipping** is permitted in the 14-mark answers.

---

### 3.1 Exhaustive Procedure — Deriving the Problem Statement (Design Brief)

**Step 1 — Capture Voice of Customer (VoC)**
- Use **surveys, interviews, ethnographic observation, complaint data**.
- Convert raw customer statements into **affinity diagrams**.
- Output: A list of 20–50 raw customer needs (e.g., *"I want clean water cheaply"*).

**Step 2 — Translate VoC into Engineering Characteristics (ECs)**
- Map each need to a measurable, technical parameter.
- Example: *"Cheap"* → *"Unit cost ≤ ₹ 500"*
- Example: *"Cleans water"* → *"Removes 99.9 % of E. coli at 10 L/h"*
- Output: A **requirements matrix** (sometimes called the **planning matrix**).

**Step 3 — Prioritise the Requirements using the Kano Model**
- Categorize each need as:
  - **Must-be** (basic) — non-negotiable safety/legal requirements
  - **One-dimensional** (performance) — more is better
  - **Attractive** (excitement) — unexpected delighters

**Step 4 — Identify Constraints (Hard and Soft)**
- **Hard constraints (mandatory):**
  - Regulatory: BIS, CE, ISO standards
  - Safety: must not electrocute, must not burn user
  - Legal: liability laws, recall laws
- **Soft constraints (preferred):**
  - Cost target
  - Weight target
  - Manufacturing process preference

**Step 5 — Define the System Boundary (Black-Box Specification)**
- Specify what enters the system (**Inputs**)
- Specify what leaves the system (**Outputs**)
- Specify what is **external environment** (out of scope)
- Output: A **black-box diagram** (input–process–output).

**Step 6 — Define Success Criteria (Acceptance Test Plan)**
- Convert each EC into a **pass/fail test** with quantified thresholds.
- Example: *Acceptance: filter must reduce turbidity from 100 NTU to ≤ 1 NTU; test: APHA method 2130B; sample size: 3 units; pass criterion: all 3 pass.*

**Step 7 — Draft the Final Problem Statement (Design Brief)**
- A single, bounded, quantified paragraph containing all six elements above.

**Final Output (Example for an electric kettle):**
> *"Design a domestic electric kettle that boils 1.5 L of water in ≤ 5 minutes from a 230 V mains supply, with a unit cost ≤ ₹ 800, that automatically cuts off on boil-dry, incorporates double-wall insulation to prevent user burns, and conforms to IS 302-2-15 and IEC 60335-2-15. The product must be portable by a single adult and operable without reading a manual."*

This single paragraph is the **derived** Problem Statement — note how **safety is embedded** as a *hard constraint*, not added later.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Procedure — Deriving the Functional Decomposition

**Step 1 — Identify the Overall Function**
- Begin with the black-box description.
- Example: *"An electric kettle converts electrical energy into thermal energy to heat water."*

**Step 2 — Sub-Function Identification (Top-Down Decomposition)**
- Ask: *"What must the system do to accomplish this?"*
- Break into **5–10 sub-functions** using the verb–noun rule.

Sub-functions for the kettle:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SF1:} \quad & \text{Receive electrical energy} \\
\text{SF2:} \quad & \text{Regulate electrical current} \\
\text{SF3:} \quad & \text{Convert electrical energy into heat} \\
\text{SF4:} \quad & \text{Transfer heat to water} \\
\text{SF5:} \quad & \text{Contain water} \\
\text{SF6:} \quad & \text{Sense water temperature} \\
\text{SF7:} \quad & \text{Interrupt power on boil / dry condition} \\
\text{SF8:} \quad & \text{Indicate state (on/off) to user} \\
\text{SF9:} \quad & \text{Support kettle on base} \\
\text{SF10:} \quad & \text{Allow user to grip safely (insulation)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Classify Flows (Energy / Material / Signal)**
- Energy flow: 230 V mains → heating element → water (heat)
- Material flow: water in → water out (boiled)
- Signal flow: thermostat temperature → switch → indicator LED

**Step 4 — Build the FAST Diagram**
- See Section 4 for the full Mermaid block diagram.
- The **critical path** is the energy-conversion chain.
- The **branches** above and below are supporting/control functions.

**Step 5 — Validate the Function Structure**
- For each function, ask: *"If this function fails, what hazard could occur?"*
- Document each hazard as a **preliminary hazard list (PHL)**.

This is the **complete derivation chain** from raw customer need → function tree → preliminary hazard list.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Procedure — Deriving the FMEA RPN from a Function

**Step 1 — Pick one sub-function from the FAST diagram.**
- Example: SF7 — *"Interrupt power on boil / dry condition"*

**Step 2 — Identify the Failure Mode**
- Failure mode = *how* the function can fail to perform.
- Example: *"Switch fails to open when thermostat triggers."*

**Step 3 — Identify the Effect**
- Effect = *consequence* on the user / system / environment.
- Example: *"Kettle continues heating → water boils dry → element overheats → fire hazard."*

**Step 4 — Identify the Cause**
- Cause = *root mechanism* of failure.
- Example: *"Welded contact in bimetallic strip; corrosion on contact surface."*

**Step 5 — Assign S, O, D Scores (1–10)**
- Use the **AIAG-VDA 2019** severity, occurrence, and detection tables.
- Example: $S = 9$ (fire = severe harm), $O = 4$ (occasional), $D = 6$ (moderate detection).

**Step 6 — Compute RPN**
$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$
$$RPN = 9 \times 4 \times 6$$
$$\boxed{RPN = 216}$$

**Step 7 — Decide Action Priority (AP)**
- In AIAG-VDA 2019, the AP is determined by a **lookup table** combining S, O, and D.
- $S=9, O=4, D=6$ typically maps to **AP = High (H)**.
- This **triggers a recommended action** (redesign thermostat, add redundant cut-off, etc.).

**Step 8 — Re-compute RPN after Action**
- After redesign (e.g., add a second independent thermal fuse): $O$ drops from 4 → 2.
$$RPN_{new} = 9 \times 2 \times 6 = 108$$
- The reduction documents the **risk reduction achieved** by the design change.

This 8-step procedure is the **analytical matrix** that links Functional Analysis directly to Safety Engineering.

---

### 3.4 Summary — Analytical Matrix Linking Problem Formulation → Functional Analysis → Safety

| Stage | Output | Safety Link |
|---|---|---|
| VoC Capture | Raw customer needs | Voice contains *latent* safety needs |
| Engineering Characteristics | Quantified requirements | Hard constraints = safety constraints |
| Kano Prioritisation | Must-be / One-dim / Attractive | Must-be = safety/non-negotiable |
| Black-box Definition | Inputs / Outputs / Boundary | Defines what is *in scope* for safety analysis |
| Sub-Function Decomposition | Function tree (verb–noun) | Each sub-function is a candidate FMEA row |
| FAST Diagram | Function flow | Highlights control/feedback loops critical to safety |
| Preliminary Hazard List | Hazard per function | Direct input to FMEA / HAZOP |
| FMEA | RPN per failure mode | Quantitative prioritisation of safety actions |
| Verification Plan | Test results | Closes the safety loop back to the original problem statement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 takeaway:** *Problem Formulation and Functional Analysis are not two separate topics — they form a single continuous chain. The problem statement is the input; the function tree is the analytical engine; the hazard list and FMEA are the safety outputs. Every 14-mark answer should make this chain explicit.*

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, all diagrams below use **purely alphanumeric Mermaid node IDs** and **plain uppercase alphanumeric labels** (no markdown bold/italics/HTML inside double-quoted labels). These diagrams are tailored to the topic of Problem Formulation and Functional Analysis in the context of Safety in Product Design.

---

### 4.1 Problem Formulation Process Flow

This block diagram shows the complete, sequential flow of Problem Formulation, from raw customer need to a fully bounded Design Brief, with safety embedded at every gate.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[VOC CAPTURE] --> A2[AFFINITY DIAGRAM]
    A2 --> A3[ENGINEERING CHARACTERISTICS]
    A3 --> A4[KANO PRIORITIZATION]
    A4 --> A5[CONSTRAINT IDENTIFICATION]
    A5 --> A6[BLACK BOX DEFINITION]
    A6 --> A7[ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA]
    A7 --> A8[FINAL DESIGN BRIEF]

    S1[SAFETY STANDARDS CHECK] -.-> A5
    S2[PRELIMINARY HAZARD LIST] -.-> A5
    S3[REGULATORY COMPLIANCE] -.-> A5
    S4[LEGAL LIABILITY CHECK] -.-> A5

    A8 --> A9[FUNCTIONAL ANALYSIS PHASE]
    A9 --> A10[SAFETY ANALYSIS FMEA FTA HAZOP]
    A10 --> A11[VERIFICATION AND VALIDATION]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **solid arrows** are the primary sequential flow.
- The **dashed arrows** from the four safety gates (S1–S4) inject safety considerations into Constraint Identification (A5) — this visually demonstrates that **safety is a *cross-cutting constraint*, not a final step**.
- A8 (Final Design Brief) is the *gating deliverable* that authorizes entry into the Functional Analysis phase (A9).
- A10 is the **safety analysis cluster** (FMEA, FTA, HAZOP) which itself depends on the function structure produced in A9.

---

### 4.2 Black-Box to Transparent-Box Functional Decomposition

This block diagram shows how a product is first represented as a black box (overall function), then decomposed into its elementary sub-functions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BB[BLACK BOX REPRESENTATION]
        E1[ELECTRICAL ENERGY IN] --> BX1[KETTLE]
        M1[WATER IN] --> BX1
        BX1 --> M2[BOILED WATER OUT]
        BX1 --> E2[HEAT TO ENVIRONMENT]
        BX1 --> S1[STATUS SIGNAL OUT]
    end

    subgraph TB[TRANSPARENT BOX FUNCTION STRUCTURE]
        SF1[RECEIVE ELECTRICAL ENERGY]
        SF2[REGULATE CURRENT]
        SF3[CONVERT ELECTRICAL ENERGY TO HEAT]
        SF4[TRANSFER HEAT TO WATER]
        SF5[CONTAIN WATER]
        SF6[SENSE WATER TEMPERATURE]
        SF7[INTERRUPT POWER ON BOIL OR DRY]
        SF8[INDICATE STATE TO USER]
        SF9[SUPPORT KETTLE ON BASE]
        SF10[INSULATE USER FROM HEAT]
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- **Left (BB)** — The kettle is a single block. Only the *external interfaces* are visible: energy, material, and signal.
- **Right (TB)** — The same kettle is now decomposed into 10 sub-functions (SF1–SF10), each in **verb–noun** form.
- This is the **exact** transition required by **ISO 12100** and **IEC 61508** before any safety analysis can begin.

---

### 4.3 FAST (Function Analysis System Technique) Diagram

This is the **schematic representation of a FAST diagram** showing critical-path, supporting, and control/feedback relationships. The diagram is adapted for the electric kettle example.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CP0[RECEIVE ELECTRICAL ENERGY] --> CP1[REGULATE CURRENT]
    CP1 --> CP2[CONVERT ELECTRICAL ENERGY TO HEAT]
    CP2 --> CP3[TRANSFER HEAT TO WATER]
    CP3 --> CP4[CONTAIN WATER]
    CP4 --> CP5[PROVIDE BOILED WATER TO USER]

    SF9[SUPPORT KETTLE ON BASE] -.-> CP0
    SF10[INSULATE USER FROM HEAT] -.-> CP5

    SF6[SENSE WATER TEMPERATURE] -.-> SF7
    SF7[INTERRUPT POWER ON BOIL OR DRY] -.-> CP1
    SF8[INDICATE STATE TO USER] -.-> CP5
```

**Reading the FAST diagram:**

- **Solid arrows (CP0 → CP5)** = *critical path* (the main left-to-right energy/material flow).
- **Dashed arrows (SF6, SF7, SF8)** = *control/feedback* signals that monitor and regulate the critical path.
- **Dashed arrows (SF9, SF10)** = *supporting* functions that enable the critical path but are not part of it.
- This is the standard FAST diagram notation used in KTU 2024 Scheme coursework.

---

### 4.4 Functional Analysis → Safety Analysis Pipeline

This is the **block-level functional architecture flow** that maps the chain from Function Analysis output directly to safety deliverables.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[STEP 1 IDENTIFY OVERALL FUNCTION] --> P2[STEP 2 DECOMPOSE INTO SUBFUNCTIONS]
    P2 --> P3[STEP 3 CLASSIFY FLOWS ENERGY MATERIAL SIGNAL]
    P3 --> P4[STEP 4 BUILD FAST DIAGRAM]
    P4 --> P5[STEP 5 BUILD PRELIMINARY HAZARD LIST]

    P5 --> H1[HAZARD IDENTIFICATION]
    P5 --> F1[FAILURE MODE LIST]

    H1 --> R1[RISK MATRIX]
    F1 --> F2[FMEA TABLE WITH RPN]

    R1 --> DEC[DESIGN DECISION GATE]
    F2 --> DEC

    DEC --> V1[VERIFICATION TEST]
    DEC --> V2[VALIDATION TEST]
    V1 --> CL[CLOSURE OF SAFETY LOOP]
    V2 --> CL
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Steps P1–P5 are the **Functional Analysis procedure** itself.
- H1, F1, F2, R1 are the **parallel safety-analysis outputs** that consume the function tree.
- The **Design Decision Gate (DEC)** is the point at which engineering decides whether to redesign, add a safety device, accept the risk, or abandon the concept.
- **V1 and V2** close the safety loop by verifying and validating that the design actually meets the original safety constraints stated in the Problem Formulation phase.

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram is the **canonical safety-in-design workflow** required by KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 and is directly mappable to **ISO 12100, ISO 14971, and IEC 61508** methodology.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled precisely on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern. Part A is 3-mark short answer; Part B is 14-mark with internal choice (you answer **either** Question A **or** Question B). Each sub-question is 7 marks. **Model solutions are exhaustive** — no steps skipped, so students can use them as full-mark reference answers.

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Problem Formulation in the context of product safety. List its six essential components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Problem Formulation is the systematic translation of an identified market, user, or societal need into a clear, bounded, and quantified design brief *before* any concept generation begins. It is the **first phase of the engineering design process** and is the **most safety-critical step**, because hazards not identified at this stage propagate through the entire design.

The six essential components are:

1. **Need Statement** — Why the product must exist
2. **Customer / User Profile** — Who will use it
3. **Functions and Specifications** — What and how well
4. **Constraints (hard and soft)** — Safety, legal, economic, environmental
5. **System Boundaries** — What is in / out of scope
6. **Success / Acceptance Criteria** — Quantified pass/fail thresholds

> [!TIP]
> **Awarding pattern (3 marks):** Definition = 1 mark; listing all six components = 1 mark; brief safety justification = 1 mark. Missing safety linkage = −1 mark.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is a verb–noun function? Give two correct and two incorrect examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **verb–noun function** is the standard syntax used in Functional Analysis to describe what a product *does*, not what it *is*. It consists of an **active verb** (action) followed by a **measurable noun** (object of the action).

- ✅ **Correct examples:**
  - *Convert electrical energy into heat*
  - *Transmit torque from motor to wheel*

- ❌ **Incorrect examples:**
  - *Brake* — this is a noun (component), not a function
  - *To brake the car* — passive voice + non-measurable noun

The verb–noun rule is mandated by **VDI 2225** and is the standard KTU expects in every Functional Analysis answer.

> [!TIP]
> **Awarding pattern (3 marks):** Definition = 1 mark; two correct examples = 1 mark; two incorrect examples with reasons = 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Answer EITHER A OR B)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **six building blocks of Problem Formulation** with reference to the design of a *domestic electric kettle*. Show how safety constraints are embedded in the design brief. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Build the **functional decomposition** of the same electric kettle into at least **8 sub-functions** using verb–noun syntax, and draw a **FAST diagram** showing critical path, supporting functions, and control feedback. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Six Building Blocks of Problem Formulation (7 marks):**

1. **Need Statement (1 mark)**
   *"Urban Indian households lack an affordable, fast, safe, and energy-efficient electric kettle that meets Indian voltage standards (230 V, 50 Hz)."*

2. **Customer / User Profile (1 mark)**
   Target user: middle-income nuclear family (4 members), age 25–55, non-technical, no reading of manuals expected.

3. **Functions and Specifications (1.5 marks)**
   - Boil 1.5 L in ≤ 5 minutes
   - Auto cut-off on boil and dry
   - Cool-touch outer body
   - Concealed heating element
   - BIS-marked 3-pin plug

4. **Constraints (1.5 marks)**
   - **Hard (safety/legal):** IS 302-2-15, IEC 60335-2-15, double-insulation Class II
   - **Hard (regulatory):** CE / BIS certification
   - **Soft:** Cost ≤ ₹ 1200, weight ≤ 1.2 kg

5. **System Boundaries (1 mark)**
   - **In:** kettle body, heating element, thermostat, base, switch, indicator
   - **Out:** water source, mains wiring, user handling, disposal

6. **Success / Acceptance Criteria (1 mark)**
   - Boil test: 1.5 L tap water (25 °C) → 100 °C in ≤ 5 min (3 of 3 samples)
   - Boil-dry test: kettle trips within 30 s of empty heating
   - Surface temperature ≤ 50 °C during operation (measured 100 mm above base)

**Safety embedded in the brief (continuous across all six blocks):**
- *Hard constraints block* includes double-insulation and boil-dry cut-off
- *Acceptance criteria block* includes quantitative surface-temperature limit
- *Specifications block* requires concealed element (prevents limescale-related fires)

---

**(b) Functional Decomposition + FAST Diagram (7 marks):**

**Sub-functions in verb–noun form (4 marks):**

1. SF1: *Receive electrical energy from mains*
2. SF2: *Regulate current to safe level*
3. SF3: *Convert electrical energy into heat*
4. SF4: *Transfer heat to water*
5. SF5: *Contain water within vessel*
6. SF6: *Sense water temperature*
7. SF7: *Interrupt power on boil or dry condition*
8. SF8: *Indicate operating state to user*
9. SF9: *Support kettle stably on base*
10. SF10: *Insulate user from hot surface*

**FAST diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SF1 RECEIVE ELECTRICAL ENERGY] --> B[SF2 REGULATE CURRENT]
    B --> C[SF3 CONVERT ELECTRICAL ENERGY TO HEAT]
    C --> D[SF4 TRANSFER HEAT TO WATER]
    D --> E[SF5 CONTAIN WATER]
    E --> F[PROVIDE BOILED WATER TO USER]

    G[SF6 SENSE WATER TEMPERATURE] -.-> H
    H[SF7 INTERRUPT POWER ON BOIL OR DRY] -.-> B
    I[SF8 INDICATE STATE TO USER] -.-> F
    J[SF9 SUPPORT KETTLE ON BASE] -.-> A
    K[SF10 INSULATE USER FROM HOT SURFACE] -.-> F
```

**Marking scheme (3 marks for the diagram):**
- Correct critical path (SF1 → SF2 → SF3 → SF4 → SF5) = 1.5 marks
- Two control/feedback functions shown = 1 mark
- Two supporting functions shown = 0.5 mark

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students *list* sub-functions but **forget the verb–noun syntax**. Writing *"thermostat"* or *"switch"* instead of *"sense water temperature"* and *"interrupt power"* will cost **at least 1 mark per sub-function**. Also, students often draw a *block diagram* but do not label it as a **FAST diagram** and do not show the **dashed control/feedback arrows** — this costs 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define **Functional Analysis**. Explain the **black-box to transparent-box** transition using any product example. Discuss how this transition enables **FMEA** and **risk assessment**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An automotive *anti-lock braking system (ABS)* has the overall function *"prevent wheel lock during braking."* Perform the following:
- (i) Decompose it into **6 sub-functions** in verb–noun form
- (ii) Identify the **failure mode, effect, cause** for the sub-function *"sense wheel deceleration"*
- (iii) Compute the **RPN** given $S=9, O=5, D=7$, and explain the **engineering action** triggered. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Functional Analysis — Black-Box to Transparent-Box (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Functional Analysis is the structured decomposition of a product into elementary verb–noun functions, showing the flow of energy, material, and signal between sub-systems and the environment.

**Black-Box representation (1.5 marks):**
- The product is shown as a single block with **inputs** and **outputs** only.
- Example — a *table fan* as a black box:
  - **Input:** 230 V electrical energy
  - **Output:** moving air (kinetic energy of air mass)
  - **Boundary:** ambient air, mains supply

**Transparent-Box representation (1.5 marks):**
The same fan is decomposed into:
- *Receive electrical energy*
- *Convert electrical energy into rotational motion*
- *Couple rotation to blades*
- *Accelerate air using blades*
- *Direct airflow directionally (grille)*
- *Indicate operating state*
- *Support fan stably on base*
- *Protect user from blades (guard)*

**Link to FMEA and risk assessment (3 marks):**
- Each transparent-box sub-function is a **candidate row in the FMEA table**.
- A failure of any sub-function becomes a *failure mode* in the FMEA.
- The **effect** of the failure is the *harm* assessed in the risk matrix.
- The **cause** drives the *occurrence score (O)*; the **effect** drives *severity (S)*; the **control mechanism** drives *detection (D)*.
- Thus, without transparent-box decomposition, FMEA cannot be performed rigorously — this is mandated by **AIAG-VDA FMEA 2019** and **ISO 12100**.

---

**(b) ABS Functional Decomposition + FMEA (7 marks):**

**(i) Six sub-functions in verb–noun form (2 marks):**

1. *Sense wheel rotational speed*
2. *Sense wheel deceleration*
3. *Compare deceleration against threshold*
4. *Generate hydraulic pressure modulation signal*
5. *Modulate brake hydraulic pressure*
6. *Indicate ABS state to driver*

**(ii) Failure mode, effect, cause for *"sense wheel deceleration"* (2.5 marks):**

- **Failure Mode:** Sensor fails to detect rapid wheel deceleration (e.g., tone ring damaged, sensor open-circuit).
- **Effect:** ABS ECU does not receive a lock-imminent signal → brake pressure is *not* modulated → wheel locks → loss of vehicle control → potential collision → severe injury.
- **Cause:** Contamination on tone ring, electromagnetic interference, sensor cable chafing, manufacturing defect in Hall-effect IC.

**(iii) RPN computation + action (2.5 marks):**

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$
$$RPN = 9 \times 5 \times 7$$
$$\boxed{RPN = 315}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):**
- $RPN = 315$ is in the **high-priority band** (threshold 200 is typical industry cut-off).
- Under **AIAG-VDA 2019**, with $S = 9$, the **Action Priority (AP) is "High"** regardless of O and D — this is a **mandatory redesign trigger**.

**Engineering action (1 mark):**
- Add **redundant wheel-speed sensor** (dual-channel)
- Add **sensor self-diagnostic** with fault lamp on driver dashboard
- Use **EMI-shielded harness** and sealed connector
- Re-score: $O$ drops to 2, $D$ drops to 3, $RPN_{new} = 9 \times 2 \times 3 = 54$ → AP drops to "Low"

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B part b-iii):** A common mistake is to **write RPN = 315 and stop.** KTU 2024 requires you to:
> (1) state the **risk band** (high/medium/low),
> (2) state the **Action Priority (AP)** per the latest AIAG-VDA 2019 methodology — *not* just the RPN number, and
> (3) propose a **specific design action** that lowers the RPN.
> Skipping any one of these three items loses **1 mark** each.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2, Problem Formulation & Functional Analysis**

- **Problem Formulation is the foundation of safety in design.** Hazards not identified here propagate through the entire product life cycle.
- The **six components** of a complete design brief are: Need, Customer, Specifications, Constraints, Boundaries, Acceptance Criteria. Memorize all six.
- **Hard constraints** are non-negotiable safety/legal/regulatory items. **Soft constraints** are cost/weight/aesthetic preferences.
- Functional Analysis uses the **verb–noun syntax** for every function — never write a noun (e.g., "brake") as a function.
- A function tree is built in **three phases**: black-box → transparent-box → FAST diagram.
- **FAST diagram notation:**
  - Solid arrows = critical path (left → right)
  - Dashed arrows = control/feedback or supporting functions
- The **three standard flows** in any function are: **energy, material, signal** — every sub-function must be classifiable under one of these.
- **FMEA scoring:**
  - $RPN = S \times O \times D$ (range 1 to 1000)
  - Maximum RPN = **1000** ($S=10, O=10, D=10$)
- Under **AIAG-VDA 2019**, **Action Priority (AP)** supersedes raw RPN as the primary decision trigger; $S = 9$ or $10$ **always** forces AP = High.
- The chain **Problem Statement → Function Tree → Preliminary Hazard List → FMEA → Design Action → Verification** is a **closed safety loop** — all six links must exist in any 14-mark answer.
- The relevant international standards are: **ISO 12100, IEC 61508, ISO 26262, IEC 62061, ISO 13849-1, AIAG-VDA FMEA 2019, ISO 14971, IEC 62304, SAE ARP 4761**. Citing the right one in your answer earns extra marks.
- The **two most common valuation pitfalls** are: (1) forgetting the verb–noun rule, and (2) writing RPN without specifying the action priority and engineering follow-up.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of 40 Principles to Solve Contradiction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT747 - SAFETY IN PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Use of 40 Principles to Solve Contradiction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Contradiction in Product Design?

In the context of systematic innovation (TRIZ – *Teoriya Resheniya Izobretatelskikh Zadatch*, the Theory of Inventive Problem Solving) developed by **Genrich Altshuller** and his team, a **contradiction** is a conflict situation in a technical system where improving one characteristic, parameter, or feature inevitably degrades another. In Safety Engineering and Product Design, contradictions are extremely common—for example, a stronger guard is safer but heavier and harder to operate; a sharper blade cuts better but is more dangerous; a stiffer structure is more stable but transmits more impact force to the user.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PESFT747 – Module 2):**
> A *Contradiction* in TRIZ is a conflict between two requirements, parameters, or states of a system such that the attempt to satisfy one requirement worsens another. Resolving such contradictions—rather than accepting trade-offs—is the foundation of inventive product design and the cornerstone of safety-by-design philosophy.

## 1.2 The Two Types of Contradictions

TRIZ formally classifies contradictions into two distinct categories:

### (a) Technical Contradiction (TC)
A **Technical Contradiction** occurs when a useful action (A) produces, along with its intended effect, a *harmful side effect* (B). Improving parameter **X** causes degradation of parameter **Y**.

- *Design example*: Increasing the wall thickness of a pressure vessel (improves burst safety) causes the vessel to become heavier (degrades portability and increases handling hazards).

### (b) Physical Contradiction (PC)
A **Physical Contradiction** occurs when a single element of the system must simultaneously possess *mutually exclusive* (opposite) physical states or properties. The same parameter must be **HOT and COLD**, **LARGE and SMALL**, **SOFT and HARD** at the same time and under the same conditions.

- *Design example*: A fire-resistant enclosure must be **closed** to block flames, yet **open** to allow heat dissipation and pressure venting during an internal explosion.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spicy Soup" Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Spicy Soup**
> Imagine cooking a soup. You want it to be **spicy** (flavor) but also want children to **enjoy it** (mildness). These two requirements directly oppose each other. The traditional (trade-off) solution is to make it *slightly* spicy. The TRIZ-based inventive solution is to make the spice active **only when the adult is eating**—e.g., by adding flavored oil droplets that release pungency only when chewed by an adult, leaving the broth mild for a child. **This is "Local Quality" (Principle 3).** The 40 Inventive Principles work the same way: they bypass the trade-off and resolve the contradiction at its root.

## 1.4 The 40 Inventive Principles — Origin & Purpose

Altshuller's team statistically analyzed over **200,000 patents** across diverse engineering domains. They discovered that fewer than 5% of patented inventions were truly novel, and the vast majority of innovative solutions reused the same small set of underlying inventive *principles*. These were distilled and codified into the famous **40 Inventive Principles**, which act as a **universal toolbox of solution strategies** for any technical contradiction.

In safety-focused product design, the 40 Principles are particularly powerful because:

- They allow **intrinsic safety** (eliminating hazards) rather than **extrinsic guarding** (adding warnings, PPE).
- They encourage **design for safer failure modes** (fail-safe, fail-soft).
- They reduce the *number* and *severity* of residual risks before adding protective devices.

![Contradiction Taxonomy](https://i.imgur.com/placeholder.png)
*(Figure 1: Schematic of how a single technical contradiction triggers a chain of design conflicts.)*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Chain of Inventive Problem Solving

The TRIZ methodology to solve contradictions follows a strict 5-step algorithm:

1. **Identify the problem** as a contradiction (do *not* accept trade-offs).
2. **Classify** the contradiction as Technical (TC) or Physical (PC).
3. **Map the TC to the Contradiction Matrix** (Altshuller's 39×39 matrix) to obtain suggested Inventive Principles.
4. **Apply the suggested principles** using domain-specific knowledge, often by combining 2–3 principles for a stronger solution.
5. **Evaluate the solution** against all 9 windows of the Substance-Field (Su-Field) model and Ideality rules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The 40 Principles are *not* techniques but **heuristic thinking patterns**—they guide the *direction* of a designer's thought process toward the kind of transformation that historically led to inventive patents. They are domain-independent, which is what makes them universally applicable in product safety.

## 2.2 The Complete 40 Inventive Principles — With Safety-Design Annotations

Below is the high-yield formula/cheat sheet for KTU exams. Each principle is paired with a *Safety-in-Product-Design* interpretation to make it directly relevant to **PESFT747**.

> [!NOTE]
> The 40 Principles can be grouped into 5 conceptual families for ease of recall: **Separation (1–7)**, **Counteraction (8–15)**, **Modulation of Action (16–24)**, **Self-Service & Substitution (25–32)**, and **Material/State Transformation (33–40)**.

| # | Principle (Name) | Core Idea | Safety-in-Product-Design Application |
|---|------------------|-----------|--------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Segmentation** | Divide an object into independent parts. | Split a long shaft into segments so a fracture does not throw the whole shaft at the operator. |
| 2 | **Taking out / Extraction** | Extract the disturbing part or only the necessary part. | Move a hot/corrosive component out of the user's reach (e.g., extract heating element from a kettle handle area). |
| 3 | **Local Quality** | Make each part of an object perform a function best suited to it. | Reinforce only the impact zone of a helmet; soft elsewhere for comfort. |
| 4 | **Asymmetry** | Replace symmetry with asymmetry. | Asymmetric tread pattern for better grip in wet conditions. |
| 5 | **Merging / Combining** | Combine similar or identical objects into one. | Combine the seatbelt and airbag into an integrated restraint system. |
| 6 | **Universality** | Make a part perform multiple functions. | A smartphone that doubles as an emergency beacon. |
| 7 | **Nested Doll (Matryoshka)** | Place one object inside another. | Telescopic guard rail that retracts into the post. |
| 8 | **Anti-weight / Counterweight** | Compensate weight with buoyancy, aerodynamic lift, etc. | Use counterweight to keep a heavy machine guard easy to lift. |
| 9 | **Preliminary Anti-action** | Pre-apply counter-acting force in advance. | Pre-stress a safety valve spring to open *before* pressure becomes critical. |
| 10 | **Preliminary Action** | Perform the required action in advance, in full or in part. | Pre-cut perforation lines on a glass panel so it shatters safely in a crash. |
| 11 | **Beforehand Cushioning** | Prepare emergency cushions in advance. | Pre-formed crumple zones; pre-deployed air-bag inflators. |
| 12 | **Equipotentiality** | Eliminate dangerous potentials (electrical, thermal, mechanical). | Equipotential bonding in electrical installations; thermal isolation in cryogenic pipes. |
| 13 | **The Other Way Round (Inversion)** | Invert the action or process. | Invert a centrifuge so the heavy side hangs down for stability. |
| 14 | **Spheroidality / Curvature** | Replace flat surfaces with curved ones. | Curved roll-edges on guardrails to deflect a falling worker. |
| 15 | **Dynamics** | Allow object characteristics to change to suit operating conditions. | Adaptive crumple zones that stiffen with impact severity. |
| 16 | **Partial or Excessive Action** | If 100% is hard to achieve, use slightly less or more. | Use partial vacuum (not full) to grip delicate surgical tissue. |
| 17 | **Another Dimension** | Move in 3-D; tilt or rotate. | Use vertical stacking in a warehouse to reduce horizontal collision risk. |
| 18 | **Mechanical Vibration** | Use oscillation. | Use ultrasonic vibration to prevent dust explosion in hoppers. |
| 19 | **Periodic Action** | Replace continuous action with periodic/pulse action. | Use pulsed braking (ABS) to prevent wheel lock and skid. |
