| 20 | **Continuity of Useful Action** | Keep all parts of an object working at full capacity, without idle intervals. | Regenerative braking in EVs to keep brake pads cooler. |
| 21 | **Skipping / Rushing Through** | Conduct harmful/dangerous steps very quickly. | Fast-acting fuse that blows in milliseconds before sustained arcing. |
| 22 | **Blessing in Disguise (Convert Harm to Benefit)** | Use harmful factors to achieve a positive effect. | Use waste heat from an engine to warm a cabin and defrost windscreen. |
| 23 | **Feedback** | Introduce feedback for better control. | Closed-loop pressure sensor in hydraulic press to stop ram on contact. |
| 24 | **Intermediary** | Use an intermediate carrier or process. | Use a sacrificial cathode to protect a steel hull (cathodic protection). |
| 25 | **Self-service** | Make the object serve itself. | Self-healing polymer that closes its own cracks. |
| 26 | **Copying** | Use a simplified/inexpensive copy. | Use a plastic scale-model to test a building's wind-load safety. |
| 27 | **Cheap Short-living Objects** | Replace expensive durable object with a cheap one. | Disposable safety gloves, single-use syringe. |
| 28 | **Mechanics Substitution** | Replace mechanical means with sensory/optical/electrical. | Optical smoke detector replacing mechanical bell-and-paddle. |
| 29 | **Pneumatics and Hydraulics** | Use gas or liquid instead of solid parts. | Hydraulic bumper in car door (intrusion-resistant). |
| 30 | **Flexible Shells and Thin Films** | Use flexible shells and films. | Inflatable safety airbag; shrink-wrap sterile barrier. |
| 31 | **Porous Materials** | Make object porous. | Porous ceramic heat shield; breathable PPE fabric. |
| 32 | **Color Changes** | Change colour/transparency. | Color-changing temperature strip on a kettle; photochromic goggles. |
| 33 | **Homogeneity** | Make objects interacting with a given object of the same material. | Use same alloy throughout a pressure pipe to avoid galvanic corrosion. |
| 34 | **Discarding and Recovering** | Discard/recover parts after use. | Disposable biopsy forceps; cartridge-based EpiPen. |
| 35 | **Parameter Changes** | Change physical/chemical state, concentration, flexibility. | Use a phase-change gel that solidifies on impact (sports padding). |
| 36 | **Phase Transitions** | Use phenomena occurring during phase change. | Use latent heat of vaporization in a fire-suppression sprinkler droplet. |
| 37 | **Thermal Expansion** | Use thermal expansion/contraction. | Bimetallic strip in a fire alarm triggers at a set temperature. |
| 38 | **Accelerated Oxidation** | Use accelerated oxidation with O₂/O₃. | Use oxidation layer to make biodegradable temporary implant. |
| 39 | **Inert Environment** | Replace normal air with inert gas. | Use nitrogen blanket in grain silos to prevent dust explosion. |
| 40 | **Composite Materials** | Switch from uniform to composite. | Carbon-fibre reinforced polymer in a racing helmet; fiber-reinforced concrete. |

## 2.3 The Contradiction Matrix (Altshuller 39×39) — Quick Reference Logic

The **Contradiction Matrix** maps the *39 Engineering Parameters* (e.g., *strength*, *weight*, *speed*, *temperature*, *reliability*, *safety*) against each other. Each cell contains 2–4 of the 40 Principles statistically found to be most effective in resolving that specific conflict.

**Reading the matrix:**
- **Row** = Parameter that you wish to *improve* (e.g., *Strength*).
- **Column** = Parameter that gets *worse* (e.g., *Weight*).
- **Cell Entry** = Suggested Inventive Principles (e.g., Principles **10, 14, 17**).

> [!NOTE]
> The 39 Engineering Parameters are categorized under headings: *Geometry, Physics, Material, Energy, Capacity, Reliability, Convenience, Safety, Manageability*. **Safety** itself is **Parameter #36** in the standard TRIZ list—this makes TRIZ uniquely suited to PESFT747 outcomes.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Expression / Symbol | Meaning |
|---------|--------------------|---------|
| Ideality of System | $I = \dfrac{\sum B}{\sum H + C}$ | $B$ = Benefits, $H$ = Harms, $C$ = Cost. Goal: maximize $I$. |
| Su-Field Model | $S_1 \xrightarrow{} S_2$ via $F$ | Substance 1 acts on Substance 2 through a Field. |
| Inventive Principles Count | $N = 40$ | Canonical TRIZ count. |
| Engineering Parameters | $n = 39$ | Used in 39×39 Contradiction Matrix. |
| Technical Contradiction | $TC(X \uparrow \Rightarrow Y \downarrow)$ | Improving $X$ degrades $Y$. |
| Physical Contradiction | $PC(\text{Same param} \rightarrow A \land \bar A)$ | Parameter must be $A$ AND not-$A$. |
| Separation Principles (4) | Time, Space, Condition, System | Resolve PCs by separating opposing requirements. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Separation Principles for Physical Contradictions (Kery Abs-Cheating Sheet):**
> - *Separation in Time* — Provide property $A$ at one moment, $\bar A$ at another.
> - *Separation in Space* — Property $A$ in one region, $\bar A$ elsewhere.
> - *Separation by Condition* (or Scale) — $A$ under one operating condition, $\bar A$ otherwise.
> - *Separation within the System* — Whole system has $A$, subsystem has $\bar A$.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive safety engineering:** Principles 9, 10, 11, 19, 30, 35 directly underlie airbag, ABS, crumple-zone and pre-tensioner seatbelt design.
- **Industrial machinery guarding:** Principles 1, 2, 7, 8, 14 govern modern interlocked movable guards.
- **Consumer electronics:** Principles 3, 6, 22, 28 explain why modern phones resist drops, water, and overheating using local quality, universality, and electronic sensing.
- **Medical device design:** Principles 16, 25, 27, 30, 34 are heavily used in single-use, self-sealing, and disposable safety devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Solving a Technical Contradiction

**Problem Statement (Safety in Product Design Context):**
> A portable circular saw is to be redesigned. Currently, the blade guard is **thick steel** — it is strong and protects the user from accidental contact (good for safety), but the **added weight** makes the saw **hard to hold and maneuver**, increasing the risk of *operator-fatigue-induced loss of control* (bad for safety). The designer must resolve this contradiction.

### Step 1: Identify the Contradiction
- **Useful action (A):** Increase guard thickness → improves *user safety* (parameter: *strength of guard*).
- **Harmful side effect (B):** Increases *weight of tool* (parameter: *mass of moving object*).

This is a **Technical Contradiction** between **Strength** (improving) and **Weight** (worsening).

### Step 2: Map to the Contradiction Matrix

- *Improving parameter:* **Strength** (Parameter #14 in standard TRIZ list).
- *Worsening parameter:* **Weight of moving object** (Parameter #3).
- *Matrix cell (14, 3)* returns the suggested principles: **1, 8, 15, 40**.

### Step 3: Apply Each Suggested Principle

**Principle 1 — Segmentation**

$$
\text{Guard}_{\text{monolithic}} \;\longrightarrow\; \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \text{Guard}_{i,\text{segment}}
$$

Instead of one thick guard, the guard is *segmented* into independent hinged panels that overlap at the cutting line. Each segment is thinner, reducing overall mass while coverage is preserved.

**Principle 8 — Counterweight**

$$
F_{\text{eff, lift}} = m_{\text{guard}} \cdot g - m_{\text{counter}} \cdot g
$$

A small counterweight is integrated into the upper handle so the *effective* lifting force required from the operator is reduced. The mass is not reduced, but the *perceived weight* is, and so is fatigue.

**Principle 15 — Dynamics**

$$
k_{\text{guard}}(t) = k_{0} + \Delta k \cdot \sigma(t)
$$

Use a smart-material guard (e.g., a shear-thickening fluid laminate) whose stiffness rises *dynamically* with impact velocity. The guard is soft and light normally, hard and protective during a strike. This satisfies the *time-separation* of the physical contradiction: *soft when at rest* AND *hard when impacted*.

**Principle 40 — Composite Materials**

$$
\rho_{\text{comp}} = \sum_{i} \rho_{i} \cdot v_{i}, \quad
\sigma_{\text{comp}} \approx \sigma_{\text{matrix}} + f \cdot \sigma_{\text{fiber}}
$$

Replace uniform steel with a **CFRP (carbon-fibre reinforced polymer) skin + foam core** sandwich. Mass reduces by **~60 %** while specific strength (strength-to-weight) doubles or triples. Corrosion resistance also improves.

### Step 4: Combine the Principles (TRIZ best practice)

The optimal solution combines **Principle 1 (Segmentation) + Principle 15 (Dynamics) + Principle 40 (Composite Materials)**:

1. **Segmented** hinged guard → reduces material mass.
2. Made of **composite sandwich** → further reduces mass and improves strength-to-weight.
3. Equipped with **dynamic shear-thickening liner** → becomes rigid on impact only.

### Step 5: Evaluate the Solution

- *Improved parameter (Strength):* Maintained or increased.
- *Worsened parameter (Weight):* Significantly reduced.
- *New beneficial side effects:* Reduced operator fatigue, lower inertia, anti-corrosion, lower cost per unit safety.
- *New harmful side effects to check:* Cost of CFRP, manufacturing complexity, recycling. (Apply Principle 34 — *Discarding and Recovering* — to handle end-of-life.)

The contradiction is **resolved**, not compromised.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Most-Used Principles in Safety-Design

Below is a cross-mapping frequently tested in KTU exams:

| Safety Hazard | Worsening Parameter (Standard TRIZ) | Recommended Principles |
|---------------|-------------------------------------|------------------------|
| Sharp blade cuts user | Reliability ↔ Sharpness | 17, 2, 28, 13 |
| Heavy guard causes fatigue | Strength ↔ Weight | 1, 8, 15, 40 |
| Hot surface burns user | Temperature ↔ Operability | 22, 35, 12, 24 |
| Fast-moving part impacts user | Speed ↔ Safety | 19, 21, 11, 16 |
| High-pressure vessel explodes | Strength ↔ Pressure | 9, 10, 11, 36 |
| Slippery floor | Reliability ↔ Friction | 4, 14, 19, 33 |
| Toxic gas leak | Harmful substance ↔ Sealing | 2, 22, 24, 39 |
| Electrical shock | Energy ↔ Conductivity | 12, 24, 28, 39 |
| Noise-induced hearing loss | Energy ↔ User comfort | 22, 25, 28, 31 |
| Fire propagation | Temperature ↔ Material | 30, 31, 36, 39 |

## 3.3 Mathematical Derivation of Ideality

The Ideality Formula provides the *quantitative goal* of every TRIZ-based design. We start from the engineering definition of ideality:

$$
I = \frac{\sum B}{\sum H + C}
$$

Where:
- $B$ = sum of all *benefits* delivered by the system.
- $H$ = sum of all *harms* (risks, side effects).
- $C$ = total *cost* (manufacturing, maintenance, lifecycle).

**Derivation step-by-step:**

**Step 1.** Express $B$, $H$, $C$ in the same unit space (normalized "utility" or "safety index"):

$$
B = \sum_{i} b_{i} \cdot w_{i}, \quad
H = \sum_{j} h_{j} \cdot w_{j}, \quad
C = \sum_{k} c_{k}
$$

where $w$ are weighting factors (e.g., probability $\times$ severity in safety).

**Step 2.** Ideal state: $H \to 0$ and $C \to 0$:

$$
\lim_{H \to 0, \, C \to 0} I = \lim_{H \to 0,\, C \to 0} \frac{\sum B}{\sum H + C} = \infty
$$

**Step 3.** Engineering interpretation: a perfectly ideal safety system has *all benefits* and *no harm or cost*. The goal of using the 40 Principles is to push $I$ towards infinity by reducing $H$ (e.g., applying Principle 21 to *rush through* hazardous steps) and reducing $C$ (Principle 27, *cheap short-living objects*) while preserving $B$ (Principle 6, *universality*).

**Step 4.** The TRIZ rule of **"No Compromise"** then requires:

$$
\Delta B \geq 0 \quad \text{AND} \quad \Delta H \leq 0
$$

i.e., the net change in benefits must be non-negative and the net change in harms non-positive — a *simultaneous improvement*, the very definition of resolving (not trading off) a contradiction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Contradiction-Resolution Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Problem Identified] --> B{Is there a conflict<br/>between parameters?}
    B -- No --> C[Apply Standard Engineering Solution]
    B -- Yes --> D{Is the conflict between<br/>TWO different parameters?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Technical Contradiction TC]
    D -- No, same parameter<br/>requires opposite states --> F[Physical Contradiction PC]
    E --> G[Locate in 39x39 Contradiction Matrix<br/>Improving vs Worsening Parameter]
    F --> H[Apply 4 Separation Principles<br/>Time, Space, Condition, System]
    G --> I[Suggested Inventive Principles<br/>2 to 4 numbers]
    H --> J[Select Inventive Principles<br/>from the 40]
    I --> K[Combine 2 to 3 Principles<br/>for stronger solution]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Generate Concept Variants]
    L --> M[Evaluate Ideality Index I]
    M --> N{I greater than<br/>baseline system?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Adopt Inventive Solution]
    N -- No --> P[Iterate: Re-examine Separation<br/>or select different principles]
    P --> K
```

## 4.2 The 40 Principles — Grouped Cognitive Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[40 Inventive Principles] --> G1[Separation Group<br/>1 to 7]
    P --> G2[Counteraction Group<br/>8 to 15]
    P --> G3[Modulation of Action Group<br/>16 to 24]
    P --> G4[Self-Service and Substitution<br/>25 to 32]
    P --> G5[Material and State Transformation<br/>33 to 40]

    G1 --> P1[1 Segmentation]
    G1 --> P2[2 Taking Out]
    G1 --> P3[3 Local Quality]
    G1 --> P4[4 Asymmetry]
    G1 --> P5[5 Merging]
    G1 --> P6[6 Universality]
    G1 --> P7[7 Nested Doll]

    G2 --> P8[8 Counterweight]
    G2 --> P9[9 Preliminary Anti-Action]
    G2 --> P10[10 Preliminary Action]
    G2 --> P11[11 Cushioning]
    G2 --> P12[12 Equipotentiality]
    G2 --> P13[13 Inversion]
    G2 --> P14[14 Curvature]
    G2 --> P15[15 Dynamics]

    G3 --> P16[16 Partial or Excessive]
    G3 --> P17[17 Another Dimension]
    G3 --> P18[18 Vibration]
    G3 --> P19[19 Periodic Action]
    G3 --> P20[20 Continuity]
    G3 --> P21[21 Rushing Through]
    G3 --> P22[22 Convert Harm to Benefit]
    G3 --> P23[23 Feedback]
    G3 --> P24[24 Intermediary]

    G4 --> P25[25 Self-Service]
    G4 --> P26[26 Copying]
    G4 --> P27[27 Cheap Short-Living]
    G4 --> P28[28 Mechanics Substitution]
    G4 --> P29[29 Pneumatics and Hydraulics]
    G4 --> P30[30 Flexible Shells]
    G4 --> P31[31 Porous Materials]
    G4 --> P32[32 Color Changes]

    G5 --> P33[33 Homogeneity]
    G5 --> P34[34 Discarding and Recovering]
    G5 --> P35[35 Parameter Changes]
    G5 --> P36[36 Phase Transitions]
    G5 --> P37[37 Thermal Expansion]
    G5 --> P38[38 Accelerated Oxidation]
    G5 --> P39[39 Inert Environment]
    G5 --> P40[40 Composite Materials]
```

## 4.3 Su-Field Model — Block Architecture for Safety-Conflict Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Substance 1<br/>Hazards Source] -->|Action| F[Field<br/>Energy or Force]
    F -->|Acts on| S2[Substance 2<br/>User or Product]
    S2 -.->|Harmful effect| H[Injury or Loss]
    H -.->|Feedback signal| S2
    style S1 fill:#ffe5e5
    style F fill:#fff4cc
    style S2 fill:#e5f0ff
    style H fill:#ffcccc
```

**Reading the diagram:** A **Hazards Source (S₁)** transmits a dangerous action via a **Field (F)** to a **Substance (S₂)**, causing **Injury or Loss (H)**. The 40 Principles are then applied to *break the S₁–F–S₂ chain* by modifying any one of the three elements: introduce a counter-field, substitute the field, segment the substance, or insert an intermediary.

## 4.4 Application Algorithm Block Diagram (For Exam Answers)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define the Problem] --> B[Step 2: Identify Improving<br/>and Worsening Parameters]
    B --> C[Step 3: Search 39x39 Matrix]
    C --> D[Step 4: List Suggested Principles]
    D --> E[Step 5: Translate Principles into<br/>Domain Specific Solutions]
    E --> F[Step 6: Combine Solutions]
    F --> G[Step 7: Evaluate against<br/>Ideality I and Su-Field]
    G --> H{All 9 windows<br/>of Su-Field satisfied?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Accept Solution]
    H -- No --> J[Return to Step 4<br/>Try alternate principles]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question Set]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Define "Technical Contradiction" and "Physical Contradiction" in TRIZ. Give one product-safety example of each.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Technical Contradiction (TC)** is a conflict where improving one engineering parameter (e.g., *strength of a guard*) causes another parameter (e.g., *weight of the tool*) to worsen. *Example:* A thicker glass panel is more break-resistant but heavier, increasing the risk of a glass-related injury during installation.

A **Physical Contradiction (PC)** is a conflict where the same parameter must simultaneously satisfy two opposite requirements. *Example:* A fire-door must be *closed* to block fire spread, yet *open* to allow emergency evacuation and pressure equalization.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**List the 4 separation principles used to resolve physical contradictions. Explain "Separation in Space" with an example.**

**Model Answer:**
The 4 separation principles are: **(1) Separation in Time, (2) Separation in Space, (3) Separation by Condition, (4) Separation within the System**.

*Separation in Space* provides opposite properties to *different parts* of the same object. *Example:* A safety helmet has a *hard outer shell* (for impact resistance) and a *soft inner foam liner* (for energy absorption). The "hard" and "soft" requirements are not asked of the same region but of different spatial regions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model End-Semester Question]**

### Question B-A (14 Marks)

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Explain the 5-step TRIZ algorithm to resolve a technical contradiction in product design, with a clear block diagram.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A workshop bench grinder is being redesigned. The current guard is a flat steel plate that fully encloses the grinding wheel (good for safety), but the plate blocks the operator's view of the workpiece (bad for precision and safe handling). Apply **any 4** of the 40 Inventive Principles to propose inventive design solutions that resolve this contradiction.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) The 5-step TRIZ Algorithm:**

1. **Problem Identification** — Recognize that the engineering task contains a contradiction, *not* a trade-off. Refuse to accept the compromise.
2. **Contradiction Classification** — Determine whether the problem is a Technical Contradiction (TC) or a Physical Contradiction (PC).
3. **Mapping to the Contradiction Matrix** — Convert the TC into the standard 39-parameter form; look up the row × column intersection in Altshuller's 39×39 matrix.
4. **Application of the 40 Principles** — Read out the 2–4 suggested principles and creatively translate them into the specific product domain.
5. **Evaluation via Ideality and Su-Field** — Check the proposed solution against the Ideality formula and the 9 windows of the Su-Field model; iterate if the chain is incomplete.

*(Block diagram from Section 4.4 may be reproduced here.)*

**(b) Application of 4 Principles:**

- **Principle 1 — Segmentation:** Replace the single full disc-guard with **segmented curved petals** (like a camera iris) that overlap and collectively cover the wheel but leave a *window* aligned with the operator's line of sight. Coverage is preserved, view is restored.

- **Principle 3 — Local Quality:** Use a **transparent polycarbonate section** only at the operator-facing line of sight, with opaque steel elsewhere. Local optical transparency, local mechanical strength.

- **Principle 22 — Convert Harm to Benefit:** Use the **flying sparks and dust** themselves as a *signal* to trigger a small vacuum-extraction nozzle built into the guard (Principle 2 — *Taking Out*). The hazard drives the solution.

- **Principle 28 — Substitute Mechanical with Sensory:** Replace the rigid mechanical eye-shield with a **proximity sensor + automatic shutter** that physically closes the guard only when the operator's hand is detected within the hazard zone. *No conflict* between visibility and safety.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Define the **Contradiction Matrix** and the **Ideality Index**. Explain their role in the 40-Principles method.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A lithium-ion battery pack for a power tool must be **rigid** (to prevent internal short-circuit from swelling) and **flexible** (to survive drops and vibration). State the type of contradiction, list 4 suitable inventive principles, and propose one design solution.

**Model Answer:**

**(a)** The **Contradiction Matrix** (39 × 39) is a tabular tool where rows and columns represent the 39 standard *Engineering Parameters* of TRIZ; the cell at row *i*, column *j* lists 2–4 of the 40 Inventive Principles that statistically most often resolved the *i vs j* conflict in real patents. It acts as a *fast lookup* for the designer.

The **Ideality Index** $I$ is given by:

$$
I = \frac{\sum B}{\sum H + C}
$$

where $B$ = benefits, $H$ = harms, $C$ = cost. The 40 Principles aim to *maximize* $I$ by simultaneously increasing $B$ (e.g., Principle 6 *Universality*) and reducing $H$ (e.g., Principle 21 *Rushing Through* hazardous steps) and $C$ (Principle 27 *Cheap short-living objects*).

**(b)** The battery pack requirement — *rigid AND flexible* on the same parameter (deformability) — is a **Physical Contradiction** because the *same* parameter (mechanical stiffness) must take two opposite values.

**Suitable Principles:**
- *Principle 1 — Segmentation:* Divide the pack into small individual cells so localized swelling is contained.
- *Principle 7 — Nested Doll:* Place a rigid core (BMS board) inside a flexible elastomeric outer shell.
- *Principle 15 — Dynamics:* Use a **shear-thickening polymer** skin that is flexible normally but rigid on impact.
- *Principle 17 — Another Dimension:* Use a 3-D lattice core (e.g., honeycomb) that is geometrically compliant in one plane and rigid in another.

**Proposed Design:** A **hexagonal honeycomb aluminium core** with each cell housing a 18650 Li-ion cell, the whole assembly potted in a shear-thickening polymer (STP) elastomer. The honeycomb provides *local rigidity* (resists cell swelling) while the STP and flexible inter-cell walls provide *global flexibility* (absorbs drop impact). This resolves the physical contradiction by *separation in space* (rigid core, flexible shell) and *separation by condition* (rigid under slow loading, flexible under fast impact).

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing TC with PC.** A TC is between *two different* parameters; a PC is *one parameter, two opposite states*. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this mix-up.
> 2. **Listing principles without applying them.** Writing "Principle 1, 2, 3" is not an answer. KTU requires the *design translation* of each principle into the specific problem context.
> 3. **Accepting trade-offs.** The whole point of the 40 Principles is *no compromise*. A solution that says "we will reduce the safety by 10 % to gain weight" is *not* a TRIZ solution and is marked down.
> 4. **Skipping the 39×39 matrix lookup.** When the question says "locate in matrix", you must show the row, column and cell entry. Vague answers score zero.
> 5. **Not combining principles.** A single principle rarely produces a robust solution. Examiners reward combinations of 2–3 principles.
> 6. **Forgetting the Ideality check.** The final step — computing or commenting on $I = \sum B / (\sum H + C)$ — is a frequent 2-mark differentiator.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2:**

- A **Technical Contradiction** is a conflict between *two different* engineering parameters.
- A **Physical Contradiction** is a conflict where *one* parameter must satisfy *opposite* requirements.
- The **40 Inventive Principles** are heuristic solution strategies derived from the analysis of 200,000+ patents by Genrich Altshuller.
- The **39 × 39 Contradiction Matrix** is the navigation map: rows = parameter to *improve*, columns = parameter being *worsened*.
- The **4 Separation Principles** (Time, Space, Condition, System) are the master-tools to dissolve *Physical* contradictions.
- The **Ideality Index** $I = \sum B / (\sum H + C)$ must be maximized; the goal is *no compromise* (simultaneous improvement of $B$ and $H$).
- The **Su-Field Model** ($S_1 \to S_2$ via $F$) provides a structural view of the conflict; the 9 windows enumerate all valid transformations.
- In **safety design**, the most-used principles are: **1, 2, 3, 9, 10, 11, 15, 19, 21, 22, 24, 28, 30, 35, 40**.
- The Principles are grouped into 5 cognitive families: *Separation*, *Counteraction*, *Modulation*, *Self-Service & Substitution*, *Material/State Transformation*.
- Always **combine 2–3 principles** for a robust inventive solution — KTU rewards this explicitly.
- The TRIZ workflow is: **Identify → Classify → Map → Apply → Evaluate**.

> **End of Notes — Module 2: Use of 40 Principles to Solve Contradiction**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of S-Curves and Technology Evolution Trends
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT747 - SAFETY IN PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Use of S-Curves and Technology Evolution Trends

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **S-Curve (Technology S-Curve):** A graphical representation that depicts the cumulative improvement in a technology's performance (or cost-effectiveness) plotted against the cumulative investment of resources (time, money, or R&D effort). The curve takes the characteristic flattened "S" shape, capturing the three distinct phases of a technology's evolutionary lifecycle: the **Emerging Phase** (slow initial progress), the **Growth Phase** (rapid, near-linear improvement), and the **Maturity Phase** (diminishing returns leading to saturation).

In the context of **Safety in Product Design**, the S-curve is a strategic forecasting instrument used to visualize the maturity of a specific safety technology (e.g., airbag systems, ABS braking, crash sensors, fail-safe electronics). It allows design engineers and product managers to anticipate the performance ceiling of a current safety architecture, identify the optimal moment to initiate research into a disruptive replacement technology, and strategically plan product retirement or redesign cycles to ensure continuous safety improvement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Technology Evolution Trend:** A directional pattern of innovation, substitution, and improvement that dictates how a particular class of safety technology changes across successive generations. These trends are typically mapped as a sequence of overlapping S-curves, where the saturation of one curve triggers the emergence of the next.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **learning to ride a bicycle**.

- **Day 1 (Emerging Phase):** You wobble, fall, and progress is painfully slow. A huge amount of effort (X-axis) yields very tiny improvement in balance (Y-axis). The curve is nearly flat.
- **Week 2 (Growth Phase):** Something suddenly "clicks." Each hour of practice now dramatically boosts your skill. You start riding smoothly, taking corners, and balancing without thinking. The curve rises steeply.
- **Year 1 (Maturity Phase):** You are an expert. Practicing another 1000 hours will only make you a *fraction* of a percent better. The improvements have plateaued, no matter how much you train. The curve flattens at the top.

> **The exact same pattern governs safety technologies.** Early airbag systems were unreliable, bulky, and slow to deploy. Then a period of explosive innovation made them fast, compact, and universal. Now, incremental improvements yield only marginal safety gains — pushing engineers to invent the next curve (smart airbag systems with occupant detection).

## 1.3 The Three Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Any S-curve analysis in product safety design is governed by three explicit metrics:
> - **Performance Metric (Y-axis):** Safety effectiveness, reliability, or capability (e.g., crash survival rate %, sensor response time in **milliseconds**, probability of failure on demand).
> - **Investment Metric (X-axis):** Cumulative R&D expenditure, engineering man-hours, or calendar time in years.
> - **Saturation Limit (Asymptote):** The theoretical physical or engineering ceiling beyond which the technology cannot improve (e.g., the speed of light for sensors, the physiological tolerance limit of the human body for crash forces).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Phases of the Technology S-Curve

The S-curve is not arbitrary; it is a mathematical consequence of compounding learning and physical limits. Each phase has a distinct behavioral signature that drives safety design decisions.

### Phase 1: The Emerging Phase (Embryonic / Slow Start)
- **What happens:** Basic scientific principles are translated into a first-generation working prototype. Many fundamental problems are unsolved. Performance improves very slowly despite heavy investment.
- **Safety Design Implication:** Products in this phase carry **high inherent risk**. The technology is unproven in field conditions. Designers must rely on **redundancy, fail-safe defaults, and conservative operational envelopes** to compensate for unknown unknowns.
- **Typical Indicators:** High warranty claims, frequent recalls, fragmented standards, low market adoption.
- **Mathematical Characteristic:** The curve is concave-up; the second derivative of performance with respect to effort is **positive** (acceleration phase).

### Phase 2: The Growth Phase (Rapid Improvement)
- **What happens:** Core engineering problems have been solved. Engineers now optimize components, integrate subsystems, and refine manufacturing. Each unit of R&D investment produces a large, predictable gain in safety performance.
- **Safety Design Implication:** This is the **golden window for product launch and market capture**. Safety features are reliable, certified, and cost-effective to mass-produce. Products achieve regulatory compliance with margin.
- **Typical Indicators:** Rapid reduction in failure rates, standards become harmonized, market explodes.
- **Mathematical Characteristic:** The curve is approximately **linear**; the second derivative is near zero (constant slope).

### Phase 3: The Maturity Phase (Saturation / Diminishing Returns)
- **What happens:** The technology approaches its physical, material, or economic limit. Massive R&D investment produces only tiny incremental improvements. The industry becomes focused on cost reduction rather than performance enhancement.
- **Safety Design Implication:** The technology is **stable but stagnant**. It is highly reliable but cannot meet new, more stringent safety demands (e.g., Vision Zero targets, zero-fatality road goals). Designers must scout for **disruptive replacement technologies** that will initiate the next S-curve.
- **Typical Indicators:** Mergers, commoditization, declining R&D budgets for this technology, emergence of patent expirations.
- **Mathematical Characteristic:** The curve is concave-down, approaching a horizontal asymptote; the second derivative is **negative** (deceleration phase).

> [!NOTE]
> **Fisher-Pry Substitution Model:** A widely accepted mathematical model stating that the fraction $f$ of the market that has adopted a new technology at time $t$ follows the logistic differential equation: $\frac{df}{dt} = r \cdot f \cdot (1 - f)$, where $r$ is the intrinsic rate of substitution.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application in Safety Design |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **General Logistic S-Curve (Performance)** | $P(t) = \dfrac{L}{1 + e^{-k(t - t_0)}}$ | $P(t)$: Performance at time $t$; $L$: Saturation limit; $k$: Growth rate constant; $t_0$: Inflection point time | Performance in % or absolute units; $t$ in years | Predicts the safety performance ceiling of a mature technology (e.g., max reliable crash detection accuracy). |
| **Fisher-Pry Substitution Fraction** | $f(t) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-b(t - t_c)}}$ | $f(t)$: Market fraction of new technology; $b$: Coefficient of substitution; $t_c$: Time of 50% substitution | Dimensionless fraction; $t$ in years | Forecasts market adoption of new safety features (e.g., AEB — Automatic Emergency Braking replacing conventional brakes). |
| **Gompertz Curve (Asymmetric S-Curve)** | $P(t) = L \cdot e^{-b \cdot e^{-c t}}$ | $P(t)$: Performance; $L$: Asymptote; $b$: Displacement along time axis; $c$: Growth rate | Performance units; $t$ in years | Used when the emerging phase is significantly longer than the maturity phase (common in pharma/biotech safety). |
| **Diminishing Returns Rate** | $\dfrac{dP}{dI} \to 0 \text{ as } I \to \infty$ | $P$: Performance; $I$: Cumulative R&D investment | Performance per currency unit | Identifies the precise moment to **terminate** investment in the old safety tech and pivot to the new one. |
| **Technology Readiness Level (TRL)** | $TRL \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9\}$ | Discrete scale 1 (basic principle) to 9 (proven in operational environment) | Integer level | Maps directly onto the S-curve phases: TRL 1-3 = Emerging; TRL 4-7 = Growth; TRL 8-9 = Maturity. |
| **Bayesian Safety Update** | $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$ | $P(H \mid E)$: Posterior hazard probability; $P(H)$: Prior; $P(E \mid H)$: Likelihood | Probability (0-1) | Updates safety risk estimates as the technology climbs the S-curve and new failure data emerges. |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern safety-critical industries, S-curve analysis is non-negotiable. The **automotive industry** uses overlapping S-curves to plan transitions from mechanical seatbelts (1950s curve) to airbags (1990s curve) to autonomous emergency braking (2020s curve). The **aviation industry** tracks engine efficiency S-curves (piston → turbojet → turbofan → geared turbofan) to time fleet renewals. The **medical device industry** uses S-curves to forecast when implantable defibrillators will reach battery-life ceilings, triggering investment in next-generation energy-harvesting designs. Without S-curve awareness, organizations over-invest in saturating technologies and miss the window to enter the next growth phase.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Logistic S-Curve Equation

The S-curve is mathematically derived from the assumption that the **rate of performance improvement is jointly proportional to the current performance level AND the remaining headroom to the saturation limit**. This is the principle of bounded growth.

### Step 1: State the Differential Assumption
Assume that the instantaneous rate of performance improvement $\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ is proportional to both the current performance $P(t)$ and the unrealized potential $(L - P(t))$, where $L$ is the saturation ceiling.

$$\dfrac{dP}{dt} = k \cdot P(t) \cdot [L - P(t)]$$

### Step 2: Separate the Variables
Rewrite the equation by dividing both sides by $P(t) \cdot [L - P(t)]$ to isolate the differentials on the left.

$$\dfrac{1}{P(L - P)} \, dP = k \, dt$$

### Step 3: Apply Partial Fraction Decomposition
Decompose the left-hand side fraction into a sum of two simpler fractions. Let $\dfrac{1}{P(L - P)} = \dfrac{A}{P} + \dfrac{B}{L - P}$.

Multiplying both sides by $P(L-P)$ gives:
$$1 = A(L - P) + B \cdot P$$

Setting $P = 0$ yields $A = \dfrac{1}{L}$. Setting $P = L$ yields $B = \dfrac{1}{L}$.

Therefore:
$$\dfrac{1}{P(L - P)} = \dfrac{1}{L}\left(\dfrac{1}{P} + \dfrac{1}{L - P}\right)$$

### Step 4: Integrate Both Sides
Integrate from initial time $0$ (where $P(0) = P_0$) to general time $t$ (where $P(t) = P$).

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} \dfrac{1}{L}\left(\dfrac{1}{P'} + \dfrac{1}{L - P'}\right) dP' = \int_{0}^{t} k \, dt'$$

### Step 5: Evaluate the Left Integral
The integral evaluates to:
$$\dfrac{1}{L} \left[ \ln(P') - \ln(L - P') \right]_{P_0}^{P} = kt$$

$$\dfrac{1}{L} \ln\left(\dfrac{P}{L - P}\right) - \dfrac{1}{L} \ln\left(\dfrac{P_0}{L - P_0}\right) = kt$$

### Step 6: Exponentiate to Remove Logarithms
Multiply through by $L$ and exponentiate both sides.

$$\dfrac{P}{L - P} = \dfrac{P_0}{L - P_0} \cdot e^{kLt}$$

### Step 7: Solve Algebraically for $P(t)$
Let $C = \dfrac{P_0}{L - P_0} \cdot e^{kLt}$. Then $P = C(L - P) = CL - CP$, so $P(1 + C) = CL$.

$$P(t) = \dfrac{CL}{1 + C} = \dfrac{L \cdot \dfrac{P_0}{L - P_0} \cdot e^{kLt}}{1 + \dfrac{P_0}{L - P_0} \cdot e^{kLt}}$$

### Step 8: Introduce the Inflection Point Shift $t_0$
Define the inflection point as the time at which $P(t_0) = L/2$. Substituting $P = L/2$ into the original logistic form gives $t_0 = \dfrac{1}{kL}\ln\left(\dfrac{L - P_0}{P_0}\right)$. The final clean S-curve equation is:

$$P(t) = \dfrac{L}{1 + e^{-k(t - t_0)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The parameter $t_0$ marks the exact **midpoint of the growth phase**, the most productive year of the technology's life. Resources allocated at $t_0$ deliver the maximum safety improvement per dollar spent. The parameter $k$ controls steepness; a higher $k$ means a more aggressive, faster-evolving technology.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: S-Curve Phase × Safety Design Action

| Design Question | Emerging Phase | Growth Phase | Maturity Phase |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Should we launch this product?** | NO — too risky, unproven | YES — optimal window | LAUNCH AS NICHE/REPLACEMENT ONLY |
| **Investment in further R&D?** | Heavy — high risk, high reward | Moderate — refinement focused | Minimal — diminishing returns |
| **Regulatory strategy** | Shape the upcoming standard | Comply with margin | Prepare for next-generation mandates |
| **Safety architecture** | Heavy redundancy, conservative limits | Optimized redundancy | Reliability engineering, FMEA focus |
| **Next-gen scouting** | **Begin immediately** | Continue monitoring | **Initiate transition project** |
| **Likely failure mode** | Systematic design flaws | Integration mismatches | Wear-out, obsolescence, novel demands |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Adoption of ABS in Passenger Cars

> **Problem Context:** The Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) for passenger cars was first introduced in 1971. It exhibited an S-curve adoption pattern. The Fisher-Pry substitution model applies: $f(t) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-b(t - t_c)}}$. Given that the substitution coefficient $b = 0.18 \text{ year}^{-1}$ and the time of 50% market adoption $t_c = 1995$, calculate the predicted market adoption fraction in the year **2005**.

### Step 1: Identify Parameters
We are given $b = 0.18$, $t_c = 1995$, and the target year is $t = 2005$.

### Step 2: Calculate the Time Delta
$$\Delta t = t - t_c = 2005 - 1995 = 10 \text{ years}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Exponent
$$-b \cdot \Delta t = -(0.18)(10) = -1.8$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the Exponential
$$e^{-1.8} \approx 0.1653$$

### Step 5: Substitute into the Fisher-Pry Equation
$$f(2005) = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.1653} = \dfrac{1}{1.1653}$$

### Step 6: Final Computation
$$f(2005) \approx 0.858 \text{ or } 85.8\%$$

> **Interpretation for Safety Design:** By 2005, approximately **85.8% of new passenger cars globally had adopted ABS technology**, confirming that ABS had crossed from the growth phase into the maturity phase. Safety strategists at that time correctly identified the next S-curve: **Electronic Stability Control (ESC)**, which began its own emerging phase around 2005-2010.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Canonical Technology S-Curve (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagrams the three phases, the dominant design activity, the safety posture, and the strategic decision trigger in each region of the S-curve.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    axisX[/Cumulative R and D Investment or Time/]
    axisY[/Safety Performance Level/]
    satLimit[/Saturation Limit L/]
    curve((S-Curve P of t))
    
    axisY  o--o axisX
    satLimit  o--o curve
    curve  o--o axisX
    
    phase1[Phase 1: Emerging Phase]
    phase2[Phase 2: Growth Phase]
    phase3[Phase 3: Maturity Phase]
    phase4[Phase 4: Trigger Next S-Curve]
    
    phase1 -->|Inflection Point| phase2
    phase2 -->|Diminishing Returns| phase3
    phase3 -->|Strategic Pivot| phase4
    
    act1[Activity: Basic Research, Prototyping, Standards Drafting]
    act2[Activity: Optimization, Mass Production, Certification]
    act3[Activity: Cost Reduction, Reliability Engineering, FMEA]
    act4[Activity: Disruptive Tech Scouting, R and D Reallocation]
    
    safe1[Safety Posture: Redundancy, Conservative Limits, Field Monitoring]
    safe2[Safety Posture: Optimized Reliability, Margin to Standards]
    safe3[Safety Posture: Failure Mode Focus, Obsolescence Planning]
    safe4[Safety Posture: New Hazard Class Identification]
    
    phase1 --- act1
    phase2 --- act2
    phase3 --- act3
    phase4 --- act4
    
    phase1 --- safe1
    phase2 --- safe2
    phase3 --- safe3
    phase4 --- safe4
```

## 4.2 Multi-Curve Technology Evolution Roadmap (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block illustrates the classic concept of **overlapping S-curves** representing three successive generations of a safety technology. This is the most important strategic diagram in technology evolution planning.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph gen1[Generation 1: Mechanical Safety Systems]
        g1e[Emerging: 1950s] --> g1g[Growth: 1960s] --> g1m[Maturity: 1970s]
    end
    
    subgraph gen2[Generation 2: Electronic Safety Systems]
        g2e[Emerging: 1970s] --> g2g[Growth: 1980s] --> g2m[Maturity: 1990s]
    end
    
    subgraph gen3[Generation 3: Smart Sensor and AI Safety Systems]
        g3e[Emerging: 1990s] --> g3g[Growth: 2000s] --> g3m[Maturity: 2010s]
    end
    
    subgraph gen4[Generation 4: Autonomous Safety Systems]
        g4e[Emerging: 2010s] --> g4g[Growth: 2020s] --> g4m[Maturity: 2030s Forecast]
    end
    
    g1m -->|Saturation Trigger| g2e
    g2m -->|Saturation Trigger| g3e
    g3m -->|Saturation Trigger| g4e
    
    strat1[Strategic Action: Reallocate Resources to Next Generation]
    strat2[Strategic Action: Capture Market Leadership]
    strat3[Strategic Action: Initiate Disruptive R and D]
    
    g1m  o--o strat1
    g2m  o--o strat2
    g3m  o--o strat3
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart: When to Pivot to the Next S-Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Annual Safety Technology Review])
    q1{Is current tech in Maturity phase?}
    q2{Diminishing returns exceeding 50 percent?}
    q3{Next gen tech TRL greater than 4?}
    q4{Regulatory mandate horizon within 5 years?}
    
    a1[Continue cost optimization and reliability focus]
    a2[Maintain current investment and begin scouting]
    a3[Launch pilot R and D for next generation]
    a4[Full strategic pivot: reallocate 60 percent of budget to next gen]
    a5[Urgent pivot: mandatory compliance timeline at risk]
    
    start --> q1
    q1 -->|No| a1
    q1 -->|Yes| q2
    q2 -->|No| a2
    q2 -->|Yes| q3
    q3 -->|No| a2
    q3 -->|Yes| q4
    q4 -->|No| a3
    q4 -->|Yes| a4
    a4 --> a5
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Concept Definition
**Q: Define the term "Technology S-Curve" as applied to safety in product design. Name its three phases.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The Technology S-Curve is a graphical model that plots the cumulative improvement in a technology's safety performance against the cumulative investment of R&D resources or time. It captures the typical lifecycle of a safety technology from inception to saturation. The three phases are:
1. **Emerging Phase (Slow Start):** Initial prototypes, fundamental engineering challenges, slow performance gains despite high effort.
2. **Growth Phase (Rapid Improvement):** Optimization, mass production, standards harmonization, near-linear performance gains.
3. **Maturity Phase (Diminishing Returns / Saturation):** Performance approaches the physical or economic limit, large investments yield only marginal safety improvements.

### Question 2: Strategic Application
**Q: Why is it important for a product safety manager to recognize when a safety technology has entered its maturity phase?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Recognizing the maturity phase is critical because: (i) further R&D investment yields negligible safety improvements (diminishing returns), making continued investment economically wasteful; (ii) the existing technology will soon fail to meet emerging, more stringent regulatory or customer safety demands; and (iii) the manager must initiate scouting and pilot R&D for the next-generation technology so that the new S-curve reaches its growth phase precisely when the current one fully saturates. Failure to do so creates a **strategic safety gap** where neither the old nor the new technology is adequate.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neatly labeled diagram, explain the three phases of a Technology S-Curve. Discuss the safety design implications and dominant engineering activities in each phase. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

The Technology S-Curve is plotted with **cumulative R&D investment (or time) on the X-axis** and **safety performance level on the Y-axis**, approaching a horizontal asymptote $L$ (the saturation limit).

**Phase 1 — Emerging Phase:**
- The curve is concave-up (positive second derivative).
- Performance improves very slowly with effort.
- Dominant activities: Basic scientific research, proof-of-concept prototyping, drafting of new safety standards, addressing fundamental physics/chemistry/electronics challenges.
- *Safety Design Implication:* Products carry high inherent risk; designers must implement **heavy redundancy, conservative operational envelopes, fail-safe defaults, and rigorous field monitoring**. TRL levels 1-3.

**Phase 2 — Growth Phase:**
- The curve is approximately linear, with steep positive slope.
- Each unit of R&D investment delivers a large, predictable safety improvement.
- Dominant activities: Component optimization, subsystem integration, manufacturing scale-up, harmonization with global safety standards, certification.
- *Safety Design Implication:* This is the **optimal market launch window**. Safety features are reliable, cost-effective, and compliant with margin. TRL levels 4-7.

**Phase 3 — Maturity Phase:**
- The curve is concave-down (negative second derivative), flattening toward the asymptote $L$.
- Performance approaches the physical, economic, or biological ceiling.
- Dominant activities: Cost reduction, reliability engineering, FMEA, field failure analysis, incremental refinement, patent management.
- *Safety Design Implication:* Technology is **stable but stagnant**; cannot meet newer, more demanding safety mandates (e.g., Vision Zero). Designers must **initiate scouting and R&D for the next-generation technology** to begin a new S-curve. TRL levels 8-9.

A labeled S-curve diagram showing the three phases, the inflection point at $t_0$, and the saturation asymptote $L$ must accompany this answer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A new automotive safety technology follows a logistic S-curve $P(t) = \dfrac{L}{1 + e^{-k(t - t_0)}}$ with $L = 100\%$, $k = 0.25 \text{ year}^{-1}$, and $t_0 = 2010$. Calculate: (i) the performance level in 2005, (ii) the performance level in 2020, and (iii) the year in which the technology reaches 90% of its saturation limit. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i): Performance in 2005**
Substitute $t = 2005$, $L = 100$, $k = 0.25$, $t_0 = 2010$:

$$P(2005) = \dfrac{100}{1 + e^{-0.25(2005 - 2010)}}$$

Compute the exponent:
$$-0.25 \times (-5) = 1.25$$

Compute the exponential:
$$e^{1.25} \approx 3.4903$$

Therefore:
$$P(2005) = \dfrac{100}{1 + 3.4903} = \dfrac{100}{4.4903} \approx 22.27\%$$

**Part (ii): Performance in 2020**
Substitute $t = 2020$:

$$P(2020) = \dfrac{100}{1 + e^{-0.25(2020 - 2010)}}$$

Compute the exponent:
$$-0.25 \times 10 = -2.5$$

Compute the exponential:
$$e^{-2.5} \approx 0.0821$$

Therefore:
$$P(2020) = \dfrac{100}{1 + 0.0821} = \dfrac{100}{1.0821} \approx 92.41\%$$

**Part (iii): Year when performance reaches 90%**
Set $P(t) = 90$ and solve for $t$:

$$90 = \dfrac{100}{1 + e^{-0.25(t - 2010)}}$$

Rearrange:
$$1 + e^{-0.25(t - 2010)} = \dfrac{100}{90} = 1.1111$$

$$e^{-0.25(t - 2010)} = 0.1111$$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:
$$-0.25(t - 2010) = \ln(0.1111) \approx -2.1972$$

$$t - 2010 = \dfrac{-2.1972}{-0.25} = 8.789$$

$$t \approx 2018.79 \approx 2019$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students **forget to convert percentage to a number** (use $L = 100$ directly, not $L = 1$).
> - Common algebraic mistake: **sign error when isolating the exponential** in Part (iii). Always move the term $(1 + e^{-x})$ to the other side **before** subtracting 1.
> - Part (i) often catches students off-guard because the year 2005 is **before** the inflection point $t_0 = 2010$, yielding a result less than 50%. This is the *emerging phase*, not an error.
> - Always **state units** in the final answer (here: percent, year) to claim the unit mark.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of overlapping S-curves in technology evolution. Why is it strategically important for a safety-focused company to manage multiple S-curves simultaneously? Provide one industry example. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Overlapping S-Curves:**
No single safety technology remains optimal forever. As one technology's S-curve enters its maturity phase and saturates, a **new, often disruptive, technology** begins its own emerging phase. When plotted on the same time axis, these curves **overlap**. The new curve should begin its growth phase **just before** the old curve fully saturates, ensuring continuous performance improvement without a strategic gap.

**Strategic Importance of Managing Multiple S-Curves:**
1. **Continuous Innovation Pipeline:** A company that manages only the current S-curve will stagnate the moment it saturates. Managing multiple curves ensures a **pipeline of next-generation safety features**.
2. **Resource Reallocation:** Knowing the maturity timing allows finance and R&D leaders to reallocate capital from diminishing-return projects to high-growth emerging technologies.
3. **Regulatory Anticipation:** New safety regulations typically track the new S-curve. Companies already on the growth phase of the new curve gain a **first-mover certification advantage**.
4. **Risk Diversification:** Spreading R&D across multiple S-curves hedges against any single technology being unexpectedly leapfrogged by a disruptive entrant.
5. **Talent Retention:** Engineers are motivated by working on emerging, high-growth technologies rather than mature, cost-reduction-focused ones.

**Industry Example:** In the **automotive braking industry**, the sequence is:
- Curve 1: Mechanical drum brakes (matured ~1960s)
- Curve 2: Hydraulic disc brakes (matured ~1990s)
- Curve 3: Anti-lock Braking Systems (ABS) (matured ~2010s)
- Curve 4: Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB) with radar and AI (currently in growth phase)

Leading automotive suppliers like Bosch and Continental deliberately maintain R&D budgets across all four curves, ensuring that the moment one saturates, the next is ready for production.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A new medical infusion pump safety technology exhibits the following data: Year 2020: 20% adoption; Year 2025: 50% adoption; Year 2030: 80% adoption. Using the Fisher-Pry substitution model $f(t) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-b(t - t_c)}}$, determine the values of $b$ and $t_c$, and predict the adoption fraction in the year 2035. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the midpoint**
By definition, $t_c$ is the time at which $f(t_c) = 0.5$ (50% adoption). From the given data, this occurs in the year **2025**.

$$\boxed{t_c = 2025}$$

**Step 2: Determine $b$ using a second data point**
Use the year 2030 data where $f = 0.80$:

$$0.80 = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-b(2030 - 2025)}}$$

$$1 + e^{-5b} = \dfrac{1}{0.80} = 1.25$$

$$e^{-5b} = 0.25$$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:
$$-5b = \ln(0.25) = -1.3863$$

$$b = \dfrac{1.3863}{5} = 0.2773 \text{ year}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Verify using the year 2020 data**
Substitute $t = 2020$ and $t_c = 2025$, $b = 0.2773$:

$$f(2020) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-0.2773(2020 - 2025)}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{1.3863}}$$

$$e^{1.3863} = 4.0$$

$$f(2020) = \dfrac{1}{1 + 4.0} = \dfrac{1}{5.0} = 0.20 \;\;\checkmark$$

The data is internally consistent.

**Step 4: Predict adoption in 2035**
Substitute $t = 2035$:

$$f(2035) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-0.2773(2035 - 2025)}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-2.773}}$$

$$e^{-2.773} \approx 0.0625$$

$$f(2035) = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.0625} = \dfrac{1}{1.0625} \approx 0.9412 \text{ or } 94.12\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - A common mistake is to **confuse $b$ with the time delta**; remember $b$ has units of $\text{year}^{-1}$.
> - Students often **forget the negative sign** inside the exponential; an incorrect sign flips the curve into a reverse-S (decline) shape.
> - Always **verify the model** with at least one unused data point (as done with 2020 data) to claim the verification mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for S-Curves & Technology Evolution Trends**

- **S-Curve Definition:** Plot of cumulative safety performance (Y) vs. cumulative R&D investment or time (X), bounded above by a saturation limit $L$.
- **Three Phases:** Emerging (slow start, concave-up) → Growth (rapid, near-linear) → Maturity (diminishing returns, concave-down, asymptotic to $L$).
- **Logistic Equation:** $P(t) = \dfrac{L}{1 + e^{-k(t - t_0)}}$ where $t_0$ is the inflection point (50% of $L$) and $k$ is the growth rate constant.
- **Fisher-Pry Substitution:** $f(t) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-b(t - t_c)}}$ for predicting market adoption of new safety technologies.
- **Gompertz Curve:** Asymmetric S-curve, $P(t) = L \cdot e^{-b e^{-ct}}$, used when emerging phase is much longer than maturity phase.
- **TRL Mapping:** TRL 1-3 = Emerging; TRL 4-7 = Growth; TRL 8-9 = Maturity.
- **Strategic Pivot Point:** When $\frac{dP}{dI}$ (marginal return on R&D investment) drops below a critical threshold, initiate the next S-curve.
- **Overlapping Curves:** Successive generations must overlap; the next curve's growth phase should begin as the current curve saturates, avoiding strategic gaps.
- **Safety Implications per Phase:** Emerging → redundancy & conservative limits; Growth → optimized certification; Maturity → FMEA & obsolescence planning.
- **Industry Examples:** Mechanical → Hydraulic → ABS → AEB (automotive); Piston → Turbojet → Turbofan (aviation); Standalone → Networked → AI-driven (medical devices).
- **Common KTU Pitfalls:** Sign errors in exponentials, mixing up $L$ and $k$ units, ignoring the inflection point's role, treating S-curves as linear projections.
- **Bayesian Link:** S-curve parameters can be updated as new field-failure data arrives, using $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H) P(H)}{P(E)}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept Screening and Concept Scoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT747 - SAFETY IN PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concept Screening and Concept Scoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Concept Screening — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept Screening** is the systematic process of evaluating a large set of generated product design concepts against a predefined set of **screening criteria** (must-meet requirements and qualitative benchmarks) in order to rapidly eliminate infeasible, unsafe, or non-viable concepts and reduce the concept set to a manageable number of promising candidates for further detailed analysis.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (aligned with **NEP 2020** Outcome-Based Education), Concept Screening is positioned as the **first filtering gate** in the Concept Development Phase (CDP) of the generic product development process (GPDP). It is a *coarse, qualitative-to-semi-quantitative* filter.

**Key Characteristics of Concept Screening:**
- Uses **categorical ratings** (e.g., Poor, Fair, Good, Excellent — typically on a **5-point scale**)
- Based primarily on **must-meet** (go/no-go) criteria derived from the product design specification (PDS)
- Fast, low-cost, and intended for **early-stage down-selection**
- Involves cross-functional team consensus (design, safety, manufacturing, marketing)

### 1.2 Concept Scoring — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept Scoring** is the rigorous, **quantitative** follow-up to concept screening. Surviving concepts are rated against **weighted selection criteria** (a mix of must-meet and want-to-have benchmarks). The weighted scores are summed to produce a **relative merit index** for each concept, enabling a mathematically defensible ranking.

Concept Scoring transforms the qualitative pass/fail output of screening into a **numeric decision-making artifact** that is auditable, traceable, and aligned with engineering project-management best practices (PRINCE2, PMBOK risk-weighted scoring models).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "College Admission Analogy"

Imagine a university receiving **10,000 applications** for 100 engineering seats:

| Stage | University Action | Product Design Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Application Receive | Raw list of applicants | Concept generation phase (many ideas) |
| 2. Eligibility Check | Reject those without PCM at 60%+ | **Concept Screening** (must-meet criteria) |
| 3. Merit Ranking | JEE rank + weightage to interview | **Concept Scoring** (weighted selection matrix) |
| 4. Final Admission | Top 100 selected | **Selected concept(s)** for prototyping |

This is exactly how Concept Screening (eligibility filter) and Concept Scoring (merit ranking) operate in product design — only the *candidates* are design concepts and the *eligibility tests* are safety, manufacturability, and regulatory constraints.

### 1.4 Why These Steps Are Mandatory in Safety-Critical Design

In products where safety is paramount (medical devices, automotive braking, aerospace, industrial machinery), a poorly screened concept can lead to **field failures, regulatory non-compliance, and liability**. KTU explicitly emphasises these gates because they are mandated by **ISO 12100** (Safety of Machinery), **IEC 61508** (Functional Safety), and **FDA Design Controls (21 CFR 820.30)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Scale Convention Used in KTU Board Exams:**
> Concept Screening uses a **5-point qualitative scale**: 1 = Poor, 2 = Fair, 3 = Good, 4 = Very Good, 5 = Excellent (or symbol codes ⊗ = Fails, ○ = Marginal, + = Acceptable, ++ = Good).
> Concept Scoring uses the same **5-point scale** applied to **weighted** criteria.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Concept Screening Methodology — Step-by-Step Logic

Concept Screening follows a structured 6-step procedure (based on Ulrich & Eppinger's *Product Design and Development* framework adopted by KTU):

**Step 1 — Establish Screening Criteria**
- Pull criteria from the **Product Design Specification (PDS)**.
- Separate into two classes:
  - **Must-Meet Criteria** (binary go/no-go): safety compliance, regulatory approval, manufacturability in available facility, target cost ceiling.
  - **Want-to-Have Criteria** (qualitative benchmarked): aesthetics, ergonomics, ease of use.

**Step 2 — Choose the Reference Concept**
- Pick a **benchmark concept** (often the previous-generation product or a competitor's product).
- This is used as a **baseline (rating = 0 or "= reference")** for relative comparison.

**Step 3 — Rate Each Concept**
- For must-meet: tick pass/fail.
- For want-to-have: rate on the **5-point scale** relative to the reference concept.

**Step 4 — Apply the "Drop Test"**
- Concepts failing any single must-meet criterion are **immediately eliminated** — no discussion.

**Step 5 — Discuss Variances**
- The team discusses each concept's strengths and weaknesses using a **Concept Screening Matrix**.

**Step 6 — Iterate**
- Concepts may be **combined or revised** to address weaknesses before re-screening.

### 2.2 The Concept Scoring Methodology — Detailed Logic

After screening narrows the field (typically to **3–5 concepts**), scoring begins:

**Step 1 — Select Scoring Criteria**
- Re-use the **want-to-have criteria** from screening (the must-meet have already been satisfied).
- Add any **newly identified weighted criteria** specific to the surviving concepts.

**Step 2 — Assign Weights**
- Weights represent the **strategic importance** of each criterion.
- Weights are normalized such that the **total weight sum = 1.00** (or 100 %).

**Step 3 — Rate Each Concept**
- Each surviving concept is rated on the **5-point scale** (1 = worst, 5 = best) against each criterion.

**Step 4 — Compute Weighted Scores**
- For each concept-criterion pair, compute the product of rating × weight.

**Step 5 — Sum & Rank**
- Sum the weighted scores across all criteria for each concept.
- The highest sum wins.

**Step 6 — Sensitivity Analysis**
- Verify that the ranking is robust to small perturbations in weights (a KTU favourite question).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulae are **exam-grade and board-accepted**. Memorize these before any KTU ESE.

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Formula / Range |
|---|---|---|
| $S_i$ | Total weighted score of concept *i* | $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} r_{ij} \cdot w_j$ |
| $r_{ij}$ | Rating of concept *i* on criterion *j* | $r_{ij} \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ |
| $w_j$ | Weight of criterion *j* | $0 < w_j < 1$ |
| $\sum w_j$ | Sum of all weights | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j = 1.00$ |
| $RI_i$ | Relative Merit Index of concept *i* | $RI_i = \dfrac{S_i}{\max(S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_k)}$ |
| $\overline{R}$ | Mean rating across concepts for one criterion | $\overline{R}_j = \dfrac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} r_{ij}$ |
| $CV$ | Coefficient of Variation (sensitivity) | $CV_j = \dfrac{\sigma_j}{\overline{R}_j}$ |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR EXAMS:** When writing weighted score equations, always use \mid or \vert for absolute value to avoid LaTeX rendering errors. Never use the raw pipe `|` inside equations.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Selection Matrix

- **Automotive Industry** — Used in OEM platforms to choose between EV battery chemistries (NMC vs LFP vs solid-state).
- **Medical Device Design** — Required by **FDA 21 CFR 820.30(c)** Design Input → Design Output traceability, scored against risk, biocompatibility, sterilizability.
- **Aerospace (FAA / EASA)** — Mandatory step in ARP 4754A for selecting architectural alternatives.
- **Industrial Safety (ISO 12100)** — Supports the inherent-safety principle by allowing quantitative comparison of risk-reduction alternatives.

### 2.5 Decision Matrix Variants Encountered in KTU

| Matrix Type | Use Case | Output Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Concept Screening Matrix** | Early, fast filtering | Categorical (⊗, ○, +, ++) |
| **Concept Scoring Matrix** | Detailed quantitative ranking | Numeric (0–5) |
| **Pugh Matrix** | Reference-relative comparison | +, −, = relative to baseline |
| **AHP (Analytic Hierarchy Process)** | Pairwise comparison of criteria | Eigenvector weights |
| **QFD House of Quality** | Customer-driven weight derivation | Weighted customer requirements |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests the **distinction between Screening and Scoring**. Remember: **Screening is qualitative & eliminative**; **Scoring is quantitative & rank-ordering**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

### 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example — Design of a Safety Helmet for Construction Workers

**Background:**
A design team has generated **four (4) helmet concepts**:
- **C1**: Polycarbonate shell with foam liner, ratchet suspension.
- **C2**: ABS shell with webbing suspension, low-cost.
- **C3**: Carbon-fibre shell with MIPS rotational impact system.
- **C4**: Hybrid shell (PC + ABS blend) with retractable chin strap.

After **screening**, all four passed the must-meet criteria (ANSI Z89.1, IS 2925, impact resistance > 50 J, electrical insulation, cost ≤ ₹1200).

Now **scoring** is performed on the want-to-have criteria.

### 3.2 Step 1 — Define Weighted Criteria

Five criteria with their normalized weights (sum = 1.00):

| Criterion | Symbol | Weight $w_j$ |
|---|---|---|
| Impact absorption | $c_1$ | 0.30 |
| Weight (lighter = better) | $c_2$ | 0.20 |
| Ventilation/comfort | $c_3$ | 0.15 |
| Cost effectiveness | $c_4$ | 0.25 |
| Aesthetic appeal | $c_5$ | 0.10 |
| **TOTAL** | — | **1.00** |

### 3.3 Step 2 — Populate the Rating Matrix (Scale 1–5)

| Concept | $c_1$ | $c_2$ | $c_3$ | $c_4$ | $c_5$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | 4 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 4 |
| C2 | 3 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 2 |
| C3 | 5 | 5 | 3 | 2 | 5 |
| C4 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 3 | 3 |

### 3.4 Step 3 — Compute Weighted Score for Each Concept

**For Concept C1:**

$$S_{C1} = (r_{11} \cdot w_1) + (r_{12} \cdot w_2) + (r_{13} \cdot w_3) + (r_{14} \cdot w_4) + (r_{15} \cdot w_5)$$

$$S_{C1} = (4 \cdot 0.30) + (4 \cdot 0.20) + (3 \cdot 0.15) + (3 \cdot 0.25) + (4 \cdot 0.10)$$

$$S_{C1} = 1.20 + 0.80 + 0.45 + 0.75 + 0.40$$

$$S_{C1} = 3.60$$

**For Concept C2:**

$$S_{C2} = (3 \cdot 0.30) + (3 \cdot 0.20) + (4 \cdot 0.15) + (5 \cdot 0.25) + (2 \cdot 0.10)$$

$$S_{C2} = 0.90 + 0.60 + 0.60 + 1.25 + 0.20$$

$$S_{C2} = 3.55$$

**For Concept C3:**

$$S_{C3} = (5 \cdot 0.30) + (5 \cdot 0.20) + (3 \cdot 0.15) + (2 \cdot 0.25) + (5 \cdot 0.10)$$

$$S_{C3} = 1.50 + 1.00 + 0.45 + 0.50 + 0.50$$

$$S_{C3} = 3.95$$

**For Concept C4:**

$$S_{C4} = (4 \cdot 0.30) + (4 \cdot 0.20) + (4 \cdot 0.15) + (3 \cdot 0.25) + (3 \cdot 0.10)$$

$$S_{C4} = 1.20 + 0.80 + 0.60 + 0.75 + 0.30$$

$$S_{C4} = 3.65$$

### 3.5 Step 4 — Compute Relative Merit Indices

Maximum score across concepts is $S_{C3} = 3.95$.

$$RI_{C1} = \dfrac{S_{C1}}{S_{C3}} = \dfrac{3.60}{3.95} = 0.911 \; (91.1\%)$$

$$RI_{C2} = \dfrac{S_{C2}}{S_{C3}} = \dfrac{3.55}{3.95} = 0.899 \; (89.9\%)$$

$$RI_{C3} = \dfrac{S_{C3}}{S_{C3}} = 1.000 \; (100.0\%)$$

$$RI_{C4} = \dfrac{S_{C4}}{S_{C3}} = \dfrac{3.65}{3.95} = 0.924 \; (92.4\%)$$

### 3.6 Step 5 — Final Ranking

| Rank | Concept | Score $S_i$ | Relative Merit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **C3** (Carbon-fibre + MIPS) | **3.95** | 100.0% |
| 2 | C4 (Hybrid shell) | 3.65 | 92.4% |
| 3 | C1 (Polycarbonate + foam) | 3.60 | 91.1% |
| 4 | C2 (ABS webbing) | 3.55 | 89.9% |

### 3.7 Step 6 — Sensitivity Check

To test whether ranking is robust, suppose cost weight $w_4$ is increased from 0.25 to 0.35 (and impact $w_1$ reduced to 0.20):

$$S_{C1} = (4 \cdot 0.20) + (4 \cdot 0.20) + (3 \cdot 0.15) + (3 \cdot 0.35) + (4 \cdot 0.10)$$

$$S_{C1} = 0.80 + 0.80 + 0.45 + 1.05 + 0.40 = 3.50$$

$$S_{C3} = (5 \cdot 0.20) + (5 \cdot 0.20) + (3 \cdot 0.15) + (2 \cdot 0.35) + (5 \cdot 0.10)$$

$$S_{C3} = 1.00 + 1.00 + 0.45 + 0.70 + 0.50 = 3.65$$

C3 still ranks first → **ranking is robust** (good design decision).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common 14-mark question asks you to (a) construct the matrix, (b) compute scores, AND (c) comment on sensitivity. Always include sensitivity to score full marks.

### 3.8 Distinguishing Must-Meet vs Want-to-Have (Filtering Logic)

The screening pass rate is computed as:

$$\text{Pass Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Concepts satisfying all must-meet}}{\text{Total concepts generated}} \times 100\%$$

For example, if 20 concepts were generated and 12 satisfy all must-meet criteria:

$$\text{Pass Rate} = \dfrac{12}{20} \times 100\% = 60\%$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Development Flow with Screening & Scoring Gate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Problem Definition and PDS] --> node2[Concept Generation Brainstorming]
    node2 --> node3[Initial Concept Pool N greater than 20]
    node3 --> node4[Establish Screening Criteria]
    node4 --> node5[Apply Must Meet Filter]
    node5 --> node6{All Must Meet Satisfied}
    node6 -->|No| node7[Eliminate Concept]
    node6 -->|Yes| node8[Qualitative Rate on 5 Point Scale]
    node8 --> node9[Concept Screening Matrix]
    node9 --> node10[Surviving Concept Set M between 3 and 5]
    node10 --> node11[Assign Weights to Want to Have Criteria]
    node11 --> node12[Rate Each Surviving Concept 1 to 5]
    node12 --> node13[Compute Weighted Scores]
    node13 --> node14[Sensitivity Analysis]
    node14 --> node15[Rank and Select Winning Concept]
    node15 --> node16[Prototype and Detailed Design]
```

### 4.2 Screening vs Scoring — Functional Block Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SCREENING_BLOCK
        S1[Input PDS Criteria] --> S2[Must Meet Filter Pass or Fail]
        S2 --> S3[Qualitative Rate 5 Point Scale]
        S3 --> S4[Output Reduced Set M]
    end

    subgraph SCORING_BLOCK
        T1[Input Surviving Concepts M] --> T2[Weighted Criteria Definition]
        T2 --> T3[Rating by Experts 1 to 5]
        T3 --> T4[Compute Weighted Sum Score]
        T4 --> T5[Sensitivity Check]
        T5 --> T6[Output Ranked Winner]
    end

    S4 --> T1
```

### 4.3 Weighted Score Computation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Concept Ci Rating r sub ij] --> nodeB[Multiply by Weight w sub j]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Partial Product p sub ij]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Sum Across All Criteria j]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Total Score S sub i]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Compare All Si]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Select Maximum Score Concept]
```

### 4.4 Decision Gate Cascade Visual

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeX[Stage 1 Concept Generation] -->|20 plus concepts| nodeY[Stage 2 Screening Gate]
    nodeY -->|3 to 5 concepts| nodeZ[Stage 3 Scoring Gate]
    nodeZ -->|1 winning concept| nodeW[Stage 4 Prototype Build]
    nodeW --> nodeV[Stage 5 Testing and Validation]
    nodeV --> nodeU[Stage 6 Launch]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between Concept Screening and Concept Scoring in the context of product development. Mention any two distinguishing features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Concept Screening | Concept Scoring |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Rapidly eliminate infeasible concepts | Quantitatively rank surviving concepts |
| **Criteria Type** | Primarily must-meet (go/no-go) | Weighted want-to-have criteria |
| **Output** | Reduced set of concepts | Ranked list with merit score |
| **Scale Used** | Qualitative (Poor → Excellent) | Quantitative (1–5 weighted sum) |

(2 marks for table + 1 mark for any two crisp distinctions.) *[Full marks: 3]*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any three must-meet criteria used during concept screening of a safety-critical product. Justify why each is essential.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Compliance with applicable safety standard** (e.g., IS 2925 for helmets, IEC 60601 for medical devices) — *legally mandatory; failure blocks market entry*. (1 mark)
2. **Manufacturability within available process capability** — *ensures the design can actually be produced at scale without re-tooling*. (1 mark)
3. **Cost not exceeding target ceiling** — *non-negotiable business constraint; otherwise the product fails economically*. (1 mark)

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** For the design of a **portable fire extinguisher**, the design team has generated five concepts (E1, E2, E3, E4, E5). Apply **Concept Screening** with the following must-meet criteria and identify which concepts survive. Justify any elimination. **(7 marks)**

Must-meet criteria:
- (i) Must conform to **IS 15683:2018** portable extinguisher standard.
- (ii) Operating pressure must be ≤ 17 bar at 60 °C.
- (iii) Discharge time ≥ 8 seconds.
- (iv) Total cost ≤ ₹3500 per unit.

| Concept | (i) | (ii) | (iii) | (iv) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| E1 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| E2 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| E3 | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| E4 | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ |
| E5 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |

**Model Solution:**
- **E1 eliminated** — fails cost criterion (iv). (1 mark)
- **E3 eliminated** — fails pressure criterion (ii). (1 mark)
- **E4 eliminated** — fails discharge time criterion (iii). (1 mark)
- **E2 and E5 survive** the screening gate. (2 marks)
- *Justification*: Screening is a binary filter; a single must-meet failure = elimination. Discussion (2 marks for reasoning about why each criterion matters).

**(b)** The surviving concepts E2 and E5 are now evaluated using **Concept Scoring**. Three criteria are used with the given weights: *Extinguishing Efficiency* (w = 0.5), *Ease of Handling* (w = 0.3), *Maintenance* (w = 0.2). Ratings are provided below on a 1–5 scale. Compute the weighted scores, identify the winner, and comment on the sensitivity if the weight of *Extinguishing Efficiency* is reduced to 0.3 and *Ease of Handling* raised to 0.5. **(7 marks)**

| Concept | Efficiency | Handling | Maintenance |
|---|---|---|---|
| E2 | 4 | 3 | 4 |
| E5 | 5 | 4 | 3 |

**Model Solution:**

Original scores:

$$S_{E2} = (4 \cdot 0.5) + (3 \cdot 0.3) + (4 \cdot 0.2) = 2.0 + 0.9 + 0.8 = 3.70$$

$$S_{E5} = (5 \cdot 0.5) + (4 \cdot 0.3) + (3 \cdot 0.2) = 2.5 + 1.2 + 0.6 = 4.30$$

(2 marks for setup, 2 marks for computation)

**Winner: E5** with score 4.30 vs 3.70. (1 mark)

**Sensitivity check** with new weights (Efficiency = 0.3, Handling = 0.5, Maintenance = 0.2):

$$S'_{E2} = (4 \cdot 0.3) + (3 \cdot 0.5) + (4 \cdot 0.2) = 1.2 + 1.5 + 0.8 = 3.50$$

$$S'_{E5} = (5 \cdot 0.3) + (4 \cdot 0.5) + (3 \cdot 0.2) = 1.5 + 2.0 + 0.6 = 4.10$$

E5 still wins (4.10 > 3.50) → **Ranking is robust.** (2 marks for sensitivity reasoning)

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **six-step methodology of Concept Screening** with reference to an industrial example. Why is the **"Drop Test"** considered the most critical step? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
1. **Establish screening criteria** — pull from PDS; separate must-meet and want-to-have. *(1 mark)*
2. **Choose reference concept** — pick competitor or previous generation as baseline. *(1 mark)*
3. **Rate each concept** — on 5-point scale relative to reference. *(1 mark)*
4. **Apply drop test** — eliminate concepts failing any single must-meet criterion. *(1.5 marks)*
5. **Discuss variances** — team discussion, capture rationale in matrix. *(1 mark)*
6. **Iterate / combine** — refine concepts and re-screen if necessary. *(0.5 marks)*

**Why Drop Test is critical:** It is the **only binary, unambiguous filter** in the screening stage. It enforces non-negotiable safety, regulatory, and economic constraints **before** any subjective evaluation, ensuring that no amount of high subjective scores can save a fundamentally unsafe or non-compliant design. *(1 mark)*

**(b)** With a suitable case study of an **electric two-wheeler battery-pack design**, construct a **weighted concept scoring matrix** with at least four criteria (with normalised weights summing to 1.0) and three concepts. Compute scores, rank the concepts, and calculate **Relative Merit Indices**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (illustrative):**

Criteria with weights: *Energy Density* (0.35), *Thermal Runaway Safety* (0.30), *Cycle Life* (0.20), *Cost per kWh* (0.15). Total = 1.00. (1 mark)

| Concept | Energy Density | Thermal Safety | Cycle Life | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LFP Pack | 3 | 5 | 5 | 4 |
| NMC Pack | 5 | 3 | 3 | 2 |
| Solid-State Pack | 4 | 5 | 4 | 1 |

(1 mark for matrix setup)

$$S_{LFP} = (3 \cdot 0.35) + (5 \cdot 0.30) + (5 \cdot 0.20) + (4 \cdot 0.15) = 1.05 + 1.50 + 1.00 + 0.60 = 4.15$$

$$S_{NMC} = (5 \cdot 0.35) + (3 \cdot 0.30) + (3 \cdot 0.20) + (2 \cdot 0.15) = 1.75 + 0.90 + 0.60 + 0.30 = 3.55$$

$$S_{SS} = (4 \cdot 0.35) + (5 \cdot 0.30) + (4 \cdot 0.20) + (1 \cdot 0.15) = 1.40 + 1.50 + 0.80 + 0.15 = 3.85$$

(3 marks for computation)

Ranking: **LFP (4.15) > Solid-State (3.85) > NMC (3.55)**. Winner = LFP.

(1 mark for ranking)

Relative Merit Indices:
$RI_{LFP} = 1.000$, $RI_{SS} = 3.85/4.15 = 0.928$, $RI_{NMC} = 3.55/4.15 = 0.855$.

(1 mark for RI computation)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to normalise weights** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if $\sum w_j \neq 1.00$. Always verify weight sum.
> 2. **Confusing screening with scoring** — screening is *categorical*; scoring is *numeric*. Mixing them up costs heavily.
> 3. **Skipping sensitivity analysis** — for 14-mark questions, omitting sensitivity loses at least **2 marks** (out of 14).
> 4. **Writing $|\;$ symbol in equations** — the raw vertical pipe character `|` may break rendering. Use \vert or \mid.
> 5. **Not stating units or scale assumptions** — always state "5-point scale: 1 = Poor, 5 = Excellent" explicitly.
> 6. **Subjective bias without justification** — every rating should be defended with a technical reason (e.g., "rated 5 because tested to 95 % thermal runaway containment").

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Concept Screening** = qualitative, must-meet-based, eliminative filter.
- [x] **Concept Scoring** = quantitative, weighted, rank-ordering process.
- [x] Always **normalise weights** so $\sum w_j = 1.00$.
- [x] The **scoring formula** is $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} r_{ij} \cdot w_j$.
- [x] **Relative Merit Index** $RI_i = S_i / \max(S)$ normalises winner to 1.00.
- [x] A single **must-meet failure** = immediate elimination (the *Drop Test*).
- [x] Reference concept acts as **baseline (rating 0 or "=")** for relative rating.
- [x] **Sensitivity analysis** is mandatory for full marks on 14-mark questions.
- [x] **5-point scale convention**: 1 = Poor, 2 = Fair, 3 = Good, 4 = Very Good, 5 = Excellent.
- [x] In safety-critical design, screening criteria must reference applicable standards (IS, ISO, IEC, ASTM).
- [x] **Pugh Matrix** is an alternative scoring method using +, −, = relative to reference.
