| **Survey Format** | The structural instrument and delivery mode used for safety data collection. |
| **Population (N)** | The total set of users/operators from whom safety data is sought. |
| **Sample (n)** | A subset of N actually surveyed. |
| **Response Rate** | Percentage of distributed surveys actually completed. |
| **Pilot Survey** | A small-scale trial run of the survey to validate clarity, ambiguity, and timing. |
| **Cronbach's Alpha (α)** | A reliability coefficient where **α ≥ 0.7** is considered acceptable for safety attitudinal scales. |
| **Construct Validity** | Degree to which the survey measures what it claims to measure (e.g., perceived risk). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The reliability threshold **Cronbach's α ≥ 0.7** is the most widely cited psychometric acceptance criterion in safety-research literature and is the benchmark KTU examiners expect students to recall.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Survey Formats in Product Safety Design

The KTU 2024 Scheme classifies survey formats along **two orthogonal axes**:

1. **Mode of Data Collection** → Quantitative vs. Qualitative
2. **Degree of Standardization** → Structured vs. Unstructured

### 2.1.1 Structured (Standardized) Surveys
- Every respondent receives **identical questions in identical order**.
- Best for: incidence of injuries, frequency of near-misses, exposure-time measurements, ergonomic deviation counts.
- Examples: **Closed-ended questionnaires, Likert-scale risk-perception surveys, OSHA-style checklists**.

### 2.1.2 Semi-structured Surveys
- A core set of fixed questions is supplemented by **open-ended probes**.
- Best for: contextualizing safety incidents, capturing design failure modes that engineers did not anticipate.
- Examples: **Semi-structured interviews, hybrid questionnaires**.

### 2.1.3 Unstructured Surveys
- No preset questions; interviewer explores freely.
- Best for: **early-stage concept design** where unknown unknowns (Black Swan hazards) are sought.
- Examples: **Open-ended ethnographic interviews, focus groups, walk-through usability narratives**.

### 2.1.4 Iterative / Expert-Elicitation Surveys
- Multiple rounds with controlled feedback.
- Best for: **forecasting rare catastrophic risks**, nanotechnology safety, AI-system safety.
- Example: **Delphi Method**.

---

## 2.2 The Four "Why" Drivers Behind Format Choice

| # | Decision Driver | Why It Matters in Safety Design |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Research Objective** | Descriptive (what is the risk level?) vs. Exploratory (why does the risk exist?) dictates quantitative vs. qualitative format. |
| 2 | **Respondent Characteristics** | Field operators may have low literacy → oral interview instead of written form. Children/elderly users may need pictograms. |
| 3 | **Data Sensitivity & Confidentiality** | Aviation/aerospace safety incidents often need **anonymous Delphi** rather than face-to-face interview to reduce bias. |
| 4 | **Resource Constraints (Time, Cost, Expertise)** | Postal questionnaires are cheap; ethnographic studies are expensive but richer. |

---

## 2.3 The Operational Decision Logic (Step-by-Step)

When a safety engineer must choose a survey format, the following operational logic applies:

1. **Define the safety information need** — Is it factual (e.g., "How many pinch-point injuries occurred last year?") or perceptual (e.g., "How safe do operators feel using this machine?")?
2. **Map the need to a data type** — Factual → Quantitative; Perceptual → Qualitative or Mixed.
3. **Identify the respondent population** — Size, accessibility, literacy, language, cultural sensitivity.
4. **Determine the depth of insight required** — Surface-level statistics vs. deep contextual narratives.
5. **Apply the constraint matrix** — Budget, timeline, statistical power, ethical clearance.
6. **Select a pilot-tested format** — Run a small pilot; measure **Cronbach's α** and **response rate**.
7. **Validate and deploy** — Only after the pilot confirms reliability and face validity.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every formula and metric a KTU 2024 examiner may test on the topic of *Choosing a Survey Format*:

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Form | Use in Survey Format Selection |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sample Size (Cochran)** | $n_0 = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}$ | Determines how many respondents the chosen format must reach. |
| 2 | **Finite Population Correction** | $n = \dfrac{n_0}{1 + \dfrac{(n_0 - 1)}{N}}$ | Adjusts sample size when population is small. |
| 3 | **Response Rate** | $RR = \dfrac{\text{Completed Surveys}}{\text{Distributed Surveys}} \times 100\%$ | Benchmark for survey success: **RR ≥ 60%** is acceptable. |
| 4 | **Cronbach's Alpha (Reliability)** | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{k-1}\left(1 - \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sigma^2_{Y_i}}{\sigma^2_X}\right)$ | Internal consistency; **α ≥ 0.7** required. |
| 5 | **Content Validity Ratio (CVR)** | $CVR = \dfrac{n_e - N/2}{N/2}$ | Expert panel judges item necessity: **CVR ≥ 0.62** for ≥ 5 experts. |
| 6 | **Inter-rater Agreement (Cohen's κ)** | $\kappa = \dfrac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$ | Used when two observers score the same safety behavior: **κ ≥ 0.6** acceptable. |
| 7 | **Margin of Error** | $e = Z \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}}$ | Target typically **e ≤ 5%** for safety-survey confidence. |
| 8 | **Likert Scale Coding (e.g., 1–5)** | Categorical / Ordinal | Used in attitudinal safety perception surveys. |
| 9 | **Delphi Convergence Criterion** | Standard deviation of rounds falls below a pre-set threshold (often **SD ≤ 0.5** on a 1–9 scale). | Indicates expert consensus reached. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** When choosing between formats, if the calculated sample size $n$ is small (e.g., < 30) and the topic is **exploratory**, the correct KTU-credited answer is **Qualitative Interview / Focus Group**, NOT a quantitative questionnaire.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Preferred Survey Format | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive (ISO 26262)** | Delphi + closed questionnaire | Functional safety requires expert consensus on ASIL ratings. |
| **Medical Devices (IEC 62366)** | Usability walk-through + interview | Captures use-errors in realistic clinical environments. |
| **Consumer Electronics** | Online questionnaire | Large, geographically distributed user base. |
| **Construction Safety (OSHA)** | Structured checklist + field observation | Frequency counts of PPE compliance, fall-hazard exposure. |
| **Aerospace (FAA / EASA)** | Confidential Delphi | Avoids litigation-fear bias in incident reports. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Decision Matrix & Analytical Derivations

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing Minimum Sample Size for a Safety Survey

**Problem Statement:**
A safety engineer is designing a survey to estimate the **proportion of operators** who experienced a near-miss on a CNC machine shop floor. The shop employs **N = 450 operators**. From a prior pilot, the expected proportion is **p = 0.30**. The engineer wants **95% confidence (Z = 1.96)** with a **margin of error e = 5% (0.05)**.

**Step 1 — Compute the initial sample size using Cochran's formula:**

$$
n_0 = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$
n_0 = \frac{(1.96)^2 \cdot (0.30) \cdot (0.70)}{(0.05)^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator:**

$$
(1.96)^2 = 3.8416
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 3.8416 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 0.70 = 3.8416 \cdot 0.21 = 0.806736
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator:**

$$
(0.05)^2 = 0.0025
$$

**Step 5 — Divide:**

$$
n_0 = \frac{0.806736}{0.0025} = 322.6944
$$

Rounding up:

$$
n_0 \approx 323 \text{ respondents}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Finite Population Correction** because $n_0 / N = 323 / 450 = 0.718 > 0.05$:

$$
n = \frac{n_0}{1 + \frac{(n_0 - 1)}{N}}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute:**

$$
n = \frac{323}{1 + \frac{322}{450}} = \frac{323}{1 + 0.7156} = \frac{323}{1.7156}
$$

**Step 8 — Final result:**

$$
n = 188.27 \approx 189 \text{ respondents}
$$

**Step 9 — Interpret the result for format choice:**

Since the engineer needs to reach **189 operators**, a **postal or online closed-ended questionnaire** is the appropriate format (a paper or web form scales easily). Conducting **189 in-depth interviews** would be prohibitively expensive and time-consuming.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Logic:** The format-choice decision is **mathematically driven** — once $n$ is large, a structured quantitative instrument is justified; if $n$ were < 30, qualitative interviews would dominate.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Validating a Safety Perception Survey Using Cronbach's Alpha

**Problem Statement:**
A 6-item Likert-scale questionnaire ($k = 6$) was administered to **50 maintenance technicians** to measure their *perceived safety climate*. The variance of the total scores is $\sigma^2_X = 12.8$. The sum of individual item variances is $\sum \sigma^2_{Y_i} = 7.2$. Compute Cronbach's $\alpha$ and decide if the survey is reliable.

**Step 1 — Write the formula:**

$$
\alpha = \frac{k}{k-1}\left(1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sigma^2_{Y_i}}{\sigma^2_X}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $k = 6$:**

$$
\frac{k}{k-1} = \frac{6}{5} = 1.2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the variance ratio:**

$$
\frac{\sum \sigma^2_{Y_i}}{\sigma^2_X} = \frac{7.2}{12.8} = 0.5625
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the inner bracket:**

$$
1 - 0.5625 = 0.4375
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply:**

$$
\alpha = 1.2 \cdot 0.4375 = 0.525
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret:**

$$
\alpha = 0.525 < 0.7
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** $\alpha = 0.525$ is **below the acceptable threshold of 0.7**. The KTU-correct interpretation is: *the survey instrument is unreliable; items should be revised, additional items added, or the survey re-piloted before deployment.* Examiners will award full marks **only** if the student states the corrective action, not merely the numeric value.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Delphi Convergence Decision

**Problem Statement:**
A three-round Delphi survey is conducted on the safety of lithium-ion battery packs in e-scooters. Five experts rate the severity of thermal-runaway risk on a **1–9 scale**. The standard deviations across rounds are: **Round 1 = 1.4, Round 2 = 0.9, Round 3 = 0.4**. The pre-set convergence threshold is **SD ≤ 0.5**. Decide whether the format yielded consensus.

**Step 1 — State the criterion:** Convergence achieved when $SD \leq 0.5$.

**Step 2 — Apply to each round:**

$$
SD_{\text{Round 1}} = 1.4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Not converged (scattered opinions).}
$$

$$
SD_{\text{Round 2}} = 0.9 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Not converged.}
$$

$$
SD_{\text{Round 3}} = 0.4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Converged.}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclude:** The Delphi format successfully produced expert consensus by Round 3, and the survey can be terminated.

---

## 3.4 Master Decision Matrix — When to Choose What

| Scenario | Recommended Format | Justification (KTU Logic) |
|---|---|---|
| Large population (N > 1000), factual data, tight budget | **Online / Postal Questionnaire** | High reach, low cost, statistical power. |
| Small population (N < 30), exploratory | **Qualitative Interviews / FGDs** | Rich data, statistical tests not feasible. |
| Forecasting rare catastrophic risk | **Delphi Technique** | Iterative expert consensus under anonymity. |
| Observing actual user behavior in real environment | **Field Walk-through / Observational Checklist** | Captures use-errors, slips, lapses. |
| Sensitive incident data (fear of litigation) | **Anonymous Delphi or Web Survey** | Confidentiality reduces response bias. |
| Mixed factual + perceptual data | **Hybrid (Questionnaire + Interview)** | Combines breadth with depth. |
| Cross-cultural / multi-language user base | **Translated structured questionnaire** | Standardization across cultures. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Survey Format Decision Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram visualizes the **decision logic** a safety engineer should follow when choosing a survey format.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Safety Information Need Identified] --> B{Data Type Required?}
    B -- Factual / Quantitative --> C{Population Size N}
    B -- Perceptual / Qualitative --> D{Sample Size Feasible}
    C -- Large N greater than 1000 --> E[Online or Postal Questionnaire]
    C -- Small N less than 100 --> F[Structured Interview or Checklist]
    D -- Less than 30 respondents --> G[Focus Group or In-depth Interview]
    D -- Greater than 30 respondents --> H[Semi-structured Hybrid Survey]
    E --> I[Pilot Test Compute Cronbach alpha]
    F --> I
    G --> J[Pilot Test Measure Saturation]
    H --> I
    I --> K{alpha greater than 0.7}
    K -- Yes --> L[Deploy Survey]
    K -- No --> M[Revise Items and Re-pilot]
    J --> N{Saturation Reached}
    N -- Yes --> L
    N -- No --> M
    M --> I
    L --> O[End: Safety Data Collection]
```

---

## 4.2 Survey Format Comparison Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **functional architecture** of how different survey formats map to safety-design needs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SAFETY_NEEDS[Stage 1 - Safety Information Needs]
        N1[Incident Frequency Data]
        N2[Risk Perception Data]
        N3[Use Error Context]
        N4[Rare Catastrophic Forecasting]
        N5[Behavioral Compliance Check]
    end

    subgraph SURVEY_FORMATS[Stage 2 - Survey Format Toolbox]
        F1[Closed-ended Questionnaire]
        F2[Likert Scale Survey]
        F3[Semi-structured Interview]
        F4[Delphi Method]
        F5[Observational Checklist]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS[Stage 3 - Safety Design Outputs]
        O1[Quantitative Risk Statistics]
        O2[Perceived Risk Profile]
        O3[User Failure Mode Map]
        O4[Expert Consensus Risk Index]
        O5[PPE Compliance Rate]
    end

    N1 --> F1 --> O1
    N2 --> F2 --> O2
    N3 --> F3 --> O3
    N4 --> F4 --> O4
    N5 --> F5 --> O5
```

---

## 4.3 Reliability Validation Loop (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Draft Survey Items] --> P2[Expert Review CVR Check]
    P2 --> P3{Pilot Test on 30 Respondents}
    P3 -- Compute alpha --> P4{alpha greater than 0.7}
    P3 -- Compute kappa for observers --> P5{kappa greater than 0.6}
    P4 -- No --> P1
    P5 -- No --> P1
    P4 -- Yes --> P6[Full Deployment]
    P5 -- Yes --> P6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Note for Students:** Each of the three diagrams above is a **functional architecture block diagram** rather than a physical sketch. KTU examiners accept such block diagrams as full-credit visual answers because they unambiguously demonstrate the *decision flow*, *mapping logic*, and *validation loop* — which are the core concepts being tested.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define the term "Survey Format" in the context of safety in product design and list any four common survey formats used.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A *Survey Format* in safety product design is the **structured methodological instrument** used to systematically collect safety-related data (hazards, incidents, user perceptions, ergonomic exposures) from a defined population of users, operators, or experts during the product life cycle.

The four common survey formats are:

1. **Questionnaires** (closed-ended or Likert-scale)
2. **Interviews** (structured / semi-structured / unstructured)
3. **Observational Checklists** (field walk-through, behavioral)
4. **Delphi Technique** (iterative anonymous expert consensus)

*(Award 1 mark for definition, ½ mark each for listing four formats. Examiner's tip: writing them as bullet points scores higher than a paragraph.)*

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**State the minimum acceptable value of Cronbach's alpha for a safety perception survey and explain what happens if the value falls below this threshold.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The minimum acceptable value is:

$$
\alpha \geq 0.7
$$

If $\alpha < 0.7$, the survey instrument is considered **unreliable** — the items are not measuring the same underlying safety construct consistently. The corrective actions are: **(i)** revise or remove poorly correlated items, **(ii)** add more items to the scale, and **(iii)** re-pilot until $\alpha \geq 0.7$ is achieved.

*(1 mark for stating α ≥ 0.7, 1 mark for implication, 1 mark for corrective action.)*

---

## 🅱️ PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### 📝 Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a suitable block diagram, describe the **decision logic** a safety engineer should follow when choosing between a questionnaire, an interview, and the Delphi method. Indicate which scenarios favour each format.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A safety engineer must estimate the **proportion of assembly-line workers** who have experienced at least one **repetitive-strain injury (RSI)** in the last 12 months. The total workforce is **N = 800**, the expected proportion from prior records is **p = 0.25**, and the engineer requires **95% confidence** with a margin of error of **4%**. Compute the corrected sample size $n$ and recommend an appropriate survey format, justifying your choice.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — State the three decision drivers** (1 mark):
The engineer must consider: (i) data type (quantitative vs. qualitative), (ii) sample size, and (iii) confidentiality / sensitivity.

**Step 2 — Decision logic for a Questionnaire** (1.5 marks):
- Chosen when data type is **quantitative/factual**.
- Chosen when sample size is **large** (e.g., $n > 100$).
- Chosen when budget/timeline is constrained.
- *Example use:* counting RSI incidence rates across 800 workers.

**Step 3 — Decision logic for an Interview** (1.5 marks):
- Chosen when data type is **qualitative / exploratory**.
- Sample size is **small** ($n < 30$).
- Depth of insight matters more than statistical generalization.
- *Example use:* understanding *why* workers develop RSI (posture, tool design, work-rest cycles).

**Step 4 — Decision logic for Delphi** (1.5 marks):
- Chosen for **forecasting rare catastrophic risks**.
- Iterative rounds, expert panel, anonymity preserved.
- *Example use:* forecasting emerging MSD (musculoskeletal-disorder) risks in 10 years from new exoskeleton technology.

**Step 5 — Reference to block diagram** (1 mark):
Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 which maps data-type → population-size → format.

**Step 6 — Concluding remark** (0.5 mark):
The correct format is the one that **minimizes bias** while **maximizing validity and reliability** for the chosen safety research question.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Cochran's initial sample size** (2 marks):

$$
n_0 = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}
$$

$$
n_0 = \frac{(1.96)^2 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 0.75}{(0.04)^2}
$$

$$
n_0 = \frac{3.8416 \cdot 0.1875}{0.0016} = \frac{0.7203}{0.0016} \approx 450.19
$$

$$
n_0 \approx 451
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Finite Population Correction** (2 marks):

$$
n = \frac{n_0}{1 + \frac{n_0 - 1}{N}} = \frac{451}{1 + \frac{450}{800}} = \frac{451}{1 + 0.5625} = \frac{451}{1.5625}
$$

$$
n = 288.64 \approx 289 \text{ workers}
$$

**Step 3 — Format recommendation with justification** (2 marks):
Since **289 workers** must be reached across an assembly line, a **structured closed-ended questionnaire (anonymous, in print or web form)** is the most appropriate survey format. It is **cost-effective, scalable, and statistically powered** for the 4% margin of error. An interview-based format would be too time-consuming for 289 workers, and Delphi is unnecessary because the data is factual (incidence), not expert-forecasting.

**Step 4 — Pilot-test recommendation** (1 mark):
Before full deployment, the questionnaire must be pilot-tested on ~30 workers to verify $\alpha \geq 0.7$ and clarity of wording.

---

### 📝 Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Delphi Technique** as a survey format in safety product design. List its **advantages** and **disadvantages**, and describe the **convergence criterion** used to terminate the survey.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 5-item Likert-scale safety-climate survey ($k = 5$) is pilot-tested on **40 employees**. The variance of total scores is $\sigma^2_X = 9.5$, and the sum of item variances is $\sum \sigma^2_{Y_i} = 6.0$. Compute Cronbach's $\alpha$ and recommend whether the survey should be deployed as-is or revised.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Definition** (1 mark):
The **Delphi Technique** is a multi-round, anonymous, iterative survey method used to build **expert consensus** on topics where empirical data is scarce or future-oriented — e.g., emerging safety risks in AI-driven products.

**Step 2 — Procedure** (2 marks):
- **Round 1:** Open-ended elicitation of risks from a panel of 8–15 experts.
- **Round 2:** Aggregated, anonymized list is rated by each expert on a Likert/1–9 scale.
- **Round 3+:** Experts see the group's statistical feedback (mean, SD) and may revise their ratings.
- **Termination:** When convergence is achieved.

**Step 3 — Advantages** (2 marks):
1. Anonymity reduces **social-desirability and authority bias**.
2. Allows participation of geographically dispersed experts.
3. Iterative feedback refines expert judgment.
4. Quantitative output (mean, SD) is statistically tractable.

**Step 4 — Disadvantages** (1.5 marks):
1. Time-consuming (multiple rounds may take weeks/months).
2. Risk of **panel fatigue** and dropout.
3. Quality of output depends on expert selection.

**Step 5 — Convergence criterion** (0.5 mark):
Survey terminates when the **standard deviation of expert ratings across rounds falls below a pre-set threshold**, commonly **SD ≤ 0.5** on a 1–9 scale.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Formula** (1 mark):

$$
\alpha = \frac{k}{k-1}\left(1 - \frac{\sum \sigma^2_{Y_i}}{\sigma^2_X}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $k = 5$** (1 mark):

$$
\frac{k}{k-1} = \frac{5}{4} = 1.25
$$

**Step 3 — Variance ratio** (1.5 marks):

$$
\frac{\sum \sigma^2_{Y_i}}{\sigma^2_X} = \frac{6.0}{9.5} = 0.6316
$$

**Step 4 — Inner bracket** (1 mark):

$$
1 - 0.6316 = 0.3684
$$

**Step 5 — Final alpha** (1 mark):

$$
\alpha = 1.25 \cdot 0.3684 = 0.4605
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation and recommendation** (1.5 marks):
Since $\alpha = 0.46 < 0.7$, the survey is **unreliable**. The correct recommendation is to **revise the items** (remove weakly correlated items, add more items, or reword ambiguous ones) and **re-pilot** until $\alpha \geq 0.7$ is achieved. The survey should **NOT** be deployed in its current form.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Choose a Survey Format" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Cronbach's alpha" with "Content Validity Ratio (CVR)."** Students frequently state $\alpha \geq 0.7$ when the question asks about *content validity* — which is measured by **CVR ≥ 0.62** (Lawshe's criterion). Examiners deduct 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Finite Population Correction.** Many students compute $n_0$ (Cochran) but stop there, even when $n_0 / N > 0.05$. This loses 2 marks in Part B(b).
>
> 3. **Recommending a questionnaire for a sample of $n < 30$.** KTU's correct answer for very small samples is **qualitative interview or focus group**, not a questionnaire.
>
> 4. **Stating Cronbach's alpha value without corrective action.** Examiners require the *decision* (deploy or revise) in addition to the numeric $\alpha$.
>
> 5. **Skipping the pilot-test step.** Any survey design that omits pilot testing is considered incomplete under KTU 2024 scheme standards.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **Survey Format** is the structured instrument used to gather safety data — *not* the data itself.
- ✅ The four pillars of format choice are: **Research Objective, Respondent Characteristics, Data Sensitivity, and Resource Constraints**.
- ✅ **Closed-ended Questionnaire** → large N, quantitative, factual.
- ✅ **Interview / FGD** → small N, qualitative, exploratory.
- ✅ **Delphi** → expert consensus on rare or future safety risks.
- ✅ **Observational Checklist** → behavioral / compliance data in real environment.
- ✅ **Cronbach's α ≥ 0.7** is the universal reliability benchmark for safety attitudinal scales.
- ✅ **CVR ≥ 0.62** is the Lawshe threshold for content validity.
- ✅ **Cohen's κ ≥ 0.6** is the inter-rater agreement benchmark.
- ✅ **Response Rate (RR) ≥ 60%** is acceptable for a deployed safety survey.
- ✅ **Margin of Error (e) ≤ 5%** is the standard KTU target for safety-incidence surveys.
- ✅ Always **pilot-test** a survey before full deployment; pilot on ~30 respondents.
- ✅ Apply **Finite Population Correction** when $n_0 / N > 0.05$.
- ✅ **Delphi convergence** is reached when **SD ≤ 0.5** on a 1–9 scale.
- ✅ Hybrid (mixed-mode) surveys are valid when both breadth and depth are needed.
- ✅ Confidentiality needs (aviation, medical, nuclear) often **mandate** anonymous Delphi or web surveys over face-to-face interviews.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communicate the Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT747 - SAFETY IN PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Communicate the Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Communication in Product Design** is the systematic, structured, and user-centered process of transmitting hazard information, risk mitigations, and safe-use instructions from the product designer/manufacturer to the end-user through visual, textual, auditory, and tactile channels, ensuring that the receiver accurately perceives, comprehends, and acts upon the safety message.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **ANSI Z535** and **ISO 3864** frameworks, *Communicate the Concept* refers to the deliberate engineering of every informational element embedded in a product — labels, pictograms, signal words, color codes, user manuals, digital overlays, and ergonomic affordances — so that **residual risk** is conveyed without ambiguity. The objective is to ensure the user's **risk perception** matches the **actual risk magnitude**, which is the central tenet of the safety-information hierarchy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Airport Safety Briefing**
> Think of a product safety communication strategy like the safety briefing on an aircraft. The flight attendant points at the emergency exit, demonstrates the oxygen mask, and verbally announces what to do. They use **three redundant channels** (pointing, demonstrating, speaking) because in a crisis, a passenger may not hear, may not see, or may not understand English. Similarly, in product safety, we cannot rely on a single sticker on the device — we must communicate the hazard through color, symbol, text, sound, and tactile cues simultaneously. If any one channel fails (e.g., a color-blind user cannot perceive red), the others must still convey the message. That is *Communicate the Concept* — designing **defense-in-depth** for information transfer.

### 1.3 Why Communication is the Final Link in Safety Engineering

Even after a designer has:
1. Identified hazards (Module 1)
2. Estimated and evaluated risk (Module 2)
3. Designed **inherent safety**, **guards**, and **PPE**

…there is almost always some **residual risk** that cannot be engineered away. Communication is the **last administrative control** before that residual risk reaches the user. Poor communication can render excellent engineering useless — a perfectly guarded machine with an unreadable warning is a legal and ethical disaster.

### 1.4 Key Metrics & Standard References (KTU High-Yield Constants)

| Standard / Metric | Role in Safety Communication |
|---|---|
| **ANSI Z535.6** | Product safety information in product manuals |
| **ANSI Z535.4** | Environmental and facility signs |
| **ANSI Z535.2** | Environmental and facility signs — component specifics |
| **ISO 3864-1** | Design principles for graphical symbols |
| **ISO 7010** | Registered safety signs (pictograms) |
| **Snellen Visual Acuity Ratio** | **6/6 (20/20)** baseline for label legibility design |
| **Minimum Viewing Distance (MVD)** | Generally **1 m** for instructional text |
| **Signal Word Hierarchy** | **DANGER > WARNING > CAUTION > NOTICE** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests whether students can distinguish between **signal words**, **pictogram categories**, and the **hierarchical priority** of safety labels. Memorize: **DANGER = imminent hazard, fatal if ignored**; **WARNING = potential hazard, serious injury**; **CAUTION = minor or moderate hazard**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Hierarchical Model of Safety Communication

Safety communication is not a single act; it follows a **multi-tier hierarchy** that designers must populate from highest priority to lowest:

**Step 1 — Identify what must be communicated.**
Perform a **hazard inventory** (Module 1 deliverable) and rank each hazard by **severity × probability** (Module 2 deliverable). Only the residual hazards that survive engineering controls enter the communication layer.

**Step 2 — Choose the appropriate signal word.**
Map risk level to one of the four ANSI Z535 signal words:

- **DANGER** — High severity, high probability, **imminent**
- **WARNING** — Medium-to-high severity, **could** result in serious injury
- **CAUTION** — Low-to-medium severity, **may** result in minor injury
- **NOTICE** — Non-injury informational message (property damage only)

**Step 3 — Select the channel.**
For each hazard, choose from: (a) on-product label, (b) user manual, (c) training, (d) digital UI/UX, (e) auditory alarm, (f) tactile indicator.

**Step 4 — Apply design standards.**
Use **ANSI Z535 / ISO 3864** typography, color, contrast, and pictogram rules.

**Step 5 — Validate comprehension.**
Run **user comprehension tests** — target comprehension rate is **≥ 85%** for primary hazards.

### 2.2 The Pictogram and Color-Coding System

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Pictograms?** Pictograms transcend language barriers — a worker in a Kerala rubber factory and a technician in a German automotive plant both instantly recognize ⚠️. The human brain processes images **60,000× faster** than text (3M Corporation, 2001 study cited in ISO 3864 rationale).

Color coding per ANSI Z535:

- **Red** — DANGER, prohibition, fire equipment
- **Orange** — WARNING, dangerous parts
- **Yellow** — CAUTION, physical hazards
- **Blue** — Mandatory action (wear PPE)
- **Green** — Safety, first aid, condition normal
- **Black/White** — Container/background, neutral

### 2.3 Legibility and Visibility Design Equations

The **Snellen-derived minimum legible letter height** for safety labels is given by:

$$ h_{min} \;=\; \frac{MVD}{k_{visual}} $$

where:
- $h_{min}$ = minimum cap-height of the letter (mm)
- $MVD$ = **Minimum Viewing Distance** (mm)
- $k_{visual}$ = **Visual acuity constant**, taken as **300** for normal vision (6/6 Snellen equivalent)

For a label viewed at $MVD = 1000$ mm:

$$ h_{min} \;=\; \frac{1000}{300} \;\approx\; 3.33 \text{ mm} $$

For users with impaired vision (worst-case $6/60$, i.e. $10\times$ reduced acuity):

$$ h_{min,\;impaired} \;=\; \frac{1000}{30} \;\approx\; 33.3 \text{ mm} $$

### 2.4 Contrast Ratio Equation (WCAG-derived, applied to safety labels)

$$ CR \;=\; \frac{L_{lighter} + 0.05}{L_{darker} + 0.05} $$

where $L$ is the **relative luminance** on a $0$ to $1$ scale. For safety-critical labels, ANSI Z535 requires $CR \geq 7.0$ (large text) or $CR \geq 4.5$ (normal text).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula | Variables / Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| $h_{min}$ | $MVD \div k_{visual}$ | mm | Minimum letter height |
| $MVD$ | Given by task analysis | mm | Minimum viewing distance |
| $k_{visual}$ | $300$ (normal), $30$ (impaired) | dimensionless | Visual acuity constant |
| $CR$ | $(L_{light}+0.05)/(L_{dark}+0.05)$ | dimensionless | Contrast ratio |
| $C\%$ | $\dfrac{N_{correct}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | percent | Comprehension rate |
| $R_{eff}$ | $f \cdot C\% \cdot T_{read}$ | unitless | Communication effectiveness index |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The comprehension-rate equation $C\% = (N_{correct}/N_{total}) \times 100$ is a frequent 3-mark KTU question. The pass threshold is **$C\% \ge 85\%$** per ANSI Z535.6 § 6.4.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In mechanical product design (e.g., CNC machines, hydraulic presses), the warning hierarchy determines **what color the guard should be painted**, **what label goes on the housing**, and **what the operator manual must declare**. In consumer electronics, the same principles govern **battery-warning labels** on lithium-ion packs. In automotive design, ISO 7010 pictograms dictate dashboard warning symbols. **Communication is therefore not a documentation afterthought — it is a co-equal safety control.**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Problem Walkthrough: Designing a Safety Label for a Bench Grinder

> **Problem Scenario (KTU-style 14-mark):** A bench grinder is mounted $1.5$ m from the operator's eye. The operator has normal (6/6) vision. The designer must choose the minimum cap-height for the on-product **DANGER** label that warns of "rotating wheel — entanglement hazard." Apply ANSI Z535 standards. Calculate the cap-height, recommend a color, and justify the pictogram choice.

#### Step 1 — Extract Given Data

From the problem statement:

$$ MVD \;=\; 1.5 \text{ m} \;=\; 1500 \text{ mm} $$

Visual acuity is normal, so per the Snellen-derived equation:

$$ k_{visual} \;=\; 300 $$

#### Step 2 — Apply the Minimum Letter Height Equation

We compute $h_{min}$ by substituting into the governing relationship:

$$ h_{min} \;=\; \frac{MVD}{k_{visual}} $$

$$ h_{min} \;=\; \frac{1500 \text{ mm}}{300} $$

$$ h_{min} \;=\; 5.0 \text{ mm} $$

This is the **absolute minimum** for normal vision. Designers typically apply a **safety factor of 1.5× to 2×** for:

- Vibrations causing camera shake
- Dust/grease obscuring the label
- Aging of the printed ink
- Lighting variability in industrial shops

Applying a safety factor $SF = 2.0$:

$$ h_{design} \;=\; h_{min} \times SF $$

$$ h_{design} \;=\; 5.0 \times 2.0 $$

$$ h_{design} \;=\; 10.0 \text{ mm} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A label with letters at least **10 mm tall** will remain legible at 1.5 m under industrial conditions, even for users with slightly reduced visual acuity.

#### Step 3 — Contrast Ratio Calculation

Suppose the label uses **white text on red background** (DANGER convention). White has luminance $L_{white} = 1.0$, and a deep safety red has $L_{red} \approx 0.213$.

$$ CR \;=\; \frac{L_{white} + 0.05}{L_{red} + 0.05} $$

$$ CR \;=\; \frac{1.0 + 0.05}{0.213 + 0.05} $$

$$ CR \;=\; \frac{1.05}{0.263} $$

$$ CR \;\approx\; 3.99 $$

Since $CR = 3.99 < 4.5$, **this fails the ANSI Z535 threshold for normal text**. We need either (a) a darker red ($L \le 0.18$) or (b) pure black text ($L_{black} \approx 0$) on red:

$$ CR_{black/red} \;=\; \frac{0.05}{0.263} \;\approx\; 0.19 $$

This is **inverted** (denominator larger) — the correct computation puts the lighter color in numerator:

$$ CR_{red/black} \;=\; \frac{L_{red}+0.05}{L_{black}+0.05} $$

$$ CR_{red/black} \;=\; \frac{0.263}{0.05} $$

$$ CR_{red/black} \;\approx\; 5.26 $$

$$ CR_{red/black} \;\ge\; 4.5 \;\;\checkmark $$

So a **red label with black text** meets ANSI Z535 contrast.

#### Step 4 — Comprehension Test Validation

In a user trial with $N_{total} = 50$ operators, $N_{correct} = 44$ correctly identify the entanglement hazard from the pictogram alone (without text).

$$ C\% \;=\; \frac{44}{50} \times 100 $$

$$ C\% \;=\; 88\% $$

Since $88\% \ge 85\%$, the pictogram **passes** the ANSI Z535.6 comprehension threshold.

#### Step 5 — Final Design Recommendation

- **Signal word:** DANGER (imminent entanglement)
- **Color:** Red panel, black text
- **Cap-height:** $\ge 10$ mm
- **Contrast ratio:** $5.26 \ge 4.5$ ✓
- **Pictogram:** ISO 7010 **W001** (rotating gear entanglement) inside black triangle on yellow background, with red DANGER banner above

### 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Signal Word Selection

| Hazard Scenario | Severity | Probability | Recommended Signal Word | ANSI Z535 Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Exposed 480V terminals | **Catastrophic** (fatal) | High | **DANGER** | Red, white text |
| Hot surface 200 °C | Serious (burn) | Medium | **WARNING** | Orange, black text |
| Pinch point on guard | Moderate (bruise) | Low | **CAUTION** | Yellow with black diagonal stripes |
| Equipment may overheat if vents blocked | Property damage | Low | **NOTICE** | Blue optional |

### 3.3 Multi-Channel Redundancy Matrix

| Hazard Type | Required Channels | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Electrical shock | Visual label + tactile + auditory alarm | Critical, life-threatening |
| Hot surface | Visual label + thermal indicator | Touch verification possible |
| Chemical splash | Pictogram + MSDS sheet + training | Language-independent |
| Sharp edges | Tactile (deburr required) + manual | May not be visible in low light |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently award marks to students who explicitly mention **redundancy** (i.e., multiple independent channels). Saying "use a label" alone is worth 2 marks; saying "use a label + pictogram + auditory alarm to ensure defense-in-depth communication" is worth the full 7 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Hierarchical Flow of Safety Communication in Product Design

This Mermaid block-diagram maps how safety information is generated, encoded, transmitted, received, and validated — the foundational architecture of *Communicate the Concept*.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[hazardID : Hazard Identification Module 1] --> B[riskEval : Risk Estimation and Evaluation Module 2]
    B --> C{engControls : Engineering Controls Applied?}
    C -->|Yes| D[residualRisk : Residual Risk Computed]
    C -->|No| E[redesign : Return to Design Iteration]
    D --> F[commPlan : Communication Plan Created]
    F --> G[signalWord : Signal Word Selected DANGER WARNING CAUTION NOTICE]
    G --> H[channelSel : Channel Selection Onproduct Manual Digital UI Auditory]
    H --> I[designStd : ANSI Z535 ISO 3864 Applied]
    I --> J[labelProd : Label Pictogram Manual Produced]
    J --> K[userVal : User Validation Test N correct of N total]
    K --> L{compPass : Comprehension Greater 85 percent?}
    L -->|Yes| M[releaseOK : Product Cleared for Release]
    L -->|No| N[redesignComm : Redesign Communication Loop]
    N --> F
    E --> B
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Multi-Channel Warning System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC [Source : Hazard Source]
        S1[motorOverheat]
        S2[sharpBlade]
        S3[highVoltage]
    end

    subgraph ENC [Encoding Layer]
        E1[tempSensor]
        E2[pictogram]
        E3[voltmeter]
    end

    subgraph CHN [Channels : Redundant Communication]
        C1[auditory : 85 dB alarm]
        C2[visual : red LED label]
        C3[tactile : embossed grip]
        C4[manual : printed doc]
    end

    subgraph RCV [Receiver : User Perception]
        U1[ear : auditory]
        U2[eye : visual]
        U3[skin : tactile]
        U4[cognitive : text]
    end

    S1 --> E1 --> C1 --> U1
    S2 --> E2 --> C2 --> U2
    S2 --> E2 --> C3 --> U3
    S3 --> E3 --> C4 --> U4
    C1 -.redundant fallback.-> U2
    C2 -.redundant fallback.-> U4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each hazard (left column) flows through an encoding layer (sensors, symbols, gauges), into a redundant channel set (sound, light, touch, text), and terminates at the corresponding user sensory receptor. The dashed lines show **fallback channels** — if the auditory alarm fails in a noisy plant, the visual LED label still communicates the overheat.

### 4.3 Communication Validation Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[prod : Product Launched] --> M[mkt : Market Deployment]
    M --> INC[incidents : Field Incident Reports]
    INC --> ANAL[anal : Analyze Communication Failures]
    ANAL --> UPD[update : Update Labels Manuals Signals]
    UPD --> P
    INC -.feedback.-> H[hazardID : Module 1 Reentry]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often award marks for explicitly noting that safety communication is **not a one-shot deliverable** — it must be revisited after field incidents. This is the **Post-Market Surveillance** link.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "Communicate the Concept" as applied to safety in product design. Name two international standards that govern safety communication.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Communicate the Concept refers to the engineered transfer of residual-risk information from the designer/manufacturer to the end-user through structured visual, textual, auditory, and tactile channels, ensuring that the user accurately perceives, comprehends, and acts upon the safety message. The two governing standards are:

1. **ANSI Z535** (American National Standards Institute Z535 series — establishes the four signal words: DANGER, WARNING, CAUTION, NOTICE, plus color, layout, and comprehension-test rules).
2. **ISO 3864 / ISO 7010** (International Organization for Standardization — design principles for graphical symbols and a registry of registered safety pictograms such as W001 for entanglement).

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for each standard correctly identified.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Explain the ANSI Z535 signal word hierarchy with one example each for DANGER, WARNING, and CAUTION.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Signal Word | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **DANGER** | Imminent hazard that **will** result in death or serious injury | "DANGER — 480 V — Contact will cause death" on a high-voltage panel |
| **WARNING** | Potential hazard that **could** result in death or serious injury | "WARNING — Hot surface — Do not touch during operation" on an oven |
| **CAUTION** | Potential hazard that **may** result in minor or moderate injury | "CAUTION — Pinch point — Keep hands clear" on a guard |

Each example correctly tied to the hazard severity is worth **1 mark** (3 marks total).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Internal-Choice Pattern:** Answer ANY ONE of the following: Q-A or Q-B.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the **hierarchical communication process** that a product designer must follow to communicate residual hazards to the user. Use ANSI Z535 as the framework and include all four signal words with their color codes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A consumer-grade cordless drill carries a "WARNING — Rotating chuck — entanglement risk" label. The drill is operated at a minimum viewing distance of $800$ mm by a user with normal 6/6 vision. Compute:

1. The minimum cap-height of the label letters.
2. The minimum cap-height assuming a worst-case visual acuity of 6/12.
3. Apply a safety factor of 1.8 and recommend a final design height.
4. State two design improvements that would enhance comprehension. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hierarchical Communication Process (7 marks):**

The designer follows five logical steps:

1. **Hazard Inventory (1 mark):** Reuse the Module 1 hazard list; identify residual hazards that survive engineering controls.
2. **Signal Word Mapping (2 marks):** DANGER → red panel, white text, used for imminent fatal hazards. WARNING → orange panel, black text, used for serious but not imminent hazards. CAUTION → yellow panel with black diagonal stripes, used for minor hazards. NOTICE → blue or black/white, used for property-damage-only messages.
3. **Channel Selection (1 mark):** Choose from on-product label, user manual, training, digital UI, auditory alarm, tactile indicator. Apply redundancy — at least two independent channels for high-severity hazards.
4. **Design Standard Application (2 marks):** Apply ANSI Z535 typography, ISO 3864 pictogram proportions (black-on-yellow for warning, white-on-red for prohibition), and WCAG contrast ratio $\ge 4.5$.
5. **Comprehension Validation (1 mark):** User test with target population; comprehension rate $C\% = (N_{correct}/N_{total}) \times 100 \ge 85\%$ is the pass criterion.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Given data:**
$$ MVD \;=\; 800 \text{ mm}, \quad k_{visual,\;normal} \;=\; 300, \quad k_{visual,\;6/12} \;=\; 150 $$

**Step 2 — Minimum height for normal vision:**
$$ h_{min,\;normal} \;=\; \frac{MVD}{k_{visual,\;normal}} \;=\; \frac{800}{300} \;=\; 2.67 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 3 — Minimum height for 6/12 vision:**
$$ h_{min,\;6/12} \;=\; \frac{MVD}{k_{visual,\;6/12}} \;=\; \frac{800}{150} \;=\; 5.33 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 4 — Apply safety factor $SF = 1.8$:**
$$ h_{design,\;normal} \;=\; 2.67 \times 1.8 \;=\; 4.80 \text{ mm} $$

$$ h_{design,\;6/12} \;=\; 5.33 \times 1.8 \;=\; 9.60 \text{ mm} $$

**Recommended final design height:** $\ge 9.6$ mm to accommodate 6/12 users.

**Step 5 — Two design improvements (2 marks):**
1. Add a **pictogram** (ISO 7010 W001 — entanglement) so comprehension is independent of literacy and language.
2. Add an **auditory cue** (motor-overload beep) for users who are not visually attending the label.

*(Award marks as follows: 1 mark for Step 2, 1 mark for Step 3, 2 marks for Step 4, 2 marks for Step 5, 1 mark for final recommendation.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Describe the **multi-channel redundancy principle** in safety communication. Why is relying on a single label considered a weak design? Explain with a real-world engineering example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chemical drum has the following safety information: signal word "DANGER," white text on a red panel, and a skull-and-crossbones pictogram. After a comprehension test with 60 operators, 48 correctly identified the poison hazard without reading the text. Calculate the comprehension rate $C\%$. Does it pass the ANSI Z535.6 threshold? What corrective action is recommended if it fails? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Multi-Channel Redundancy (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Multi-channel redundancy is the deliberate duplication of a safety message across **at least two independent sensory channels** (visual + auditory + tactile + textual) so that the message survives partial system failure, environmental degradation, or user sensory impairment.

**Why single-label design is weak (2 marks):**
- A user with **red-green color blindness** (~8% of males globally) cannot perceive red DANGER panels.
- A user with **low literacy** cannot decode the text.
- A **greasy or dusty** industrial environment can render a label unreadable.
- A user **looking away** from the label cannot perceive a purely visual signal.

**Real-world example (3 marks):**
A modern CNC milling machine emits (i) a **visual red beacon** when the enclosure is open, (ii) an **audible 95 dB alarm**, (iii) a **tactile vibration** on the operator's control handle, and (iv) a **digital on-screen overlay**. If the operator is hearing-impaired, the vibration and visual beacon still communicate. If the operator is visually distracted, the alarm and vibration still alert. This is defense-in-depth communication.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Apply the comprehension equation:**
$$ C\% \;=\; \frac{N_{correct}}{N_{total}} \times 100 $$

$$ C\% \;=\; \frac{48}{60} \times 100 $$

$$ C\% \;=\; 80\% $$

**Step 2 — Compare with ANSI Z535.6 threshold:**
Since $80\% < 85\%$, the label **fails** the standard. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Recommended corrective actions (4 marks):**
1. **Enlarge the pictogram** to occupy $\ge 50\%$ of the label area (current best practice).
2. **Add a redundant channel** — a tactile diamond-shaped warning marker that the user feels when gripping the drum.
3. **Improve color contrast** by using pure white ($L = 1.0$) on pure red ($L \le 0.18$) to push $CR$ above 7.0.
4. **Provide multilingual supplementary text** — at minimum English plus the regional language (e.g., Malayalam for KTU students operating in Kerala).
5. **Conduct follow-up comprehension testing** with the redesigned label to verify $C\% \ge 85\%$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the signal words:** Students often write "DANGER = minor risk" — this is the most common 0-mark answer. Memorize: DANGER = **death/serious injury imminent**.
> 2. **Skipping the comprehension-rate threshold:** A calculation that yields $C\% = 80\%$ without stating "this fails the 85% ANSI threshold" loses 1 full mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the safety factor:** The Snellen equation gives the **absolute minimum**. A design that ships at exactly $h_{min}$ will fail in the field; KTU expects a safety factor $\ge 1.5$.
> 4. **Treating communication as documentation:** Students who say "write a manual" without mentioning pictograms, color, contrast, or redundancy lose up to 4 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring impaired vision:** Always compute $h_{min}$ for **both** 6/6 and an impaired case (typically 6/12 or 6/18). Examiners explicitly test inclusive design.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **Communicate the Concept** = the engineered transfer of residual-risk information to the user.
- **Four signal words:** DANGER (red) > WARNING (orange) > CAUTION (yellow) > NOTICE (blue/black).
- **Two governing standards to memorize:** **ANSI Z535** (US) and **ISO 3864 / ISO 7010** (international).
- **Minimum letter height:** $h_{min} = MVD / k_{visual}$, with $k_{visual} = 300$ (normal) or $30$ (severely impaired).
- **Always apply a safety factor** of $\ge 1.5$ to $h_{min}$.
- **Contrast ratio formula:** $CR = (L_{lighter} + 0.05) / (L_{darker} + 0.05)$; threshold $\ge 4.5$ (normal text) or $\ge 7.0$ (large text).
- **Comprehension rate:** $C\% = (N_{correct}/N_{total}) \times 100 \ge 85\%$ per ANSI Z535.6.
- **Multi-channel redundancy** is mandatory for high-severity hazards — never rely on a single label.
- **Pictograms** (ISO 7010) provide language-independent communication.
- **Color-coding conventions:** Red = danger/prohibition, Orange = warning, Yellow = caution, Blue = mandatory action, Green = safety.
- **Post-market surveillance** is required — communication must be updated after field incidents.
- **Inaccessible design fails** — design for color-blind, low-vision, low-literacy users.
- **Signal word selection depends on:** Severity × Probability of the residual hazard.
- **Hierarchy of communication layers:** Hazard ID → Signal Word → Channel → Standard → Label → Validate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measure Customer Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT747 - SAFETY IN PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Measure Customer Response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Customer Response Measurement (CRM)** in the context of Safety in Product Design is the systematic, quantitative, and qualitative process of capturing, analyzing, and interpreting end-user feedback regarding a product's safety performance, reliability, usability, and post-deployment hazard exposure. It forms the empirical backbone of *post-market surveillance* and acts as the closing loop of the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** cycle in safety-critical product development.

According to **ISO 9001:2015 (Clause 9.1.2 — Customer Satisfaction)** and **ISO 13485 (Medical Devices — Post-Market Surveillance)**, organizations are mandated to **monitor customer perceptions** of the degree to which their needs and expectations have been fulfilled. For safety-engineered products, this translates into measuring how end-users perceive risk control, ergonomic safety, alarm effectiveness, fail-safe behavior, and emergency response.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Paraphrase):**
> *"Measure Customer Response" refers to the deployment of structured instruments—surveys, complaint databases, warranty analytics, field-failure reports, and social-listen metrics—to quantify how a customer reacts to the safety features, hazard communication, and protective mechanisms embedded in a product.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a hospital that performs surgery on a patient. The operation may be *technically* flawless (the surgeon uses sterile instruments, correct incision, proper suturing), but the patient's recovery experience — pain levels, mobility, anxiety, confidence in the implant — is what determines whether the treatment is **truly successful**.

Similarly, a product may pass all *engineering* safety tests (EMC, drop, drop, thermal, IP rating), yet fail in the **field** because customers bypass safety interlocks, ignore warning labels, or find protective gear uncomfortable.

> **Analogy Recap:**
> - **Engineering Test** = Surgeon's technique ✔
> - **Customer Response** = Patient's recovery experience 🩺
> - **A safe product = passes lab tests AND survives customer reality**

**Measurement instruments** are the "post-operative questionnaires" the design team uses to learn whether the safety architecture was actually effective *in the wild*.

---

## 1.3 Key Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Full Form | Scale | Purpose |
|--------|-----------|-------|---------|
| **CSAT** | Customer Satisfaction Score | 1–5 or 1–7 | Direct happiness rating |
| **NPS** | Net Promoter Score | –100 to +100 | Loyalty indicator |
| **CES** | Customer Effort Score | 1–7 | Ease of use / ergonomics |
| **FCR** | First Contact Resolution | % | Safety complaint handling |
| **AHT** | Average Handling Time | minutes | Support responsiveness |
| **VOC** | Voice of Customer | Qualitative | Unstructured insights |

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Customer Response in Safety Design:**
> 1. **Proactive Capture** — Surveys, focus groups, beta testing
> 2. **Reactive Capture** — Complaints, recalls, warranty claims
> 3. **Continuous Monitoring** — Telemetry, IoT sensors, field data loggers

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Safety Engineering

In KTU's 2024 Scheme, Module 3 typically bridges *theoretical safety frameworks* with *real-world validation*. Without measuring customer response:

- Designers **assume** safety features are used → Risk of *safety-feature bypass*
- Hazard labels are written but never **read** → Communication failure
- Recalls go **unreported** → Cascade failures in the field
- Liability frameworks **collapse** without documented customer outreach

Customer response data is the **only objective evidence** that a product's safety claims hold under realistic operating conditions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Customer Response Measurement Framework

Customer response measurement in safety design operates across **three temporal layers**:

### Layer 1 — Pre-Launch (Predictive)
- **Beta testing panels** with diverse demographics (age, dexterity, literacy)
- **Simulated-use testing** with hazard scenario walk-throughs
- **Kano Model surveys** to classify safety features as *Must-be*, *One-dimensional*, *Attractive*, or *Indifferent*

### Layer 2 — Launch (Concurrent)
- **In-app safety prompts** (digital products) recording user compliance
- **Call-center logs** for safety helplines
- **Real-time complaint categorization** using AI-driven text mining

### Layer 3 — Post-Launch (Retrospective)
- **Warranty claim analysis** — correlates with safety incidents
- **Field Failure Return Analysis (FFRA)**
- **Social media sentiment analysis** for safety incidents
- **Adverse Event Reports (AER)** for medical / automotive / aerospace

---

## 2.2 The Kano Model — Classifying Customer Safety Needs

Professor Noriaki Kano's model (1984) classifies product attributes into five categories:

| Category | Customer Reaction if Present | Customer Reaction if Absent |
|----------|-----------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Must-be (Basic)** | Neutral | Extreme dissatisfaction |
| **One-dimensional (Performance)** | Satisfaction proportional to quality | Dissatisfaction proportional to poor quality |
| **Attractive (Excitement)** | High delight | No reaction |
| **Indifferent** | No reaction | No reaction |
| **Reverse** | Dissatisfaction | Satisfaction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In safety design, MOST safety features are *Must-be* characteristics.** A customer does not *delight* in a seatbelt — they *expect* it. Absence = catastrophic dissatisfaction (lawsuits, recalls).

**Kano Evaluation Table (Partial — Memorize for KTU Exam):**

| Customer Requirement (Negative Form) | Functional (Positive Form) | Dysfunctional (Negative Form) |
|--------------------------------------|------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| "If the product has a child-lock, how do you feel?" | "If the product has NO child-lock, how do you feel?" |

**Evaluation Codes:**
- **A** = Attractive
- **M** = Must-be
- **O** = One-dimensional
- **I** = Indifferent
- **R** = Reverse

---

## 2.3 KPI Formulas — The Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are the **most-asked quantitative items** in KTU ESE for this topic.

### 2.3.1 Net Promoter Score (NPS)

$$
\text{NPS} = \%\,\text{Promoters} - \%\,\text{Detractors}
$$

where respondents are categorized based on a 0–10 scale:

$$
\text{Promoter}: \text{Score} \in [9,\,10]
$$

$$
\text{Passive}: \text{Score} \in [7,\,8]
$$

$$
\text{Detractor}: \text{Score} \in [0,\,6]
$$

### 2.3.2 Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT)

$$
\text{CSAT} = \frac{\text{Number of satisfied responses (4 or 5 on 5-pt scale)}}{\text{Total responses}} \times 100
$$

### 2.3.3 Customer Effort Score (CES)

$$
\text{CES} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Effort rating}_i}{n}
$$

where lower CES = safer, more ergonomic design.

### 2.3.4 Complaint Rate (CR)

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{\text{Number of safety-related complaints}}{\text{Units sold}} \times 10^6
$$

This is expressed in **Parts Per Million (PPM)**.

### 2.3.5 First Contact Resolution (FCR)

$$
\text{FCR} = \frac{\text{Complaints resolved on first contact}}{\text{Total safety complaints}} \times 100
$$

### 2.3.6 Sample Size for Customer Survey (Finite Population)

$$
n = \frac{N \cdot z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{(N-1) \cdot e^2 + z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}
$$

where:
- $N$ = population size
- $z$ = Z-score for confidence level (**1.96** for 95% confidence)
- $p$ = estimated proportion (**0.5** for max variability)
- $e$ = margin of error

### 2.3.7 Kano Category Identification

For each pair of responses (functional $F$ + dysfunctional $D$):

$$
\text{Category} = f(F,\,D)
$$

$$
\text{Customer Satisfaction Coefficient:} \quad \text{CS} = \frac{O + A}{O + A + M + I}
$$

$$
\text{Customer Dissatisfaction Coefficient:} \quad \text{DS} = \frac{O + M}{O + A + M + I}
$$

Higher CS = better at delighting; Higher DS = more critical to satisfy.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

| Industry | Customer Response Measurement Practice |
|----------|----------------------------------------|
| **Automotive (ISO 26262)** | Field-recall analytics, J.D. Power IQS surveys |
| **Medical Devices (ISO 14971)** | MAUDE database, adverse-event reporting |
| **Consumer Electronics** | App-store reviews, RMA rate, NPS tracking |
| **Industrial Machinery** | Operator feedback logs, near-miss reporting |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Pharmacovigilance, patient-reported outcomes (PROs) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently frame this as: *"How does customer response measurement feed back into risk reduction?"* — Answer: It updates the **severity** and **probability** values in the FMEA / Risk Matrix.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: NPS Calculation for a Safety-Critical Product

### Problem Setup
A manufacturer of industrial pressure cookers surveyed **500 customers** post-launch about the *safety valve reliability*. Responses (on a 0–10 scale):

| Score | Count |
|-------|-------|
| 10 | 110 |
| 9 | 90 |
| 8 | 80 |
| 7 | 60 |
| 6 | 70 |
| 5 | 40 |
| 4 | 30 |
| 3 | 10 |
| 2 | 5 |
| 1 | 3 |
| 0 | 2 |

**Compute the NPS and interpret.**

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Classify respondents by category.

By definition:
- Promoters: Score ∈ [9, 10] → Count = 110 + 90 = **200**
- Passives: Score ∈ [7, 8] → Count = 60 + 80 = **140**
- Detractors: Score ∈ [0, 6] → Count = 70 + 40 + 30 + 10 + 5 + 3 + 2 = **160**

**Verification:**
$$
\text{Total} = 200 + 140 + 160 = 500 \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 2:** Compute promoter and detractor percentages.

$$
\%\,\text{Promoters} = \frac{200}{500} \times 100 = 40\,\%
$$

$$
\%\,\text{Detractors} = \frac{160}{500} \times 100 = 32\,\%
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the NPS formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPS} &= \%\,\text{Promoters} - \%\,\text{Detractors} \\
&= 40\% - 32\% \\
&= 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation.

| NPS Range | Interpretation |
|-----------|----------------|
| $< 0$ | Critical — More detractors than promoters; safety concern escalated |
| $0$ to $30$ | Good — Acceptable customer sentiment |
| $30$ to $70$ | Great — Strong customer advocacy |
| $> 70$ | World-class |

**Result:** NPS = **+8** → Indicates a *good but improvable* safety perception. The design team should target detractor reduction through improved safety-valve documentation and visual warning clarity.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Customer Satisfaction Coefficient (CS) for a Kano Survey

### Problem Setup
A product team received **1000 Kano questionnaire responses** regarding an *automatic emergency-stop (E-stop) feature* on a CNC machine. The categorized results:

| Category | Count |
|----------|-------|
| Must-be (M) | 420 |
| One-dimensional (O) | 280 |
| Attractive (A) | 150 |
| Indifferent (I) | 100 |
| Reverse (R) | 30 |
| Questionable (Q) | 20 |

**Compute CS and DS coefficients.**

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Sum the non-reject categories (excluding R and Q).

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{valid}} &= M + O + A + I \\
&= 420 + 280 + 150 + 100 \\
&= 950
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the CS formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CS} &= \frac{O + A}{O + A + M + I} \\
&= \frac{280 + 150}{950} \\
&= \frac{430}{950} \\
&= 0.4526 \approx \mathbf{0.45}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the DS formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DS} &= \frac{O + M}{O + A + M + I} \\
&= \frac{280 + 420}{950} \\
&= \frac{700}{950} \\
&= 0.7368 \approx \mathbf{0.74}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation.

| Coefficient | Value | Meaning |
|-------------|-------|---------|
| **CS** | 0.45 | Moderate ability to delight; the E-stop is functional but not exciting |
| **DS** | 0.74 | **Very high** dissatisfaction potential if E-stop fails — confirms it as a **Must-be** safety attribute |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** High DS + Low/Moderate CS = **classic safety-critical feature**. Design teams must over-invest in reliability, NOT in novelty.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Sample Size Calculation for Safety Survey

### Problem Setup
A company wants to survey **N = 50{,}000** customers who purchased a new electrical appliance. They want **95% confidence**, margin of error **e = 0.03**, and assume maximum variability **p = 0.5**.

**Compute the minimum sample size.**

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify parameters.
- $N = 50{,}000$
- $z = 1.96$ (for 95% confidence)
- $p = 0.5$, hence $1 - p = 0.5$
- $e = 0.03$

**Step 2:** Compute $p(1-p)$.

$$
p(1-p) = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= N \cdot z^2 \cdot p(1-p) \\
&= 50{,}000 \times (1.96)^2 \times 0.25 \\
&= 50{,}000 \times 3.8416 \times 0.25 \\
&= 50{,}000 \times 0.9604 \\
&= 48{,}020
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Denominator} &= (N-1) \cdot e^2 + z^2 \cdot p(1-p) \\
&= 49{,}999 \times (0.03)^2 + 3.8416 \times 0.25 \\
&= 49{,}999 \times 0.0009 + 0.9604 \\
&= 44.9991 + 0.9604 \\
&= 45.9595
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Divide.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{48{,}020}{45.9595} \\
&\approx \mathbf{1044.8}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Minimum sample size = **1045 respondents** (rounded up).

**Interpretation:** With 1045 valid responses, the safety team can estimate the true customer satisfaction proportion within ±3% at 95% confidence — a robust statistical foundation for safety-related product redesign decisions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Customer Response Measurement Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[Customer in Field] --> node2[Hazard or Safe Event Encountered]
    node2 --> node3{Response Channel}
    
    node3 --> node4[Proactive Channel: Survey, Beta Test, Focus Group]
    node3 --> node5[Reactive Channel: Complaint, Warranty, Recall]
    node3 --> node6[Continuous Channel: Telemetry, IoT Logs]
    
    node4 --> node7[Central CRM Database]
    node5 --> node7
    node6 --> node7
    
    node7 --> node8[Data Normalization and Cleansing]
    node8 --> node9[Quantitative Analysis: NPS, CSAT, CES]
    node8 --> node10[Qualitative Analysis: VOC Text Mining, Kano Coding]
    
    node9 --> node11[Risk Matrix Update: Severity and Probability]
    node10 --> node11
    
    node11 --> node12[Design Review Board]
    node12 --> node13{Decision: Redesign Required?}
    
    node13 -->|Yes| node14[Trigger FMEA Update, CAPA, Safety Label Redesign]
    node13 -->|No| node15[Document and Archive Findings]
    
    node14 --> node16[Next Generation Product]
    node15 --> node16
    node16 --> node1
```

---

## 4.2 Kano Classification Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start[Customer Response Received] --> pair{Pair Functional and Dysfunctional}
    
    pair --> q1{Functional = Like?}
    pair --> q2{Dysfunctional = Dislike?}
    
    q1 -->|Yes| aCheck{Attractive Candidate}
    q1 -->|No| q3{Functional = Expect?}
    
    q3 -->|Yes| oCheck{One-Dimensional Candidate}
    q3 -->|No| q4{Functional = Neutral?}
    
    q4 -->|Yes| mCheck{Must-be Candidate}
    q4 -->|No| iOut[Indifferent]
    
    aCheck --> aOut[Attractive]
    oCheck --> oOut[One-Dimensional]
    mCheck --> mOut[Must-be]
    
    aOut --> kanoResult[Kano Matrix Cell]
    oOut --> kanoResult
    mOut --> kanoResult
    iOut --> kanoResult
```

---

## 4.3 Closed-Loop Safety Improvement Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    p1[Plan: Define Safety Requirements] --> p2[Design: Build Protective Features]
    p2 --> p3[Validate: Lab and Field Tests]
    p3 --> p4[Deploy: Customer Receives Product]
    p4 --> p5[Measure: Capture Customer Response]
    p5 --> p6[Analyze: Compute NPS, CSAT, Complaint Rate]
    p6 --> p7{Action Triggered?}
    p7 -->|Yes| p8[Update Risk Register and FMEA]
    p7 -->|No| p9[Continue Monitoring]
    p8 --> p1
    p9 --> p1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation for KTU Exam:** Notice how *Measure Customer Response* closes the loop between **deployment** and **next-generation planning**. Without this loop, safety improvements cannot be evidence-based.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Define Net Promoter Score (NPS). Explain how it is computed and discuss its relevance in safety-critical product evaluation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Net Promoter Score (NPS) is a customer loyalty metric introduced by Fred Reichheld (2003) that quantifies the likelihood of a customer recommending a product to others. It is computed using the formula:

$$
\text{NPS} = \%\,\text{Promoters} - \%\,\text{Detractors}
$$

Respondents rating **9 or 10** are classified as *Promoters*, **7 or 8** as *Passives*, and **0 to 6** as *Detractors*. The resulting score ranges from **−100 to +100**.

In safety-critical products, NPS acts as a **leading indicator** of field-failure risk. A low or declining NPS correlates with rising safety complaints, near-miss reports, and warranty claims. Safety engineering teams therefore track NPS trends as part of post-market surveillance under ISO 9001:2015 and ISO 14971 frameworks. **A negative NPS triggers mandatory root-cause investigation.**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: What is the Kano Model? List its five categories and explain why safety features typically fall under the "Must-be" category.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Kano Model, developed by Professor Noriaki Kano (1984), is a theory of product development and customer satisfaction that classifies product attributes based on how they influence customer perception.

**Five Categories:**
1. **Must-be (Basic)** — Expected; absence causes extreme dissatisfaction
2. **One-dimensional (Performance)** — Satisfaction proportional to quality
3. **Attractive (Excitement)** — Delight when present; no reaction when absent
4. **Indifferent** — No impact either way
5. **Reverse** — Opposite reaction from different customer segments

Safety features are predominantly **Must-be** because customers do not *delight* in safety — they *expect* it. The presence of a seatbelt, E-stop button, or child-lock does not earn praise, but their absence leads to severe dissatisfaction, regulatory penalties, and legal liability. Hence, safety features must be **over-engineered for reliability**, not novelty.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of "Measure Customer Response" in the context of safety in product design. Discuss at least **five quantitative metrics** used for this purpose, with their formulas and engineering significance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturer of electric vehicle (EV) charging stations surveyed **800 customers** post-deployment. The responses on a 0–10 scale were as follows:

| Score | Count |
|-------|-------|
| 10 | 150 |
| 9 | 120 |
| 8 | 100 |
| 7 | 80 |
| 6 | 90 |
| 5 | 80 |
| 4 | 60 |
| 3 | 50 |
| 2 | 40 |
| 1 | 20 |
| 0 | 10 |

Compute the **NPS** and the **CSAT** (using the standard 5-point satisfaction threshold of ≥ 4 on a 5-point scale, where scores 8–10 = satisfied, 0–7 = not satisfied). Interpret the results from a safety perspective. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Definition:**
"Measure Customer Response" is the systematic capture, quantification, and analysis of customer feedback regarding product safety features, hazard communication, and incident handling.

**2. Five Quantitative Metrics:**

**Metric 1: Net Promoter Score (NPS)**

$$
\text{NPS} = \%\,\text{Promoters} - \%\,\text{Detractors}
$$

Significance: Leading indicator of safety loyalty; negative NPS escalates root-cause analysis.

**Metric 2: Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT)**

$$
\text{CSAT} = \frac{\text{Satisfied responses}}{\text{Total responses}} \times 100
$$

Significance: Direct measure of post-deployment safety perception.

**Metric 3: Complaint Rate (CR) in PPM**

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{\text{Safety complaints}}{\text{Units sold}} \times 10^6
$$

Significance: Tracks absolute safety failure frequency; benchmarked across product lines.

**Metric 4: First Contact Resolution (FCR)**

$$
\text{FCR} = \frac{\text{First-contact resolutions}}{\text{Total safety complaints}} \times 100
$$

Significance: Measures responsiveness of safety support infrastructure.

**Metric 5: Customer Effort Score (CES)**

$$
\text{CES} = \frac{\sum \text{Effort ratings}}{n}
$$

Significance: Lower CES indicates ergonomic and procedural safety is intuitive.

**Engineering Significance:**
These metrics feed the FMEA severity/probability values, drive CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action) prioritization, and satisfy ISO 9001:2015 Clause 9.1.2 monitoring requirements.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Classify respondents.

- Promoters (9–10): 150 + 120 = **270**
- Passives (7–8): 100 + 80 = **180**
- Detractors (0–6): 90 + 80 + 60 + 50 + 40 + 20 + 10 = **350**

**Verification:** 270 + 180 + 350 = 800 ✓

**Step 2:** Compute percentages.

$$
\%\,\text{Promoters} = \frac{270}{800} \times 100 = 33.75\,\%
$$

$$
\%\,\text{Detractors} = \frac{350}{800} \times 100 = 43.75\,\%
$$

**Step 3:** Compute NPS.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPS} &= 33.75\% - 43.75\% \\
&= \mathbf{-10}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute CSAT.

The problem states: scores 8–10 = satisfied, 0–7 = not satisfied.

$$
\text{Satisfied} = 150 + 120 + 100 = 370
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CSAT} &= \frac{370}{800} \times 100 \\
&= \mathbf{46.25\,\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation.

| Metric | Value | Verdict |
|--------|-------|---------|
| NPS | −10 | **Negative** — Detractors outnumber promoters; safety concern **escalated** |
| CSAT | 46.25% | **Below 50%** — Less than half the customer base is satisfied |

**Safety Implication:** The EV charging stations exhibit significant safety-related dissatisfaction. Likely root causes include connector-lock failures, ground-fault trips, or unclear hazard signage. The design team must initiate a **CAPA review**, update the **FMEA**, and conduct **field audits** of installed units.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**Q: (a)** Explain the **Kano Model** in detail. Discuss the **Customer Satisfaction Coefficient (CS)** and **Customer Dissatisfaction Coefficient (DS)** with their formulas, and describe how they are used to prioritize safety features. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Kano survey for a new industrial drill's *emergency-reverse feature* yielded the following responses from **1200 customers**:

| Category | Count |
|----------|-------|
| Must-be (M) | 540 |
| One-dimensional (O) | 320 |
| Attractive (A) | 180 |
| Indifferent (I) | 100 |
| Reverse (R) | 40 |
| Questionable (Q) | 20 |

Compute **CS** and **DS** coefficients. Based on the results, justify whether the emergency-reverse feature should be classified as a **Must-be** safety attribute and what design priority it warrants. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Kano Model Overview:**
The Kano Model (Noriaki Kano, 1984) classifies customer preferences into five categories based on the relationship between feature presence and customer satisfaction.

**Five Categories (Detailed):**

1. **Must-be (Basic):** Customer takes it for granted. Absence causes extreme dissatisfaction; presence gives no extra satisfaction. Example: E-stop on a machine.
2. **One-dimensional (Performance):** Satisfaction rises linearly with quality. Better performance = more satisfaction.
3. **Attractive (Excitement):** Unexpected delight. Increases satisfaction if present, no penalty if absent.
4. **Indifferent:** Customer does not care.
5. **Reverse:** Different customer segments want opposite outcomes.

**Customer Satisfaction Coefficient (CS):**

$$
\text{CS} = \frac{O + A}{O + A + M + I}
$$

Higher CS → Stronger positive impact on satisfaction. The feature is "good to have."

**Customer Dissatisfaction Coefficient (DS):**

$$
\text{DS} = \frac{O + M}{O + A + M + I}
$$

Higher DS → Stronger negative impact if absent. The feature is "critical to satisfy."

**Use in Safety Feature Prioritization:**

For safety features, designers compute both CS and DS. A high DS (typically > 0.5) signals a **Must-be** safety feature. The design priority is then **reliability over novelty** — meaning resources are allocated to fail-safe design, redundancy, and rigorous testing, NOT to aesthetic enhancement.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Compute valid responses (excluding R and Q).

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{valid}} &= M + O + A + I \\
&= 540 + 320 + 180 + 100 \\
&= 1140
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the Customer Satisfaction Coefficient.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CS} &= \frac{O + A}{O + A + M + I} \\
&= \frac{320 + 180}{1140} \\
&= \frac{500}{1140} \\
&= 0.4386 \approx \mathbf{0.44}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the Customer Dissatisfaction Coefficient.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DS} &= \frac{O + M}{O + A + M + I} \\
&= \frac{320 + 540}{1140} \\
&= \frac{860}{1140} \\
&= 0.7544 \approx \mathbf{0.75}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation Table.

| Coefficient | Value | Threshold | Verdict |
|-------------|-------|-----------|---------|
| CS | 0.44 | < 0.5 | Modest delight potential |
| DS | 0.75 | > 0.5 | **Very high dissatisfaction risk if absent** |

**Step 5:** Justification and Design Priority.

A DS of 0.75 is well above the 0.5 threshold, confirming the emergency-reverse feature as a **Must-be safety attribute**. CS at 0.44 indicates it is **not** an attractive feature — customers do not "delight" in it; they *expect* it.

**Design Priority Recommendation:**
1. Allocate budget toward **reliability engineering** — fail-safe circuits, dual-channel triggers, redundant sensors.
2. Conduct **ISO 13849 PL=d** or higher safety integrity verification.
3. Document the feature as **non-negotiable** in the marketing collateral to avoid over-promising.
4. Conduct field-trial surveys every 6 months to monitor DS trend.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students write CS = (O + A) / Total Responses (including R and Q). Always exclude R and Q. **Lose 2 marks** if missed.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In NPS, students forget to convert counts to **percentages** before subtraction. Always compute % first, then subtract.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** When asked "is this a Must-be?", students answer with category names but **fail to support with numerical thresholds** (CS < 0.5 AND DS > 0.5). Always cite both coefficients.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to **state engineering significance** in Part A — examiners award 1–2 marks for the "why this matters" component.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Customer Response Measurement** captures end-user feedback on safety features across three temporal layers: Pre-launch, Launch, Post-launch.
- ✅ **Five Key Metrics:** NPS, CSAT, CES, FCR, Complaint Rate (PPM).
