\text{Score}_{\text{Integral}} &= (L_{\text{variety}}, L_{\text{std}}, H_{\text{mfg}}, L_{\text{change}}, H_{\text{perf}}, H_{\text{cost,NRE}})
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Apply Weighted Decision Logic

Weight each implication according to **strategic priority**:

$$
\text{Architecture Score} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i \cdot s_i
$$

where $w_i$ is the strategic weight of implication $i$ and $s_i$ is the normalized score (1 = Low, 2 = Medium, 3 = High).

> **Example calculation** for a **safety-critical medical infusion pump**:
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \text{Modular Score} &= (3 \times 0.20) + (3 \times 0.10) + (2 \times 0.10) + (3 \times 0.25) + (2 \times 0.20) + (1 \times 0.15) \\
> &= 0.60 + 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.75 + 0.40 + 0.15 \\
> &= 2.40
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \text{Integral Score} &= (1 \times 0.20) + (1 \times 0.10) + (3 \times 0.10) + (1 \times 0.25) + (3 \times 0.20) + (3 \times 0.15) \\
> &= 0.20 + 0.10 + 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.60 + 0.45 \\
> &= 1.90
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> Since $2.40 > 1.90$, the **modular architecture** wins because safety-critical change-out and variety outweigh manufacturing cost.

#### Step 6 — Document the Safety Justification

For KTU PESFT747, every architecture decision must include a **safety justification clause** referencing:
- Hazard identification
- Failure mode tolerance
- Maintainability and serviceability
- Recall feasibility

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture Implications Flow (Mermaid Block-Level Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Product Architecture Decision"] --> B["Architectural Strategy"]
    B --> C1["Modular Mapping"]
    B --> C2["Integral Mapping"]
    C1 --> D1["High Variety"]
    C1 --> D2["High Standardization"]
    C1 --> D3["Medium Manufacturability"]
    C1 --> D4["High Product Change"]
    C1 --> D5["Medium Performance"]
    C1 --> D6["Low Development Cost"]
    C2 --> E1["Low Variety"]
    C2 --> E2["Low Standardization"]
    C2 --> E3["High Manufacturability"]
    C2 --> E4["Low Product Change"]
    C2 --> E5["High Performance"]
    C2 --> E6["High Development Cost"]
    D1 --> F["Safety and Lifecycle Outcome"]
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    D4 --> F
    D5 --> F
    D6 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    E6 --> F
    F --> G["Design for Safety Decision"]
```

### 4.2 Six Implications Spider Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IM["Six Implications of Architecture"]
        direction TB
        N1["Variety"]
        N2["Standardization"]
        N3["Manufacturability"]
        N4["Product Change"]
        N5["Product Performance"]
        N6["Cost"]
    end
    HUB["Architecture Choice"] --- N1
    HUB --- N2
    HUB --- N3
    HUB --- N4
    HUB --- N5
    HUB --- N6
    N1 --> SAFE["Safety and Serviceability"]
    N4 --> SAFE
    N5 --> SAFE
```

### 4.3 Modular vs. Integral Architecture Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph MOD["MODULAR ARCHITECTURE"]
        M1["Function A → Block A"]
        M2["Function B → Block B"]
        M3["Function C → Block C"]
        M1 -.->|Standard Interface| M2
        M2 -.->|Standard Interface| M3
    end
    subgraph INT["INTEGRAL ARCHITECTURE"]
        I1["Function A"]
        I2["Function B"]
        I3["Function C"]
        I1 --> X["Shared Block XYZ"]
        I2 --> X
        I3 --> X
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "explain with a diagram," always **label** the nodes with the implication names (e.g., "Variety", "Standardization") rather than just "Box A" / "Box B." Examiners reward **explicit labeling**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**Q1. Define product architecture and list any four implications of the architectural choice.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Product architecture is the scheme by which the functional elements of a product are arranged into physical building blocks and by which these blocks interact. The four key implications are:

1. **Variety** — ability to create product variants.
2. **Standardization** — reuse of common components.
3. **Manufacturability** — ease of production.
4. **Product Change** — ease of upgrading or servicing.

*(Fourth point can be any two of: Performance, Cost, Development Time.)* ✅

---

**Q2. Differentiate between modular and integral architecture with one product example each.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Modular Architecture** | **Integral Architecture** |
|---|---|---|
| Function-to-Block Mapping | One-to-one | Many-to-one |
| Interfaces | Standard, clean | Complex, coupled |
| Example | Desktop computer (CPU, RAM, GPU as separate modules) | Single-blade kitchen knife (handle and blade forged as one piece) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Adapted Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **six major implications** of product architecture with reference to variety, standardization, and manufacturability. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A consumer electronics company is launching a **family of three smart speakers** (Mini, Standard, Pro) targeting different price points. Discuss how the choice of **modular architecture** affects product variety, change, and cost in this scenario. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

The six major implications of product architecture are:

1. **Implications for Variety** — A modular architecture enables creation of a product family with multiple variants by combining different modules. An integral architecture restricts variety because each variant requires a near-complete redesign.

2. **Implications for Standardization** — Modular architectures promote reuse of standardized components (e.g., common screws, connectors, PCBs). Integral architectures have low standardization because parts are unique to each design.

3. **Implications for Manufacturability** — Integral architectures may have fewer parts and simpler final assembly, reducing direct labor. Modular architectures benefit from **postponement** — assembling the common base first and differentiating later.

4. **Implications for Product Change** — Modular designs support field upgrades and easy service. Integral designs make changes expensive.

5. **Implications for Product Performance** — Integral designs allow holistic optimization (better aerodynamics, tighter tolerances). Modular designs may incur interface losses.

6. **Implications for Cost** — Modular designs reduce NRE (non-recurring engineering) cost but increase BOM cost. Integral designs lower per-unit cost but raise development risk.

*(7 marks awarded for: 6 implications × 1 mark each + 1 mark for structured presentation.)* ✅

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

For the smart-speaker family, a **modular architecture** is highly advantageous:

- **Variety Impact (≈ 2.5 marks):** The Mini, Standard, and Pro can share a **common core module** (microcontroller, power supply, Wi-Fi chip) while differing only in their **audio output module** (small driver, mid driver, large driver + subwoofer). This yields 3 distinct SKUs from 2 module types, dramatically increasing variety without tripling development effort.

- **Change Impact (≈ 2.5 marks):** When a new Wi-Fi standard (e.g., Wi-Fi 7) is released, only the **communications module** needs to be redesigned and re-certified. The audio and housing modules remain untouched. This reduces engineering change lead time from months to weeks.

- **Cost Impact (≈ 2 marks):** Development cost is amortized across all three variants, lowering the per-variant NRE. However, the BOM cost may be slightly higher due to connectors and module housings. The net effect is a **lower total cost of ownership** for the product family.

*(7 marks awarded for: clarity of implication mapping × 3, example justification × 2, conclusion × 1, presentation × 1.)* ✅

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss how **product architecture** influences **product performance** and **product change** with suitable engineering examples. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A manufacturer of **industrial safety helmets** must choose between modular and integral architecture. Evaluate the choice using a **weighted decision matrix** and recommend the safer option. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

- **Performance Influence (≈ 3.5 marks):** Integral architectures allow designers to optimize the product as a **single system**. Example: A monocoque car chassis integrates the frame and body, reducing weight by 20–30% and improving crash energy absorption. Modular architectures, by contrast, introduce **interface penalties** — connectors add electrical resistance, joints add mechanical play.

- **Change Influence (≈ 3.5 marks):** Modular architectures dominate here. Example: Modern smartphones allow **battery replacement** and **camera module upgrade** independently. Integral architectures (e.g., early iPhones with glued-in batteries) make changes difficult and **unsafe to service**, often requiring full device replacement.

> **Trade-off synthesis:** High-performance products (Formula 1 cars) lean integral; high-change products (personal computers) lean modular. Most safety-critical products (medical devices, avionics) adopt **hybrid modular-integral** architectures.

*(7 marks awarded.)* ✅

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Weighted Decision Matrix for Safety Helmet:**

| **Implication** | **Weight** | **Modular Score (1–3)** | **Integral Score (1–3)** | **Modular Weighted** | **Integral Weighted** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Safety (Impact attenuation) | 0.30 | 2 | 3 | 0.60 | 0.90 |
| Manufacturability | 0.10 | 2 | 3 | 0.20 | 0.30 |
| Change (Liner replacement) | 0.25 | 3 | 1 | 0.75 | 0.25 |
| Standardization | 0.10 | 3 | 1 | 0.30 | 0.10 |
| Cost (Per-unit) | 0.10 | 2 | 3 | 0.20 | 0.30 |
| Serviceability | 0.15 | 3 | 1 | 0.45 | 0.15 |
| **TOTAL** | **1.00** | — | — | **2.50** | **2.00** |

**Calculation for Modular Score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Modular Total} &= (0.30 \times 2) + (0.10 \times 2) + (0.25 \times 3) + (0.10 \times 3) + (0.10 \times 2) + (0.15 \times 3) \\
&= 0.60 + 0.20 + 0.75 + 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.45 \\
&= 2.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Calculation for Integral Score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Integral Total} &= (0.30 \times 3) + (0.10 \times 3) + (0.25 \times 1) + (0.10 \times 1) + (0.10 \times 3) + (0.15 \times 1) \\
&= 0.90 + 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.10 + 0.30 + 0.15 \\
&= 2.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recommendation:** Although integral architecture slightly outperforms in raw impact attenuation, the **modular architecture scores higher overall (2.50 vs 2.00)** because **liner replacement after impact** is a safety-critical service action (a helmet must be discarded after a major strike, but modular padding allows controlled re-inspection). The modular design also supports **field retrofitting** of improved padding materials as impact-data evolves.

*(7 marks awarded for: matrix construction × 3, arithmetic × 2, justification × 2.)* ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not list implications without explanation.** Writing only "Variety, Standardization, Cost..." without elaborating *how* architecture affects them will earn only 1 of 7 marks.
> 2. **Always tie architecture back to safety** in PESFT747. A purely commercial answer (cost, variety) without a safety angle is marked down 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Numerical errors in weighted matrices** are heavily penalized. Show **every arithmetic step** explicitly.
> 4. **Diagrams must be labeled.** An unlabeled block diagram is treated as "no diagram" by strict examiners.
> 5. **Don't confuse modularity with standardization.** Modularity is a *structural* property; standardization is an *outcome* enabled by modularity.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Keep This for Last-Minute Review**

- ✅ **Definition**: Product architecture = mapping of functional elements to physical blocks + their interactions.
- ✅ **Six Implications** (memorize in order): **V**ariety, **S**tandardization, **M**anufacturability, **C**hange, **P**erformance, **C**ost. Mnemonic: **"VSM-CPC"** or **"Very Smart Managers Change Poor Cars."**
- ✅ **Modular architecture** → favors variety, standardization, and change; penalizes performance.
- ✅ **Integral architecture** → favors performance and per-unit cost; penalizes variety and change.
- ✅ **Safety-critical products** (helmets, infusion pumps, brakes) generally prefer **modular** for serviceability unless performance is paramount (e.g., monocoque chassis).
- ✅ **Common Parts Index (CPI)** is a quantitative metric of standardization.
- ✅ **Weighted Decision Matrix** is the standard KTU tool for justifying architecture choice.
- ✅ **Hybrid architectures** (modular core + integral sub-assembly) are common in industry and earn bonus marks.
- ✅ **Always close your answer** with a safety justification sentence for PESFT747.
- ✅ **Recall the example**: Kettle, smartphone, car, helmet, infusion pump — at least one must appear in any 14-mark answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Establishing the Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT747 - SAFETY IN PRODUCT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Establishing the Architecture # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the discipline of **Safety in Product Design**, *Establishing the Architecture* refers to the systematic engineering process of defining, structuring, and organizing the functional, logical, and physical components of a product such that **inherent safety**, **operational safety**, and **fail-safe behavior** are guaranteed across the entire product lifecycle. According to the framework followed in the KTU 2024 Scheme (aligned with IEC 61508, ISO 12100, and ISO 26262 principles), *Establishing the Architecture* is the phase where the high-level safety strategy is decomposed into concrete subsystems, modules, hardware/software blocks, and interfaces — each with assigned **Safety Integrity Levels (SIL)**, **ASIL** ratings (automotive), or **Performance Levels (PL)** (machinery). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Architecture in Safety Design** is NOT just about drawing block diagrams. It is the formal mapping of safety functions to physical/logical elements such that every identified hazard has a documented risk-control mechanism, redundancy (if needed), and verification pathway. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are designing a **commercial passenger aircraft**. Before building it, you don't just list parts — you first decide: - *Which system controls the landing gear?* - *What happens if the primary hydraulic system fails during landing?* - *Is there a backup? Is the backup different from the primary (to avoid common-cause failures)?* That decision-making — deciding **which component does what safety function, what happens if it fails, and what the backup is** — is essentially *Establishing the Architecture*. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — Hospital ICU Architecture** > In an Intensive Care Unit (ICU), life-support systems have: > - A **primary oxygen line** (main architecture) > - A **backup oxygen cylinder** (redundancy) > - An **alarm** that sounds if pressure drops (diagnostics) > - A **manual override** (fail-safe) > - **Independent electrical circuits** (segregation) > > Establishing the architecture means designing this *layered safety web* before a single patient is admitted. The same principle applies to any engineered product — from an electric car to a CNC machine. ## 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Safety Function (SF)** | A function whose failure can immediately result in an unacceptable risk | | **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** | Discrete level (1–4) for specifying safety integrity of E/E/PE systems | | **Redundancy** | Provision of additional elements beyond those needed for normal operation | | **Diversity** | Use of different technologies/principles to avoid common-cause failures | | **Segregation** | Physical/logical separation to prevent fault propagation | | **Fail-Safe State** | A predetermined safe condition the system enters upon failure | | **Defence-in-Depth** | Layered protection strategy with multiple independent barriers | ## 1.4 Why Establishing the Architecture is Critical > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Architecture decisions made early in design determine **>70% of the lifecycle safety cost**. A poorly architected safety system cannot be "patched in" later without expensive retrofits. The architecture phase translates the **risk assessment output** (from Module 2) into a **buildable specification**. It answers four pivotal questions: 1. **WHO** performs each safety function? (Allocation) 2. **HOW** does each subsystem respond to faults? (Behaviour) 3. **WHAT IF** a single component fails? (Fault tolerance) 4. **WHEN** does the system declare itself unsafe? (Diagnostic coverage) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Architecture Establishment Process — Step-by-Step The KTU 2024 Scheme structures architecture establishment into **five sequential phases**: ### Phase 1: Functional Decomposition - Break the overall product function into elementary sub-functions. - Use **Functional Analysis System Technique (FAST)** diagrams or **IDEF0** modelling. - Each sub-function is mapped to a *node* in the architecture. ### Phase 2: Hazard-to-Function Mapping - Every hazard identified in Module 2 must be assigned to **at least one safety function**. - Cross-reference matrix is built: **Hazard × Safety Function**. - Gaps in this matrix indicate **unprotected hazards** (a critical design defect). ### Phase 3: Safety Requirement Allocation - Each safety function is assigned a target: - **SIL** (IEC 61508 — process industries) - **ASIL** (ISO 26262 — automotive, levels A–D) - **PL** (ISO 13849 — machinery, levels a–e) - These targets drive architectural choices (single channel, dual channel, etc.). ### Phase 4: Architectural Selection - Choose an architecture **type** for each safety function: - **Category 1 (1oo1)**: One-out-of-One — single channel, no redundancy. - **Category 2 (1oo2)**: One-out-of-Two — single channel with diagnostics. - **Category 3 (2oo2)**: Two-out-of-Two — both must agree. - **Category 4 (2oo3)**: Two-out-of-Three — voting logic. - Higher categories = higher reliability but higher cost and complexity. ### Phase 5: Verification & Validation Strategy - Define how the architecture will be tested. - Specify **diagnostic coverage (DC)** targets. - Plan **Proof Test Intervals (PTI)** for safety instruments. ## 2.2 Core Architectural Patterns for Safety ### 2.2.1 Redundancy Strategies | Type | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | **Homogeneous** | Identical backup components | Two identical pumps in parallel | | **Heterogeneous (Diverse)** | Different technology backup | Mechanical + Electronic braking | | **Active (Hot)** | Backup runs continuously | Dual flight computers in Boeing 777 | | **Passive (Cold)** | Backup activated only on demand | Fire sprinkler backup pump | ### 2.2.2 Fail-Safe Design Principles - **Fail-Safe to a De-Energized State**: Valve closes on power loss (fail-closed). - **Fail-Safe to an Energized State**: Fire pump starts on power loss (fail-open). - **Fail-Operational**: System continues operation despite faults (aircraft fly-by-wire). - **Fail-Silent**: Faulty node stops communicating, peers take over. ### 2.2.3 Defence-in-Depth (DiD) Levels The KTU framework typically presents **five levels of defence**: 1. **Level 1 — Conservative Design**: Inherent safety through design choices. 2. **Level 2 — Control Systems**: Operational parameters monitored and controlled. 3. **Level 3 — Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)**: Automatic protective responses. 4. **Level 4 — Emergency Response**: Alarms, evacuation, shutdowns. 5. **Level 5 — Emergency Planning**: Post-incident containment and recovery. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following equations are essential for KTU 2024 ESE questions on architecture establishment. | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description | |---|---|---| | **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T_I}{2}$ | For single-channel system with proof test interval $T_I$ | | **Average Unavailability (MooN)** | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg,1ooN}} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_D^{\,N} \cdot T_I^{N-1}}{N!}$ | For N identical parallel channels (1ooN voting) | | **Hardware Fault Tolerance (HFT)** | $\text{HFT} = N - M$ | $N$ = total channels, $M$ = minimum required | | **Safe Failure Fraction (SFF)** | $\text{SFF} = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_D + \lambda_S}$ | Where $\lambda_{DD}$ = dangerous undetected, $\lambda_S$ = safe failure rate | | **Average Probability of Failure (PFH)** | $\text{PFH} \approx \lambda_D$ | For high-demand / continuous mode | | **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** | $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{f_{\text{unmitigated}}}{f_{\text{mitigated}}}$ | Where $f$ is the frequency of hazardous event | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mark-Saver Tip (KTU Valuation)**: Always state the **assumptions** before applying any of the above formulas. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for missing assumptions like "identical components," "independent failures," or "constant failure rate." ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive**: ISO 26262 mandates ASIL decomposition during architecture phase. - **Aerospace**: DO-178C (software) and DO-254 (hardware) define architectural Design Assurance Levels (DAL A–E). - **Medical Devices**: IEC 62304 requires software architectural safety classification. - **Process Industry**: IEC 61511 uses SIL allocation during architecture definition. - **Machinery**: ISO 13849-1 uses Performance Level (PL) and Category (B, 1, 2, 3, 4). ## 2.5 Why "Establishing" Matters More Than "Designing" | Aspect | Designing | Establishing | |---|---|---| | Focus | Detailed component design | System-level structure | | Scope | Individual subsystems | Whole product interaction | | Output | CAD, BOM, schematics | Functional architecture, safety plan | | Timing | Late phase (detailed design) | Early phase (concept/embodiment) | | Cost Impact | Minor retrofits possible | Lock-in of major decisions | > The architecture, once established, becomes the **safety contract** for the entire design team. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: PFD Average for 1oo2 Architecture This is the most commonly asked derivation in KTU 2024 ESE for safety-related modules. ### 3.1.1 Problem Setup A safety system uses a **1oo2 voting architecture** (1-out-of-2 — system fails only if *both* channels fail). Both channels have: - Identical dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D$ - Identical proof test interval $T_I$ - Independent failures (no common-cause failures considered yet) - Constant failure rate (exponential distribution) We need to derive the **average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD$_{\text{avg}}$)**. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Express the Unreliability of a Single Channel** For a single channel under constant failure rate, the probability of failure over time $t$ is given by the exponential distribution: $$ R(t) = e^{-\lambda_D t} $$ Thus, the unreliability (probability of having failed) is: $$ F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_D t} $$ **Step 2: Linearise for Small $\lambda_D t$** Since $\lambda_D t \ll 1$ for safety systems (low failure rates), we can use the Taylor series approximation: $$ e^{-\lambda_D t} \approx 1 - \lambda_D t $$ Therefore: $$ F(t) \approx \lambda_D t $$ **Step 3: Average Over the Proof Test Interval** The PFD must be averaged over the entire proof test interval $T_I$: $$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{T_I} \int_{0}^{T_I} F(t) \, dt $$ **Step 4: Substitute and Integrate** Substituting the linearised unreliability: $$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1}{T_I} \int_{0}^{T_I} \lambda_D t \, dt $$ $$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\lambda_D}{T_I} \int_{0}^{T_I} t \, dt $$ Performing the integration: $$ \int_{0}^{T_I} t \, dt = \left[ \dfrac{t^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{T_I} = \dfrac{T_I^{\,2}}{2} $$ So we get: $$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\lambda_D}{T_I} \cdot \dfrac{T_I^{\,2}}{2} $$ **Step 5: Final Simplification** $$ \boxed{\;\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo1}} = \dfrac{\lambda_D \, T_I}{2}\;} $$ This is the well-known result for a single-channel (1oo1) architecture. **Step 6: Extend to 1oo2 Architecture** For a 1oo2 voting system, BOTH channels must fail simultaneously for system failure. The unreliability of the parallel combination is the **product** of individual unreliabilities: $$ F_{\text{1oo2}}(t) = \left( F_{\text{1oo1}}(t) \right)^2 = (\lambda_D t)^2 = \lambda_D^{\,2} t^2 $$ **Step 7: Compute Average PFD for 1oo2** $$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} = \dfrac{1}{T_I} \int_{0}^{T_I} \lambda_D^{\,2} t^2 \, dt $$ $$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} = \dfrac{\lambda_D^{\,2}}{T_I} \int_{0}^{T_I} t^2 \, dt $$ Computing the integral: $$ \int_{0}^{T_I} t^2 \, dt = \left[ \dfrac{t^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{T_I} = \dfrac{T_I^{\,3}}{3} $$ Substituting back: $$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} = \dfrac{\lambda_D^{\,2}}{T_I} \cdot \dfrac{T_I^{\,3}}{3} $$ **Step 8: Final Result** $$ \boxed{\;\text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} = \dfrac{\lambda_D^{\,2} \, T_I^{\,2}}{3}\;} $$ ### 3.1.3 Comparison Table — Reduction in PFD via Redundancy | Architecture | PFD$_{\text{avg}}$ | Numerical Example ($\lambda_D = 10^{-6}$/h, $T_I = 8760$ h) | |---|---|---| | **1oo1** | $\dfrac{\lambda_D T_I}{2}$ | $\dfrac{10^{-6} \times 8760}{2} = 4.38 \times 10^{-3}$ | | **1oo2** | $\dfrac{\lambda_D^{\,2} T_I^{\,2}}{3}$ | $\dfrac{(10^{-6})^2 \times (8760)^2}{3} = 2.56 \times 10^{-5}$ | | **2oo3** | $\approx 3 \cdot \text{PFD}_{1oo2}^2$ | $\approx 1.96 \times 10^{-9}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight**: A 1oo2 architecture reduces PFD by roughly **two orders of magnitude** versus 1oo1 — but at the cost of doubling hardware and adding voting logic. ## 3.2 Derivation: Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) **Step 1: Define the Mitigated Risk** The mitigated event frequency is: $$ f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{\text{unmitigated}} \cdot \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} $$ **Step 2: Substitute into the RRF Formula** $$ \text{RRF} = \dfrac{f_{\text{unmitigated}}}{f_{\text{unmitigated}} \cdot \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}} $$ **Step 3: Cancel** $$ \boxed{\;\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}}\;} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: SIL vs Target PFD vs Architecture | SIL | Target PFD$_{\text{avg}}$ (Low Demand) | Target PFH (High Demand) | Typical Architecture | |---|---|---|---| | **SIL 1** | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$ /h | 1oo1 with diagnostics | | **SIL 2** | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$ /h | 1oo2 or 2oo2 | | **SIL 3** | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-7}$ /h | 1oo2D or 2oo3 | | **SIL 4** | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ | $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-8}$ /h | 2oo3D or higher | > [!NOTE] > The notation "1oo2D" means 1-out-of-2 **with Diagnostics**. The diagnostic coverage significantly reduces the dangerous undetected failure rate. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Safety Architecture Establishment The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional flow** of establishing a safety architecture, mapping the interactions between the design phases, inputs, outputs, and feedback loops. ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Inputs from Module 2
Risk Assessment Results] A1[Hazard Log
Identified Hazards] A2[Safety Goal List
Top-Level Safety Requirements] A0 --> A1 A0 --> A2 B1[Phase 1
Functional Decomposition] B2[Phase 2
Hazard to Function Mapping] B3[Phase 3
Safety Requirement Allocation] B4[Phase 4
Architectural Selection] B5[Phase 5
Verification and Validation Strategy] A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B2 --> B3 B3 --> B4 B4 --> B5 C1[Elementary Functions] C2[Cross Reference Matrix
Hazard x Safety Function] C3[SIL ASIL PL Allocation Table] C4[Architecture Type Selection
1oo1 1oo2 2oo3] C5[Test Plan and Diagnostic Coverage] B1 --> C1 B2 --> C2 B3 --> C3 B4 --> C4 B5 --> C5 D1[Output 1
Safety Architecture Specification] D2[Output 2
Verification Matrix] D3[Output 3
Residual Risk Register] C5 --> D1 C5 --> D2 C2 --> D3 E1{Feedback Loop
Gaps in Safety Coverage?} D2 --> E1 E1 -->|Yes| B2 E1 -->|No| F1[Design Phase Ready
Architecture Established] D1 --> F1 D3 --> F1 ``` ## 4.2 Redundancy Architecture — Comparative Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A1oo1[1oo1 Single Channel] IN1[Input] --> CH1[Channel 1] CH1 --> OUT1[Output] end subgraph A1oo2[1oo2 Redundant] IN2[Input] --> CH2A[Channel A] IN2 --> CH2B[Channel B] CH2A --> V1[Voter OR Logic] CH2B --> V1 V1 --> OUT2[Output] end subgraph A2oo3[2oo3 Triple Modular] IN3[Input] --> CH3A[Channel A] IN3 --> CH3B[Channel B] IN3 --> CH3C[Channel C] CH3A --> V2[Majority Voter] CH3B --> V2 CH3C --> V2 V2 --> OUT3[Output] end ``` ## 4.3 Defence-in-Depth Layered Model ```mermaid flowchart TB L1[Level 1 Conservative Design
Inherent Safety] L2[Level 2 Control Systems
Process Automation] L3[Level 3 Safety Instrumented Systems
Automatic Protection] L4[Level 4 Emergency Response
Alarms and Shutdowns] L5[Level 5 Emergency Planning
Post Incident Containment] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 H[Hazard Source] -.danger passes through.-> L1 L1 -.blocks majority.-> L2 L2 -.reduces residual.-> L3 L3 -.automatically protects.-> L4 L4 -.activates.-> L5 L5 -.contains.-> P[People Asset Environment] ``` ## 4.4 Safety Function Allocation Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Identified Safety Function] --> S2{Severity of Harm?} S2 -->|S1 Minor| P1[PL a or SIL 1] S2 -->|S2 Serious| P2[PL c or SIL 2] S2 -->|S3 Severe| P3[PL d or SIL 3] S2 -->|S4 Catastrophic| P4[PL e or SIL 4] P1 --> A1[Single Channel OK] P2 --> A2{Continuous Operation?} P3 --> A3[Redundant Architecture Required] P4 --> A4[TMR or Higher Redundancy] A2 -->|Yes| A3 A2 -->|No| A2B[Dual Channel with Diagnostics] ``` > [!NOTE] > These block diagrams are designed to give a **systems-level understanding** of how architecture establishment integrates with the upstream risk assessment and downstream detailed design. In KTU examinations, students are expected to draw **functional architecture diagrams** (not necessarily detailed circuits) that show safety functions, redundancies, and interfaces. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *What is meant by "Establishing the Architecture" in the context of safety in product design? List any four key decisions taken during this phase.* **Model Answer:** Establishing the architecture in safety in product design refers to the structured process of defining how safety functions are allocated to physical and logical components of a product so as to achieve the required level of risk reduction throughout the product lifecycle. The four key decisions are: 1. **Functional Decomposition** — Breaking the product into elementary sub-functions. 2. **Hazard-to-Function Mapping** — Assigning each hazard to a safety function. 3. **Safety Requirement Allocation** — Assigning SIL/ASIL/PL levels to each safety function. 4. **Architectural Pattern Selection** — Choosing redundancy (1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo3, etc.). ### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Distinguish between "Fail-Safe" and "Fail-Operational" design strategies with one example each.* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Fail-Safe | Fail-Operational | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | System transitions to a predetermined safe state upon failure | System continues to operate safely despite one or more faults | | **Power Loss Response** | Valve closes; pump stops | Backup channel takes over | | **Example** | Spring-return valve that closes on power loss (gas burner) | Boeing 777 fly-by-wire: if one computer fails, the other takes over | | **Cost** | Lower | Higher (requires redundancy) | | **Application** | Process plants, fire systems | Aircraft, nuclear reactors | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** *Explain the concept of Defence-in-Depth with a neat block diagram. Describe any THREE levels of defence in detail, with one engineering example for each.* **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** *A safety system uses a 1oo2 architecture with identical channels. Each channel has a dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour and the proof test interval is $T_I = 4380$ hours (6 months). Compute:* - *(i) The average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD$_{\text{avg}}$).* - *(ii) The Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) achieved.* - *(iii) Comment on whether this architecture meets SIL 2 requirements.* **Model Answer:** **Part (a) Solution:** Defence-in-Depth (DiD) is a multi-layered safety strategy where independent protective barriers are arranged in series such that the failure of one barrier does not lead directly to harm — the next barrier catches it. The strategy is widely used in nuclear, process, and aerospace industries. **Block Diagram:** ``` Hazard → [Level 1: Inherent Design] → [Level 2: Control] → [Level 3: SIS] → [Level 4: Emergency] → [Level 5: Planning] → People/Asset ``` **Detailed Explanation of Three Levels:** **Level 1 — Conservative Design (Inherent Safety):** This is the strongest and most fundamental defence. The hazard is reduced at source by design choices such as reducing inventories, lowering operating temperatures, or substituting hazardous chemicals. **Example**: In a chemical plant, using water-based paint instead of solvent-based paint eliminates flammability risk. **Level 2 — Control Systems:** Automated control loops maintain process parameters within safe operating limits (e.g., temperature, pressure, flow). If a parameter drifts, the controller corrects it. **Example**: A PID controller regulating reactor temperature within ±2°C of setpoint. **Level 3 — Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS):** If the control system cannot prevent the hazard, the SIS automatically activates protective action (e.g., emergency shutdown). **Example**: A high-pressure alarm and shutdown valve on a steam boiler. --- **Part (b) Solution:** **Given:** - Architecture: 1oo2 (1-out-of-2 voting) - $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour - $T_I = 4380$ hours **Step 1: Recall the PFD Formula for 1oo2** $$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} = \dfrac{\lambda_D^{\,2} \, T_I^{\,2}}{3} $$ **Step 2: Substitute the Values** $$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-6})^{2} \times (4380)^{2}}{3} $$ Computing the squares: $$ (5 \times 10^{-6})^{2} = 25 \times 10^{-12} $$ $$ (4380)^{2} = 1.91844 \times 10^{7} $$ Multiplying: $$ 25 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.91844 \times 10^{7} = 47.961 \times 10^{-5} = 4.7961 \times 10^{-4} $$ Dividing by 3: $$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg, 1oo2}} = \dfrac{4.7961 \times 10^{-4}}{3} = 1.5987 \times 10^{-4} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \approx 1.6 \times 10^{-4}\;} $$ **Step 3: Calculate the RRF** $$ \text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}} = \dfrac{1}{1.5987 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 6255 $$ $$ \boxed{\;\text{RRF} \approx 6255\;} $$ **Step 4: Check SIL 2 Compliance** The target PFD range for SIL 2 is $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$. Since our computed $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = 1.6 \times 10^{-4}$ is **lower than** $10^{-3}$, the architecture **exceeds SIL 2 requirements** and can also qualify for SIL 3 applications. --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** *Discuss the different types of redundancy used in safety architecture. Explain how diversity complements redundancy to mitigate common-cause failures.* **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** *A process plant has an emergency shutdown system with HFT = 1 (Hardware Fault Tolerance of 1). Justify the architectural choice and compute the average PFD if $\lambda_D = 2 \times 10^{-6}$/h and $T_I = 8760$ hours (1 year). Identify which SIL level this corresponds to.* **Model Answer:** **Part (a) Solution:** Redundancy is the provision of additional components or systems beyond what is minimally required so that the safety function is maintained even after one or more failures. **Types of Redundancy:** 1. **Homogeneous Redundancy**: All redundant components are identical. Simple and easy to maintain but vulnerable to common-cause failures (e.g., two identical pumps from the same batch). 2. **Heterogeneous (Diverse) Redundancy**: Redundant components use different technologies or manufacturers. **Example**: Primary braking system (hydraulic) and secondary braking system (electric). 3. **Active (Hot) Redundancy**: All redundant components operate simultaneously. Fault is masked; no switching delay. **Example**: Dual flight control computers. 4. **Passive (Cold) Redundancy**: Backup components are idle until activated. Lower wear but introduces switching delay. **Example**: Standby emergency generator. 5. **Voting Redundancy (TMR)**: Three or more components vote on output. 2oo3 (Triple Modular Redundancy) tolerates one fault and continues operating. **Diversity Complementing Redundancy:** Redundancy alone is insufficient if all redundant channels fail due to the **same root cause** (e.g., same design flaw, same corrosion mechanism, same software bug). This is the **Common-Cause Failure (CCF)** problem. Diversity addresses CCF by: - Using **different physical principles** (e.g., optical + magnetic sensors). - Using **different manufacturers** (reduces systematic design errors). - Using **different software teams** for redundant channels. - Using **different calibration intervals**. The **β-factor model** quantifies CCF: $$ \lambda_{\text{CCF}} = \beta \cdot \lambda_D $$ where $\beta$ is the CCF factor (typically 2–10%). --- **Part (b) Solution:** **Given:** - Hardware Fault Tolerance HFT = 1 - $\lambda_D = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour - $T_I = 8760$ hours **Step 1: Interpret HFT = 1** HFT = 1 means the system continues to perform its safety function even after **one** component fails. This corresponds to a **1oo2 (or equivalent)** voting architecture — two channels, only one required for safe operation. **Justification:** - HFT = 1 is required for **SIL 2** in low-demand mode (per IEC 61508 Route 1H). - Provides tolerance to a single dangerous failure without losing the safety function. **Step 2: Apply the 1oo2 Formula** $$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\lambda_D^{\,2} \, T_I^{\,2}}{3} $$ **Step 3: Substitute and Solve** $$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{(2 \times 10^{-6})^{2} \times (8760)^{2}}{3} $$ $$ (2 \times 10^{-6})^{2} = 4 \times 10^{-12} $$ $$ (8760)^{2} = 7.674 \times 10^{7} $$ $$ \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^{-12} \times 7.674 \times 10^{7}}{3} = \dfrac{3.0696 \times 10^{-4}}{3} = 1.0232 \times 10^{-4} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \approx 1.02 \times 10^{-4}\;} $$ **Step 4: Map to SIL** | SIL | PFD$_{\text{avg}}$ Range | |---|---| | SIL 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | | **SIL 2** | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | | **SIL 3** | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | | SIL 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ | Our $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = 1.02 \times 10^{-4}$ falls **between SIL 3 and SIL 4**. With proper diagnostics (DC > 90%), this architecture qualifies for **SIL 3**. Without diagnostics, it crosses into SIL 4 territory. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Students Make in Architecture Establishment Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing PFD and PFH**: PFD is for low-demand mode (one demand per year or less); PFH is for high-demand or continuous mode. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for misuse. > > 2. **Forgetting the Constant Failure Rate Assumption**: The $\lambda_D T_I / 2$ formula is only valid under constant failure rate. Always state this assumption. > > 3. **Wrong Architecture Formula**: Students often confuse 1oo2 with 2oo2 formulas. Remember: > - 1oo2 (OR logic) = both must fail → $\lambda_D^2 T_I^2 / 3$ > - 2oo2 (AND logic) = any one failure → roughly $2 \lambda_D T_I / 3$ (higher PFD) > > 4. **Not Drawing a Block Diagram**: For 14-mark questions, a diagram without explanation loses 2 marks, and an explanation without a diagram loses 3 marks. > > 5. **Confusing SIL with ASIL**: SIL is for process/industrial systems; ASIL is for automotive. Don't mix the two in answers. > > 6. **Skipping the Conclusion**: After computing PFD, always explicitly state the **SIL level** achieved. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Establishing the Architecture** - **Definition**: Establishing the architecture = structuring a product so safety functions are assigned, redundancies planned, and verification strategies defined. - **Five Phases**: Decomposition → Mapping → Allocation → Selection → Verification. - **Safety Integrity Levels**: - SIL 1: PFD $\sim 10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ - SIL 2: PFD $\sim 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ - SIL 3: PFD $\sim 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ - SIL 4: PFD $\sim 10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ - **Core Formulas**: - 1oo1 PFD: $\lambda_D T_I / 2$ - 1oo2 PFD: $\lambda_D^2 T_I^2 / 3$ - RRF: $1 / \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}$ - SFF: $1 - \lambda_{DD} / (\lambda_D + \lambda_S)$ - **Architectural Patterns**: 1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, 2oo3, 1oo2D, TMR. - **Defence-in-Depth**: 5 levels — Inherent Design → Control → SIS → Emergency Response → Emergency Planning. - **Common Pitfalls**: Forgetting assumptions, wrong voting formula, missing diagrams, confusing SIL/ASIL. - **Standards to Remember**: IEC 61508, IEC 61511, ISO 26262 (ASIL), ISO 13849-1 (PL). - **Key Concept**: Diversity ≠ Redundancy. Diversity protects against common-cause failures. - **Mark Allocation Tip**: In a 14-mark question, expect ~4 marks for definitions, ~4 marks for diagrams, and ~6 marks for calculation/justification.

topics.title: Variety and Supply Chain Considerations
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT747 - SAFETY IN PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Variety and Supply Chain Considerations

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Product Variety** refers to the range of distinct product configurations, models, options, and versions that a manufacturer offers to its customer base within a single product family. **Supply Chain Considerations** in the context of safety engineering refer to the systematic evaluation of how sourcing, procurement, logistics, inventory, distribution, and end-of-life recovery networks influence the **safety integrity**, **traceability**, and **reliability** of a product throughout its lifecycle.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for Safety in Product Design, *Variety and Supply Chain Considerations* is defined as the integrated set of design and procurement decisions that determine how a manufacturer's portfolio breadth (SKUs, options, modular variants) interacts with its upstream supplier network and downstream distribution channels, with explicit attention to:

- **Component Homogeneity** vs. **Functional Heterogeneity**
- **Supplier Qualification** for safety-critical parts
- **Traceability chains** for hazard reporting and recall
- **Inventory decoupling** to absorb supply disruptions

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 3):** This topic belongs to the design-for-safety cluster where students must demonstrate the ability to map product architecture decisions to supply-chain safety outcomes (CO3: *Apply variety management principles to maintain safety integrity across product configurations*).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a pharmaceutical company that sells **one universal paracetamol tablet** versus a company selling **30 different variants** (different dosages, flavours, packaging, blister counts). The second company faces exponentially more supply-chain touchpoints: 30 distinct Bill-of-Materials (BOMs), 30 artwork versions, 30 packaging line changeovers, and 30 potential recall scenarios. Now imagine each variant must meet pharmacopoeial safety standards — the **complexity multiplier** becomes a **safety risk multiplier**.

**In simple words:**
- *More variety* → *more part numbers* → *more suppliers* → *more chances for a safety defect to slip through*.
- *More variety* → *more SKUs in warehouses* → *harder to recall a specific defective batch quickly*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Safety-Variety Paradox:** Customers demand more variety (personalisation), but each new variant introduces new safety-validation burden. The designer's job is to maximise *external* variety (what the customer sees) while minimising *internal* variety (what the factory makes). This is achieved through **modular architecture** and **platform sharing**.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU-Exam-Relevant)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range / Unit | Safety Implication |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Number of active SKUs | $N_{SKU}$ | 10 – 10 000 | Higher = more recall risk |
| Commonality Index | $CI$ | 0 – 1 (dimensionless) | Higher = lower safety risk |
| Supplier Base Size | $n_{sup}$ | Integer count | Concentrated base = lower disruption risk |
| Lead Time | $L$ | days | Longer = higher safety-stock need |
| Safety Stock | $SS$ | units | Buffer against defective batches |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Triadic Relationship: Variety ↔ Supply Chain ↔ Safety

The relationship can be decomposed into three coupled sub-systems:

### 2.1.1 Variety-Induced Supply-Chain Complexity

When a designer introduces $N$ distinct modules that can combine into $V$ product variants, the combinatorial explosion is given by:

$$V = \prod_{i=1}^{N} (n_i + 1)$$

where $n_i$ is the number of optional sub-modules in module $i$. The "+1" accounts for the *base configuration* (no optional module).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** Reducing $n_i$ from 4 to 2 across 8 modules cuts variants from $5^8 = 390\,625$ down to $3^8 = 6\,561$ — a **98.3 % reduction** in combinatorial burden with negligible customer-visible variety loss if architecture is modular.

### 2.1.2 Safety Implications Through the Supply Chain

Each supply-chain node introduces a potential **failure mode**:

1. **Tier-1 Supplier** → raw-material defect (e.g., contaminated steel)
2. **Tier-2 Supplier** → sub-component failure (e.g., faulty IC)
3. **Inbound Logistics** → damage in transit
4. **In-house Assembly** → mis-assembly
5. **Outbound Logistics** → wrong SKU shipped
6. **Field Use** → misuse → incident

The cumulative defect escape probability across $k$ independent stages is:

$$P_{escape} = \prod_{j=1}^{k} (1 - d_j)$$

where $d_j$ is the detection probability at stage $j$. To achieve $P_{escape} \leq 10^{-6}$ (a common safety target), each stage must have $d_j \geq 0.9$ for a 6-stage chain.

### 2.1.3 The Commonality Index (CI)

The **Commonality Index** quantifies the degree to which components are shared across variants. For a part $p$ used in $V_p$ out of $V_{total}$ variants:

$$CI = \frac{\sum_{p=1}^{P} V_p}{P \cdot V_{total}}$$

- $CI = 1$ → every part is shared by all variants (maximum safety/standardisation).
- $CI = 0$ → every part is unique to one variant (maximum risk).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | LaTeX Form | Engineering Utility |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | Combinatorial Variety | $V = \prod_{i=1}^{N} (n_i + 1)$ | Predicts SKU explosion from module options |
| 2 | Commonality Index | $CI = \dfrac{\sum V_p}{P \cdot V_{total}}$ | Measures design standardisation; ranges 0–1 |
| 3 | Cumulative Escape Probability | $P_{esc} = \prod_{j=1}^{k} (1 - d_j)$ | Computes defect-leakage through supply chain |
| 4 | Safety Stock (statistical) | $SS = z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | Buffer for lead-time variability (z = service-level factor) |
| 5 | Reorder Point | $ROP = \bar{D} \cdot L + SS$ | Triggers purchase order to avoid stock-out of safety-critical spares |
| 6 | Risk Pooling Factor | $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{N_{retailers}}{N_{centralised}}}$ | Variance reduction through centralisation |
| 7 | Supplier Concentration Ratio | $SCR = \sum_{i=1}^{m} s_i^2$ (Herfindahl) | $0 \leq SCR \leq 1$; lower = safer diversified base |
| 8 | Recall Effectiveness Index | $REI = \dfrac{N_{recalled}}{N_{defective\,shipped}}$ | Compliance metric; must be $\geq 0.95$ for safety recalls |
| 9 | Total Cost of Variety | $TCV = C_{inv} + C_{chg} + C_{cplx}$ | Guides optimal variety level |
| 10 | Mean Time To Recall (MTTR) | $MTTR = T_{detect} + T_{identify} + T_{notify}$ | Lower MTTR → lower aggregate safety harm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary conditions to remember for KTU exams:**
> - $CI$, $P_{esc}$, $REI$ are dimensionless and bounded in $[0, 1]$.
> - $SS$, $ROP$, $MTTR$ carry **units of [units]** and **[time]** respectively.
> - $z_{\alpha}$ is the standard-normal quantile (e.g., $z_{0.95} = 1.645$, $z_{0.997} = 2.75$ for automotive safety stock).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Platform sharing (e.g., VW MQB) allows 4 brands × 10 models from one platform → dramatically reduces $N_{sup}$ and improves recall traceability.
- **Medical Devices:** FDA 21 CFR 820 mandates full traceability → $REI \geq 0.95$ is a legal requirement. High $CI$ reduces the validation burden per new variant.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Apple uses *delayed differentiation* — common sub-assemblies are built, then customised late → reduces inventory risk for safety-critical Li-ion cells.
- **Aerospace:** AS9100D requires every safety-critical part to have a single source qualified → $SCR$ is intentionally near 1, but with rigorous audit.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Combinatorial Variety Explosion

**Problem Setup:** A refrigerator has 4 modular subsystems (compressor type, door configuration, drawer layout, control panel), each with 3 options including a base. Find the total variants.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \prod_{i=1}^{N} (n_i + 1) \\
&= (3)(3)(3)(3) \\
&= 81 \text{ variants}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Even a "modest" 4-module, 3-option architecture generates **81 distinct SKUs**. If the designer reduces each module to 2 options (base + one alternative):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V' &= (2)(2)(2)(2) \\
&= 16 \text{ variants}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reduction Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= \frac{V - V'}{V} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{81 - 16}{81} \times 100\% \\
&= 80.25 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> An 80 % cut in combinatorial variety is achievable by sacrificing only **one option per module** — a powerful design-for-safety lever.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cumulative Escape Probability Through the Supply Chain

**Problem Setup:** A safety-critical brake-caliper casting passes through 5 supply-chain stages. Stage detection probabilities are $d_1 = 0.85, d_2 = 0.90, d_3 = 0.70, d_4 = 0.95, d_5 = 0.99$. Compute $P_{escape}$.

**Step 1 — Write the general formula:**

$$P_{esc} = \prod_{j=1}^{k} (1 - d_j)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute each $(1 - d_j)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{esc} &= (1 - 0.85)(1 - 0.90)(1 - 0.70)(1 - 0.95)(1 - 0.99) \\
&= (0.15)(0.10)(0.30)(0.05)(0.01)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply stepwise:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.15)(0.10) &= 0.0150 \\
(0.0150)(0.30) &= 0.00450 \\
(0.00450)(0.05) &= 0.000225 \\
(0.000225)(0.01) &= 2.25 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{P_{esc} = 2.25 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

**Engineering Decision:** Stage 3 has the *weakest* detection ($d_3 = 0.70$). The designer should invest in **incoming-quality inspection upgrade** at Tier-2 supplier to raise $d_3$ from 0.70 to 0.95:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{esc,new} &= (0.15)(0.10)(0.05)(0.05)(0.01) \\
&= 3.75 \times 10^{-7}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **6× safety improvement** at low cost.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Commonality Index for a Product Family

**Problem Setup:** A motorcycle family has 5 variants. A given part set $\{A, B, C, D, E, F, G\}$ has the following usage matrix:

| Part | Variant 1 | Variant 2 | Variant 3 | Variant 4 | Variant 5 | $V_p$ |
|:----:|:---------:|:---------:|:---------:|:---------:|:---------:|:-----:|
| A    | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | 5 |
| B    | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | 4 |
| C    | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | 2 |
| D    | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | 5 |
| E    | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | 2 |
| F    | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | 5 |
| G    | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | 1 |

**Step 1 — Sum the per-part variant counts:**

$$\sum_{p=1}^{7} V_p = 5 + 4 + 2 + 5 + 2 + 5 + 1 = 24$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Commonality Index formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CI &= \frac{\sum V_p}{P \cdot V_{total}} \\
&= \frac{24}{7 \times 5} \\
&= \frac{24}{35} \\
&= 0.686
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** $CI = 0.686$ indicates **moderate commonality**. Industry best-practice for safety-critical families targets $CI \geq 0.80$. The designer should:

- Eliminate part **G** (used in only 1 variant) → re-specify to merge with **B**.
- Consolidate parts **C** and **E** into a unified design → both currently used in only 2 variants each.

Recomputed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum V_p' &= 5 + 4 + 2 + 5 + 2 + 5 + 4 = 27 \\
CI' &= \frac{27}{7 \times 5} = \frac{27}{35} = 0.771
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Safety Stock with Risk-Pooling Adjustment

**Problem Setup:** A regional distributor serves 5 retailers independently for a safety-critical spare (e.g., smoke-detector head). Mean daily demand $\bar{D} = 10$ units, std. dev. $\sigma_D = 4$, lead time $L = 6$ days. Without pooling, what is the safety stock at service level 95 %? With centralisation, what is the new safety stock?

**Step 1 — Independent (decentralised) safety stock:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{LT,indep} &= \sigma_D \cdot \sqrt{L} = 4 \cdot \sqrt{6} = 9.798 \text{ units} \\
SS_{indep} &= z_{0.95} \cdot \sigma_{LT,indep} = 1.645 \times 9.798 \\
&= 16.12 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply risk-pooling factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \sqrt{\frac{N_{retailers}}{N_{centralised}}} = \sqrt{5} = 2.236 \\
\sigma_{LT,pooled} &= \frac{\sigma_{LT,indep}}{\sqrt{N}} = \frac{9.798}{\sqrt{5}} = 4.382 \text{ units} \\
SS_{pooled} &= 1.645 \times 4.382 = 7.21 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare:**

$$\Delta SS = 16.12 - 7.21 = 8.91 \text{ units saved (55.3 % reduction)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This reduction directly lowers **stock-out risk** of safety-critical spares, thereby lowering *aggregate* field-failure exposure.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Safety Impact by Supply-Chain Lever

| Design Lever | Variety Effect | Supply-Chain Effect | Net Safety Impact | KTU Exam Tip |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| Platform sharing | ↓ $V$ | ↓ $N_{sup}$ | ↑↑ Traceability, ↑ Validation efficiency | Quote $CI$ formula |
| Modular architecture | ↑ external, ↓ internal | Delayed differentiation | ↑ Field-serviceability, ↑ Recall precision | Use delayed-differentiation diagram |
| Single-sourcing (safety parts) | = 0 | ↑ $SCR$ | ↑ Audit depth, ↑ Audit depth | Mention AS9100/FDA |
| Supplier co-location | = 0 | ↓ Lead time, ↓ Damage | ↑ Incoming quality | Link to JIT & Q-bahn |
| Standardised fasteners | ↓ Internal | ↓ $N_{SKU}$ inventory | ↑ Assembly torque reliability | Cite torque-audit case |
| Postponement | = 0 externally | ↓ $SS$ | ↓ Stock-out of safety-critical spares | Risk-pooling derivation |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Variety-Safety-Supply-Chain Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Customer Demand\nfor Variety] --> B[Product Family\nArchitecture]
    B --> C[Bill of Materials\nBOM Explosion]
    C --> D[Supplier Network\nn_sup increases]
    D --> E[Manufacturing\n& Assembly]
    E --> F[Field Use\n& Hazard Data]
    F --> G[Recall &\nField Action]
    G --> H[Safety Lessons\nLearned]
    H --> B
    F --> A
    D --> I[Safety Stock\nSS Buffer]
    I --> E
    H --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> The loop closes through *Safety Lessons Learned*: every recall forces a redesign that may *reduce* variety to *increase* safety — the **Variety-Safety Paradox** in diagram form.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Modular Architecture for Safety — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PL[Platform Layer Common to all Variants]
        P1[Chassis Module]
        P2[Safety Subsystem\nABS Airbag Harness]
        P3[Power Module]
    end
    subgraph OL[Option Layer Per Variant]
        O1[Comfort Option A]
        O2[Comfort Option B]
        O3[Infotainment Pack]
    end
    P1 --> V1[Variant 1]
    P1 --> V2[Variant 2]
    P2 --> V1
    P2 --> V2
    P3 --> V1
    P3 --> V2
    O1 -. optional .-> V1
    O2 -. optional .-> V2
    O3 -. optional .-> V1
    O3 -. optional .-> V2
```

**Functional Annotation:** The **Platform Layer** carries the safety-critical validated subsystems (P2). The **Option Layer** carries customer-visible customisation but must never compromise the P2 safety envelope. This is the **Delayed Differentiation** strategy.

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Supply-Chain Tier Map with Safety Gates

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Raw Material\nSupplier Tier 3] -->|Heat Number\nCertificate| T2[Sub-component\nSupplier Tier 2]
    T2 -->|PPAP / FAI\nSafety Gate 1| T1[Component\nSupplier Tier 1]
    T1 -->|IPQC / FMEA\nSafety Gate 2| OEM[OEM Assembly\nPlant]
    OEM -->|End-of-line Test\nSafety Gate 3| DIST[Distributor]
    DIST -->|Traceability Label\nSafety Gate 4| CUST[Customer]
    CUST -. Field Hazard .-> OEM
    OEM -. 8D Report .-> T1
    T1 -. CAPA .-> T2
    T2 -. CAPA .-> R
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Each Safety Gate is a $d_j$ in the $P_{esc}$ formula.** A missing gate directly multiplies into the defect-leakage probability.

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Variety Reduction Roadmap

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Pareto\n80-20 SKU Analysis] --> S2[Step 2: Identify\nLow CI Parts]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Re-specify\nto Shared Parts]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Validate\nSafety Subsystems]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Phased\nSKU Retirement]
    S5 --> S6[Higher CI\nLower Recall Risk]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Define "Product Variety" in the context of safety in product design. How does an increase in product variety impact the safety performance of a supply chain?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Product Variety refers to the number of distinct configurations, models, or options a manufacturer offers within a product family (KTU definition). An increase in product variety impacts supply-chain safety in the following three ways: **(1 mark each)**

1. **BOM Explosion:** More variants mean a larger Bill of Materials, raising the number of unique parts and thus the *number of potential failure modes* that must be independently validated.

2. **Recall Complexity:** A higher SKU count ($N_{SKU}$) makes it harder to identify, locate, and recall every defective unit quickly — directly increasing the Mean Time To Recall (MTTR) and aggregate customer harm.

3. **Supplier Base Inflation:** Each new variant often requires new sub-components, expanding $n_{sup}$ and reducing the ability to deeply audit every safety-critical supplier — increasing $P_{escape}$.

Thus, variety must be managed via **modular architecture** to preserve safety integrity.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**What is the Commonality Index (CI)? Write its formula and state the safety implication when CI approaches 1.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Commonality Index (CI)** is a dimensionless metric (0 to 1) that quantifies the degree of part-sharing across variants of a product family. **(1 mark)**

$$
CI = \frac{\sum_{p=1}^{P} V_p}{P \cdot V_{total}}
$$

**(1 mark)** — When $CI \to 1$, every part is used in every variant, meaning maximum standardisation. The safety implications are: lower $N_{SKU}$, lower $P_{escape}$, faster recall execution, and lower validation cost per variant — all of which strengthen the safety case. **(1 mark)**

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch how a **modular product architecture** enables safety integrity while preserving customer-visible variety. Discuss the role of *delayed differentiation*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A product has **4 modules** with options $(n_1, n_2, n_3, n_4) = (4, 2, 3, 5)$. Calculate:
  (i) the total number of variants $V$, 
  (ii) the percentage reduction in $V$ if each module is restricted to a maximum of **3 options**, and 
  (iii) the resulting **Cumulative Escape Probability** if detection probabilities across 4 supply-chain stages are $d_1 = 0.90, d_2 = 0.85, d_3 = 0.80, d_4 = 0.95$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Modular Architecture & Delayed Differentiation (7 marks)**

A modular architecture separates the product into two layers: the **Platform Layer** carrying safety-critical validated subsystems (chassis, braking, airbags), and the **Option Layer** carrying customer-visible customisation (trim, infotainment). Because the platform is invariant across variants, the safety validation done on the platform applies to every variant — **one safety case, many SKUs**. **(3 marks)**

**Delayed Differentiation** is the strategy of building *common* sub-assemblies first and applying customer-specific features as late as possible in the supply chain. This keeps the safety-critical inventory pooled while still offering external variety. Sketch: refer to *Diagram 4.2 (Modular Architecture Block Diagram)*. **(2 marks)**

**Safety benefit summary:** The platform layer concentrates the safety-critical supplier base, simplifies FMEA, enables rapid recall (one platform recall covers all variants), and reduces $N_{SKU}$ in safety-critical parts. **(2 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**(i) Total Variants (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \prod_{i=1}^{4} (n_i + 1) \\
&= (4+1)(2+1)(3+1)(5+1) \\
&= (5)(3)(4)(6) \\
&= 360 \text{ variants}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Reduction on Capping at 3 Options (3 marks):**

Restricted values: $(3, 2, 3, 3)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{new} &= (3+1)(2+1)(3+1)(3+1) \\
&= (4)(3)(4)(4) \\
&= 192 \text{ variants}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V \,(\%) &= \frac{V - V_{new}}{V} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{360 - 192}{360} \times 100 \\
&= 46.67 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Cumulative Escape Probability (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{esc} &= \prod_{j=1}^{4}(1 - d_j) \\
&= (1 - 0.90)(1 - 0.85)(1 - 0.80)(1 - 0.95) \\
&= (0.10)(0.15)(0.20)(0.05) \\
&= 1.5 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget the "+1" in the combinatorial formula, forgetting that the *base configuration* is itself a variant. Always include $n_i + 1$. Also, $P_{esc}$ is dimensionless; **do not** carry units in the final answer.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Define **Safety Stock** and **Risk Pooling**. Derive the safety-stock formula and show how centralisation reduces required safety stock by the factor $\sqrt{N}$, where $N$ is the number of decentralised nodes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A safety-critical spare part has demand $\bar{D} = 50$ units/day with $\sigma_D = 12$ units/day. Lead time $L = 9$ days. Service level target = 99 % ($z = 2.33$). 
  (i) Calculate decentralised safety stock for **4 retailers operating independently**.
  (ii) Calculate the pooled safety stock after centralisation.
  (iii) Comment on the safety implication of the reduction. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Safety Stock & Risk Pooling Derivation (7 marks)**

**Safety Stock (SS)** is the inventory buffer held to protect against demand and lead-time variability, ensuring a target *service level* (probability of no stock-out). **(1 mark)**

The lead-time demand is a random variable $D_{LT}$ with mean $\bar{D}L$ and variance $\sigma_D^2 L$ (assuming independent daily demands).

**Decentralised (independent) safety stock:** **(2 marks)**

$$
SS_{indep} = z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma_{LT} = z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma_D \cdot \sqrt{L}
$$

**Centralised (pooled) safety stock:** When $N$ independent retailers are merged into one central warehouse, demands aggregate. By the additivity of independent normal variances:

$$
\sigma_{pooled} = \sigma_D \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L}{N}}
$$

**Therefore:** **(2 marks)**

$$
SS_{pooled} = z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma_D \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L}{N}} = \frac{SS_{indep}}{\sqrt{N}}
$$

This is the **square-root law of risk pooling**: centralisation reduces safety stock by $\sqrt{N}$ while keeping the same service level. **(1 mark)**

**Safety implication:** Lower pooled safety stock lowers inventory carrying cost and obsolescence risk for safety-critical spares. However, transit time to the field increases — a *trade-off* the designer must evaluate. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Numerical (7 marks)**

**(i) Decentralised Safety Stock (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{LT} &= \sigma_D \cdot \sqrt{L} = 12 \cdot \sqrt{9} = 12 \cdot 3 = 36 \\
SS_{indep} &= z \cdot \sigma_{LT} = 2.33 \times 36 = 83.88 \approx 84 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Pooled Safety Stock (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SS_{pooled} &= \frac{SS_{indep}}{\sqrt{N}} = \frac{83.88}{\sqrt{4}} = \frac{83.88}{2} \\
&= 41.94 \approx 42 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Safety Implication (2 marks):**

The central warehouse holds only **42 units** instead of $4 \times 84 = 336$ units across four retailers — an **87.5 %** inventory reduction. For a safety-critical spare, this means:

- **Lower obsolescence:** Expired or superseded stock is reduced → fewer unvalidated parts in the field.
- **Lower total handling damage:** Fewer touch-points reduce ESD, contamination, and mishandling risks.
- **Faster traceability:** One warehouse makes one-stop recall execution easier.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly write $SS = z \cdot \sigma_D$ (forgetting $\sqrt{L}$). Watch the lead-time factor. Also, when answering "comment on safety implication", do **not** just repeat the numerical answer — explicitly link to *recall*, *traceability*, or *field-failure exposure*.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **Product Variety:** The number of distinct configurations/SKUs offered; quantified by $V = \prod (n_i + 1)$. **(Don't forget the +1!)**
- **Supply-Chain Safety:** Every tier (T1, T2, T3) and every logistics stage introduces a $d_j$ into the cumulative escape probability.
- **Commonality Index (CI):** $CI = \dfrac{\sum V_p}{P \cdot V_{total}}$; range $[0, 1]$; higher = safer.
- **Safety Stock Formula:** $SS = z_\alpha \cdot \sigma_D \cdot \sqrt{L}$; always include the $\sqrt{L}$ term.
- **Risk-Pooling Square-Root Law:** $SS_{pooled} = SS_{indep} / \sqrt{N}$.
- **Recall Effectiveness Index (REI):** $\geq 0.95$ for safety-critical recalls (regulatory).
- **Modular Architecture:** Platform layer (safety-critical, invariant) + Option layer (customisable, validated against platform envelope).
- **Delayed Differentiation:** Build common sub-assemblies first; differentiate late to pool safety-stock.
- **Supplier Concentration:** Safety-critical parts favour fewer, deeper-audited suppliers (high $SCR$).
- **KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:** Always expect a 7-mark *theory-with-sketch* + 7-mark *numerical* combination. Practice the derivation of $P_{esc}$ and $SS$.
- **Examiner Traps:** Missing $+1$ in $V$ formula; missing $\sqrt{L}$ in $SS$; giving $P_{esc}$ with units; writing $CI > 1$.
- **Real-world link:** Mention industry examples (automotive platform sharing, FDA traceability, AS9100 single-sourcing) to earn *application-level* marks.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Platform Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT747 - SAFETY IN PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Platform Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Platform Planning** in the context of Safety in Product Design is a strategic product development methodology wherein a common technological, structural, and functional **product platform** is established to serve as the foundation for developing a family of related products, variants, or generations. The platform encapsulates shared subsystems, safety-critical components, design rules, manufacturing processes, and compliance frameworks that can be reused, scaled, and customized across multiple end products while maintaining uniform safety performance standards.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, platform planning is the **front-end architectural decision-making process** that determines which safety features, structural elements, control modules, and risk-mitigation subsystems will be standardized (shared across the family) and which will be customized (variant-specific), with the explicit goal of maximizing **safety reusability, regulatory compliance efficiency, and lifecycle cost optimization**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Platform planning sits at the intersection of *Design for Safety (DfS)*, *Modular Product Architecture*, and *Product Family Engineering*. It directly addresses the engineering trade-off between **economies of scale** and **variant diversity**, while ensuring that safety integrity levels (SIL) are preserved or improved across all derivatives.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of Platform Planning like building a **skyscraper using a standardized foundation system**. Imagine a real estate developer in Kochi who plans to construct 10 different apartment towers in the same locality. Instead of designing 10 completely independent buildings, the developer creates one **common foundation, structural grid, plumbing backbone, and electrical busway** (the platform). Each tower then customizes only the façade, floor plans, and interior finishes (the variants).

In exactly the same way, an automotive manufacturer like Toyota develops the **Toyota New Global Architecture (TNGA)** platform — one chassis, one drivetrain mounting scheme, one set of crumple-zone geometries, and one standardized airbag/ECU architecture — and then builds the Camry, RAV4, Highlander, and Lexus ES on top of it. The **crash safety performance** validated on the platform is *inherited* by every derivative, saving hundreds of millions in repeated safety testing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea in One Line:** Platform Planning is the discipline of deciding *what to share* and *what to vary* so that safety, quality, and cost scale together — not against each other.

### 1.3 Why Platform Planning Matters in Safety Engineering

| Driver | Impact on Safety |
|---|---|
| **Regulatory Repeat Testing Avoidance** | A safety-validated subsystem (e.g., ABS module) need not be re-certified for every variant. |
| **Failure Mode Reuse** | FMEA and FTA libraries can be partially reused across the family. |
| **Standardized Safety Interfaces** | Common connector, signal, and mechanical interfaces reduce integration errors. |
| **Economies of Scale in Safety Components** | Bulk procurement of certified safety parts lowers per-unit cost. |
| **Lifecycle Safety Tracking** | Field-failure data from one product feeds back into improving all platform derivatives. |

### 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Expected Definitions)

- **Product Platform:** The set of common assets (components, modules, processes, knowledge) shared across a product family.
- **Carryover Parts:** Components used unchanged across all derivatives (e.g., standardized safety relay).
- **Common Module:** A functional subsystem shared across ≥ 2 variants with identical interface specification.
- **Variant-Specific Module:** A subsystem customized per market/regulatory requirement (e.g., left-hand vs. right-hand drive headlamp).
- **Platform Leader:** A senior engineering role responsible for the roadmap and integrity of the platform.
- **Safety Carryover Justification (SCJ):** Documented evidence that a safety-critical component retains its integrity across variant conditions.
- **Differentiators:** Features unique to a derivative that provide market/performance distinction.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Platform Planning Decision Process (Step-by-Step Logic)

Platform Planning is executed in **five sequential engineering phases**. Each phase answers a specific design question and feeds the next.

**Phase 1 — Market & Safety Requirement Segmentation**
- Decompose the target market into distinct customer segments.
- For each segment, identify the **applicable safety regulations** (e.g., ECE R94 for frontal crash, IEC 61508 for functional safety, ISO 13849 for machinery).
- Build a **Segmentation Matrix** mapping segments ↔ safety requirements.
- *Why:* Different geographies (EU, US, India) impose different mandatory safety standards. The platform must accommodate the *superset* of all requirements.

**Phase 2 — Functional Decomposition & Commonality Analysis**
- Break the product into functional blocks (e.g., Energy Source → Power Conversion → Motion Control → Safeguarding → HMI).
- For each functional block, perform a **Commonality Analysis**: classify each component as *Carryover*, *Common Module*, or *Variant-Specific*.
- *How:* Use a **Design Structure Matrix (DSM)** to visualize interactions and identify cluster boundaries.

**Phase 3 — Safety Architecture Allocation**
- Assign **Safety Integrity Levels (SIL 1 to SIL 4)** per IEC 61508, or **Performance Levels (PL a to PL e)** per ISO 13849, to each safety function.
- Decide which safety functions reside at the *platform layer* (shared) and which at the *variant layer* (customized).
- *Rule of Thumb (KTU Board Favourite):* **High-SIL functions belong on the platform; low-SIL cosmetic functions belong on the variant.**

**Phase 4 — Interface & Modularity Specification**
- Define the **physical, electrical, logical, and human** interfaces between platform and variant layers.
- Use **modular interface contracts**: standardized pin-outs, signal protocols (CAN, LIN, Ethernet), mechanical mounting patterns.
- *Why this matters for safety:* Poorly defined interfaces are the #1 source of *integration-induced* safety failures in product families.

**Phase 5 — Validation, Carryover Justification & Lifecycle Governance**
- Validate the platform with the *worst-case* safety scenario from the variant set.
- Issue a **Safety Carryover Justification (SCJ)** document for every carryover part.
- Establish a **Platform Change Control Board (PCCB)** to govern future modifications without violating family-wide safety integrity.

### 2.2 The Three Pillars of Platform Planning

1. **Commonality** — Maximize shared safety assets to leverage testing, certification, and field data.
2. **Modularity** — Enable independent substitution of variants without affecting platform safety integrity.
3. **Scalability** — Permit graceful upgrade of safety performance (e.g., from SIL 2 to SIL 3) at variant level without redesigning the platform.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** These three pillars are in **mutual tension**. Pushing commonality too hard reduces modularity (rigid platform). Pushing modularity too hard reduces commonality (fragmented parts list). Platform Planning is the art of finding the engineering optimum.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board-Critical Equations)

| # | Formula / Metric | LaTeX Expression | Variables | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Commonality Index (CI)** | $CI = \dfrac{N_{\text{shared}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | $N_{\text{shared}}$ = number of shared components; $N_{\text{total}}$ = total components in product family | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Measures degree of platform leverage |
| 2 | **Platform Leverage Factor (PLF)** | $PLF = \dfrac{N_{\text{derivatives}}}{C_{\text{platform}}}$ | $N_{\text{derivatives}}$ = number of products built on platform; $C_{\text{platform}}$ = platform development cost | products per million currency | Measures ROI of platform investment |
| 3 | **Degree of Modularity (DM)** | $DM = 1 - \dfrac{I_{\text{unique}}}{I_{\text{total}}}$ | $I_{\text{unique}}$ = number of variant-unique interfaces; $I_{\text{total}}$ = total interfaces | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Measures decoupling quality |
| 4 | **Per-Unit Cost Savings (ΔC)** | $\Delta C = C_{\text{standalone}} - C_{\text{platform}}$ | $C_{\text{standalone}}$ = cost if developed independently; $C_{\text{platform}}$ = cost on shared platform | currency per unit | Direct cost benefit |
| 5 | **Break-Even Point (BEP)** | $BEP = \dfrac{C_{\text{platform\_dev}}}{\Delta C_{\text{per\_unit}} \cdot V_{\text{annual}}}$ | $C_{\text{platform\_dev}}$ = platform development cost; $\Delta C_{\text{per\_unit}}$ = per-unit savings; $V_{\text{annual}}$ = annual volume | years | Time to recover platform investment |
| 6 | **Platform Family Risk Reduction (PFRR)** | $PFRR = 1 - \dfrac{R_{\text{family}}}{R_{\text{standalone}}}$ | $R_{\text{family}}$ = aggregate risk across product family; $R_{\text{standalone}}$ = sum of individual product risks | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Safety impact of platform standardization |
| 7 | **Variant Distinctiveness Ratio (VDR)** | $VDR = \dfrac{N_{\text{variant\_specific}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | $N_{\text{variant\_specific}}$ = components unique to any single derivative | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Inverse measure of platform commonality |
| 8 | **Safety Carryover Ratio (SCR)** | $SCR = \dfrac{N_{\text{safety\_carryover}}}{N_{\text{safety\_total}}}$ | $N_{\text{safety\_carryover}}$ = safety-critical parts reused unchanged; $N_{\text{safety\_total}}$ = total safety-critical parts | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Measures safety-asset reuse efficiency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU Valuation:** When examiners ask for the *optimum* commonality, the academically accepted range is **CI ∈ [0.6, 0.85]**. Below 0.6, the platform fails to deliver economies of scale. Above 0.85, variant differentiation collapses.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Platform planning is deployed across virtually every safety-critical industry:

- **Automotive:** VW MQB, Toyota TNGA, Hyundai E-GMP (electric vehicle platforms) share battery enclosures, airbag controllers, and ADAS sensor stacks.
- **Aviation:** Boeing 737 MAX shares the type-rated cockpit with 737 NG, allowing pilot cross-qualification — a *human factors safety* benefit.
- **Medical Devices:** Philips Intellivue patient monitor platform shares SpO2/ECG modules across ICU, OR, and transport variants.
- **Industrial Automation:** Siemens SIMATIC platform shares PLC CPUs and safety I/O modules (F-CPU) across factory automation lines.
