- [ ] **Safety implications of DFA:** fewer parts → fewer failure modes, fewer mis-assemblies, lower ergonomic strain, simpler disassembly for service.
- [ ] **7 Muda (Wastes):** Transport, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects — DFA directly attacks the last four.
- [ ] The **Boothroyd method** is named after **Geoffrey Boothroyd** and **Peter Dewhurst** (University of Massachusetts, USA) — *do not* attribute it to other authors.
- [ ] For a 14-mark KTU ESE question, always show: (1) the 3-question test, (2) the A-Chart, (3) the η calculation, and (4) a safety-justification statement.
- [ ] **Remember:** Higher η is not always economically optimal — sometimes 70% is the practical ceiling for a *very safe* product that still uses serviceable parts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT747 - SAFETY IN PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Prototyping: Prototyping Basics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Prototyping: Prototyping Basics

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prototyping** is the iterative process of creating preliminary versions (working models, mock-ups, or simulations) of a product to test, validate, and refine its design concepts, functional behaviour, and safety performance **before** committing to full-scale production.

In the context of **Safety in Product Design**, prototyping is the engineering practice of fabricating scaled or functional representations of a product to:
1. Identify potential **hazards** (mechanical, electrical, thermal, chemical).
2. Evaluate **failure modes** under controlled test conditions.
3. Validate that the design conforms to relevant **safety standards** (e.g., IEC, ISO, BIS, ASTM).
4. Generate empirical data for **Design Validation (DV)** and **Design Verification (DVE)**.

**Standard Metrics Used in Prototyping Evaluation:**

- **Dimensional Accuracy (DA):** typically expressed as a **tolerance of $\pm 0.1$ mm to $\pm 0.5$ mm** for engineering prototypes.
- **Lead Time:** measured in **days or weeks** depending on fidelity.
- **Cost Ratio:** $\text{Cost Ratio} = \dfrac{C_{\text{prototype}}}{C_{\text{final product}}}$, usually between **0.01 and 0.30** for low-fidelity prototypes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The Architect's Scale Model**
>
> Imagine an architect designing a 30-storey apartment. Before laying a single brick on real soil, she builds a **scale model** on a table. She shakes the table to simulate earthquakes, blows a fan to simulate wind, and pours coloured water on the roof to study rain drainage. By doing so, she discovers that the balcony railing is too low (a fall hazard) and the kitchen exhaust vent faces the neighbour's window (a health hazard). She fixes both *before* a single rupee is spent on concrete.
>
> That scale model is the **prototype**. In product design, the architect is the design engineer, the table-shaking is the safety test, and the building is the final consumer product.

This analogy captures the **three cardinal purposes** of prototyping in safety engineering:

| Purpose | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Discover hidden hazards** | Unintended failure modes only become visible under physical testing. |
| **Validate safety margins** | Confirm that design clearances, insulation gaps, and structural loads meet standards. |
| **Communicate risk to stakeholders** | A physical model lets non-engineers (managers, regulators, users) *see* and *feel* the risk. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Prototyping Lifecycle (Structured Logic)

A safety-driven prototyping effort follows a **closed-loop, iterative cycle**. The steps below are the logical backbone of any Module-4 answer.

- **Step 1 — Concept Freeze:** The design intent, functional requirements, and applicable safety standard are locked. No prototype is built without a written safety specification.
- **Step 2 — Fidelity Selection:** The engineer decides *which type* of prototype is needed (low-, mid-, or high-fidelity). This choice is governed by the **risk class** of the product (see Table below).
- **Step 3 — Fabrication:** Physical or digital construction using appropriate rapid-prototyping technology (3D printing, CNC, vacuum casting, breadboard electronics, etc.).
- **Step 4 — Safety Testing:** Subjecting the prototype to controlled hazards — drop, impact, ingress (IP rating), thermal cycling, dielectric strength, overload, ergonomics.
- **Step 5 — Failure Documentation:** Every observed defect is logged in a **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** sheet with **Severity (S)**, **Occurrence (O)**, **Detection (D)** scores.
- **Step 6 — Design Iteration:** The CAD model is updated to eliminate or mitigate the failure. A new prototype is fabricated.
- **Step 7 — Sign-off:** Only when the prototype passes the safety test plan does the design progress to **tooling and mass production**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this loop so critical for safety?**
> Because a defect discovered at the *prototype stage* costs **\mathbf{1\times}** to fix, while the same defect caught *after launch* can cost **\mathbf{100\times} to \mathbf{1000\times}** in recalls, lawsuits, and reputational damage.

## 2.2 Classification of Prototypes (Engineering Taxonomy)

Prototypes are classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

### Axis A — By Fidelity
- **Low-Fidelity (Lo-Fi):** Foam models, paper sketches, cardboard mock-ups, "looks-like" models. Used for **ergonomic and conceptual** safety checks.
- **Mid-Fidelity (Mi-Fi):** 3D-printed single-material parts, off-the-shelf microcontroller boards. Used for **fit, form, and basic functional** tests.
- **High-Fidelity (Hi-Fi):** Production-intent materials, CNC-machined, painted, assembled. Used for **certification, regulatory, and user-acceptance** safety tests.

### Axis B — By Function
- **Visual Prototype:** For geometry and aesthetics review.
- **Functional Prototype:** Demonstrates the *working principle* (e.g., a working latch, a circuit).
- **Structural Prototype:** Validates load-bearing capacity.
- **User-Experience (UX) Prototype:** Validates human factors and safe interaction.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use in Safety Design |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Risk Priority Number (FMEA) | Quantifying prototype test findings |
| 2 | $DA = \dfrac{\vert x_{\text{meas}} - x_{\text{design}} \vert}{x_{\text{design}}} \times 100\%$ | Dimensional Accuracy (%) | Verifying prototype geometric conformance |
| 3 | $CR = \dfrac{C_{\text{proto}}}{C_{\text{final}}}$ | Cost Ratio | Justifying prototyping budget |
| 4 | $S_{f} = \dfrac{F_{\text{applied}}}{F_{\text{allowable}}}$ | Safety Factor (must be $\geq n_{\text{code}}$) | Structural prototype validation |
| 5 | $t_{\text{cycle}} = t_{\text{fab}} + t_{\text{test}} + t_{\text{review}}$ | Iteration cycle time (days) | Schedule planning |
| 6 | $SF_{d} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{\text{ref}}}{p_{\text{meas}}}\right)$ | Sound reduction (dB) | Acoustic safety of prototype enclosures |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Every formula above is *directly* traceable to a **Clause in a Safety Standard**. For instance, $S_{f}$ ties to ISO 12100 (Safety of Machinery — General Principles), while $SF_{d}$ ties to ISO 3744 (Acoustics).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Prototyping is the **bridge** between abstract Computer-Aided Design (CAD) and the physical product. In safety-critical domains — medical devices (ISO 13485), automotive (ISO 26262), children's toys (IS 9873), electrical appliances (IEC 60335) — a **physical prototype tested in a certified lab is non-negotiable** before market release.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Risk Priority Number (RPN) for Prototype Safety Evaluation

The RPN is the workhorse metric for ranking safety risks discovered during prototype testing. We derive its components step by step.

### Step 1 — Define the Severity Score ($S$)

Severity is a **discrete integer rating from 1 to 10**, assigned by the safety engineer based on the consequence of failure.

$$S = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if failure has no effect (negligible)} \\ 5 & \text{if failure causes minor injury (first-aid)} \\ 7 & \text{if failure causes serious injury} \\ 10 & \text{if failure causes death or permanent disability} \end{cases}$$

### Step 2 — Define the Occurrence Score ($O$)

Occurrence rates are also mapped to a 1–10 scale using historical failure data or supplier data.

$$O = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{very unlikely (} \lt 1 \text{ in 1,000,000)} \\ 5 & \text{occasional (} \approx 1 \text{ in 2,000)} \\ 10 & \text{very high (} \geq 1 \text{ in 2)} \end{cases}$$

### Step 3 — Define the Detection Score ($D$)

Detection measures how easily the failure mode is caught *before* reaching the user. **Higher $D$ means harder to detect.**

$$D = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{almost certain to detect via automated test} \\ 5 & \text{moderate detection probability} \\ 10 & \text{no known inspection method} \end{cases}$$

### Step 4 — Combine into the RPN

The RPN is the product of the three independent scores.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN} &= S \times O \times D \\[4pt]
\text{with} \quad & 1 \leq S, O, D \leq 10 \\[4pt]
\text{hence} \quad & 1 \leq \text{RPN} \leq 1000
\end{aligned}$$

**Row-by-row logic:**
- Multiplying, rather than adding, ensures that *any* single catastrophic dimension (e.g., $S = 10$ for a fatal hazard) produces a high RPN even if the other two scores are moderate.
- A **threshold** is typically set by the company; failures with $\text{RPN} \geq 100$ are mandatory "must-fix" items before the next prototype.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

A **handheld electric drill prototype** is being safety-tested. The design team records the following from drop-test, insulation, and thermal runs:

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= 8 \quad (\text{electric shock can cause serious injury}) \\
O &= 4 \quad (\text{occasional in field}) \\
D &= 6 \quad (\text{detectable by routine Hi-Pot test but not by user})
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN} &= S \times O \times D \\
&= 8 \times 4 \times 6 \\
&= 192
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\text{RPN} = 192 > 100$, this failure mode **must** be addressed before the next prototype iteration. The mitigation could be adding double insulation (lowers $S$) or a user-visible insulation-warning label (lowers $D$).

## 3.2 Derivation of Cost Ratio Justification for a Prototype Programme

To convince management to fund a prototyping programme, the safety engineer often expresses savings in terms of the **cost-of-error multiplier**.

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{total}} &= C_{\text{proto}} + C_{\text{design}} + C_{\text{tooling}} + C_{\text{recall}} \cdot P_{\text{failure}}
\end{aligned}$$

where $P_{\text{failure}}$ is the probability that an undetected design defect reaches the market. Taking the derivative with respect to $P_{\text{failure}}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dC_{\text{total}}}{dP_{\text{failure}}} &= C_{\text{recall}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** A small reduction in $P_{\text{failure}}$ (achieved by thorough prototype testing) yields a saving proportional to the *enormous* recall cost. This is why even expensive prototyping programmes are economically justified for safety-critical products.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Prototype Fidelity vs. Safety Use

| Prototype Type | Typical Cost Ratio $CR$ | Cycle Time $t_{\text{cycle}}$ | Best Safety Use | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paper / Sketch** | $\mathbf{0.001}$ | $< \mathbf{1}$ day | Conceptual hazard brainstorming | No measurable data |
| **Cardboard / Foam** | $\mathbf{0.01}$ | $\mathbf{1\text{–}3}$ days | Ergonomic / reach / pinch-point checks | Cannot survive load tests |
| **3D-Printed (FDM/SLA)** | $\mathbf{0.05}$ | $\mathbf{2\text{–}5}$ days | Form-fit, basic mechanical trials | Limited material data |
| **CNC Machined** | $\mathbf{0.20}$ | $\mathbf{1\text{–}2}$ weeks | Structural load-bearing tests | Higher cost |
| **Soft Tooling / Vacuum Cast** | $\mathbf{0.30}$ | $\mathbf{2\text{–}4}$ weeks | Limited pre-production validation | Tool wear limits |
| **Production-Intent (Hi-Fi)** | $\mathbf{0.80\text{–}1.00}$ | $\mathbf{4\text{–}8}$ weeks | Final certification / regulatory tests | Nearly full product cost |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Prototyping Lifecycle Flow


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank | Module 4]**
**Q:** Define *prototyping* in the context of safety in product design. List **any four** purposes a prototype serves.

**Model Answer:**
> Prototyping is the engineering practice of constructing a preliminary, tangible, or simulated version of a product to evaluate its safety, function, and form before committing to mass production.
>
> Four key purposes:
> 1. **Hazard identification** — discover hidden failure modes.
> 2. **Design validation** — verify conformance to safety standards.
> 3. **Risk communication** — let stakeholders physically see risks.
> 4. **Iterative refinement** — feed test data back into CAD for redesign.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank | Module 4]**
**Q:** Differentiate between **low-fidelity** and **high-fidelity** prototypes. Give one engineering example of each.

**Model Answer:**
> | Aspect | Low-Fidelity | High-Fidelity |
> |---|---|---|
> | Cost Ratio $CR$ | $\mathbf{0.001\text{–}0.05}$ | $\mathbf{0.80\text{–}1.00}$ |
> | Materials | Paper, foam, cardboard | Production-grade metals, plastics |
> | Use | Concept, ergonomics | Certification, regulatory |
> | Example | Foam mock-up of a TV remote | CNC-machined aluminium TV remote body with final paint |
>
> Low-fidelity answers *"Does it feel right?"* while high-fidelity answers *"Does it pass the safety standard?"*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper | Module 4, Q Choice A]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **classification of prototypes** by fidelity and by function. Why is a *mid-fidelity* prototype often the **most cost-effective choice** for early safety validation?

**Model Solution:**

Prototypes are classified along two independent axes:

**Axis 1 — By Fidelity:**
- **Low-fidelity:** Paper, foam, cardboard. Cheap and fast. Used for ergonomic and conceptual safety reviews (e.g., pinch-point detection on a paper mock-up of a power tool).
- **Mid-fidelity:** 3D-printed (FDM/SLA) single-material parts, breadboard electronics. Gives measurable data on fit, form, and basic mechanical behaviour.
- **High-fidelity:** CNC-machined, painted, assembled with production-intent materials. Used for final regulatory certification.

**Axis 2 — By Function:**
- **Visual:** Geometry/aesthetics review only.
- **Functional:** Working principle demonstration.
- **Structural:** Load-bearing validation.
- **UX / Human-Factors:** Interaction safety.

**Why mid-fidelity is often optimal for early safety validation:**
1. **Cost-effective:** $CR \approx 0.05$ versus $0.80\text{–}1.00$ for Hi-Fi — affordable to make multiple iterations.
2. **Fast turnaround:** $t_{\text{cycle}} \approx 2\text{–}5$ days.
3. **Sufficient accuracy:** SLA prints achieve $\pm 0.1$ mm tolerance, enough for fit checks and most mechanical safety tests.
4. **Allows "fail fast" philosophy** — multiple design ideas can be tested cheaply before committing to expensive high-fidelity tooling.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A safety engineer tests a prototype **kitchen blender**. During drop-test, the blade shaft loosens. Using the FMEA technique, calculate the **RPN** given $S = 9$, $O = 5$, $D = 7$. Justify whether the design must be re-iterated.

**Model Solution:**

Applying the formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN} &= S \times O \times D \\
&= 9 \times 5 \times 7 \\
&= 315
\end{aligned}$$

**Justification:**
- Threshold for mandatory re-iteration is conventionally $\text{RPN} \geq 100$.
- Here $\text{RPN} = 315 \gg 100$, so the design **must be re-iterated**.
- Recommendations:
  - Add a **mechanical locking feature** (e.g., circlip or thread-locker) to lower $O$.
  - Use a **higher-grade stainless steel** shaft to lower $S$.
  - Add a **post-assembly torque audit** to lower $D$.
- A revised prototype should be fabricated and the blade-shaft loosening failure re-tested before sign-off.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper | Module 4, Q Choice B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **step-by-step prototyping lifecycle** for a safety-critical product. Highlight the role of **iteration**.

**Model Solution:**

The safety-driven prototyping lifecycle consists of seven iterative steps:

1. **Concept Freeze:** Lock the design intent and write a Safety Specification citing applicable standards (e.g., IEC 60335 for home appliances).
2. **Fidelity Selection:** Choose Lo-Fi, Mi-Fi, or Hi-Fi based on the risk class of the product.
3. **Fabrication:** Build the prototype using appropriate rapid-prototyping methods.
4. **Safety Testing:** Subject the prototype to drop, impact, thermal, electrical, ingress (IP), and ergonomic tests.
5. **Failure Documentation:** Log every defect in an FMEA sheet with $S$, $O$, $D$ scores.
6. **Iteration:** If any $\text{RPN} \geq 100$, modify the CAD model and re-enter at Step 2.
7. **Sign-off:** Only after all RPNs fall below threshold is the design released for tooling.

**Role of Iteration:** Iteration is the *heart* of safe prototyping. It transforms a flawed design into a robust one by repeatedly testing, learning, and refining. The **economic value** of iteration lies in the principle *"fail cheap, fail early."*

**(b) [7 Marks]** A startup has a budget of **₹ 5,00,000** for prototyping a new electric iron. If each prototype costs **₹ 25,000**, how many prototypes can be built? If each prototype eliminates defects that would otherwise cost **₹ 10,00,000** in recalls with 30 % probability, justify whether the budget is justified.

**Model Solution:**

**Number of prototypes affordable:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{\text{Total Budget}}{\text{Cost per Prototype}} \\
&= \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000}{25{,}000} \\
&= 20 \text{ prototypes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Expected cost of NOT prototyping (recall cost):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E[C_{\text{recall}}] &= P_{\text{failure}} \times C_{\text{recall}} \\
&= 0.30 \times 10{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 3{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Net Benefit Analysis:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Saving} &= E[C_{\text{recall avoided}}] - C_{\text{proto}} \\
&= 3{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 \\
&= -2{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

At first glance, this is a **net loss** of ₹ 2,00,000. However, the *true* justification includes:
1. **Brand reputation** saved (unquantifiable but very large).
2. **Legal liability** under the Consumer Protection Act 2019 (penalties can exceed ₹ 1 crore).
3. **Insurance premium** reduction (lower product liability rating).
4. Each iteration also lowers $P_{\text{failure}}$ — so 20 prototypes are not redundant but **progressively reduce risk**.

Therefore, the prototyping budget is **ethically and strategically justified**, even when the direct cost-benefit arithmetic is marginal.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module-4 Answers:**
> 1. **Confusing prototype "types" with prototype "purposes."** Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you list foam/cardboard as "purposes" instead of types.
> 2. **Forgetting to compute RPN.** A 7-mark FMEA question **always** requires the numerical RPN; qualitative answer alone gets at most 3 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the iteration loop.** Many students describe a linear flow (Design → Build → Test). Examiners look for the **closed feedback loop** — mention iteration explicitly.
> 4. **Omitting standard references.** Always cite *at least one* relevant standard (ISO, IEC, BIS) when discussing safety in product design.
> 5. **Writing "prototype = model."** A model is a *subset* of prototypes. Prototypes include functional, structural, and digital (simulation) variants.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Prototyping Basics**
>
> - **Definition:** Preliminary version of a product to test safety, function, form.
> - **Three fidelities:** Lo-Fi (cheap, fast, ergonomic), Mi-Fi (measurable, optimal for early safety), Hi-Fi (certification-grade).
> - **Four functions:** Visual, Functional, Structural, UX.
> - **Seven-step lifecycle:** Concept → Fidelity Selection → Fabrication → Testing → FMEA → Iteration → Sign-off.
> - **Key metric — RPN:** $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$, threshold usually $\mathbf{\geq 100}$.
> - **Cost Ratio:** $CR = C_{\text{proto}} / C_{\text{final}}$, range $\mathbf{0.001\text{–}1.00}$.
> - **Safety Factor:** $S_{f} = F_{\text{applied}} / F_{\text{allowable}}$; must exceed code minimum.
> - **Iteration is mandatory:** Any failure with $\text{RPN} \geq 100$ forces a re-design and a new prototype.
> - **Always cite a standard** (ISO 12100, IEC 60335, ISO 13485, ISO 26262) when writing safety-prototyping answers.
> - **"Fail cheap, fail early"** is the guiding principle of safety prototyping economics.
> - **Closed-loop feedback** (test → FMEA → redesign → re-fabricate) is the single most mark-fetching concept in Module-4 answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Prototyping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT747 - SAFETY IN PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principles of Prototyping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Safety in Product Design**, **Prototyping** is defined as the iterative construction and evaluation of a preliminary, simplified, and often partial version of a product (or system) intended to expose design assumptions, validate functional behaviour, uncover latent hazards, and gather user-feedback data *before* committing to expensive tooling, mass production, or final certification. A **Prototype** is therefore a tangible (or virtual) instantiation of a design hypothesis.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme outcome-based framework, prototyping is positioned as a **DFM/DFA (Design for Manufacturing / Design for Assembly)** validation tool, sitting at the intersection of *Concept Generation*, *Embodiment Design*, and *Safety Risk Assessment (FMEA/FTA)*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner phrasing):**
> *"A prototype is an experimental sample or model built to test a concept, demonstrate functional feasibility, identify failure modes, and reduce design uncertainty prior to full-scale production."*

**Key vocabulary to commit to memory:**
- **Fidelity** — the degree to which a prototype approximates the final product (Low / Medium / High).
- **Throwaway vs. Evolutionary prototype** — built once for learning vs. iteratively refined.
- **Horizontal vs. Vertical prototype** — broad shallow features vs. deep narrow features.
- **Pilot Production Run** — small-batch prototype used to validate manufacturing process safety.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a chef designing a new dish for a 5-star restaurant. Before printing 5,000 menus, you would:

1. **Taste-test a single portion** (a *low-fidelity prototype*) to check flavour balance.
2. **Serve it to a few trusted customers** (a *medium-fidelity, user-feedback prototype*).
3. **Plate and present it like the final dish** (a *high-fidelity prototype*) to verify visual appeal and service logistics.
4. **Document allergens and kitchen-safety risks** (a *safety-validation prototype* / FMEA trial).

If the dish causes any issue, you throw the test version away — *throwaway prototype*. If the recipe is great and you just refine spices, the dish *evolves* into the final menu item — *evolutionary prototype*.

**In product design, the prototype IS the taste-test of engineering.** It costs far less to discover a sharp edge, a flammable material, or a confusing interface in a prototype than in a 50,000-unit production recall.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why prototyping matters in SAFETY specifically:**
> Approximately **70%–80% of a product's total life-cycle cost** is committed during the early design phase. Safety hazards identified *after* tooling cost up to **100× more** to correct. Prototyping shifts hazard discovery *upstream*, where it is both cheaper and more forgiving.

## 1.3 Core Principles (KTU-Mandated Keywords)

1. **Purpose-Driven Design** — every prototype must answer a specific question (e.g., "Will users mistake this switch for an emergency stop?").
2. **Iterative Refinement** — prototypes are improved in cycles, not in one shot.
3. **Fail-Fast Philosophy** — surface defects as early as possible.
4. **Cost-Time-Fidelity Balance** — higher fidelity costs more time and money; the trick is to match fidelity to the question being asked.
5. **User-in-the-Loop** — prototypes must be evaluated against real user interaction to catch ergonomic and behavioural safety risks.
6. **Safety-First Validation** — every prototype is also a *hazard-discovery tool*, not just a function test.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Prototypes

A clean engineering taxonomy (the one examiners prefer to see) is:

| **Dimension** | **Category A** | **Category B** |
|---|---|---|
| **Form** | Physical Prototype | Virtual (Digital) Prototype |
| **Purpose** | Proof-of-Concept Prototype | User-Experience Prototype |
| **Purpose (alt)** | Form/Visual Prototype | Functional Prototype |
| **Fidelity** | Low-Fidelity | High-Fidelity |
| **Lifecycle** | Throwaway (Rapid) | Evolutionary |
| **Coverage** | Horizontal (breadth) | Vertical (depth) |
| **Material** | Mock-up (foam, cardboard) | Production-Intent (same materials) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** Examiners often pose a 3-mark question: *"Differentiate between throwaway and evolutionary prototyping."* Memorise the trade-off — Throwaway is *fast and cheap*; Evolutionary is *closer to final product* but commits you to the early design choices.

## 2.2 The Prototyping Process Flow

Theoretically, prototyping follows the *Plan–Build–Test–Learn* loop (also called the **Build–Measure–Learn** cycle in Lean Startup literature, which is acceptable in KTU answers).

**Step-by-step logic (suitable for 7-mark answers):**

1. **Identify the design question** — what unknown must be resolved? (e.g., ergonomic safety of a handle).
2. **Choose the appropriate fidelity** — match fidelity to the *risk* of being wrong.
3. **Select the prototype type** — physical, virtual, or hybrid.
4. **Construct the prototype** — using rapid tools (3D printing, CNC, mock-up, simulation).
5. **Test under realistic conditions** — including *abuse* and *misuse* scenarios for safety.
6. **Capture data & hazards** — log failures, near-misses, ergonomic stress.
7. **Decide: Refine / Pivot / Proceed** — feed learning back to the next design iteration.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following relations are commonly tested in 7–14 mark questions and in numerical sub-parts. All constants are **empirical engineering estimates** consistent with KTU board keys.

| **Concept** | **Equation / Relation** | **Variables & Units** | **Safety Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cost of Change Curve | $C_{\text{change}}(s) \;=\; C_{0}\cdot e^{k\,s}$ | $C_{0}$ = base design cost (₹); $s$ = stage number; $k$ = escalation constant (≈ 0.4 to 0.6) | Cost grows **exponentially** as stage advances. Prototype early to keep $s$ small. |
| Relative Cost Ratio | $R \;=\; \dfrac{C_{\text{prototype}}}{C_{\text{tooling}}}$ | $R \ll 1$ (typically 0.001 – 0.05) | Proves why prototyping is economically rational. |
| Time-to-Hazard-Detection | $t_{\text{hazard}} \;=\; t_{\text{design}} \;+\; n\cdot t_{\text{iteration}}$ | $t_{\text{iter}}$ = one prototype cycle time | Each iteration $n$ shifts hazard detection *left* in time. |
| Prototype Fidelity Index | $F \;=\; \dfrac{\sum w_{i}\,f_{i}}{\sum w_{i}}$ | $w_{i}$ = weight of feature $i$; $f_{i}$ ∈ [0,1] fidelity score | Quantifies "how close to final" a prototype is. |
| Safety Defect Discovery Rate | $D_{\text{rate}} \;=\; \dfrac{N_{\text{defects found}}}{N_{\text{prototypes tested}}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Benchmarks testing thoroughness. |
| Risk Reduction via Iteration | $R_{n+1} \;=\; R_{n}\,(1-\alpha)$ | $R_{n}$ = residual risk at iteration $n$; $\alpha$ = risk-reduction factor per cycle (0.1–0.3) | Each prototype iteration reduces residual risk geometrically. |
| Effective Sample Cost | $C_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \dfrac{C_{\text{proto}} + C_{\text{failure}}}{P_{\text{success}}}$ | $P_{\text{success}}$ = probability prototype achieves its question | Decision metric for "build or skip" prototype. |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical LaTeX Substitution Rule (markdown table safety):**
> Whenever absolute value would normally be written as `|x|`, **never** use the vertical pipe character inside a markdown table cell — it will break the table rendering. Instead write $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in inline math mode.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Safety**: Crash-test prototypes (crash-dummies in sled rigs) are *evolutionary, high-fidelity, vertical* prototypes used to validate airbag deployment, seat-belt load paths, and bumper energy-absorption **before** homologation.
- **Medical Devices**: Surgical instruments are prototyped in sterilizable polymers and tested for **biocompatibility, slip, and pinch hazards** using FMEA-driven prototype matrices.
- **Consumer Electronics**: Low-fidelity foam mock-ups are built to study *grip ergonomics* and *accidental button-press hazards* long before injection moulds are cut.
- **Aerospace**: Virtual (digital-twin) prototypes simulate thermal, structural, and electromagnetic safety envelopes, replacing dozens of physical wind-tunnel runs.

> [!NOTE]
> In each case, prototyping converts **unknown safety risk** into **known, quantified, mitigable risk** — which is the central mission of the PESFT747 syllabus.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the "Cost-of-Change" Exponential Model

The empirical observation in product development is that the cost to correct a design defect grows roughly **tenfold** with each successive stage (concept → detail design → tooling → production → field use). This is the **1-10-100-1000 Rule** widely used in KTU valuations.

**Starting assumption:**
The marginal cost to fix a defect at stage $s+1$ is $k$ times the cost at stage $s$.

$$
C_{s+1} \;=\; k \cdot C_{s}
$$

**Step 1 — Expand the recurrence** by unrolling the recurrence over $s$ stages:

$$
C_{s} \;=\; k \cdot C_{s-1} \;=\; k \cdot (k \cdot C_{s-2}) \;=\; k^{2}\,C_{s-2} \;=\; \cdots
$$

**Step 2 — Generalise** after $s$ unrollings:

$$
C_{s} \;=\; k^{\,s}\,C_{0}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert discrete $k^{s}$ to continuous form** by writing $k = e^{\,\ln k}$ and recognising that the literature uses a continuous growth constant $\beta$ such that $k \equiv e^{\beta}$:

$$
C_{s} \;=\; \left(e^{\,\beta}\right)^{s}\,C_{0} \;=\; C_{0}\,e^{\,\beta s}
$$

**Step 4 — Relabel** $\beta$ as the stage-escalation constant $k$ (the symbol KTU books conventionally use):

$$
\boxed{\,C_{\text{change}}(s) \;=\; C_{0}\,e^{\,k s}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:**
- If a defect costs ₹1,000 to fix at the concept stage ($s = 0$),
- and $k = 0.5$ (typical empirical value),

then by the production stage ($s = 4$):

$$
C_{\text{change}}(4) \;=\; 1000 \cdot e^{(0.5)(4)} \;=\; 1000 \cdot e^{2}
$$

$$
C_{\text{change}}(4) \;=\; 1000 \cdot 7.389
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C_{\text{change}}(4) \;\approx\; \text{₹}\,7{,}389\,}
$$

> This numerical value demonstrates the *economic urgency* of early-stage prototyping — catching the same defect in a foam mock-up (₹1,000) versus in production (₹7,389) is a **7× saving**, *before* the customer-incurred cost of field recall is added.

## 3.2 Derivation of Geometric Risk Reduction

A safety-driven design team is said to perform *iterative prototyping* if each cycle reduces residual risk by a constant multiplicative factor $\alpha$.

**Recurrence relation:**

$$
R_{n+1} \;=\; R_{n}\,(1-\alpha)
$$

**Step 1 — Unroll the recurrence** for $n$ iterations:

$$
R_{n} \;=\; R_{0}\,(1-\alpha)^{n}
$$

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm** to study the iteration count needed to reach a target residual risk $R_{\text{target}}$:

$$
\ln R_{n} \;=\; \ln R_{0} \;+\; n\,\ln(1-\alpha)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $n$:**

$$
n \;=\; \dfrac{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{R_{\text{target}}}{R_{0}}\right)}{\ln(1-\alpha)}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration.** Suppose initial risk $R_{0} = 100$ units, target risk $R_{\text{target}} = 5$ units, and $\alpha = 0.3$:

$$
n \;=\; \dfrac{\ln(5/100)}{\ln(1-0.3)} \;=\; \dfrac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(0.7)} \;=\; \dfrac{-2.9957}{-0.3567}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,n \;\approx\; 8.40 \;\Rightarrow\; 9 \text{ prototype iterations}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** Roughly **9 prototyping cycles** are required to bring risk from 100 → 5 units at 30% reduction per cycle. This is why safety-critical products (aircraft, medical implants) undergo *hundreds* of small, low-cost prototype iterations.

## 3.3 Effective Sample Cost Decision Rule

To decide whether a prototype is worth building, compare its **Effective Sample Cost** $C_{\text{eff}}$ to the **Expected Loss without prototyping** $L_{\text{skip}}$.

$$
C_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \dfrac{C_{\text{proto}} + C_{\text{failure}}}{P_{\text{success}}}
$$

**Decision rule:**

$$
\text{Build prototype} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad C_{\text{eff}} \;<\; P_{\text{hazard}}\cdot L_{\text{skip}}
$$

**Step-by-step worked example:**

- $C_{\text{proto}}$ = ₹50,000 (3D-printed dummy for drop test)
- $C_{\text{failure}}$ = ₹20,000 (rejected/rebuilt dummy)
- $P_{\text{success}}$ = 0.8
- $P_{\text{hazard}}$ = 0.4 (probability of latent hazard if skipped)
- $L_{\text{skip}}$ = ₹5,00,000 (cost of one field recall)

$$
C_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \dfrac{50{,}000 + 20{,}000}{0.8} \;=\; \dfrac{70{,}000}{0.8} \;=\; \text{₹}\,87{,}500
$$

$$
P_{\text{hazard}}\cdot L_{\text{skip}} \;=\; 0.4 \times 5{,}00{,}000 \;=\; \text{₹}\,2{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{\,87{,}500 \;<\; 2{,}00{,}000 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Build the prototype.}\,}
$$

> The arithmetic argument is decisive: spend ₹87,500 to *guarantee* avoidance of a ₹2,00,000 expected loss. This is the core safety-economics justification of prototyping.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Prototyping Process Cycle (Build–Measure–Learn)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Unresolved Design Question]) --> planB[Identify Design Question]
    planB --> fidC[Select Fidelity Level]
    fidC --> typD[Choose Prototype Type]
    typD --> buildE[Construct Prototype]
    buildE --> testF[Test Under Realistic Conditions]
    testF --> dataG[Capture Hazards and Data]
    dataG --> decideH{Decision Gate}
    decideH -- Refine --> planB
    decideH -- Pivot --> startA
    decideH -- Proceed --> endI([Proceed to Final Production])
```

> **Reading aid:** The diagram maps the *Plan → Build → Test → Learn* loop. The decision gate `decideH` is the heart of safety-driven prototyping — every loop iteration converts an *unknown hazard* into a *known, quantified, mitigated hazard*.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Prototype Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root1[Prototype Universe] --> phys2[Physical]
    root1 --> virt3[Virtual Digital]
    phys2 --> proof4[Proof of Concept]
    phys2 --> form5[Form and Visual]
    phys2 --> func6[Functional]
    proof4 --> throw7[Throwaway Low Cost]
    form5 --> evo8[Evolutionary High Fidelity]
    func6 --> vert9[Vertical Depth Focus]
    func6 --> horz10[Horizontal Breadth Focus]
    virt3 --> sim11[Simulation CAE FEA]
    virt3 --> twin12[Digital Twin]
```

> **Reading aid:** This taxonomy tree is a frequent 7-mark KTU question. Be ready to place a *given product scenario* into exactly one branch of the tree, and justify your placement.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Safety Validation Layer Added to Prototyping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userX[User Interaction Data] --> safA[Hazard Identification Log]
    buildB[Build Phase] --> safA
    testC[Functional and Abuse Tests] --> safA
    safA --> fmeaD[FMEA Risk Score Update]
    fmeaD --> gatego{Acceptable Risk Level}
    gatego -- Yes --> shipE[Proceed to Production]
    gatego -- No --> iterateF[Trigger Extra Prototype Iteration]
    iterateF --> buildB
```

> **Reading aid:** This diagram answers the KTU 2024 module-specific framing — *how does prototyping reduce safety risk?* Answer: by **feeding real prototype data into FMEA risk scores**, gating production on acceptable risk, and looping back automatically when risk is unacceptable.

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Fidelity vs Cost vs Time Trade-Off (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    lowF[Low Fidelity Foam Mock-up] -- low cost, fast feedback --> earlyS[Concept Stage s equals 0]
    midF[Medium Fidelity Functional] -- moderate cost, real ergonomics --> midS[Embodiment Stage s equals 2]
    highF[High Fidelity Production Intent] -- high cost, certification grade --> lateS[Tooling Stage s equals 4]
    earlyS --> midS --> lateS
```

> **Reading aid:** The cost-and-time budget *increases* with fidelity, but the *value of information* is highest at the **lowest** fidelity that resolves the design question. The art of prototyping is choosing the *cheapest fidelity that still answers the question*.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define prototyping. List any four principles of prototyping relevant to safety-oriented product design.**

**Model Answer (Examiner-Key Wording):**

> **Prototyping** is the iterative construction and evaluation of a simplified, partial, or scaled model of a product to test design assumptions, validate functionality, and identify hazards before committing to full production.
>
> *Four principles relevant to safety:*
> 1. **Purpose-driven design** — every prototype must answer a specific safety question.
> 2. **Fail-fast philosophy** — expose hazards early when cost of correction is low.
> 3. **User-in-the-loop testing** — real users reveal ergonomic and behavioural hazards.
> 4. **Iterative refinement** — successive cycles geometrically reduce residual risk $R_{n+1} = R_{n}(1-\alpha)$.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between *throwaway* and *evolutionary* prototypes. Give one engineering example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **Throwaway Prototype** | **Evolutionary Prototype** |
|---|---|---|
| Intent | Built once for learning, then discarded | Progressively refined into the final product |
| Cost | Low | High (committed to early choices) |
| Lead time | Hours to days | Weeks to months |
| Fidelity | Usually low | Progressively higher |
| Example | Foam mock-up of a kettle handle to test grip safety | A working pre-production aircraft wing-section that survives multiple test cycles |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Total 14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the *Cost-of-Change* model in product development. Using the equation $C_{\text{change}}(s) = C_{0}\,e^{k s}$, compute the cost to fix a defect at stage $s = 5$ if $C_{0} = \text{₹}\,2{,}000$ and $k = 0.45$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the *Build–Measure–Learn* prototyping cycle and explain how it integrates with FMEA for safety validation.

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — State the model:**

$$
C_{\text{change}}(s) \;=\; C_{0}\,e^{k s}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values** $C_{0} = 2000$, $k = 0.45$, $s = 5$:

$$
C_{\text{change}}(5) \;=\; 2000 \cdot e^{(0.45)(5)} \;=\; 2000 \cdot e^{2.25}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate** $e^{2.25}$:

$$
e^{2.25} \;\approx\; 9.4877
$$

**Step 4 — Final calculation:**

$$
C_{\text{change}}(5) \;=\; 2000 \times 9.4877 \;\approx\; \text{₹}\,18{,}975
$$

**Conclusion:** The cost has grown by nearly **9.5×** between the concept stage and stage 5. This is the empirical justification for early prototyping.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Diagram (drawn in answer book):**

```
[Identify Question] → [Select Fidelity] → [Build Prototype]
        ↑                                          ↓
[Decide: Refine / Pivot / Proceed]   ← [Capture FMEA Risk Data] ← [Test]
```

**Step 1** — Identify the unresolved design safety question.
**Step 2** — Choose an appropriate fidelity level.
**Step 3** — Build the prototype using rapid methods (3D printing, foam, virtual CAE).
**Step 4** — Test under realistic AND abuse conditions.
**Step 5** — Feed *all test failures* into the FMEA risk-priority-number update:

$$
\text{RPN} \;=\; S \times O \times D
$$

where $S$ = Severity, $O$ = Occurrence, $D$ = Detection score.

**Step 6** — Decide at the gate: if RPN exceeds threshold, *iterate*; else, proceed.

**Safety-validation integration:** Each iteration systematically lowers the *Occurrence* $O$ and improves *Detection* $D$, while severity $S$ is constrained by the inherent design — the prototype's role is to *quantify* the residual RPN before mass production.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Total 14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** A safety-critical automotive component has an initial residual risk $R_{0} = 80$ units. Each prototype iteration reduces risk by $\alpha = 0.25$. How many iterations are needed to bring the risk below **5 units**?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a classification diagram, discuss the different types of prototypes. State the *fidelity-selection rule* an engineer should follow.

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Use the geometric risk-reduction formula:**

$$
R_{n} \;=\; R_{0}\,(1-\alpha)^{n}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute** $R_{0} = 80$, $R_{n} < 5$, $\alpha = 0.25$:

$$
5 \;>\; 80 \cdot (0.75)^{n}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide both sides:**

$$
(0.75)^{n} \;<\; \dfrac{5}{80} \;=\; 0.0625
$$

**Step 4 — Take natural logarithm:**

$$
n\,\ln(0.75) \;<\; \ln(0.0625)
$$

**Step 5 — Solve** (note: $\ln(0.75) < 0$, so inequality flips):

$$
n \;>\; \dfrac{\ln(0.0625)}{\ln(0.75)} \;=\; \dfrac{-2.7726}{-0.2877} \;\approx\; 9.64
$$

**Step 6 — Round up to whole iterations:**

$$
\boxed{\,n \;=\; 10 \text{ prototype iterations required.}\,}
$$

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Classification diagram (text rendering for answer book):**

```
Prototype
 ├── Physical ─── Proof-of-Concept
 │            ├── Form/Visual Mock-up
 │            └── Functional (Horizontal / Vertical)
 └── Virtual  ─── Simulation / CAE
               └── Digital Twin
```

**Types and definitions:**

1. **Proof-of-Concept** — minimal model to verify *one* core principle (e.g., does the airbag inflate fast enough?).
2. **Form/Visual Prototype** — focuses on geometry, ergonomics, perceived safety.
3. **Functional Prototype** — performs the intended function but may use cheaper materials.
4. **Virtual Prototype** — CAE / FEA / digital twin; safe for hazard simulation (e.g., crash, thermal runaway).
5. **Pilot / Pre-production Prototype** — small production batch for certification testing.

**Fidelity-Selection Rule:**

> **Choose the *lowest* fidelity that can still answer the design question, but no lower.**

Justification: lower fidelity = lower cost, lower lead-time, faster iteration. Higher fidelity is reserved for questions that *require* production-grade materials or behaviour (e.g., crash tests, biocompatibility).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing "prototype" with "model"** — a *model* is representational; a *prototype* is *functional* (or at least testable). 1-mark deduction each time the student uses the wrong term.
> 2. **Skipping the cost-arithmetic justification** in 7-mark answers — students often *describe* prototyping without computing $C_{\text{change}}$ or $R_{n}$. Examiners explicitly look for a numerical or formulaic anchor.
> 3. **Ignoring the safety framing** — this is a *Safety in Product Design* course. Pure mechanical/electronics descriptions without FMEA, hazard, or risk language will lose **2–3 marks** per 7-mark sub-question.
> 4. **Failing to state the *fidelity rule*** — board keys always include the line *"match fidelity to the question being asked."* Omitting it = 1 mark lost.
> 5. **Rounding errors in iteration counts** — write $n \approx 9.64$ explicitly, then state *"round up to 10"*. Writing only "10" loses the 1 mark for showing work.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition mantra:** A prototype is a *tangible or virtual instantiation* of a design *hypothesis* — used to *test* assumptions and *uncover hazards*.
- **Three pillars of prototyping:** *Form*, *Function*, *User-Experience*. Most prototypes excel in only one or two.
- **The 1-10-100-1000 rule:** cost of correcting a defect rises roughly tenfold per stage — *catch it in the foam, not in the field*.
- **Throwaway vs. Evolutionary:** throwaway = cheap, fast, learn-and-discard; evolutionary = progressive refinement, commits you to early choices.
- **Horizontal vs. Vertical:** horizontal covers *breadth* (many features shallowly); vertical covers *depth* (few features fully).
- **Fidelity selection rule:** use the *lowest* fidelity that still answers the design question.
- **Safety link:** every prototype iteration should update the FMEA **RPN** = $S \times O \times D$ and reduce residual risk geometrically via $R_{n+1} = R_{n}(1-\alpha)$.
- **Numerical anchors to remember:** $C_{\text{change}}(s) = C_{0}e^{ks}$, $C_{\text{eff}} = (C_{\text{proto}} + C_{\text{failure}})/P_{\text{success}}$, $F = \sum w_i f_i / \sum w_i$.
- **Process loop:** *Identify question → Choose fidelity → Build → Test → Capture data → Decide (refine / pivot / proceed)*.
- **Examiner mantra:** every prototyping answer in *Safety in Product Design* **must** end with a sentence connecting back to *hazard reduction, risk mitigation, or FMEA update*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product Development Economics: Elements of Economic Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT747 - SAFETY IN PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Product Development Economics: Elements of Economic Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Product Development Economics** is the systematic application of microeconomic and engineering-economic principles to evaluate the financial viability, cost structure, profitability, and risk profile of a product throughout its entire lifecycle—from concept generation and design, through prototyping, manufacturing, market launch, and eventual phase-out. It integrates **engineering decision-making** with **financial modeling** to ensure that the safety, functionality, and quality objectives of a product are achieved within acceptable economic constraints.

Within the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (Course Code: PESFT747), the **Elements of Economic Analysis** specifically refer to the foundational building blocks used by a product designer or safety engineer to make quantitative, defensible, and ethically responsible financial decisions. These elements include cost categorization, revenue forecasting, break-even determination, time-value-of-money computations, return-on-investment metrics, sensitivity analysis, and make-or-buy trade-offs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):** *"Elements of Economic Analysis"* encompasses the quantitative tools—cost estimation, pricing strategy, depreciation, break-even analysis, payback period, Net Present Value (NPV), Internal Rate of Return (IRR), and cost-benefit modeling—that a safety engineer applies during product conceptualization and detailed design to guarantee that safety features are not economically unjustified and that product liability costs are minimized.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are designing a new electric two-wheeler helmet with a built-in crash sensor and emergency alert system. Before you invest ₹50 lakhs in tooling, plastics, electronics, and certification, you must answer some very real questions:

1. **How much does each helmet cost to manufacture?** (Cost Estimation)
2. **At what selling price will you neither lose money nor make a profit?** (Break-Even Point)
3. **Will the ₹50 lakh investment recover within 3 years or will it take 10?** (Payback Period)
4. **If inflation is 6% and your discount rate is 12%, what is the *today's* value of the future profits?** (Net Present Value)
5. **Should you manufacture the crash sensor in-house or buy it from Bosch?** (Make-or-Buy Analysis)

**The Elements of Economic Analysis are the toolkit that lets you answer these questions numerically.** Think of it like the **seatbelt and airbag of a business decision**—they protect the project from financial harm in exactly the same way that safety engineering protects the user from physical harm. Both disciplines are about *systematic risk reduction under uncertainty*.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

- **Time Value of Money (TVM) discount rate (i):** typically **8% to 15%** for Indian manufacturing firms in 2024.
- **Corporate tax rate (India, AY 2024–25):** **25.17%** for domestic companies (22% base + 10% surcharge + 4% cess).
- **Standard depreciation rates (Companies Act 2013, Schedule II):**
  - Plant & Machinery: **15%** (Written Down Value method)
  - Tools & Dies: **11.31%**
- **Minimum attractive rate of return (MARR):** typically **12%–18%** for SMEs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Board Examiner expects students to clearly differentiate between **sunk cost** (irrecoverable, must be ignored in forward decisions), **fixed cost** (independent of output volume), **variable cost** (proportional to output volume), and **opportunity cost** (value of the next-best alternative foregone). Confusing these is the single most common reason students lose marks in Module 4 questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Core Elements of Economic Analysis

The KTU Module 4 syllabus groups economic analysis into the following seven elements. Each must be understood in terms of its *engineering purpose* (Why?), its *mathematical mechanism* (How?), and its *decision output* (What?).

### 2.1.1 Cost Classification & Estimation
- **Why?** Safety features (airbags, ABS, sensors) add cost. Without classification, designers cannot tell whether the cost is per-unit (recoverable) or one-time (capital).
- **How?** Costs are classified along two orthogonal axes:
  - *By behavior:* Fixed Cost (FC) vs. Variable Cost (VC).
  - *By traceability:* Direct Cost vs. Indirect Cost (overhead).
- **Decision Output:** Total Cost (TC) curve used for pricing and BEP.

### 2.1.2 Revenue & Profit Modeling
- **Why?** A safety-enhanced product usually commands a price premium, but the engineer must quantify this premium.
- **How?** Total Revenue (TR) = Price × Quantity; Profit = TR − TC.
- **Decision Output:** Target selling price for a desired profit margin.

### 2.1.3 Break-Even Analysis (BEA)
- **Why?** Identifies the minimum sales volume at which the product covers all costs—critical for safety investments where regulatory compliance may force production even if profitability is borderline.
- **How?** Set TR = TC and solve for Q.
- **Decision Output:** Break-Even Quantity (Q_BEP) and Break-Even Sales (₹).

### 2.1.4 Time Value of Money (TVM)
- **Why?** A rupee earned in 2030 is worth less than a rupee spent today due to inflation and opportunity cost.
- **How?** Discount future cash flows back to "present" using a discount rate *i*.
- **Decision Output:** Present Worth (PW), Future Worth (FW), Annual Equivalent (AE).

### 2.1.5 Capital Budgeting Metrics (NPV, IRR, BCR)
- **Why?** Safety capital projects (e.g., installing a ₹2 crore robotic weld cell to reduce worker injury risk) compete with other investments.
- **How?** Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) techniques.
- **Decision Output:** Accept if NPV > 0, or IRR > MARR, or BCR > 1.

### 2.1.6 Payback Period & Discounted Payback
- **Why?** Many SMEs ignore NPV and ask the simple question: "When do I get my money back?"
- **How?** Cumulative cash flow crosses zero.
- **Decision Output:** Years to recover initial investment.

### 2.1.7 Make-or-Buy, Sensitivity & Risk Analysis
- **Why?** Safety components may be cheaper to buy, but sourcing risks (single-supplier) may justify in-house production.
- **How?** Compare in-house unit cost vs. vendor quote; vary key inputs (±20%) to test robustness.
- **Decision Output:** Robust make/buy recommendation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **entire set of equations** you must memorize for Module 4. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid markdown table breakage.

| # | Element | Formula | Variables & Units | Decision Rule |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Total Cost | $TC = FC + (VC_u \times Q)$ | $FC$ = Fixed Cost (₹), $VC_u$ = Variable cost/unit (₹), $Q$ = Quantity (units) | — |
| 2 | Total Revenue | $TR = P \times Q$ | $P$ = Selling price/unit (₹), $Q$ = units | — |
| 3 | Profit | $\pi = TR - TC = (P - VC_u)Q - FC$ | All in ₹ | Maximize |
| 4 | Break-Even Quantity | $Q_{BEP} = \dfrac{FC}{P - VC_u}$ | $P - VC_u$ = Contribution margin (₹) | Reject if forecast $Q < Q_{BEP}$ |
| 5 | Break-Even Sales (₹) | $S_{BEP} = \dfrac{FC}{1 - \dfrac{VC_u}{P}}$ | Ratio = Variable cost ratio | — |
| 6 | Margin of Safety (MoS) | $MoS\% = \dfrac{Q_{actual} - Q_{BEP}}{Q_{actual}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Higher is safer |
| 7 | Single Payment PW | $P = F(1+i)^{-n}$ | $F$ = Future (₹), $i$ = discount rate (decimal), $n$ = years | — |
| 8 | Single Payment FW | $F = P(1+i)^{n}$ | Same as above | — |
| 9 | Uniform Series PW | $P = A \left[\dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}}\right]$ | $A$ = Annual amount (₹) | — |
| 10 | Capital Recovery (A from P) | $A = P \left[\dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}\right]$ | — | — |
| 11 | Sinking Fund (F from A) | $F = A \left[\dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i}\right]$ | — | — |
| 12 | Net Present Value | $NPV = -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^{t}}$ | $C_0$ = Initial investment (₹), $CF_t$ = Net cash flow in year $t$ | Accept if $NPV \geq 0$ |
| 13 | Internal Rate of Return | $NPV(i=IRR) = 0$ | $IRR$ solved by interpolation | Accept if $IRR \geq MARR$ |
| 14 | Benefit-Cost Ratio | $BCR = \dfrac{PV(\text{Benefits})}{PV(\text{Costs})}$ | Ratio | Accept if $BCR \geq 1$ |
| 15 | Payback Period | $T_{pb} = \dfrac{C_0}{\overline{CF_{annual}}}$ | Years (undiscounted) | Reject if $T_{pb}$ > target |
| 16 | Learning Curve | $Y_n = Y_1 \cdot n^{b}$ | $Y_n$ = time/cost of $n^{th}$ unit, $b = \dfrac{\log(\text{learning}\%)}{\log 2}$ | — |
| 17 | Depreciation (SLM) | $D_t = \dfrac{C - S}{N}$ | $C$ = Cost, $S$ = Salvage, $N$ = Life | — |
| 18 | Depreciation (WDV) | $BV_t = C(1-d)^{t}$ | $d$ = WDV rate | — |
| 19 | Make-or-Buy | Compare $C_{make} = VC_u + \dfrac{FC}{Q}$ with $C_{buy} = P_{vendor}$ | ₹/unit | Buy if $C_{buy} < C_{make}$ |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Safety

- **Automotive Safety Engineering:** ABS (Anti-lock Braking System) costs ~₹8,000 extra per vehicle; economic analysis proves the breakeven to the OEM is achieved within 18 months through reduced warranty claims.
- **Aerospace:** Composite material selection is decided using NPV—lighter composites have higher unit cost but massive fuel-burn savings over 25 years.
- **Medical Devices:** FDA-cleared implants must undergo cost-effectiveness analysis (CEA) combining safety and economics.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Lithium-ion battery safety circuits (BMS) are evaluated through learning-curve models—unit cost falls 20% for every doubling of cumulative production.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic step below is shown explicitly. No "it can be shown that…" shortcuts. This is what the KTU External Examiner expects in a 14-mark answer.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Break-Even Quantity (Q_BEP)

**Starting Premise:** Break-even is the operating point where Total Revenue equals Total Cost (no profit, no loss).

$$\pi = TR - TC = 0$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the cost and revenue functions.**

We know:
- Total Cost: $TC = FC + VC_u \cdot Q$
- Total Revenue: $TR = P \cdot Q$

Therefore:

$$\pi = (P \cdot Q) - (FC + VC_u \cdot Q) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Factor out Q on the revenue side.**

$$P \cdot Q - VC_u \cdot Q - FC = 0$$

$$Q(P - VC_u) = FC$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Q.**

$$Q_{BEP} = \frac{FC}{P - VC_u}$$

**Interpretation:** The numerator is the fixed cost that must be recovered; the denominator $(P - VC_u)$ is the **contribution margin**—the portion of every rupee of sales that contributes toward recovering fixed costs. Each additional unit sold after BEP contributes *pure profit*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Net Present Value (NPV) from First Principles

**Starting Premise:** Money has a time value. A future cash flow $CF_t$ occurring in year $t$ is equivalent to a *smaller* present amount $P_t$ today, because today's money could be invested at discount rate $i$ and grow.

**Step 1 — Single-period present value relationship.**

If you invest $P$ today at rate $i$ for one year, you receive $P(1+i)$. Inverting:

$$P = \frac{F}{(1+i)}$$

**Step 2 — Extend to n periods (compounding).**

After $n$ years, $F = P(1+i)^{n}$, so:

$$P = \frac{F}{(1+i)^{n}}$$

**Step 3 — Sum all discounted future cash flows.**

For a project with initial investment $C_0$ (outflow at $t=0$) and net cash inflows $CF_1, CF_2, \ldots, CF_n$:

$$NPV = -C_0 + \frac{CF_1}{(1+i)^{1}} + \frac{CF_2}{(1+i)^{2}} + \cdots + \frac{CF_n}{(1+i)^{n}}$$

**Step 4 — Compact sigma notation (KTU board-friendly form).**

$$NPV = -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+i)^{t}}$$

**Step 5 — Decision Rule.**

- If $NPV > 0$: project adds value → **Accept**.
- If $NPV = 0$: indifferent.
- If $NPV < 0$: project destroys value → **Reject**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Learning Curve Equation

**Empirical Observation (Wright, 1936):** Every time cumulative production doubles, the unit labor hours (or unit cost) fall by a constant percentage (the "learning rate" $L$).

**Step 1 — Define the parameters.**

- $Y_1$ = time/cost of the *first* unit.
- $Y_n$ = time/cost of the *n-th* unit.
- $L$ = learning rate (e.g., 0.80 means 20% improvement on doubling).

**Step 2 — Write the doubling condition.**

When cumulative production goes from $n$ to $2n$:

$$Y_{2n} = L \cdot Y_n$$

**Step 3 — Solve the recurrence.**

Starting from $Y_1$, after $k$ doublings we have produced $n = 2^{k}$ units:

$$Y_{2^{k}} = L^{k} \cdot Y_1$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to any $n$ (not just powers of 2).**

Take $\log_2$ of both sides: $k = \log_2 n$.

$$Y_n = Y_1 \cdot L^{\log_2 n}$$

Using the change-of-base identity $L^{\log_2 n} = n^{\log_2 L}$:

$$\boxed{Y_n = Y_1 \cdot n^{b}, \quad \text{where } b = \log_2 L}$$

**Example (worked numerical):** If $Y_1 = 100$ hours, $L = 0.80$, $n = 100$ units:

$$b = \log_2(0.80) = \frac{\ln(0.80)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{-0.2231}{0.6931} \approx -0.3219$$

$$Y_{100} = 100 \times 100^{-0.3219} \approx 100 \times 0.2166 \approx 21.66 \text{ hours/unit}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Payback Period (Undiscounted)

**Step 1 — Define cumulative cash flow.**

Let $CCF_t = \sum_{k=1}^{t} CF_k$ be the cumulative cash flow at the end of year $t$.

**Step 2 — Find the first year in which $CCF_t \geq C_0$.**

Let $T$ be this year. Then the payback period is:

$$T_{pb} = T - 1 + \frac{C_0 - CCF_{T-1}}{CF_T}$$

where the second term is the **fractional year** required during year $T$ to fully recover the unrecovered portion of $C_0$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Tool

| Situation | Best Tool | Reason |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| Single product, single year, no time effect | Break-Even Analysis | Simple, visually intuitive |
| Multi-year project with clear cash flows | NPV | Theoretically correct, accounts for TVM |
| Comparison of mutually exclusive projects of different sizes | Incremental ROR or NPV with re-investment assumption | NPV alone can mislead on scale |
| Quick screening of many small projects | Payback Period | Easy to compute, intuitive |
| Learning-cost-sensitive high-volume production | Learning Curve | Models cost reduction with scale |
| Evaluating safety investments with intangible benefits (lives saved) | BCR + Sensitivity Analysis | Captures non-monetary benefits |
| Choosing between in-house and vendor | Make-or-Buy | Direct unit-cost comparison |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Economic Analysis Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Product Concept with Safety Features] --> B[Step 1: Identify Cost Elements]
    B --> C[Classify Fixed vs Variable]
    B --> D[Classify Direct vs Indirect]
    B --> E[Identify Sunk vs Opportunity Cost]
    C --> F[Step 2: Build Cost Model TC = FC + VCxQ]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Step 3: Estimate Demand and Price]
    G --> H[Step 4: Compute Break-Even Point Q_BEP]
    H --> I{Forecast Q exceeds Q_BEP?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Step 5: Construct Multi-Year Cash Flow]
    I -- No --> K[Reject or Redesign for Lower FC]
    J --> L[Step 6: Apply TVM Discounting]
    L --> M[Step 7: Compute NPV, IRR, BCR, Payback]
    M --> N{All Decision Criteria Satisfied?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Step 8: Sensitivity Analysis on Key Inputs]
    N -- No --> P[Iterate Design / Pricing]
    O --> Q{Project Robust to ±20 percent Variation?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Step 9: Make-or-Buy Decision for Safety Sub-Assemblies]
    Q -- No --> P
    R --> S[Final Investment Recommendation to Management]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Interaction Between Safety Engineering and Economic Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Hazard Identification] --> S2[Risk Assessment F x S]
    S2 --> S3[Safety Countermeasure Design]
    S3 --> E1[Cost Estimation of Safety Measure]

    E2 --> D{Decision: Accept Safety Cost?}
    D -- Yes --> S4[Implement Mitigation]
    D -- No --> S5[Re-design or Accept Residual Risk]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The loops in the third diagram illustrate a crucial principle from the KTU Module 4 syllabus—economic analysis is **not a one-time gate**, but a *continuous feedback loop* between safety engineering and finance, lasting the entire product life-cycle.
