$w_{lead}=0.15$, $w_{cult}=0.10$, $w_{team}=0.15$, $w_{risk}=0.15$, $w_{scope}=0.10$, $w_{sup}=0.10$, $w_{chg}=0.05$. **Compute the Qualitative Success Index and recommend action.** --- ### Step 1 — Write the General Formula $$ P_{success} = \sum_{i=1}^{8} w_i \cdot s_i $$ Each term $w_i \cdot s_i$ represents the *weighted contribution* of factor $i$ to the overall qualitative health. --- ### Step 2 — Compute Each Weighted Term $$ w_{stake} \cdot s_{stake} = 0.20 \times 4 = 0.80 $$ $$ w_{lead} \cdot s_{lead} = 0.15 \times 3 = 0.45 $$ $$ w_{cult} \cdot s_{cult} = 0.10 \times 5 = 0.50 $$ $$ w_{team} \cdot s_{team} = 0.15 \times 4 = 0.60 $$ $$ w_{risk} \cdot s_{risk} = 0.15 \times 2 = 0.30 $$ $$ w_{scope} \cdot s_{scope} = 0.10 \times 3 = 0.30 $$ $$ w_{sup} \cdot s_{sup} = 0.10 \times 4 = 0.40 $$ $$ w_{chg} \cdot s_{chg} = 0.05 \times 3 = 0.15 $$ --- ### Step 3 — Sum All Weighted Contributions $$ P_{success} = 0.80 + 0.45 + 0.50 + 0.60 + 0.30 + 0.30 + 0.40 + 0.15 $$ $$ P_{success} = 3.50 $$ --- ### Step 4 — Interpret the Result Referring to the **2.2 Band Table**, a score of **3.50** falls in the **3.40 – 4.19 "Good" band**. **Qualitative Conclusion:** The project is viable but has qualitative weaknesses — specifically in *Risk Perception* (score 2) and *Scope Clarity* (score 3). --- ### Step 5 — Recommended Interventions (Engineering Reasoning) 1. **Conduct a structured Risk Workshop** using a **premortem technique** to lift $s_{risk}$ from 2 toward 4. 2. **Freeze the charge-time specification** through a formal change-control board meeting to lift $s_{scope}$ from 3 toward 4. 3. **Re-score after 2 weeks**; target $P_{success} \geq 3.80$ before entering the **prototype fabrication Stage-Gate.** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Validation Note:** This worked example demonstrates the **three expected cognitive moves** in a 14-mark answer — (i) correct formula selection, (ii) correct substitution and arithmetic, and (iii) **engineering judgement-based recommendation**. Examiners specifically award the final 2–3 marks for the recommendation, not just the math. --- ### Step 6 — Sensitivity Check (Bonus Higher-Order Reasoning) A **sensitivity analysis** tests how much the index changes if one factor improves by 1 point. If $s_{risk}$ improves from 2 to 3, the new contribution becomes: $$ w_{risk} \cdot s_{risk}^{new} = 0.15 \times 3 = 0.45 $$ Therefore the new index becomes: $$ P_{success}^{new} = 3.50 - 0.30 + 0.45 = 3.65 $$ **Insight:** Improving risk perception alone shifts the project from *Good* toward *Good+*, demonstrating that **risk perception carries high leverage** despite its moderate weight — because the *current* score is the lowest. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Qualitative Factor Influence Network The following Mermaid diagram visualizes the **8 pillars** and their **interconnections**, mapped exactly to the Analytical Matrix in Section 3.1. ```mermaid graph TD A[Leadership and Governance] B[Stakeholder Engagement] C[Organizational Safety Culture] D[Team Competence and Morale] E[Risk Perception] F[Scope Clarity] G[Supplier Relationship Quality] H[Change Management Discipline] R[Qualitative Project Success Index] A -->|Strong tone at top| C A -->|Vision and priority| D A -->|Decision authority| E A -->|Approves changes| H B -->|Requirements clarity| F B -->|Vendor coordination| G B -->|User feedback loop| E C -->|Reporting openness| E C -->|Just culture| D C -->|Process adherence| H D -->|Hazard identification| E D -->|Discipline| H E -->|Risk drives change| H E -->|Risk drives scope| F F -->|Frozen baseline| H F -->|Stable specs| G G -->|Component quality| R H -->|Verified state| R C -->|Safety climate| R A -->|Leadership| R B -->|Stakeholders| R D -->|Team| R E -->|Risk view| R F -->|Scope| R classDef pillar fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000; classDef outcome fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:3px,color:#000; class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H pillar; class R outcome; ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** The **blue nodes** are the 8 qualitative pillars. The **green node (R)** is the resulting Qualitative Project Success Index. Arrows indicate the *direction of influence*. Notice the **centrality of Culture (C)** and **Leadership (A)** — they feed the outcome node and also influence most other pillars. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Stage-Gate Safety Decision Flow This block-level flow shows **how qualitative factors gate the project** at each stage, an exam-favorite schematic. ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Project Concept Stage] -->|Qualitative Gate 1
Stakeholder and Scope Check| P2[Design Development Stage] P2 -->|Qualitative Gate 2
Risk and Culture Check| P3[Prototype Build Stage] P3 -->|Qualitative Gate 3
Team and Supplier Check| P4[Verification and Validation] P4 -->|Qualitative Gate 4
Change and Leadership Review| P5[Product Launch] P1 -.->|If fails| R1[Restart with new scope] P2 -.->|If fails| R2[Return to concept redesign] P3 -.->|If fails| R3[Re-enter design with risk workshop] P4 -.->|If fails| R4[Hold launch; conduct change audit] classDef gateOk fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000; classDef stageOk fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000; classDef outcomeOk fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:3px,color:#000; class P1,P2,P3,P4 stageOk; class P5 outcomeOk; class R1,R2,R3,R4 gateOk; ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** Each stage transition is guarded by a **Qualitative Gate** — a structured review of the relevant qualitative factors before the project is allowed to advance. If a gate fails, the project loops back rather than proceeding with hidden qualitative debt. --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Failure Causal Chain When Qualitative Factors Are Ignored This diagram is the *inverse* perspective — highly valuable for KTU "Discuss the consequences" type questions. ```mermaid graph LR Q1[Weak Leadership] --> Q2[Scope Creep Allowed] Q3[Poor Safety Culture] --> Q4[Near-miss Reporting Suppressed] Q5[Low Risk Perception] --> Q6[Unacceptable Hazard Accepted] Q7[Weak Change Mgmt] --> Q8[Undocumented Design State] Q2 --> F1[Unverified Feature Shipped] Q4 --> F1 Q6 --> F2[Residual Risk to User] Q8 --> F2 F1 --> F3[Field Failure or Safety Incident] F2 --> F3 F3 --> X[Project Failure: Recall, Injury, Litigation] classDef cause fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000; classDef fail fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000; class Q1,Q3,Q5,Q7 cause; class Q2,Q4,Q6,Q8 cause; class F1,F2,F3,X fail; ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** Each qualitative weakness is a **latent root cause**. They converge into observable project failures. This is the *Swiss Cheese Model* (Reason, 1990) applied qualitatively to product safety projects. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PESFT747, Module 4]** ### Question A1 (3 Marks) *"Define qualitative factors in the context of engineering project success. Give three examples relevant to safety in product design."* **Model Answer:** Qualitative factors are **non-numeric, descriptive variables** that influence project outcomes through human behavior, organizational dynamics, and stakeholder perception rather than through direct measurement. They complement quantitative factors such as cost, schedule, and technical specifications. **Three examples relevant to safety in product design:** 1. **Safety culture** — whether employees feel psychologically safe to report near-misses without fear of blame. 2. **Risk perception** — the team's collective judgement of how likely and severe a hazard is, which drives ALARP decisions. 3. **Communication effectiveness** — clarity and timeliness of safety-critical information across design silos (mechanical, electrical, software). > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Comment:** Award full 3 marks only if the student gives a *definition* plus *three distinct* examples. Generic answers like "teamwork" alone fetch only 2 marks. --- ### Question A2 (3 Marks) *"Why is leadership quality considered the most influential qualitative factor in a product safety project?"* **Model Answer:** Leadership quality is considered most influential because the project leader (i) sets the **priority hierarchy** between safety, cost, and schedule; (ii) allocates resources for hazard analysis and verification; (iii) decides whether to **halt production** when a safety concern is raised; and (iv) shapes the **safety culture** through visible behavior. Even with strong quantitative safety metrics, weak leadership can override them under deadline pressure, leading to acceptance of unverified designs. Hence leadership acts as the **central leverage point** in the qualitative factor network. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Essay / Analytical Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Model Paper Pattern, PESFT747, Module 4]** > *(Note: KTU Part B carries 14 marks; students must attempt one full question with sub-parts.)* --- ### Question A — 14 Marks **A.** *(7 Marks)* Explain the **eight pillars of qualitative factors** that influence project success. Illustrate each pillar with **one engineering safety-relevant example** from a product design context. **B.** *(7 Marks)* A student design team is developing a **portable oxygen concentrator for hospital use** (a Class IIB medical device under Indian MDR 2017). The faculty mentor scores the qualitative project health using the weighted 8-pillar Likert framework. Given the data: | Factor | Weight $w_i$ | Score $s_i$ (1–5) | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Leadership | 0.15 | 4 | | Stakeholders | 0.20 | 3 | | Safety Culture | 0.15 | 5 | | Team Competence | 0.10 | 4 | | Risk Perception | 0.15 | 2 | | Scope Clarity | 0.10 | 4 | | Supplier Quality | 0.10 | 3 | | Change Management | 0.05 | 4 | **Compute the Qualitative Success Index and recommend two specific interventions to bring the project into the "Excellent" band ( $\geq 4.20$ ).** --- ### Model Solution — Part A (7 Marks) The eight pillars are: 1. **Leadership & Governance** — *Example:* A project manager halting a release because biocompatibility test reports are pending. 2. **Stakeholder Engagement** — *Example:* Regular clinician feedback sessions revealing unintended oxygen flow-rate settings. 3. **Safety Culture** — *Example:* A "Just Culture" policy where reporting a near-miss does not attract penalty. 4. **Team Competence** — *Example:* Cross-trained designers who understand IEC 60601-1 medical electrical safety standards. 5. **Risk Perception** — *Example:* Recognizing that a fire-risk under single-fault condition is *intolerable*, not *acceptable*. 6. **Scope Clarity** — *Example:* A locked Requirements Traceability Matrix mapping each clause of ISO 14971 to a verified design output. 7. **Supplier Quality** — *Example:* Audit of the sieve-bed manufacturer for batch-to-batch consistency. 8. **Change Management** — *Example:* Engineering Change Order required for any firmware update affecting flow algorithm. *(Award 0.5 mark per pillar explanation + 0.5 mark per example = up to 7 marks. Examiner expects at least 6 pillars with examples for full marks.)* --- ### Model Solution — Part B (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Apply the formula:** $$ P_{success} = \sum_{i=1}^{8} w_i \cdot s_i $$ **Step 2 — Substitute values:** $$ P_{success} = (0.15)(4) + (0.20)(3) + (0.15)(5) + (0.10)(4) + (0.15)(2) + (0.10)(4) + (0.10)(3) + (0.05)(4) $$ Compute each term individually: $$ (0.15)(4) = 0.60 $$ $$ (0.20)(3) = 0.60 $$ $$ (0.15)(5) = 0.75 $$ $$ (0.10)(4) = 0.40 $$ $$ (0.15)(2) = 0.30 $$ $$ (0.10)(4) = 0.40 $$ $$ (0.10)(3) = 0.30 $$ $$ (0.05)(4) = 0.20 $$ **Step 3 — Sum the contributions:** $$ P_{success} = 0.60 + 0.60 + 0.75 + 0.40 + 0.30 + 0.40 + 0.30 + 0.20 $$ $$ P_{success} = 3.55 $$ **Step 4 — Interpret using the Band Table:** A score of **3.55** lies in the **3.40 – 4.19 "Good" band** — project is viable but qualitative risks remain, especially in Risk Perception (score = 2). **Step 5 — Compute the gap to "Excellent" (≥ 4.20):** $$ \Delta P = 4.20 - 3.55 = 0.65 $$ **Step 6 — Recommend two specific interventions:** 1. **Risk Perception Workshop:** Conduct a formal premortem session with the clinical and design teams, with an independent safety expert facilitator. **Target:** Raise $s_{risk}$ from 2 to 4. This increases the index by: $$ \Delta_1 = 0.15 \times (4 - 2) = 0.30 $$ 2. **Stakeholder Deep-Engagement Cycle:** Implement bi-weekly clinician-in-the-loop usability sessions and re-baseline the requirements. **Target:** Raise $s_{stake}$ from 3 to 5. This increases the index by: $$ \Delta_2 = 0.20 \times (5 - 3) = 0.40 $$ **Step 7 — Verify combined effect:** $$ P_{success}^{new} = 3.55 + 0.30 + 0.40 = 4.25 \geq 4.20 $$ Hence, the project moves into the **"Excellent" band** after these two targeted interventions. *(Mark split: 2 marks for correct formula, 2 marks for correct arithmetic, 2 marks for band interpretation, 1 mark for engineering-grounded interventions.)* --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative) **A.** *(7 Marks)* With the aid of a **labelled block diagram**, explain how **poor change management** and **weak safety culture** together create a *latent failure pathway* that can cause a product safety incident. Use a real engineering example. **B.** *(7 Marks)* Discuss **five actionable measures** that a project manager of a safety-critical product (e.g., an EV battery pack) can implement to strengthen the qualitative project health, mapping each measure to the relevant pillar(s) from the 8-pillar framework. --- ### Model Solution — Part A (7 Marks) **Block Diagram (drawn in exam; textual reproduction below):** $$ \text{[Weak Safety Culture]} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{[Near-miss suppressed]} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{[Unknown hazard persists]} $$ $$ \text{[Weak Change Management]} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{[Undocumented design change]} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{[Verification bypassed]} $$ The two pathways converge at **Unsafe Design State in Field**, which under operational stress leads to **Safety Incident**. **Engineering Example — Samsung Galaxy Note 7 (2016):** - The lithium-ion cells from Supplier A had electrode misalignment and welding defects causing internal short circuits. - **Weak Change Management:** Samsung switched to Supplier B mid-development without a full re-qualification of the safety verification regime. - **Weak Safety Culture:** Early field reports of "battery overheating" and a few fires were downplayed internally as isolated incidents rather than triggering an immediate hazard re-analysis. - **Outcome:** Combined qualitative weakness led to a global recall costing **>$5 billion** and severe brand damage. **Mark split:** 3 marks for the block diagram and pathway explanation, 2 marks for the real example, 2 marks for the *corrective lesson* (mandate independent re-qualification for any supplier change; mandate culture of immediate escalation on any field report). --- ### Model Solution — Part B (7 Marks) **Five Actionable Measures Mapped to Pillars:** | # | Measure | Pillar(s) Strengthened | |:---:|:---|:---| | 1 | Establish a **Stage-Gate Safety Review Board** with mandatory independent safety expert sign-off before each gate. | **Leadership, Culture, Change Mgmt** | | 2 | Implement a **No-Blame Near-Miss Reporting System** with monthly dashboard review by top management. | **Safety Culture, Risk Perception** | | 3 | Freeze the **Requirements Traceability Matrix** after concept stage and route any change through a formal CCB (Configuration Control Board). | **Scope Clarity, Change Mgmt** | | 4 | Conduct **Supplier Audits and Joint Design Reviews** for all safety-critical components (cells, BMS, thermal fuses). | **Supplier Quality, Team Competence** | | 5 | Run a **Quarterly Premortem Workshop** where the team imagines the project has failed and works backward to identify hidden qualitative risks. | **Risk Perception, Team Cohesion** | **Conclusion (1 mark):** These measures shift the project's qualitative health from "Marginal/At Risk" toward "Good/Excellent" by addressing the most leveraged pillars — **Leadership, Culture, and Risk Perception** — first, since they have the highest outbound influence in the qualitative network. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 Questions:** > > 1. **Treating qualitative factors as "soft" and therefore undeserving of quantification.** Students who refuse to score them lose 3–4 marks. KTU expects at minimum a *Likert-scale scoring* demonstration. > 2. **Giving generic answers like "good communication is important" without mapping to a project stage or pillar.** Always anchor to a specific pillar and stage. > 3. **Skipping the recommendation step in numerical problems.** The recommendation step carries **1–2 marks** in any 14-mark answer. A perfectly computed index with no recommendation is treated as an incomplete answer. > 4. **Forgetting to use $\sum$ notation or the explicit formula.** Writing only the final number without showing the formula forfeits 1 mark. > 5. **Ignoring the safety-design context.** PESFT747 specifically evaluates *safety in product design*, not generic project management. Always tie back to a safety hazard, standard (e.g., ISO 14971, IEC 60601, ISO 26262), or a recall case study. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Influence of Qualitative Factors on Project Success** - ☐ Qualitative factors are **non-numeric** variables (people, culture, perception) that govern whether a project *will* succeed. - ☐ Eight core pillars: **Leadership, Stakeholders, Safety Culture, Team, Risk Perception, Scope Clarity, Suppliers, Change Management.** - ☐ Use the **5-point Likert weighted scoring** framework: $P_{success} = \sum_{i=1}^{8} w_i \cdot s_i$, with weights normalized so $\sum w_i = 1$. - ☐ Interpret $P_{success}$ using the **5-band health table**: Excellent (≥ 4.20), Good (3.40–4.19), Marginal, At Risk, Critical. - ☐ **Leadership and Culture are the most central pillars** in the influence network — prioritize interventions there. - ☐ **Risk perception carries high leverage** when its current score is low (sensitivity check is a high-value higher-order move). - ☐ **Change management + weak safety culture** is the classic Swiss-Cheese latent failure pathway to product safety incidents. - ☐ Always **anchor your answer in a safety context** — recall cases, safety standards (ISO 14971 / IEC 60601 / ISO 26262), or hazard analysis. - ☐ In numerical answers, **show the formula, substitution, arithmetic, band interpretation, and recommendation** — all five steps are required for full marks. - ☐ Use `\vert` instead of `$\mid$` or `$\vert x \vert$` with bare pipes when writing inside markdown tables. ---

topics.title: Qualitative Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT747 - SAFETY IN PRODUCT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Qualitative Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Qualitative Analysis** in the context of safety in product design refers to a systematic, non-numerical (or semi-numerical) set of hazard identification and risk evaluation techniques used during the conceptual, preliminary, and detailed design phases of a product's lifecycle. It relies on engineering judgment, structured brainstorming, predefined checklists, and standardized keyword-driven protocols to uncover potential failure modes, hazards, and operability problems associated with a product, system, or process.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT747) framework, Qualitative Analysis is positioned as a foundational pillar of the Safety Engineering workflow, preceding quantitative methods such as Fault Tree Analysis (quantitative), Event Tree Analysis, or probabilistic risk assessment. It provides the **engineering "screening layer"** that filters out obvious hazards before committing resources to expensive numerical modeling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** Qualitative Analysis is a *judgment-based, structured, systematic* hazard identification and risk categorization technique that classifies hazards by **severity**, **likelihood**, and **detectability** without resorting to deep numerical probability computation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are a **doctor performing a first-round medical check-up** on a patient. Before ordering expensive MRI scans or blood tests (these are like *quantitative analysis*), the doctor first asks questions, observes symptoms, checks reflexes, and reviews the patient's history. This initial screening — based on experience and structured questions — is exactly what qualitative analysis does for a product. The doctor (engineer) uses keywords like "Fever? Cough? Pain?" just as a safety engineer uses keywords like "No flow? Reverse flow? More of?" in a HAZOP study.

| Aspect | Doctor's Check-Up | Safety Engineer's Qualitative Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Patient visits | New product design phase begins |
| **Tool** | Symptom checklist, stethoscope | HAZOP, FMEA, What-If, Checklists |
| **Output** | Preliminary diagnosis | Preliminary hazard list & risk ranking |
| **Next Step** | Quantitative tests (blood/MRI) | Quantitative analysis (FTA, ETA) |

### 1.3 Core Terminology Anchors

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary You Must Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE:**
> - **Hazard:** A potential source of harm (intrinsic property).
> - **Risk:** The combination of the probability of occurrence of harm and the severity of that harm. Mathematically expressed as **Risk = Severity × Likelihood**.
> - **Hazard Identification (HAZID):** The process of finding, listing, and characterizing hazards.
> - **Top Event:** The undesired event at the top of a fault tree (used in both qualitative and quantitative FTA).
> - **Cut Set:** A combination of basic events that, when they all occur, cause the top event.
> - **Risk Matrix:** A semi-qualitative 2D grid mapping Severity vs. Likelihood.

### 1.4 Why Qualitative Analysis Exists in the KTU Safety Curriculum

In product design, **over 70% of design safety decisions are made before any numerical data is available.** At this early stage, you cannot yet calculate failure rates (λ in failures/hour) because no field data exists for a brand-new product. Qualitative methods are the *only viable option* during these formative phases. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically places Qualitative Analysis in Module 4 because it consolidates all the screening techniques that any product designer must apply *before* committing to fabrication.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Qualitative Analysis Techniques

Qualitative Analysis is **not a single method** but a *family of techniques*. The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes the following primary members:

#### 2.1.1 Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)

- **Purpose:** Earliest design-phase screening of system-level hazards.
- **When Used:** Concept and early development phase — *before detailed drawings exist*.
- **How it Works:** A multidisciplinary team reviews energy sources, materials, operating environments, and interfaces. Hazards are listed and ranked on a coarse 4-tier scale (Catastrophic / Critical / Marginal / Negligible).
- **Output:** PHA Worksheet listing Hazards → Causes → Effects → Recommended Actions.

#### 2.1.2 Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP)

- **Purpose:** Deviation-based identification of process hazards using *guide words*.
- **When Used:** Detailed design phase when Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs) are available.
- **How it Works:** Apply standard **guide words** (No, Not, More, Less, As Well As, Part Of, Reverse, Other Than) to each process parameter (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level) and node.
- **Output:** Cause–Consequence–Action register.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HAZOP Guide Words Table (HIGH YIELD for KTU 2024):**

| Guide Word | Meaning with respect to "Flow" |
|---|---|
| **NO / NOT** | No flow (blocked line, pump failure) |
| **MORE** | More flow (valve stuck open, over-pumping) |
| **LESS** | Less flow (partial blockage, leak) |
| **REVERSE** | Reverse flow (backflow, check valve failure) |
| **AS WELL AS** | Contamination (air in liquid line) |
| **PART OF** | Reduced concentration (dilution) |
| **OTHER THAN** | Wrong fluid present (misrouting) |

#### 2.1.3 Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)

- **Purpose:** Component-level systematic study of *every possible failure mode* of every part.
- **When Used:** Detailed design and manufacturing phase.
- **How it Works:** For each component, list: Failure Mode → Effect on System → Cause → Current Controls → Recommended Actions.
- **Output:** FMEA Worksheet + **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**.

#### 2.1.4 What-If Analysis

- **Purpose:** Open-ended brainstorming using "What if..." questions.
- **When Used:** Any phase, especially when time/resources are limited.
- **How it Works:** Team asks hypothetical questions: *"What if the power fails during operation?"*, *"What if a user inserts the battery reversed?"*

#### 2.1.5 Checklist Analysis

- **Purpose:** Verify compliance against known standards, codes, and prior incident lists.
- **When Used:** As a final verification or when a very mature design is being reviewed.
- **Limitation:** Cannot identify *new* hazards — only verifies absence of *known* ones.

### 2.2 The Risk Matrix — The Heart of Qualitative Risk Ranking

The **Risk Matrix** is the central visual tool used across all qualitative techniques to translate subjective engineering judgment into a comparable risk rating.

**Standard KTU/Industry 5×5 Risk Matrix:**

| Severity ↓ \ Likelihood → | **L1 - Rare** | **L2 - Unlikely** | **L3 - Possible** | **L4 - Likely** | **L5 - Almost Certain** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S5 - Catastrophic** | M | H | H | E | E |
| **S4 - Major** | M | M | H | H | E |
| **S3 - Moderate** | L | M | M | H | H |
| **S2 - Minor** | L | L | M | M | H |
| **S1 - Negligible** | L | L | L | M | M |

Where **L = Low Risk (Accept)**, **M = Medium Risk (Monitor)**, **H = High Risk (Mitigate)**, **E = Extreme Risk (Stop / Redesign)**.

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix above uses `\vert` or word equivalents because Markdown tables cannot contain the `|` pipe character inside cells. For inline math describing the function `Severity × Likelihood`, we use $\vert S \vert \cdot \vert L \vert$.

### 2.3 The Risk Priority Number (RPN) — FMEA's Key Output

For **FMEA**, qualitative risk is expressed numerically via the Risk Priority Number. While it *looks* quantitative, KTU classifies it under qualitative analysis because the inputs (S, O, D scores) are expert-assigned ordinal ratings, not measured probabilities.

$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$

Where:
- $S$ = **Severity** (1 to 10): How serious is the effect?
- $O$ = **Occurrence** (1 to 10): How frequently does the failure mode occur?
- $D$ = **Detection** (1 to 10): How difficult is it to detect before reaching the customer? *(Note: A higher D score means POORER detectability)*.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — direct questions (3 marks) and FMEA numericals (14 marks) are routinely asked.**

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Range / Units | Method Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R$ | Risk Rating | L / M / H / E (Categorical) | All qualitative methods |
| $S$ | Severity Score | 1 to 10 (ordinal) | FMEA |
| $O$ | Occurrence Score | 1 to 10 (ordinal) | FMEA |
| $D$ | Detection Score | 1 to 10 (ordinal) | FMEA |
| $\text{RPN}$ | Risk Priority Number | 1 to 1000 | FMEA |
| $\text{SIL}$ | Safety Integrity Level | 1 to 4 (categorical) | IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 |
| $P_f$ | Probability of Failure on Demand | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-5}$ | Semi-quantitative |
| $\lambda$ | Failure rate | failures/hour | Used in Quantitative FTA |

### 2.5 Hierarchy of Application in the Product Design Lifecycle

A crucial conceptual understanding for KTU 2024: Qualitative techniques are applied in a **specific sequence** during the product lifecycle:

$$\text{Concept} \rightarrow \text{PHA} \rightarrow \text{What-If} \rightarrow \text{HAZOP} \rightarrow \text{FMEA} \rightarrow \text{Checklist} \rightarrow \text{Quantitative FTA/ETA}$$

Each successive technique is *more detailed* and requires *more design data*. PHA works without any drawings; HAZOP needs P&IDs; FMEA needs part lists; quantitative FTA needs failure rate databases (like MIL-HDBK-217, OREDA, or IEEE 500).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example: FMEA on a Lithium-Ion Power Bank (KTU Style 14-Mark Problem)

> **Problem Context:** A consumer electronics company is conducting a Design FMEA (DFMEA) on a 20,000 mAh Lithium-Ion power bank intended for airline carry-on use. Three components are flagged for detailed risk analysis: (i) the Lithium Cell, (ii) the USB-C Charging Port, and (iii) the Battery Management System (BMS) IC. Compute the RPN for each, rank them, and recommend engineering actions.

#### Step 1: Build the Severity (S) Scale

The KTU standard 1–10 scale (qualitative but numerically tabulated):

| Score | Severity Level | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Negligible | No injury, no functional loss |
| 5 | Moderate | Minor injury OR recoverable malfunction |
| 8 | Major | Serious injury OR system damage |
| 10 | Catastrophic | Loss of life OR total destruction |

For the Lithium Cell failure mode "Internal short circuit":
- The cell can undergo **thermal runaway**, venting toxic electrolyte, and causing fire.
- Severity rating: $S = 9$ (Catastrophic is possible; 9 is "Catastrophic with marginal chance of survival").

#### Step 2: Build the Occurrence (O) Scale

| Score | Occurrence Level | Failure Rate (per million parts) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Very Low | $\leq 1$ |
| 5 | Moderate | $\approx 100$ |
| 7 | High | $\approx 1{,}000$ |
| 10 | Very High | $\geq 50{,}000$ |

For internal short circuit in a properly manufactured Li-ion cell, industry data suggests approximately $1$ to $10$ per million. We assign $O = 4$.

#### Step 3: Build the Detection (D) Scale

| Score | Detection Difficulty |
|---|---|
| 1 | Almost Certain — automated 100% screening catches it |
| 5 | Moderate — sampled inspection catches it |
| 10 | Absolute Uncertainty — no inspection can catch it |

Internal shorts are notoriously hard to detect post-manufacturing (they often develop from dendrite growth over time). Assign $D = 8$.

#### Step 4: Calculate the RPN for the Lithium Cell

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{\text{Cell}} &= S \times O \times D \\
&= 9 \times 4 \times 8 \\
&= 288
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5: Repeat for the USB-C Port

- Failure mode: Bent pin causing arc / fire
- $S = 8$, $O = 3$, $D = 4$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{\text{USB-C}} &= 8 \times 3 \times 4 \\
&= 96
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6: Repeat for the BMS IC

- Failure mode: BMS fails to disconnect cell on over-voltage
- $S = 9$, $O = 2$, $D = 6$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{\text{BMS}} &= 9 \times 2 \times 6 \\
&= 108
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 7: Rank and Interpret

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rank Order: } & \text{Cell (288)} \; > \; \text{BMS (108)} \; > \; \text{USB-C (96)} \\
\text{Industry Action Threshold: } & \text{RPN} \geq 100 \text{ requires mitigation}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 8: Engineering Recommendation

The **Lithium Cell** and **BMS IC** both exceed the action threshold. Required mitigations:

1. Add a **positive temperature coefficient (PTC) resettable fuse** in series with the cell (lowers $D$).
2. Use a **flame-retardant V-0 rated** enclosure (lowers effective $S$ to $\leq 7$).
3. Require the BMS to meet **ASIL-C** under ISO 26262 if used in EV-grade products, or **SIL-2** under IEC 61508 (lowers $O$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Many students forget that RPN is NOT just severity. A low-severity failure that occurs **very frequently** and is **undetectable** can have a *higher* RPN than a catastrophic one-off failure. Always compute **all three** scores before ranking.

### 3.2 Worked Example: HAZOP Node Analysis

**Node:** Jacketed reactor with steam heating.
**Process Parameter:** Temperature.
**Guide Word applied: MORE**

We systematically fill the standard HAZOP worksheet:

| Column | Entry |
|---|---|
| **Node** | Reactor R-101 |
| **Parameter** | Temperature |
| **Guide Word** | MORE |
| **Cause** | Steam control valve stuck open; sensor failure |
| **Consequence** | Exothermic reaction accelerates → runaway |
| **Safeguards** | Pressure relief valve, high-temperature interlock |
| **Action** | Add redundant temperature transmitter + SIL-1 alarm |

This systematic worksheet is the **canonical KTU answer format** for any 14-mark HAZOP question.

### 3.3 Risk Matrix Worked Example

A mechanical arm in a packaging machine can crush a worker's hand (**S = S5 - Catastrophic**). The probability based on near-miss reports is "Likely" (**L = L4**).

Reading from the master matrix above:

$$
\text{Cell}(S5, L4) = \text{Extreme (E)}
$$

Action: **Stop operation, install light curtain, redesign guard interlocks, redo CE/UL risk assessment.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Qualitative Analysis Method Selection Flowchart

This flowchart guides engineers in **selecting the correct qualitative technique** based on the design phase and available data. It is a high-yield diagram for KTU 14-mark descriptive questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Product Design] --> B{Available Design Data?}
    B -- No drawings only --> C[PHA: Preliminary Hazard Analysis]
    B -- P and ID available --> D[HAZOP Study]
    B -- Bill of Materials ready --> E[FMEA]
    B -- Limited time --> F[What-If Analysis]
    B -- Mature standard product --> G[Checklist Review]

    C --> H{Hazards Found?}
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H

    H -- Yes --> I[Build Risk Matrix]
    H -- No --> J[Proceed to Detailed Design]

    I --> K{Risk Level?}
    K -- L Low --> J
    K -- M Medium --> L[Document and Monitor]
    K -- H High --> M[Engineering Mitigation Required]
    K -- E Extreme --> N[Stop and Redesign]
```

### 4.2 FMEA Workflow Block Diagram

This block diagram represents the **RPN computation pipeline** at the heart of every FMEA exercise. It is functionally equivalent to the data flow you would draw on a whiteboard during a KTU viva.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Component List] --> B[Identify Failure Modes]
    B --> C[Determine Effects]
    C --> D[Identify Causes]
    D --> E[Assign Severity S]
    D --> F[Assign Occurrence O]
    D --> G[Assign Detection D]
    E --> H[Compute RPN]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{RPN Threshold Exceeded?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Recommend Actions]
    I -- No --> K[Document as Acceptable]
    J --> L[Re-evaluate After Mitigation]
    L --> I
```

### 4.3 Risk Matrix Schematic Representation

Below is the schematic layout of a typical Risk Assessment Matrix as it appears in design review documents. It visually reinforces the **Severity × Likelihood** concept.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SeverityAxis[Severity Increases Upward]
        S1[S1 Negligible]
        S2[S2 Minor]
        S3[S3 Moderate]
        S4[S4 Major]
        S5[S5 Catastrophic]
    end
    subgraph LikelihoodAxis[Likelihood Increases Rightward]
        L1[L1 Rare]
        L2[L2 Unlikely]
        L3[L3 Possible]
        L4[L4 Likely]
        L5[L5 Almost Certain]
    end
    S5 -.->|Catastrophic vs Likely gives Extreme| X[Extreme Risk Cell]
    L4 -.-> X
```

### 4.4 Hierarchy of Risk Reduction (KTU Favorite Diagram)

This is the **"Inherent → Added → Residual"** risk reduction hierarchy from **ISO 12100**, which is a direct KTU 2024 Module 4 high-priority topic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inherent Risk of Design] --> B{Step 1: Inherently Safe Design}
    B --> C[Eliminate hazard or reduce energy]
    C --> D{Step 2: Safeguards}
    D --> E[Guards, interlocks, sensors]
    E --> F{Step 3: Information for Use}
    F --> G[Warnings, labels, training]
    G --> H[Residual Risk Acceptable?]
    H -- No --> B
    H -- Yes --> I[Release Product]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1 (3 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**"Differentiate between Qualitative and Quantitative Analysis in the context of safety in product design."**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

1. **Qualitative Analysis** uses engineering judgment, structured keywords, and categorical scales (e.g., L/M/H/E). It identifies hazards *without* numerical probability. Examples: HAZOP, FMEA, What-If.
2. **Quantitative Analysis** uses numerical failure rate data ($\lambda$), probability ($P$), and Boolean algebra to compute exact system reliability. Examples: Quantitative FTA, ETA, Markov models.
3. Qualitative methods are applied **earlier** in the design lifecycle when no statistical data exists; quantitative methods are applied **later** when component failure data is available.
4. Qualitative output is a **risk category** (Low/Medium/High); quantitative output is a **numerical probability** (e.g., $P_f = 2.4 \times 10^{-4}$ per year).

---

#### **Question A2 (3 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**"List any six standard HAZOP guide words and briefly explain their meaning with respect to the process parameter 'Flow'."**

**Model Answer:**

1. **NO / NOT** → No flow (line blockage, pump failure).
2. **MORE** → More flow than intended (valve stuck open).
3. **LESS** → Less flow than intended (partial blockage, leak).
4. **REVERSE** → Flow in opposite direction (check-valve failure).
5. **AS WELL AS** → Extra phase present (air ingress into liquid line).
6. **PART OF** → Reduced concentration of intended material (dilution).
7. **OTHER THAN** → Completely different fluid (cross-connection).

*(Any six with correct flow-context meaning = full 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question B1 — Option A (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Model Paper Adapted]`**

**(a)** Explain the methodology of **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** in detail. Describe the meaning of **Severity (S)**, **Occurrence (O)**, and **Detection (D)** scores. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a **hydraulic press** used in an automotive workshop, an FMEA study identified the following failure modes. Compute the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** for each and recommend engineering actions for any item exceeding an RPN threshold of **150**.

| Component | Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hydraulic Hose | Burst under pressure | 9 | 4 | 5 |
| Solenoid Valve | Stuck open | 7 | 5 | 4 |
| Pressure Gauge | False low reading | 8 | 3 | 7 |
| Emergency Stop | Mechanical jam | 10 | 2 | 3 | **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) FMEA Methodology (7 marks):**

FMEA is a systematic, component-level qualitative analysis technique. Procedure:

1. **Define the Scope:** Identify the system, boundaries, and level of analysis (System/Design/Process FMEA).
2. **Construct Block Diagrams:** Show component interconnections.
3. **List All Components and Functions:** For each, specify its intended function.
4. **Identify Failure Modes:** All possible ways each component can fail (open, short, fracture, leak, drift, etc.).
5. **Determine Effects:** Local effect, next-higher-level effect, and end effect on the customer/user.
6. **Determine Causes:** Root causes of each failure mode.
7. **Assign Severity (S):** A 1–10 rating of the seriousness of the *effect*.
8. **Assign Occurrence (O):** A 1–10 rating of how often the failure mode occurs, based on field data or expert estimate.
9. **Assign Detection (D):** A 1–10 rating of how difficult it is to *detect* the failure *before* it reaches the user (higher = harder to detect).
10. **Compute RPN:** $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$.
11. **Prioritize:** Items with RPN above threshold (typically 100 or 150) need action.
12. **Recommend Actions:** Design changes, added controls, inspection procedures.
13. **Re-evaluate:** After actions, recompute the RPN to confirm reduction.

---

**(b) RPN Calculations (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{\text{Hose}} &= 9 \times 4 \times 5 = 180 \quad (\text{Exceeds 150 — Action Required}) \\
\text{RPN}_{\text{Solenoid}} &= 7 \times 5 \times 4 = 140 \quad (\text{Below threshold — Monitor}) \\
\text{RPN}_{\text{Pressure Gauge}} &= 8 \times 3 \times 7 = 168 \quad (\text{Exceeds 150 — Action Required}) \\
\text{RPN}_{\text{E-Stop}} &= 10 \times 2 \times 3 = 60 \quad (\text{Low — Acceptable})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recommended Actions:**

- **Hose (RPN 180):** Replace standard hose with **4-ply wire-braided** hose rated $2\times$ working pressure. Add **visual wear indicator** (lowers $D$).
- **Pressure Gauge (RPN 168):** Replace analog gauge with **redundant digital transducer** + audible alarm (lowers $D$ from 7 to 3, new RPN $\approx 72$).
- **E-Stop (RPN 60):** Although RPN is low, severity = 10 demands periodic mechanical test every 6 months to ensure no jam.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse Detection ($D$) with Occurrence ($O$). *Detection* is about whether your **current control catches the failure before it reaches the customer** — *not* about the failure occurring. A self-test circuit that catches $99\%$ of failures has $D = 2$, not $O = 2$.

---

#### **Question B1 — Option B (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Model Paper Adapted]`**

**(a)** Describe the **Hazard and Operability (HAZOP)** study procedure. Explain the use of guide words with at least **two examples** for the parameter **"Pressure"**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct a **5×5 Risk Matrix** with appropriate Severity and Likelihood descriptors. A CNC milling machine has a hazard "operator entanglement in rotating spindle" with **Severity = 5 (Catastrophic)** and **Likelihood = 3 (Possible)**. Determine the risk level and recommend **two engineering controls** to reduce the risk. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) HAZOP Procedure (7 marks):**

HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study) is a structured qualitative technique developed by ICI in the 1960s, formalized in **IEC 61882**. Procedure:

1. **Define Scope and Objectives:** Establish system boundaries and study goals.
2. **Assemble the Team:** Multidisciplinary — Process Engineer, Safety Engineer, Operator, Instrument Engineer, Facilitator.
3. **Obtain Design Information:** P&IDs, process flow diagrams, operating procedures.
4. **Divide the Plant into Nodes:** Sections of pipework or equipment that share a common intent.
5. **Apply Guide Words to Parameters:** For each node, apply each guide word to each parameter (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase, etc.).
6. **Identify Deviations:** Each guide word + parameter combination generates a deviation (e.g., "MORE Pressure").
7. **Determine Causes and Consequences:** Brainstorm what could cause the deviation and what would result.
8. **Identify Existing Safeguards:** List current protections.
9. **Recommend Actions:** For unacceptable risks, propose engineering or procedural controls.
10. **Document in HAZOP Worksheet:** Standard tabular format.

**Two Examples for Parameter "Pressure":**

- **MORE Pressure:** Steam header pressure rises above design limit. *Cause:* Boiler pressure controller fails. *Consequence:* Pipe rupture. *Action:* Install pressure safety valve (PSV) and high-pressure interlock.
- **LESS Pressure:** Reactor operating below required pressure. *Cause:* Condenser over-cooling. *Consequence:* Reaction stops, batch loss. *Action:* Add low-pressure alarm and condensate flow control.

---

**(b) Risk Matrix and Decision (7 marks):**

The 5×5 Risk Matrix is as constructed in Section 2.2 above (L/M/H/E coding).

For the **CNC spindle entanglement hazard**:
- Severity $S = 5$ (Catastrophic).
- Likelihood $L = 3$ (Possible).

From the matrix cell $(S5, L3)$:

$$
\text{Risk Level} = \text{High (H)}
$$

**Two Engineering Controls:**

1. **Interlocked Guarding:** Install a Category 4 / PLe safety light curtain (per ISO 13849-1) that **immediately stops spindle rotation** when broken, with a redundant monitored contactor pair.
2. **Safe Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM):** Limit spindle RPM to ≤ 50 rpm when the guard is open (ISO 10218 collaborative robotics principle), reducing kinetic energy and entanglement risk.

After applying these controls, re-evaluate: $S$ drops to 4 (because injury now requires deliberate bypass), $L$ drops to 1 (interlocks are SIL-3 rated). New cell $(S4, L1) = \text{Medium}$, which is acceptable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Many students state "add a warning label" as their first control. Under **ISO 12100's hierarchy of risk reduction**, *information for use* (labels, training) is the **last** resort. KTU examiners deduct marks if you skip inherently safe design and safeguards. Always try: (1) Eliminate, (2) Substitute, (3) Engineer controls, (4) Training/labels.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision List — Tick Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- [ ] **Qualitative Analysis** = judgment-based, structured, systematic, no numerical probability.
- [ ] It is applied **early in design**, before quantitative FTA/ETA.
- [ ] **PHA** is the *earliest* method (concept phase, no drawings needed).
- [ ] **HAZOP** uses **guide words** (No, Not, More, Less, Reverse, As Well As, Part Of, Other Than) on **parameters** (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level).
- [ ] **FMEA** uses $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ where each is scored 1–10.
- [ ] Higher **Detection** score = **worse** detectability (counter-intuitive trap).
- [ ] Standard **Risk Matrix** is 5×5 with **L, M, H, E** categorization.
- [ ] **Risk = Severity × Likelihood** (conceptual; not a strict arithmetic product, but guides matrix placement).
- [ ] **HAZOP team** must be multidisciplinary, led by an independent **facilitator**.
- [ ] **What-If Analysis** is open-ended and best when time/data are limited.
- [ ] **Checklist Analysis** can only find *known* hazards, never *new* ones.
- [ ] **ISO 12100** hierarchy: Inherent → Safeguards → Information.
- [ ] **FMEA threshold** for action is typically **RPN ≥ 100** or **RPN ≥ 150** depending on company policy.
- [ ] Always recommend actions **with cost/benefit justification** in KTU answers.
- [ ] Draw **HAZOP worksheet tables** and **Risk Matrix grids** neatly — examiners reward visual clarity.
- [ ] FMEA is **component-level**; HAZOP is **system/node-level**; PHA is **system-level (coarse)**.
- [ ] Quantitative analysis follows qualitative once failure-rate data ($\lambda$) becomes available.
- [ ] Severity ratings must always consider **end effect on the user/customer**, not just the local component effect.
- [ ] When recommending controls, prefer **engineering controls** over administrative ones (examiners' favorite discriminator question).
